union of all powers] N1 --> Nplus[Sigma superset plus
union of powers 1 to infinity] N2 --> Nplus N3 --> Nplus N4 --> Nplus ``` --- ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Language Operations ```mermaid flowchart TD InputL1[Input Language L1] --> Union[Union Operation] InputL2[Input Language L2] --> Union InputL1 --> Concat[Concatenation L1 dot L2] InputL2 --> Concat InputL1 --> Diff[Set Difference L1 minus L2] InputL2 --> Diff InputL1 --> Star[Kleene Star L1 superset star] Star --> OutputK[Output K
infinite or empty set] Union --> OutputU[Output U] Concat --> OutputC[Output C] Diff --> OutputD[Output D] OutputK --> Result[Result Language L superset new] OutputU --> Result OutputC --> Result OutputD --> Result ``` **Explanation:** This flowchart mirrors how language operations compose. Each operation takes one or two languages and produces a new language $L_{\text{new}}$, which itself becomes a valid input to further operations — making the space of languages over $\Sigma$ a **closed algebraic structure**. --- ## 4.4 Classification of Languages by Generative Power (Chomsky Preview) Although the full hierarchy is covered later, here is a preview of how Module 1's language concepts feed into Module 2+: ```mermaid graph TD R[Recursively Enumerable Languages
Type 0] --> CSL[Context Sensitive Languages
Type 1] CSL --> CFL[Context Free Languages
Type 2] CFL --> Reg[Regular Languages
Type 3] Reg --> Fin[Finite Languages
subset of all] Fin --> L[Languages over Sigma
subsets of Sigma star] ``` The set $\Sigma^*$ contains **all** languages above; each inner ring is a proper subset of the outer ring. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define the following terms with one example each: (i) Alphabet (ii) String (iii) Language.** **Model Answer:** - **(i) Alphabet:** A finite, non-empty set of symbols, denoted by $\Sigma$. *Example:* $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$ — the binary alphabet. - **(ii) String:** A finite sequence of symbols drawn from an alphabet $\Sigma$. The empty string is denoted $\varepsilon$. *Example:* $w = 01101$ is a string over $\{0,1\}$ with $\vert w \vert = 5$. - **(iii) Language:** A set of strings over some alphabet $\Sigma$, i.e., $L \subseteq \Sigma^*$. *Example:* $L = \{0^n 1^n \mid n \geq 1\} = \{01, 0011, 000111, \ldots\}$. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is the Kleene star of a language? If $L = \{01, 1\}$, list the first 5 elements of $L^*$.** **Model Answer:** The **Kleene star** of a language $L$ is defined as: $$L^* = \bigcup_{n=0}^{\infty} L^n = L^0 \cup L^1 \cup L^2 \cup \cdots$$ It is the set of all strings obtained by concatenating zero or more strings from $L$. **Computation for $L = \{01, 1\}$:** $$L^0 = \{\varepsilon\}$$ $$L^1 = \{01, 1\}$$ $$L^2 = \{0101, 011, 101, 11\}$$ $$L^3 = \{010101, 01011, 01101, 0111, 10101, 1011, 1101, 111\}$$ The set $L^*$ contains $\varepsilon$ and **all** finite concatenations of $01$ and $1$. The first 5 elements in lexicographic order are: $$\{\varepsilon,\ 1,\ 01,\ 11,\ 011\}$$ --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Explain the following operations on languages with formal definitions and one example each: (i) Union (ii) Concatenation (iii) Kleene Star. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Let $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$. **(i)** Find $\Sigma^3$. **(ii)** Determine the number of strings in $\Sigma^*$ of length at most 4. **(iii)** Is the language $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 0\}$ finite or infinite? Justify. **(7 marks)** --- #### Solution to Q.A(a) **(i) Union** of two languages $L_1$ and $L_2$ over the same alphabet: $$L_1 \cup L_2 = \{\,x \mid x \in L_1 \text{ or } x \in L_2\,\}$$ *Example:* $L_1 = \{a, ab\}$, $L_2 = \{b, ab\}$. Then $L_1 \cup L_2 = \{a, b, ab\}$. **(ii) Concatenation** of two languages: $$L_1 L_2 = \{\,xy \mid x \in L_1,\ y \in L_2\,\}$$ *Example:* $L_1 = \{a, b\}$, $L_2 = \{0, 1\}$. Then $L_1 L_2 = \{a0, a1, b0, b1\}$. **(iii) Kleene Star** of a language: $$L^* = \bigcup_{n=0}^{\infty} L^n$$ *Example:* $L = \{0, 11\}$. Then $L^* = \{\varepsilon, 0, 11, 00, 011, 110, 1111, \ldots\}$ — all strings over $\{0,1\}$ with no leading zeros in any "block" structure, except $\varepsilon$. --- #### Solution to Q.A(b) **(i) Compute $\Sigma^3$ where $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$:** $$\Sigma^3 = \{xyz \mid x, y, z \in \{a, b\}\}$$ Explicitly: $$\Sigma^3 = \{aaa, aab, aba, abb, baa, bab, bba, bbb\}$$ Total: $\vert \Sigma^3 \vert = 2^3 = 8$ strings. **(ii) Number of strings of length at most 4:** We sum over lengths 0, 1, 2, 3, 4: $$\sum_{n=0}^{4} \vert \Sigma \vert^n = \sum_{n=0}^{4} 2^n = 1 + 2 + 4 + 8 + 16 = 31$$ Using the closed-form formula: $$\frac{2^{4+1} - 1}{2 - 1} = \frac{32 - 1}{1} = 31$$ **(iii) Is $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 0\}$ finite or infinite?** $L$ is **infinite**. For every $n \geq 0$, the string $a^n b^n$ is a distinct element. Since $n$ can take infinitely many values, $L$ contains infinitely many strings. The first few elements are: $$\varepsilon,\ ab,\ aabb,\ aaabbb,\ aaaabbbb,\ \ldots$$ Each has a unique length $2n$, so no two elements are equal, confirming infiniteness. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Define Kleene star $L^*$ and Kleene plus $L^+$. Prove that $L^+ = L^*$ if and only if $\varepsilon \in L$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$, consider the language $L = \{a, ab\}$. Compute the following and list the first 6 elements of each: (i) $L^+$ (ii) $L^*$ (iii) $L^3$. **(7 marks)** --- #### Solution to Q.B(a) **Definitions:** $$L^* = \bigcup_{n=0}^{\infty} L^n = L^0 \cup L^1 \cup L^2 \cup \cdots$$ $$L^+ = \bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty} L^n = L^1 \cup L^2 \cup L^3 \cup \cdots$$ The only formal difference is the lower limit: $L^*$ starts from $n=0$ (including $L^0 = \{\varepsilon\}$), while $L^+$ starts from $n=1$. **Proof of $L^+ = L^* \iff \varepsilon \in L$:** **($\Leftarrow$) Suppose $\varepsilon \in L$.** We show $L^* \subseteq L^+$ (the reverse inclusion $L^+ \subseteq L^*$ is trivial). Take any $x \in L^*$. Then $x \in L^n$ for some $n \geq 0$. - If $n = 0$, then $x = \varepsilon$. Since $\varepsilon \in L = L^1$, we have $x \in L^+$. - If $n \geq 1$, then $x \in L^n \subseteq L^+$. Hence $x \in L^+$, proving $L^* \subseteq L^+$. **($\Rightarrow$) Suppose $L^+ = L^*$.** We show $\varepsilon \in L$. Since $\varepsilon \in L^* = L^0$ by definition, and $L^+ = L^*$, we have $\varepsilon \in L^+$. Now $\varepsilon \in L^+$ means $\varepsilon \in L^m$ for some $m \geq 1$. Since $\vert xy \vert = \vert x \vert + \vert y \vert \geq 1$ when both are non-empty, we must have $m = 1$ and the empty string in $L^1 = L$. Hence $\varepsilon \in L$. $\blacksquare$ --- #### Solution to Q.B(b) Given $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$ and $L = \{a, ab\}$. **(i) Compute $L^+$:** $$L^+ = L \cup L^2 \cup L^3 \cup \cdots$$ - $L^1 = \{a, ab\}$ - $L^2 = \{aa, aab, aba, abab\}$ - $L^3 = \{aaa, aaab, aaba, aabab, abaa, abaab, ababa, ababab\}$ **First 6 elements of $L^+$ (in lexicographic order):** $$\{a,\ aa,\ ab,\ aab,\ aaa,\ aba\}$$ **(ii) Compute $L^*$:** $L^* = \{\varepsilon\} \cup L^+$. Adding $\varepsilon$ to the front: $$\{\varepsilon,\ a,\ aa,\ ab,\ aab,\ aaa\}$$ **(iii) Compute $L^3$:** Already enumerated above: $$L^3 = \{aaa,\ aaab,\ aaba,\ aabab,\ abaa,\ abaab,\ ababa,\ ababab\}$$ Count: $\vert L^3 \vert = 2^3 = 8$ (since each "slot" picks one of the 2 elements of $L$). --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls (where KTU students lose marks):** > > 1. **Forgetting the empty string $\varepsilon$ in $L^*$:** When asked for $L^*$, ALWAYS include $\varepsilon$ first. Students routinely miss this and lose 2-3 marks. > > 2. **Confusing $L^n$ with $\vert L \vert^n$:** $L^n$ is a set of strings (concatenated), NOT the size of $L$ raised to $n$. State clearly: $\vert L^n \vert = \vert L \vert^n$ when $L$ is finite. > > 3. **Mixing up Kleene star and Kleene plus:** $L^*$ includes $\varepsilon$ always; $L^+$ includes $\varepsilon$ only if $L$ already does. This is the single most-tested distinction. > > 4. **Off-by-one in counting:** "Length at most 4" means $\sum_{n=0}^{4}$, not $\sum_{n=1}^{4}$. Read the question carefully. > > 5. **Writing $L^*$ without listing even one element:** Examiners want to see $\varepsilon \in L^*$ explicitly written. Always open your $L^*$ answer with "$\varepsilon \in L^*$". > > 6. **Forgetting to define concatenation** when computing $L^2$ or higher: Always state "concatenate each string of $L^{n-1}$ with each string of $L$". --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Alphabet** $\Sigma$ = finite, non-empty set of symbols. *Not infinite, not empty.* - [x] **String** $w$ = finite sequence from $\Sigma$. *Has a length $\vert w \vert \in \mathbb{N}_0$.* - [x] **Empty string** $\varepsilon$ has $\vert \varepsilon \vert = 0$ and is the identity for concatenation. - [x] **Language** $L \subseteq \Sigma^*$ — any subset of all possible strings. - [x] $\Sigma^*$ = all strings (including $\varepsilon$); $\Sigma^+$ = all non-empty strings. - [x] **Cardinality formula:** $\vert \Sigma^n \vert = \vert \Sigma \vert^n$. - [x] **Summing up to length $N$:** $\sum_{n=0}^{N} k^n = \dfrac{k^{N+1}-1}{k-1}$ for $k \geq 2$. - [x] **Concatenation** $L_1 L_2 = \{xy : x \in L_1, y \in L_2\}$. - [x] **Kleene star** $L^* = \bigcup_{n \geq 0} L^n$ — always contains $\varepsilon$. - [x] **Kleene plus** $L^+ = \bigcup_{n \geq 1} L^n = L \cdot L^*$. - [x] **Identity:** $L^+ = L^* \iff \varepsilon \in L$; else $L^+ = L^* - \{\varepsilon\}$. - [x] **Edge cases to memorize:** $\emptyset^* = \{\varepsilon\}$; $\{\varepsilon\}^* = \{\varepsilon\}$; $L \cdot \emptyset = \emptyset$. - [x] **Length of concatenation:** $\vert xy \vert = \vert x \vert + \vert y \vert$. - [x] **Reversal:** $(xy)^R = y^R x^R$. - [x] **Chomsky Hierarchy preview:** Regular $\subset$ CFL $\subset$ CSL $\subset$ Recursively Enumerable.

topics.title: Deterministic Finite State Automata (DFA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET414 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Deterministic Finite State Automata (DFA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA)** is a mathematical model of computation that recognizes *regular languages*. Formally, a DFA is a **5-tuple** (quintuple):

$$M = (Q,\ \Sigma,\ \delta,\ q_0,\ F)$$

where each component has a precise meaning under the KTU 2024 syllabus:

| Symbol | Name | Type / Description |
|---|---|---|
| $Q$ | Finite set of states | A non-empty, finite set of internal configurations |
| $\Sigma$ | Input alphabet | A finite, non-empty set of input symbols ($\Sigma \neq \emptyset$) |
| $\delta$ | Transition function | $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ (a total, single-valued function) |
| $q_0$ | Start state | $q_0 \in Q$ (the unique initial state) |
| $F$ | Set of final (accepting) states | $F \subseteq Q$ (may be empty, in which case $L(M) = \emptyset$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Determinism Constraint** — For *every* state $q \in Q$ and *every* input symbol $a \in \Sigma$, the transition $\delta(q,a)$ must be **defined exactly once**. This is what makes the automaton *deterministic* and what distinguishes it from a Non-deterministic Finite Automaton (NFA).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Coin-Operated Turnstile**
> Imagine a subway turnstile with **two internal states**: *Locked* and *Unlocked*. 
> - The *Locked* state represents "no one is currently inside" — pushing the turnstile bar does nothing. 
> - When you **insert a coin (input 'c')** while Locked, it deterministically transitions to *Unlocked*. 
> - When you **push through (input 'p')** while Unlocked, it transitions back to *Locked*. 
> - The state *Unlocked* is the *accepting* state because that's the moment a valid trip has been paid for. 
>
> A DFA behaves identically: the *current state* is the only "memory" of the machine, and the *next input symbol* is the **only** thing that can change it — there is no guessing, no parallelism, and no silent moves.

## 1.3 Acceptance Criterion

A string $w = a_1 a_2 \ldots a_n \in \Sigma^*$ is **accepted** by $M$ if and only if, starting from $q_0$ and processing the symbols one by one, the machine halts in a state belonging to $F$. Otherwise, $w$ is **rejected**. The language recognized by $M$ is:

$$L(M) = \{\, w \in \Sigma^{*} \mid \delta^{*}(q_0, w) \in F \,\}$$

where $\delta^{*} : Q \times \Sigma^{*} \rightarrow Q$ is the **extended transition function** (defined recursively over the length of the string).

> [!TIP]
> A language is called **regular** if and only if there exists *some* DFA that accepts it. This is the central theorem you must internalise: *regular language $\Leftrightarrow$ DFA-recognisable*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Transition Function — Deconstructed

The transition function $\delta$ can be specified by three equivalent representations, all of which are routinely asked in KTU examinations:

1. **State Diagram (Transition Graph)** — Directed graph; nodes = states, labelled arcs = transitions, double circle = accepting state.
2. **Transition Table** — A $\vert Q \vert \times \vert \Sigma \vert$ matrix; each cell contains exactly one state.
3. **Formal Set of Triples** — e.g. $\delta(q_0, a) = q_1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **valid DFA transition table has exactly one entry per cell**. If you ever write two states in the same cell, the structure is an **NFA**, not a DFA — a classic KTU trap.

## 2.2 The Extended Transition Function $\delta^{*}$

The function $\delta$ handles a *single* symbol. To process an entire string, we define $\delta^{*}$ recursively:

$$\delta^{*}(q,\ \varepsilon) = q$$

$$\delta^{*}(q,\ wa) = \delta(\delta^{*}(q,\ w),\ a) \quad \text{for } w \in \Sigma^{*},\ a \in \Sigma$$

> This recursion says: *"to process the string $wa$, first process the prefix $w$ to reach a new state, then consume the final symbol $a$."* This is the engine of every string-trace question in the KTU paper.

## 2.3 Properties of DFA (KTU Frequently Asked)

- **Determinism:** $\forall q \in Q,\ \forall a \in \Sigma : \vert \delta(q,a) \vert = 1$
- **Completeness:** $\delta$ is *total* (defined for every $(q,a)$ pair). A "partial" DFA must first be **completed** with a trap (dead) state before it is theoretically valid.
- **No $\varepsilon$-moves:** Unlike NFA/$\varepsilon$-NFA, a DFA *never* changes state without consuming a symbol.
- **Single start state:** Exactly one $q_0$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Use-Case in Exam |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DFA tuple | $M = (Q,\Sigma,\delta,q_0,F)$ | Definition question (3 marks) |
| 2 | Number of strings of length $n$ over $\Sigma$ | $\vert\Sigma\vert^{n}$ | Counting problems |
| 3 | Max. number of distinct languages over $\Sigma$ with $n$ states | $\Sigma^{n \cdot 2^{n}}$ *states* in the power set of $Q$ | Theoretical questions |
| 4 | $\delta^{*}$ base case | $\delta^{*}(q,\varepsilon) = q$ | Trace derivations |
| 5 | $\delta^{*}$ recursive step | $\delta^{*}(q,wa) = \delta(\delta^{*}(q,w),a)$ | Trace derivations |
| 6 | Acceptance | $w \in L(M) \Leftrightarrow \delta^{*}(q_0,w) \in F$ | All design questions |
| 7 | Rejection | $w \notin L(M) \Leftrightarrow \delta^{*}(q_0,w) \notin F$ | All design questions |
| 8 | Pumping Lemma (regularity test) | If $L$ is regular, $\exists p \geq 1$ such that $\forall w \in L$ with $\vert w \vert \geq p$, $w = xyz$ with $\vert xy \vert \leq p$, $\vert y \vert \geq 1$, $xy^{i}z \in L\ \forall i \geq 0$ | Prove *non*-regularity |
| 9 | Min. number of states for any DFA on $\Sigma$ accepting $L$ | Myhill–Nerode equivalence classes | Minimization problems |
| 10 | Complement language | $L^{c}$ accepted by DFA with $F$ replaced by $Q \setminus F$ | Closure properties |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice in row 3 we wrote $\Sigma^{n \cdot 2^{n}}$ using the symbol $\Sigma$ on the left to mean "the number of transition functions possible", not the alphabet. In KTU scripts, **always specify whether $\Sigma$ refers to the alphabet or the number $\vert\Sigma\vert$** to avoid examiner confusion.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

DFAs are *not* merely theoretical curiosities — they are the computational backbone of:

- **Lexical analysers** in compilers (e.g., tokenising identifiers, numbers, keywords using tools like Lex/Flex).
- **Pattern-matching engines** in text editors (the *grep* utility, regex libraries).
- **Network protocol verification** — TCP state machines are formal DFAs.
- **Digital circuit design** — sequential logic (flip-flops) is mathematically a DFA.
- **Vending machines, traffic-light controllers, elevator logic** — all engineered as DFAs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Design Procedure & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical KTU Question Type: Design a DFA for a Given Language

We will solve the prototypical question — *"Design a DFA over $\Sigma = \{a,b\}$ that accepts all strings ending in $ab$"* — in full, showing every cognitive step a topper would write.

### Step 1 — Understand the Language $L$

The language is

$$L = \{\, w \in \{a,b\}^{*} \mid w \text{ ends with the substring } ab \,\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The student must first *precisely* specify the language. Writing only "strings ending in ab" without a set-builder expression costs marks.

### Step 2 — Identify the Necessary "Memory" (States)

Ask: *what information must the machine remember as it scans the input?* The history we care about is **the longest suffix of the string read so far that is also a prefix of the target pattern "ab"**.

| State | Suffix memory |
|---|---|
| $q_0$ | Nothing useful (no suffix matching a prefix of "ab") |
| $q_1$ | The last symbol read was 'a' (suffix "a") |
| $q_2$ | The last two symbols read were "ab" (suffix "ab") — **accepting** |

### Step 3 — Define the Tuple

$$M = (Q,\ \Sigma,\ \delta,\ q_0,\ F)$$

with

$$Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}, \quad \Sigma = \{a,b\}, \quad q_0 = q_0, \quad F = \{q_2\}.$$

### Step 4 — Build the Transition Table

For *every* state and *every* input symbol, we must write exactly one next state. We reason cell by cell:

| State | On input $a$ | On input $b$ |
|---|---|---|
| $q_0$ (no useful suffix) | $a$ extends the suffix to "a" $\rightarrow q_1$ | $b$ produces suffix "b", which is not a prefix of "ab" $\rightarrow q_0$ |
| $q_1$ (suffix = "a") | $a$ extends suffix to "aa"; the longest matching prefix of "ab" is still "a" $\rightarrow q_1$ | $b$ extends suffix to "ab" $\rightarrow q_2$ |
| $q_2$ (suffix = "ab", accepting) | $a$ gives suffix "ba", longest matching prefix is "a" $\rightarrow q_1$ | $b$ gives suffix "bb", no match $\rightarrow q_0$ |

Therefore

$$\delta = \{(q_0,a)\!\mapsto\!q_1,\ (q_0,b)\!\mapsto\!q_0,\ (q_1,a)\!\mapsto\!q_1,\ (q_1,b)\!\mapsto\!q_2,\ (q_2,a)\!\mapsto\!q_1,\ (q_2,b)\!\mapsto\!q_0\}.$$

### Step 5 — Verify with Test Strings

We use the extended transition function to trace the machine on three sample strings.

**Trace 1 — String $w_1 = ab$ (should be ACCEPTED):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta^{*}(q_0,\ \varepsilon) &= q_0 \\
\delta^{*}(q_0,\ a) &= \delta(\delta^{*}(q_0,\varepsilon),\ a) = \delta(q_0,\ a) = q_1 \\
\delta^{*}(q_0,\ ab) &= \delta(\delta^{*}(q_0,\ a),\ b) = \delta(q_1,\ b) = q_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Final state $q_2 \in F$ → **ACCEPTED** ✓

**Trace 2 — String $w_2 = aab$ (should be ACCEPTED):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta^{*}(q_0,\ aa) &= \delta(\delta(q_0,a),a) = \delta(q_1,a) = q_1 \\
\delta^{*}(q_0,\ aab) &= \delta(q_1,b) = q_2 \in F
\end{aligned}
$$

**ACCEPTED** ✓

**Trace 3 — String $w_3 = ba$ (should be REJECTED):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta^{*}(q_0,\ b) &= q_0 \\
\delta^{*}(q_0,\ ba) &= \delta(q_0,a) = q_1 \notin F
\end{aligned}
$$

**REJECTED** ✓

## 3.2 Minimization Procedure (Table-Filling Algorithm) — Worked Example

Consider the DFA $M'$ with $Q = \{A, B, C, D, E\}$, $F = \{C, E\}$ and transitions:

| State | a | b |
|---|---|---|
| A | B | C |
| B | A | D |
| C | B | E |
| D | B | C |
| E | B | A |

**Step A — Initial partition** of $Q$ into *accepting* and *non-accepting*:

$$P_0 = \{\{C, E\},\ \{A, B, D\}\}$$

**Step B — Refine by checking distinguishability of pairs.** Two states in the same block are *distinguishable* if any input takes them to different blocks. We iteratively split:

- In $\{C, E\}$: $\delta(C,a)=B \in \{A,B,D\}$; $\delta(E,a)=B \in \{A,B,D\}$ (same block). $\delta(C,b)=E \in \{C,E\}$; $\delta(E,b)=A \in \{A,B,D\}$ (**different block**). So $\{C, E\}$ splits into $\{C\}$ and $\{E\}$.
- In $\{A, B, D\}$: $\delta(A,b)=C \in \{C\}$; $\delta(B,b)=D \in \{A,B,D\}$ (split). After the previous split, $C$ is in its own block. So $\{A,B,D\}$ splits.

$$P_1 = \{\{C\},\ \{E\},\ \{A\},\ \{B\},\ \{D\}\} \quad \text{(fully minimised)}$$

Hence the **minimised DFA has 5 states** — in this case, $M'$ was already minimal.

> [!TIP]
> KTU asks you to *show the partition sequence* $P_0 \to P_1 \to \ldots$ explicitly. Skipping a step loses 2 marks.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common DFA Design Patterns

| Language to accept | Key memory required | Number of states | Trap state needed? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ends with a fixed string $u$ | Longest suffix of input that is prefix of $u$ | $\vert u \vert + 1$ | No |
| Contains substring $u$ | Same as above, plus an explicit "found $u$" accepting sink | $\vert u \vert + 1$ | No |
| Strings with $a \bmod n = 0$ (mod-$n$ counter) | Count of $a$'s seen so far, modulo $n$ | $n$ | No |
| At least $k$ occurrences of $a$ | "How many $a$'s so far, capped at $k$" | $k + 1$ | No |
| Number of $a$'s divisible by 3 | Count mod 3 | $3$ | No |
| Binary strings representing multiples of 3 | Remainder mod 3 | $3$ | No |
| Strings NOT in some known DFA's language | Same states; swap accepting set | $\vert Q \vert$ | No |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 State Diagram (Mermaid) — DFA for $L = \{w \in \{a,b\}^{*} : w \text{ ends with } ab\}$

This is the canonical DFA drawn in KTU answer scripts. The double circle denotes the accepting state $q_2$.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    [*] --> q0
    q0 : q0 START
    q1 : q1 SAW A
    q2 : q2 SAW AB
    q0 --> q0 : b
    q0 --> q1 : a
    q1 --> q1 : a
    q1 --> q2 : b
    q2 --> q1 : a
    q2 --> q0 : b
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — How a DFA Processes a String

When a string $w = a_1 a_2 \ldots a_n$ is fed into a DFA, the following internal data flow occurs at every clock tick:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Tape\nw = a1 a2 ... an] --> B[Read Head\nat position i]
    B --> C[Current State\nq_cur in Q]
    C --> D{Transition\nFunction delta}
    B --> D
    D --> E[Next State\nq_next in Q]
    E --> F{Halted and\nq_next in F?}
    F -- Yes --> G[ACCEPT string]
    F -- No --> H[Continue reading]
    H --> B
    G --> I[Output: 1]
    F -- No end reached --> J[REJECT string]
    J --> K[Output: 0]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The tape supplies the next symbol; the transition function combines the *current state* and *next symbol* (the two inputs to $\delta$) to deterministically produce the *next state*; the loop continues until the tape is exhausted, at which point the final state is tested for membership in $F$.

## 4.3 DFA for $L = \{w \in \{0,1\}^{*} : w \text{ has even number of 0s and odd number of 1s} \}$

This is a high-yield KTU question. We need four states — one per combination of parity of 0s and 1s.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    [*] --> s00
    s00 : s00 EVEN 0s EVEN 1s
    s01 : s01 EVEN 0s ODD 1s
    s10 : s10 ODD 0s EVEN 1s
    s11 : s11 ODD 0s ODD 1s
    s00 --> s10 : 0
    s00 --> s01 : 1
    s01 --> s11 : 0
    s01 --> s00 : 1
    s10 --> s00 : 0
    s10 --> s11 : 1
    s11 --> s01 : 0
    s11 --> s10 : 1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The start state is $s_{00}$ (zero 0s read, zero 1s read — both counts are *even*, and zero is conventionally even). The only accepting state is $s_{01}$ — the state where 0-count is even **and** 1-count is odd.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert**
> 1. Forgetting to mark the start state with an arrow in the state diagram costs 1 mark.
> 2. Writing **two transitions** out of one state on the same input symbol converts your DFA into an NFA — the examiner will award **zero** marks for the design.
> 3. When asked to "design" a DFA, you must provide **all three** of: the 5-tuple, the state diagram, and a trace on a sample string. Missing any one costs 2–3 marks.
> 4. $\varepsilon$ (epsilon) is **never** a legal input symbol in a DFA. Writing $\delta(q, \varepsilon)$ is a common KTU blunder.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define a Deterministic Finite Automaton. Write the formal 5-tuple notation and explain the role of each component.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A DFA is a 5-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ where:
- $Q$ is a finite non-empty set of **states** (1 mark).
- $\Sigma$ is a finite non-empty set of **input symbols** called the alphabet (½ mark).
- $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ is the **transition function**, which for every state and every input symbol gives *exactly one* next state (1 mark).
- $q_0 \in Q$ is the unique **start state** (½ mark).

A language is *accepted* by $M$ if processing the string from $q_0$ leaves the machine in some $f \in F$ — the **set of accepting states** (½ mark).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**State and briefly explain the Pumping Lemma for regular languages.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
If $L$ is a regular language, there exists a constant $p \geq 1$ (the *pumping length*) such that every string $w \in L$ with $\vert w \vert \geq p$ can be decomposed as $w = xyz$ satisfying three conditions: (i) $\vert xy \vert \leq p$, (ii) $\vert y \vert \geq 1$, and (iii) for every $i \geq 0$, the *pumped* string $xy^{i}z \in L$ (2 marks). It is primarily used in the **contrapositive** form to prove that a given language is *not* regular — pick a clever $w$ and show that no decomposition can pump it back into $L$ (1 mark).

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> KTU 2024 scheme rule: each Part-B question is internally split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Solve *either* the full Q-A *or* the full Q-B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** Design a DFA that accepts the language $L_1 = \{\, w \in \{0,1\}^{*} \mid w \text{ contains the substring } 001 \,\}$. Draw the state diagram and the transition table. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Trace the processing of the string $w = 10010$ through your DFA, and state whether it is accepted or rejected. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Design — 7 marks**

*Identifying states (1 mark):* The machine must remember the longest suffix of the input that is also a prefix of "001". The necessary states are:

- $q_0$: nothing useful remembered (no suffix is a prefix of "001").
- $q_1$: the last symbol read is 0 (suffix "0" is a prefix of "001").
- $q_2$: the last two symbols are "00" (suffix "00" is a prefix of "001").
- $q_3$: substring "001" has been seen — **accepting**.

*5-tuple (1 mark):* $M_1 = (\{q_0,q_1,q_2,q_3\},\ \{0,1\},\ \delta,\ q_0,\ \{q_3\})$.

*Transition table (3 marks):*

| State | 0 | 1 |
|---|---|---|
| $\rightarrow q_0$ | $q_1$ | $q_0$ |
| $q_1$ | $q_2$ | $q_0$ |
| $q_2$ | $q_2$ | $q_3$ |
| $*q_3$ | $q_3$ | $q_3$ |

*State diagram (2 marks):* See Mermaid below.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    [*] --> q0
    q0 : q0 START
    q1 : q1 SAW 0
    q2 : q2 SAW 00
    q3 : q3 SAW 001
    q0 --> q1 : 0
    q0 --> q0 : 1
    q1 --> q2 : 0
    q1 --> q0 : 1
    q2 --> q2 : 0
    q2 --> q3 : 1
    q3 --> q3 : 0
    q3 --> q3 : 1
```

**(b) Trace — 7 marks**

We use $\delta^{*}$ recursively on $w = 10010$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta^{*}(q_0,\ \varepsilon) &= q_0 \\
\delta^{*}(q_0,\ 1) &= \delta(q_0,\ 1) = q_0 \\
\delta^{*}(q_0,\ 10) &= \delta(q_0,\ 0) = q_1 \\
\delta^{*}(q_0,\ 100) &= \delta(q_1,\ 0) = q_2 \\
\delta^{*}(q_0,\ 1001) &= \delta(q_2,\ 1) = q_3 \\
\delta^{*}(q_0,\ 10010) &= \delta(q_3,\ 0) = q_3
\end{aligned}
$$

Sequence of states visited: $q_0 \to q_0 \to q_1 \to q_2 \to q_3 \to q_3$ **(4 marks for stepwise trace + 1 mark for correctly applying $\delta$ at each step)**.

Since the final state $q_3 \in F$, the string $w = 10010$ is **ACCEPTED** ✓ **(2 marks)**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` *(Alternative choice)*

**(a)** Construct a DFA over $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$ that accepts the language $L_2 = \{\, w \mid w \text{ has an even number of } a\text{'s and any number of } b\text{'s} \,\}$. Provide the formal 5-tuple, the state diagram, and the transition table. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply the **table-filling minimization algorithm** to your DFA, showing the partition sequence $P_0, P_1, P_2, \ldots$ explicitly. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Design — 7 marks**

*Intuition (1 mark):* We only need to count $a$'s modulo 2. The $b$'s do not affect acceptance.

*5-tuple (1 mark):* $M_2 = (\{q_0, q_1\},\ \{a, b\},\ \delta,\ q_0,\ \{q_0\})$, where $q_0$ = "even number of $a$'s seen" and $q_1$ = "odd number of $a$'s seen".

*Transition table (2 marks):*

| State | a | b |
|---|---|---|
| $\rightarrow *q_0$ | $q_1$ | $q_0$ |
| $q_1$ | $q_0$ | $q_1$ |

*State diagram (3 marks):*

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    [*] --> q0
    q0 : q0 EVEN a count
    q1 : q1 ODD a count
    q0 --> q1 : a
    q0 --> q0 : b
    q1 --> q0 : a
    q1 --> q1 : b
```

*Verification on $w = abba$ (for full marks, include a 1-line trace showing the machine ends in $q_0$):*

$$\delta^{*}(q_0,\ abba) = \delta(\delta(\delta(\delta(q_0,a),b),b),a) = \delta(\delta(\delta(q_1,b),b),a) = \delta(\delta(q_1,b),a) = \delta(q_1,a) = q_0 \in F.\ \checkmark$$

**(b) Minimization — 7 marks**

Initial partition into accepting vs non-accepting (1 mark):

$$P_0 = \{\{q_0\},\ \{q_1\}\} \quad \text{(since } F = \{q_0\}\text{)}$$

Check whether the partition can be refined. We test the pair $(q_0, q_1)$ on each input symbol (3 marks for analysis):

- On input $a$: $\delta(q_0, a) = q_1$; $\delta(q_1, a) = q_0$. They go to **different** blocks — already distinguishable, so no further merging is possible.
- On input $b$: $\delta(q_0, b) = q_0$; $\delta(q_1, b) = q_1$. Also different blocks.

Hence $P_0$ is stable and

$$P_1 = P_0 = \{\{q_0\},\ \{q_1\}\} \quad (2\text{ marks for stating final partition and concluding the DFA is already minimal})$$

The DFA has the minimum possible number of states (2). **(1 mark for the final conclusion.)**

---

## 5.1 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [ ] A DFA is a 5-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ — write this from memory in under 30 seconds.
- [ ] $\delta$ is a *total* function: **one** output state per (state, symbol) pair.
- [ ] A DFA has **no $\varepsilon$-transitions**; convert $\varepsilon$-NFAs to DFAs via subset construction.
- [ ] Acceptance: $w \in L(M) \iff \delta^{*}(q_0, w) \in F$.
- [ ] $\delta^{*}$ is defined recursively: $\delta^{*}(q, \varepsilon) = q$ and $\delta^{*}(q, wa) = \delta(\delta^{*}(q,w), a)$.
- [ ] **Designing a DFA** = (i) identify the "memory" the machine needs, (ii) assign a state per memory value, (iii) fill in the transition table by considering how each input updates the memory.
- [ ] State diagram conventions: arrow into the start state, double circle for accepting states, **single arrow per symbol per state**.
- [ ] Minimization uses the **table-filling / partition-refinement** algorithm — keep refining until the partition stops changing.
- [ ] **Complement** of a DFA-recognised language: swap $F$ with $Q \setminus F$ — only works because DFAs are deterministic.
- [ ] **Pumping Lemma** is the standard tool to *disprove* regularity; contrapositive is used in proofs.
- [ ] Every **regular language** is recognisable by *some* DFA, and vice versa — this is the foundation of Module 1.
- [ ] In KTU scripts, always show the **5-tuple, the state diagram, the transition table, and at least one sample-string trace** to secure full marks on a DFA design question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nondeterministic Finite State Automata (NFA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET414 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Nondeterministic Finite State Automata (NFA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Nondeterministic Finite State Automaton (NFA)** is a mathematical model of computation that generalizes the Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA) by relaxing the uniqueness constraint on state transitions. It is formally defined as a **5-tuple** (quintuple):

$$M = (Q, \; \Sigma, \; \delta, \; q_0, \; F)$$

where each component has a precise meaning under the KTU 2024 Theory of Computation syllabus:

| Component | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Finite set of states | $Q$ | A non-empty, finite set of internal configurations |
| Input alphabet | $\Sigma$ | A finite, non-empty set of input symbols |
| Transition function | $\delta$ | $Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \rightarrow 2^{Q}$ |
| Initial state | $q_0$ | The unique start state, $q_0 \in Q$ |
| Set of final/accepting states | $F$ | $F \subseteq Q$ |

The defining distinction of an NFA is the **transition function codomain**: instead of mapping to a *single* next state (as in a DFA), $\delta$ maps to a **set of possible next states** ($2^{Q}$ is the power set of $Q$). Furthermore, an NFA may include $\varepsilon$-transitions (epsilon-transitions), allowing state changes without consuming any input symbol.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> An NFA is said to **accept** a string $w$ if there exists **at least one** computation path (sequence of transitions) that leads from $q_0$ to some state in $F$ after consuming all symbols of $w$. This existential quantification is the heart of nondeterminism.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Parallel Universe Reader"

Imagine you are reading a choose-your-own-adventure book, but unlike a real reader who must physically turn to one page, you are a *magical reader* who **simultaneously explores every possible page** at each choice point.

- **DFA analogy**: You must physically flip to the *only* page indicated. There is exactly one path forward at every step.
- **NFA analogy**: The book magically *clones* you at every branching point. One clone follows path A, another follows path B, another follows path C, and so on — all in parallel universes. The string is **accepted** if **even a single clone** ends up on a "happy ending" page (final state).

This magical cloning is not a physical process; it is a *mathematical abstraction*. It captures the idea that a computation may have multiple valid continuations, and acceptance only requires **one successful continuation**.

## 1.3 Variants of NFA

| Variant | Description | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **NFA without $\varepsilon$-moves** | Transitions only on input symbols, but multiple per symbol | Covered in standard NFA definition |
| **NFA with $\varepsilon$-moves ($\varepsilon$-NFA)** | Adds spontaneous transitions on empty string | High — frequently examined |
| **NFA-$\Lambda$** | Synonym for $\varepsilon$-NFA using $\Lambda$ notation | Alternate textbook terminology |
| **GNFA (Generalized NFA)** | Transitions labeled with arbitrary regular expressions | Advanced — Module 2 prerequisite |

> [!NOTE] **Core Takeaway**
> Every NFA — whether with or without $\varepsilon$-transitions — can be converted to an equivalent DFA that recognizes the **exact same language**. NFAs and DFAs have **identical expressive power**; NFAs are simply often *easier to design*, while DFAs are often *easier to implement*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Semantics — How an NFA Processes a String

The processing of an input string $w = w_1 w_2 \ldots w_n$ by an NFA proceeds as follows:

1. **Initialization**: The machine begins in state $q_0$. The set of "active" states is $\{q_0\}$.
2. **Symbol consumption**: For each symbol $w_i$, the active set is updated by taking the union of all transitions from any current state on $w_i$.
   $$S_{i+1} = \bigcup_{q \in S_i} \delta(q, w_i)$$
3. **Branching semantics**: Because $\delta(q, w_i)$ returns a *set* of states, the active set $S_i$ may grow (branch), shrink (prune dead paths), or stay the same at each step.
4. **$\varepsilon$-closure (for $\varepsilon$-NFA)**: Before and after each symbol consumption, all states reachable via zero or more $\varepsilon$-transitions are added to the active set.
   $$\varepsilon\text{-closure}(S) = \{q \in Q \mid q \text{ reachable from some } p \in S \text{ via } \varepsilon\text{-moves}\}$$
5. **Acceptance**: After processing the final symbol $w_n$, the string is **accepted** if and only if:
   $$S_n \cap F \neq \emptyset$$
   Equivalently, the final active set shares at least one state with the accepting set.

## 2.2 The NFA-to-DFA Conversion — Subset Construction

The **subset construction** (also called the *powerset construction*) is the canonical algorithm for converting an NFA into an equivalent DFA. The procedure is:

1. Start with the $\varepsilon$-closure of $\{q_0\}$ as the initial DFA state.
2. For each DFA state $R$ (which represents a subset of NFA states) and each input symbol $a \in \Sigma$:
   - Compute the union of $\delta(q, a)$ for all $q \in R$.
   - Take the $\varepsilon$-closure of that union.
   - This becomes the corresponding transition in the DFA.
3. Mark any DFA state as accepting if its corresponding NFA subset contains at least one accepting state.
4. Repeat until no new subsets are generated.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why Subset Construction Works**
> The mathematical justification rests on the equivalence: $L(M_{NFA}) = L(M_{DFA})$. Each DFA state encodes *all possible NFA states* the machine could be in at that point in the computation, collapsing parallel branches into a single deterministic set-valued state.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Statement | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|
| NFA formal definition | $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ | Foundation for all proofs |
| Transition function range | $\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \rightarrow 2^{Q}$ | Distinguishes NFA from DFA |
| Acceptance condition | $S_n \cap F \neq \emptyset$ | Decides language membership |
| $\varepsilon$-closure | $\varepsilon\text{-closure}(S) = S \cup \bigcup_{p \in S} \varepsilon\text{-closure}(\delta(p, \varepsilon))$ | Required for $\varepsilon$-NFA processing |
| Subset construction transition | $\delta_{DFA}(R, a) = \varepsilon\text{-closure}\!\left(\bigcup_{q \in R} \delta_{NFA}(q, a)\right)$ | Core NFA $\rightarrow$ DFA algorithm |
| Maximum number of DFA states | $2^{\vert Q \vert}$ | Upper bound on converted DFA size |
| Language class | $L_{NFA} = L_{DFA} =$ Regular Languages | Expressive power equivalence |
| Number of NFA transitions | $\leq \vert Q \vert \times (\vert \Sigma \vert + 1)$ per state | Complexity measurement |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

NFAs are not merely theoretical curiosities; they power several practical engineering domains:

- **Lexical analyzers in compilers**: Tools like `lex` and `flex` internally generate NFAs from regular expressions, then convert them to optimized DFAs for token recognition.
- **Network intrusion detection systems (NIDS)**: Pattern matchers (e.g., Snort, regex-based deep packet inspection) use NFA backtracking engines.
- **Bioinformatics**: DNA/protein sequence matching frequently uses NFA-based regular expression engines (e.g., grep, awk) to identify motifs in genome data.
- **Digital circuit design**: NFA equivalence checking is used in formal hardware verification to validate sequential circuit behaviors.
- **Search engines**: Query rewriting and pattern matching over massive text corpora leverage NFA simulation for efficiency.

## 2.5 Comparison: NFA vs DFA

| Property | DFA | NFA |
|---|---|---|
| Transitions per symbol | Exactly one | Zero, one, or many |
| $\varepsilon$-transitions | Not allowed | Allowed |
| Implementation complexity | Simple table lookup | Requires set tracking |
| Design complexity | Often harder | Usually easier |
| State count for equivalent language | $\leq 2^{\vert Q_{NFA} \vert}$ | Minimal in many cases |
| Expressive power | Regular | Regular (identical) |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Example 1 — Constructing an NFA That Accepts Strings Ending in "01"

**Problem**: Design an NFA over $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$ that accepts all binary strings ending in the substring "01".

**Step 1: Conceptual Design**
The NFA must remember only the *last two symbols* read. We use a "guessing + verification" strategy: the NFA nondeterministically *guesses* that the suffix "01" is about to begin, then *verifies* the next two symbols are 0 and 1.

**Step 2: State Design**
- $q_0$ — start state, scanning initial portion (guessing not yet activated)
- $q_1$ — guessing activated; have seen a tentative '0'
- $q_2$ — have seen "01" suffix; accept state
- $q_3$ — dead/trap state (optional in NFA)

**Step 3: Formal Tuple Definition**
$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$$
where:
- $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$
- $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$
- $q_0 = q_0$
- $F = \{q_2\}$

**Step 4: Transition Function Definition**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0, 0) &= \{q_0, q_1\} \quad \text{(stay guessing, or commit to suffix)} \\
\delta(q_0, 1) &= \{q_0\} \quad \text{(keep scanning, suffix still possible)} \\
\delta(q_1, 0) &= \emptyset \quad \text{(saw 0, need 1 next, but got 0)} \\
\delta(q_1, 1) &= \{q_2\} \quad \text{(saw "01", accept)} \\
\delta(q_2, 0) &= \{q_2\} \quad \text{(after accepting, loop to keep accepting)} \\
\delta(q_2, 1) &= \{q_2\} \quad \text{(after accepting, loop to keep accepting)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE] **Why This Works**
> The nondeterminism is in state $q_0$ reading '0': the machine *clones* into two paths — one continues scanning ($q_0$), one commits to the suffix pattern ($q_1$). If the guess was wrong, the clone dies (empty set). If the guess was right, it reaches $q_2$.

**Step 5: Verify with Sample String** $w = \text{"1101"}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Start: } & S_0 = \{q_0\} \\
\text{Read '1': } & S_1 = \delta(q_0, 1) = \{q_0\} \\
\text{Read '1': } & S_2 = \delta(q_0, 1) = \{q_0\} \\
\text{Read '0': } & S_3 = \delta(q_0, 0) = \{q_0, q_1\} \\
\text{Read '1': } & S_4 = \delta(q_0, 1) \cup \delta(q_1, 1) = \{q_0\} \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0, q_2\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $S_4 \cap F = \{q_2\} \neq \emptyset$, the string "1101" is **accepted**. ✓

**Step 6: Verify Rejection** $w = \text{"1100"}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_0 &= \{q_0\} \\
S_1 = \delta(q_0, 1) &= \{q_0\} \\
S_2 = \delta(q_0, 1) &= \{q_0\} \\
S_3 = \delta(q_0, 0) &= \{q_0, q_1\} \\
S_4 = \delta(q_0, 0) \cup \delta(q_1, 0) &= \{q_0, q_1\} \cup \emptyset = \{q_0, q_1\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $S_4 \cap F = \emptyset$, the string "1100" is **rejected**. ✓

## 3.2 Example 2 — Converting an NFA to DFA via Subset Construction

**Given NFA**: Accepts strings over $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$ containing "a" as a substring somewhere.

**Step 1: NFA Tuple**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \{q_0, q_1, q_2\} \\
\Sigma &= \{a, b\} \\
q_0 &= q_0 \\
F &= \{q_2\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: NFA Transition Table**

| State | $a$ | $b$ |
|---|---|---|
| $\rightarrow q_0$ | $\{q_0, q_1\}$ | $\{q_0\}$ |
| $q_1$ | $\{q_1, q_2\}$ | $\{q_1\}$ |
| $*q_2$ | $\{q_2\}$ | $\{q_2\}$ |

**Step 3: Apply Subset Construction — Iteration 1**

The initial DFA state corresponds to the NFA state set $\{q_0\}$. We denote it as $[q_0]$ for clarity.

Compute transitions for $[q_0]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_{DFA}([q_0], a) &= \delta_{NFA}(q_0, a) = \{q_0, q_1\} \;\Rightarrow\; [q_0, q_1] \\
\delta_{DFA}([q_0], b) &= \delta_{NFA}(q_0, b) = \{q_0\} \;\Rightarrow\; [q_0]
\end{aligned}
$$

A new subset $[q_0, q_1]$ is discovered and added to the worklist.

**Step 4: Apply Subset Construction — Iteration 2**

Compute transitions for $[q_0, q_1]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_{DFA}([q_0, q_1], a) &= \delta_{NFA}(q_0, a) \cup \delta_{NFA}(q_1, a) \\
&= \{q_0, q_1\} \cup \{q_1, q_2\} = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\} \;\Rightarrow\; [q_0, q_1, q_2] \\
\delta_{DFA}([q_0, q_1], b) &= \delta_{NFA}(q_0, b) \cup \delta_{NFA}(q_1, b) \\
&= \{q_0\} \cup \{q_1\} = \{q_0, q_1\} \;\Rightarrow\; [q_0, q_1]
\end{aligned}
$$

A new subset $[q_0, q_1, q_2]$ is discovered.

**Step 5: Apply Subset Construction — Iteration 3**

Compute transitions for $[q_0, q_1, q_2]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_{DFA}([q_0, q_1, q_2], a) &= \{q_0, q_1\} \cup \{q_1, q_2\} \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\} \;\Rightarrow\; [q_0, q_1, q_2] \\
\delta_{DFA}([q_0, q_1, q_2], b) &= \{q_0\} \cup \{q_1\} \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\} \;\Rightarrow\; [q_0, q_1, q_2]
\end{aligned}
$$

No new subsets are generated. The algorithm terminates.

**Step 6: Identify Accepting DFA States**

A DFA state is accepting if its corresponding NFA subset intersects $F = \{q_2\}$:

| DFA State | Contains $q_2$? | Accepting? |
|---|---|---|
| $[q_0]$ | No | No |
| $[q_0, q_1]$ | No | No |
| $[q_0, q_1, q_2]$ | **Yes** | **Yes** ✓ |

**Step 7: Final Equivalent DFA Transition Table**

| DFA State | $a$ | $b$ | Accepting? |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\rightarrow [q_0]$ | $[q_0, q_1]$ | $[q_0]$ | No |
| $[q_0, q_1]$ | $[q_0, q_1, q_2]$ | $[q_0, q_1]$ | No |
| $*[q_0, q_1, q_2]$ | $[q_0, q_1, q_2]$ | $[q_0, q_1, q_2]$ | **Yes** |

The resulting DFA has **3 states**, identical to the original NFA — a case where the conversion is size-preserving.

## 3.3 Example 3 — Worked NFA on $\varepsilon$-Transitions

**Problem**: Construct an $\varepsilon$-NFA for $L = a^* b^*$ using $\varepsilon$-transitions to "concatenate" two simpler sub-NFAs.

**Step 1: Decompose** $L = a^* b^* = L_1 \cdot L_2$ where $L_1 = a^*$ and $L_2 = b^*$.

**Step 2: Build Component NFAs**

For $L_1 = a^*$:
- States: $\{p_0, p_1\}$, $p_0$ start, $p_1$ accepting
- $\delta(p_0, a) = \{p_1\}$, $\delta(p_1, a) = \{p_1\}$

For $L_2 = b^*$:
- States: $\{r_0, r_1\}$, $r_0$ start, $r_1$ accepting
- $\delta(r_0, b) = \{r_1\}$, $\delta(r_1, b) = \{r_1\}$

**Step 3: Concatenate via $\varepsilon$-transition**

Add $\delta(p_1, \varepsilon) = \{r_0\}$ to chain the two sub-NFAs.

**Step 4: Combined $\varepsilon$-NFA**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \{p_0, p_1, r_0, r_1\} \\
\Sigma &= \{a, b\} \\
q_0 &= p_0 \\
F &= \{r_1\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Transitions:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(p_0, a) &= \{p_1\} \\
\delta(p_1, a) &= \{p_1\} \\
\delta(p_1, \varepsilon) &= \{r_0\} \\
\delta(r_0, b) &= \{r_1\} \\
\delta(r_1, b) &= \{r_1\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Trace** $w = \text{"aab"}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_0 &= \varepsilon\text{-closure}(\{p_0\}) = \{p_0\} \\
S_1 &= \varepsilon\text{-closure}(\delta(\{p_0\}, a)) = \varepsilon\text{-closure}(\{p_1\}) = \{p_1, r_0\} \\
S_2 &= \varepsilon\text{-closure}(\delta(\{p_1, r_0\}, a)) = \varepsilon\text{-closure}(\{p_1\}) = \{p_1, r_0\} \\
S_3 &= \varepsilon\text{-closure}(\delta(\{p_1, r_0\}, b)) = \varepsilon\text{-closure}(\{r_1\}) = \{r_1\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $S_3 \cap F = \{r_1\} \neq \emptyset$, the string "aab" is **accepted**. ✓

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid State Diagram — NFA Accepting Strings Ending in "01"

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> q0
    q0 --> q0 : 1
    q0 --> q0 : 0
    q0 --> q1 : 0
    q1 --> q2 : 1
    q2 --> q2 : 0
    q2 --> q2 : 1
    q2 --> [*] : accept
```

**Diagram Reading Guide**:
- Each directed edge is labeled with an input symbol that triggers the transition.
- The `[*]` notation denotes the start arrow (entry to $q_0$) and the accepting exit from $q_2$.
- The self-loops on $q_0$ allow the machine to scan arbitrary prefixes.
- The branching transition $q_0 \xrightarrow{0} q_1$ represents the **nondeterministic guess** that the suffix "01" begins now.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Architecture of NFA String Processing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input String w] --> B[Initialize Active Set S = closure of q0]
    B --> C{Read Next Symbol?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Compute delta on S for symbol]
    D --> E[Apply epsilon closure to result]
    E --> F[Update Active Set S]
    F --> C
    C -- No --> G{Is S intersect F nonempty?}
    G -- Yes --> H[ACCEPT string w]
    G -- No --> I[REJECT string w]
```

**Reading the Flow**:
- The loop on the right ($C \rightarrow D \rightarrow E \rightarrow F \rightarrow C$) represents the per-symbol processing cycle.
- The decision diamond $G$ implements the acceptance condition $S_n \cap F \neq \emptyset$.
- This is the **universal architecture** for any NFA simulator, regardless of the specific transition table.

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Subset Construction Visualization

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SQ0
    SQ0 --> SQ0 : b
    SQ0 --> SQ01 : a
    SQ01 --> SQ01 : b
    SQ01 --> SQ012 : a
    SQ012 --> SQ012 : a
    SQ012 --> SQ012 : b
    SQ012 --> [*] : accept
```

**Reading the Construction**:
- `SQ0` represents the DFA state $[q_0]$
- `SQ01` represents the DFA state $[q_0, q_1]$
- `SQ012` represents the DFA state $[q_0, q_1, q_2]$
- Notice the **deterministic** nature of the converted machine: exactly one outgoing edge per symbol per state, contrasting with the NFA's nondeterminism.

## 4.4 Mermaid Comparison — DFA vs NFA Transition Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DFA_Model
        D1[State A] -- symbol a --> D2[State B]
        D1 -- symbol b --> D3[State C]
        D2 -- symbol a --> D2
        D2 -- symbol b --> D4[State D]
    end
    subgraph NFA_Model
        N1[State P] -- symbol a --> N2[State Q]
        N1 -- symbol a --> N3[State R]
        N2 -- symbol b --> N4[State S]
        N1 -- epsilon --> N5[State T]
    end
```

**Reading the Comparison**:
- **DFA subgraph**: Each state-symbol pair has exactly one outgoing edge.
- **NFA subgraph**: Notice state $P$ has **two edges labeled 'a'** going to different states (nondeterminism) and an **$\varepsilon$-edge** to state $T$ (spontaneous transition).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a Nondeterministic Finite Automaton (NFA). How does it differ from a Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA)?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Nondeterministic Finite Automaton is a 5-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ where $\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \rightarrow 2^{Q}$ is a transition function that maps each (state, symbol) pair to a *set of states* rather than a single state. **(1 mark for definition)**

A DFA, in contrast, has $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$, yielding a single deterministic next state. **(1 mark for DFA definition)**

Key differences: (i) an NFA may have zero, one, or many transitions on the same symbol, while a DFA has exactly one; (ii) NFAs allow $\varepsilon$-transitions whereas DFAs do not; (iii) NFA acceptance requires only *one* accepting computation path, while a DFA has a single deterministic path whose final state must be accepting. **(1 mark for differences)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the concept of $\varepsilon$-closure with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The $\varepsilon$-closure of a set of states $S$, denoted $\varepsilon\text{-closure}(S)$, is the set of all states reachable from any state in $S$ using **zero or more** $\varepsilon$-transitions. It is formally defined as: **(1 mark)**

$$\varepsilon\text{-closure}(S) = S \cup \bigcup_{q \in S} \varepsilon\text{-closure}(\delta(q, \varepsilon))$$

By definition, every state in $S$ is trivially in its $\varepsilon$-closure (using "zero" $\varepsilon$-moves). **(1 mark for the "zero" part)**

**Example**: Consider an NFA with transitions $\delta(q_0, \varepsilon) = \{q_1\}$ and $\delta(q_1, \varepsilon) = \{q_2\}$. Then $\varepsilon\text{-closure}(\{q_0\}) = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$, since $q_1$ is reachable from $q_0$ via one $\varepsilon$-move, and $q_2$ is reachable via two $\varepsilon$-moves. **(1 mark for example)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Construct an NFA that accepts all strings over the alphabet $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$ such that the string contains the substring "101". Show the formal tuple definition, transition table, and verify the NFA on the strings $w_1 = \text{"11010"}$ and $w_2 = \text{"00011"}$.** (14 marks)

#### **Part (a): NFA Construction and Formal Definition** (7 marks)

**Solution:**

We design an NFA with four states corresponding to the progress made in matching the substring "101":
- $q_0$: start state — no progress, scanning initial portion
- $q_1$: matched "1" so far
- $q_2$: matched "10" so far
- $q_3$: matched "101" — **accepting state**

**Formal tuple**: **(1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3\} \\
\Sigma &= \{0, 1\} \\
q_0 &= q_0 \\
F &= \{q_3\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Transition function $\delta$**: **(4 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0, 0) &= \{q_0\} \\
\delta(q_0, 1) &= \{q_0, q_1\} \\
\delta(q_1, 0) &= \{q_2\} \\
\delta(q_1, 1) &= \{q_1\} \\
\delta(q_2, 0) &= \{q_2\} \\
\delta(q_2, 1) &= \{q_2, q_3\} \\
\delta(q_3, 0) &= \{q_3\} \\
\delta(q_3, 1) &= \{q_3\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Transition Table** (1 mark):

| State | $0$ | $1$ |
|---|---|---|
| $\rightarrow q_0$ | $\{q_0\}$ | $\{q_0, q_1\}$ |
| $q_1$ | $\{q_2\}$ | $\{q_1\}$ |
| $q_2$ | $\{q_2\}$ | $\{q_2, q_3\}$ |
| $*q_3$ | $\{q_3\}$ | $\{q_3\}$ |

**State Diagram Logic** (1 mark): The nondeterminism is in $q_0$ reading '1' (continue scanning or commit to pattern) and in $q_2$ reading '1' (stay in pattern, or finish the substring).

---

#### **Part (b): Verification on $w_1 = \text{"11010"}$ and $w_2 = \text{"00011"}$** (7 marks)

**Solution for $w_1 = \text{"11010"}$**: **(3.5 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_0 &= \{q_0\} \\
S_1 = \delta(\{q_0\}, 1) &= \{q_0, q_1\} \\
S_2 = \delta(\{q_0, q_1\}, 1) &= \delta(q_0, 1) \cup \delta(q_1, 1) = \{q_0, q_1\} \cup \{q_1\} = \{q_0, q_1\} \\
S_3 = \delta(\{q_0, q_1\}, 0) &= \delta(q_0, 0) \cup \delta(q_1, 0) = \{q_0\} \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0, q_2\} \\
S_4 = \delta(\{q_0, q_2\}, 1) &= \delta(q_0, 1) \cup \delta(q_2, 1) = \{q_0, q_1\} \cup \{q_2, q_3\} = \{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3\} \\
S_5 = \delta(\{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3\}, 0) &= \{q_0\} \cup \emptyset \cup \{q_2\} \cup \{q_3\} = \{q_0, q_2, q_3\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $S_5 \cap F = \{q_3\} \neq \emptyset$, the string $w_1 = \text{"11010"}$ is **ACCEPTED**. ✓

**Solution for $w_2 = \text{"00011"}$**: **(3.5 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_0 &= \{q_0\} \\
S_1 = \delta(\{q_0\}, 0) &= \{q_0\} \\
S_2 = \delta(\{q_0\}, 0) &= \{q_0\} \\
S_3 = \delta(\{q_0\}, 0) &= \{q_0\} \\
S_4 = \delta(\{q_0\}, 1) &= \{q_0, q_1\} \\
S_5 = \delta(\{q_0, q_1\}, 1) &= \delta(q_0, 1) \cup \delta(q_1, 1) = \{q_0, q_1\} \cup \{q_1\} = \{q_0, q_1\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $S_5 \cap F = \emptyset$, the string $w_2 = \text{"00011"}$ is **REJECTED**. ✓

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**
> A very common error is to **forget the union** in the active set update. When computing $\delta(\{q_a, q_b\}, x)$, students often compute only $\delta(q_a, x)$ or only $\delta(q_b, x)$, missing one of the parallel computation paths. Each active state contributes its *own* transition set, and the **union** of all such sets forms the new active set. Forgetting this loses 1–2 marks consistently. Additionally, students sometimes mistakenly check $S_{n-1} \cap F$ instead of $S_n \cap F$ — the check must be performed *after* processing the **final** symbol.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Convert the following NFA to an equivalent DFA using the subset construction algorithm. The NFA is defined over $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$ with $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$, $F = \{q_2\}$, and transitions: $\delta(q_0, a) = \{q_0, q_1\}$, $\delta(q_0, b) = \{q_0\}$, $\delta(q_1, a) = \{q_2\}$, $\delta(q_1, b) = \emptyset$, $\delta(q_2, a) = \{q_2\}$, $\delta(q_2, b) = \{q_2\}$.** (14 marks)

#### **Part (a): Apply Subset Construction Algorithm** (7 marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the initial DFA state**: **(0.5 marks)**

The initial DFA state is the subset containing the NFA's start state:
$$D_0 = [q_0]$$

**Step 2 — Compute transitions from $D_0 = [q_0]$**: **(1.5 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_{DFA}([q_0], a) &= \delta_{NFA}(q_0, a) = \{q_0, q_1\} \;\Rightarrow\; D_1 = [q_0, q_1] \\
\delta_{DFA}([q_0], b) &= \delta_{NFA}(q_0, b) = \{q_0\} \;\Rightarrow\; D_0 = [q_0] \text{ (self-loop)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Process new state $D_1 = [q_0, q_1]$**: **(2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_{DFA}([q_0, q_1], a) &= \delta_{NFA}(q_0, a) \cup \delta_{NFA}(q_1, a) = \{q_0, q_1\} \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\} \;\Rightarrow\; D_2 \\
\delta_{DFA}([q_0, q_1], b) &= \delta_{NFA}(q_0, b) \cup \delta_{NFA}(q_1, b) = \{q_0\} \cup \emptyset = \{q_0\} \;\Rightarrow\; D_0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Process new state $D_2 = [q_0, q_1, q_2]$**: **(2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_{DFA}([q_0, q_1, q_2], a) &= \{q_0, q_1\} \cup \{q_2\} \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\} \;\Rightarrow\; D_2 \text{ (self-loop)} \\
\delta_{DFA}([q_0, q_1, q_2], b) &= \{q_0\} \cup \emptyset \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0, q_2\} \;\Rightarrow\; D_3 = [q_0, q_2]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Process new state $D_3 = [q_0, q_2]$**: **(1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_{DFA}([q_0, q_2], a) &= \{q_0, q_1\} \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\} \;\Rightarrow\; D_2 \\
\delta_{DFA}([q_0, q_2], b) &= \{q_0\} \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0, q_2\} \;\Rightarrow\; D_3 \text{ (self-loop)}
\end{aligned}
$$

No new states generated. Algorithm terminates.

---

#### **Part (b): Final DFA Transition Table and Accepting States** (7 marks)

**Solution:**

**Accepting states** (those whose subset intersects $F = \{q_2\}$): **(2 marks)**

| DFA State | NFA Subset | Intersects $\{q_2\}$? | Accepting? |
|---|---|---|---|
| $D_0 = [q_0]$ | $\{q_0\}$ | No | No |
| $D_1 = [q_0, q_1]$ | $\{q_0, q_1\}$ | No | No |
| $D_2 = [q_0, q_1, q_2]$ | $\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$ | **Yes** | **Yes** ✓ |
| $D_3 = [q_0, q_2]$ | $\{q_0, q_2\}$ | **Yes** | **Yes** ✓ |

**Final Equivalent DFA Transition Table** (4 marks):

| DFA State | $a$ | $b$ | Accepting? |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\rightarrow [q_0]$ | $[q_0, q_1]$ | $[q_0]$ | No |
| $[q_0, q_1]$ | $[q_0, q_1, q_2]$ | $[q_0]$ | No |
| $*[q_0, q_1, q_2]$ | $[q_0, q_1, q_2]$ | $[q_0, q_2]$ | **Yes** |
| $*[q_0, q_2]$ | $[q_0, q_1, q_2]$ | $[q_0, q_2]$ | **Yes** |

**Verification of equivalence** (1 mark): The original NFA accepts strings containing at least one 'a' that is followed by something matching the "101" pattern. The converted DFA recognizes the same language — confirmed by the fact that $L(M_{NFA}) = L(M_{DFA})$ by the correctness theorem of the subset construction.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**
> Two very common errors in subset construction questions: (1) **Forgetting to include the initial state's $\varepsilon$-closure** — if the NFA has $\varepsilon$-transitions from $q_0$, the initial DFA state must be $\varepsilon\text{-closure}(\{q_0\})$, not just $\{q_0\}$. (2) **Premature termination** — students stop after generating 2 or 3 states and miss the closure under the transition function. Always recheck every newly generated state until a full pass yields no new states. Each missed state costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- **NFA Formal Definition**: Always quote the 5-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ with the *exact* signature $\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \rightarrow 2^{Q}$.
- **Acceptance is Existential**: An NFA accepts a string $w$ if **at least one** path leads to a final state. This is the single most important conceptual distinction from DFAs.
- **$\varepsilon$-closure Definition**: Set of all states reachable from a given set using zero or more $\varepsilon$-moves. Always include the starting set itself (the "zero moves" case).
- **Subset Construction Formula**: $\delta_{DFA}(R, a) = \varepsilon\text{-closure}\!\left(\bigcup_{q \in R} \delta_{NFA}(q, a)\right)$ — memorize this exactly.
- **Maximum DFA Size**: An NFA with $n$ states converts to a DFA with **at most $2^n$** states. The bound is tight for some languages (e.g., "contains the $k$-th-to-last symbol as 1").
- **Expressive Power Equality**: $L_{NFA} = L_{DFA} = L_{Reg}$ (regular languages). NFAs and DFAs recognize the **same class** of languages.
- **NFA Advantages**: Easier to design for pattern-matching tasks; fewer states for some languages; $\varepsilon$-transitions simplify composition of sub-NFAs.
- **NFA Disadvantages**: Harder to implement directly; subset construction can cause exponential blowup.
- **Active Set Notation**: Use $S_i$ to denote the set of active states after reading $i$ input symbols. Track via $S_{i+1} = \bigcup_{q \in S_i} \delta(q, w_{i+1})$.
- **Common Exam Mistake**: In subset construction, students often misidentify accepting states. Remember: a DFA state is accepting **if and only if its corresponding NFA subset shares at least one element with $F$**.
- **Practical Tools**: `lex`/`flex` (compiler generators) internally use NFA-to-DFA conversion. `grep` uses NFA backtracking. `re2` uses NFA-based simulation with explicit states.
- **Standard References**: Hopcroft, Motwani & Ullman (*Introduction to Automata Theory, Languages, and Computation*); Sipser (*Introduction to the Theory of Computation*) — both treat NFAs in early chapters with rigorous formal proofs of equivalence to DFAs.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equivalence of DFA and NFA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET414 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Equivalence of DFA and NFA # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition — Finite Automaton (FA) A **Finite Automaton (FA)** is the simplest and most fundamental computational model in the Theory of Computation. It is a mathematical abstraction of a digital computer with a finite memory. Formally, it is a **5-tuple** defined as: $$M = (Q, \ \Sigma, \ \delta, \ q_0, \ F)$$ where each component has a precise meaning: | Symbol | Component | Meaning | |--------|-----------|---------| | $Q$ | Finite set of **states** | Represents the internal memory configurations | | $\Sigma$ | Finite non-empty set of **input symbols** (alphabet) | The allowed input characters | | $\delta$ | **Transition function** | Maps current state and input to next state(s) | | $q_0$ | **Start/Initial state** | The unique state where computation begins, $q_0 \in Q$ | | $F$ | Set of **final/accepting states** | $F \subseteq Q$ — these are the "yes" configurations | > [!NOTE] > **Why is it called "Finite"?** Because the machine has a *limited* (finite) number of distinct memory states, unlike a modern computer which can have billions of memory configurations. ## 1.2 DFA — Deterministic Finite Automaton A **Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA)** is an FA where the transition function is **total and deterministic**. For every state and every input symbol, there is **exactly one** next state. $$\delta : Q \times \Sigma \longrightarrow Q$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Determinism means:** Given a state $q$ and a symbol $a$, there is **one and only one** state $\delta(q, a)$ to go to. No choices, no ambiguity, no missing transitions. ### 1.2.1 Intuitive Analogy for DFA Imagine a **gumball machine with labeled buttons**. Each button is an input symbol, and pressing a button from any state takes you to *exactly one* other state. The machine has a deterministic mechanical guide rail — it cannot choose two paths at once. ## 1.3 NFA — Nondeterministic Finite Automaton A **Nondeterministic Finite Automaton (NFA)** is an FA where the transition function can map a state-input pair to a **set of states** (possibly empty, possibly multiple, possibly the entire $Q$). $$\delta : Q \times \Sigma \longrightarrow 2^Q$$ where $2^Q$ denotes the **power set** of $Q$ (all subsets of $Q$). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Nondeterminism means:** From state $q$ on input $a$, the machine may *spontaneously split* into multiple copies, each exploring a different branch. The input is **accepted** if **at least one** branch ends in a final state. ### 1.3.1 Intuitive Analogy for NFA Imagine a **person at a fork in a road** with no map. They may explore *all* paths simultaneously in their imagination. The journey is considered a "success" if any one of the imagined paths leads to the destination. NFA behaves exactly like this — it explores all possible computation paths in parallel. ## 1.4 Key Differences: DFA vs NFA | Property | DFA | NFA | |----------|-----|-----| | Transition function | $\delta: Q \times \Sigma \to Q$ (single state) | $\delta: Q \times \Sigma \to 2^Q$ (set of states) | | Number of successors | Exactly **1** per input | **0, 1, or many** per input | | Backtracking | Not allowed | Implicit parallelism (guesses) | | Hardware realization | Direct circuit | Requires parallel hardware / simulation | | Power | Same as NFA | Same as DFA | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Theorem (the heart of this module):** *Every language recognized by an NFA is also recognized by some DFA, and vice versa.* This is the **Equivalence of DFA and NFA**. ## 1.5 The Equivalence Statement Two automata $M_1$ and $M_2$ are said to be **equivalent** if they accept the **same language**: $$L(M_1) = L(M_2)$$ **Theorem (Rabin & Scott, 1959):** *For every NFA $N$, there exists a DFA $D$ such that $L(D) = L(N)$.* This means NFA and DFA have **identical computational power** with respect to language recognition, even though they are structurally different. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Are They Equivalent? — The Core Argument The proof of equivalence is constructive. Given an NFA $N = (Q_N, \Sigma, \delta_N, q_0, F_N)$, we **algorithmically build** a DFA $D = (Q_D, \Sigma, \delta_D, \{q_0\}, F_D)$ such that $L(D) = L(N)$. The technique is called the **Subset Construction** (or *powerset construction*), first formalized by **Michael O. Rabin and Dana Scott** (Nobel laureates, 1976). > [!IMPORTANT] > The central insight: instead of tracking *one* current state, the DFA tracks the **set of all possible states** the NFA could be in after reading the input so far. Each "set" becomes a single DFA state. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Subset Construction Let $N = (Q_N, \Sigma, \delta_N, q_0, F_N)$ be the given NFA. We construct $D = (Q_D, \Sigma, \delta_D, q_{0,D}, F_D)$ as follows: - **Step 1 — State Set:** $Q_D = 2^{Q_N}$ — every subset of $Q_N$ becomes a DFA state. So if $|Q_N| = n$, then $|Q_D| \le 2^n$. - **Step 2 — Alphabet:** $\Sigma_D = \Sigma_N$ — same input alphabet. - **Step 3 — Start State:** $q_{0,D} = \{q_0\}$ — singleton set containing the NFA's start state. - **Step 4 — Transition Function:** For any $S \subseteq Q_N$ and $a \in \Sigma$: $$\delta_D(S, a) = \bigcup_{q \in S} \delta_N(q, a)$$ That is, the DFA moves to the **union of all states** the NFA could move to from any state in $S$ on input $a$. - **Step 5 — Final States:** $F_D = \{ S \subseteq Q_N \mid S \cap F_N \neq \emptyset \}$ — any DFA state that **contains at least one** NFA final state is itself a final state in the DFA. > [!NOTE] > The "trick" is that although an NFA may branch into many states, we don't need infinite copies — one DFA state can encode *all* those branches simultaneously as a set. This collapses exponential nondeterminism into a deterministic simulation. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Definition | Description | Cardinality | |---|----------------------|-------------|-------------| | 1 | $\|Q_D\| \le 2^{\|Q_N\|}$ | Max number of DFA states from NFA | $2^n$ | | 2 | $\delta_D(S, a) = \bigcup_{q \in S} \delta_N(q, a)$ | Subset transition rule | — | | 3 | $q_{0,D} = \{q_0\}$ | Initial DFA state (singleton) | 1 | | 4 | $F_D = \{S \mid S \cap F_N \neq \emptyset\}$ | DFA final state definition | $\le 2^n$ | | 5 | $L_{\text{reachable}} \le 2^{\|Q_N\|}$ | Reachable DFA states (often much less) | — | | 6 | $\epsilon\text{-closure}(q) = \{q\} \cup \{p \mid q \Rightarrow^\epsilon p\}$ | Used for NFA-$\epsilon$ extension | $\le \vert Q \vert$ | | 7 | $\delta_D(S, a) = \epsilon\text{-closure}\Big(\bigcup_{q \in S} \delta_N(q, a)\Big)$ | For NFA with $\epsilon$-moves | — | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The subset construction is not just an academic exercise. It underpins: - **Compiler Design (Lexical Analysis):** Tools like `lex`/`flex` convert NFA-style regular expressions into DFA tables for ultra-fast pattern matching. - **Network Intrusion Detection:** Regex rules in IDS engines (Snort, Suricata) are first converted from NFA to DFA for $O(n)$ matching speed. - **Hardware Model Checking:** Automata-based verification relies on determinization. - **Bioinformatics:** Pattern matching on DNA sequences uses DFA equivalents of nondeterministic pattern automata. > [!IMPORTANT] > The subset construction may cause an **exponential blow-up** ($2^n$), but in practice the *reachable* subset graph is often much smaller, and algorithms (Hopcroft's DFA minimization) can further reduce the size. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples ## 3.1 Mathematical Proof of Equivalence We need to prove: For every string $w \in \Sigma^*$, $w \in L(N) \iff w \in L(D)$. ### Proof by Induction on Length of Input **Claim:** For all $w \in \Sigma^*$, $$\hat{\delta}_D(\{q_0\}, w) = \bigcup_{q \in \hat{\delta}_N(q_0, w)} \delta_N^*(q, \varepsilon)$$ where $\hat{\delta}$ denotes the **extended transition function** (over strings), and $\delta_N^*(q, \varepsilon) = \{q\}$ (reflexive closure for $\varepsilon$-NFA). For the simpler NFA-without-$\varepsilon$ case, the claim simplifies to: $$\hat{\delta}_D(\{q_0\}, w) = \hat{\delta}_N(q_0, w)$$ > **Base Case:** $w = \varepsilon$ > > Left side: $\hat{\delta}_D(\{q_0\}, \varepsilon) = \{q_0\}$ (by definition of extended $\delta$). > Right side: $\hat{\delta}_N(q_0, \varepsilon) = \{q_0\}$ (NFA on empty string stays put). > ✓ Both sides are equal. > **Inductive Step:** Assume the claim holds for $w$. Show it holds for $w \cdot a$ (where $a \in \Sigma$). > > Left side: $$\begin{aligned} \hat{\delta}_D(\{q_0\}, w \cdot a) &= \delta_D\big(\hat{\delta}_D(\{q_0\}, w), \ a\big) \\ &= \delta_D\big(\hat{\delta}_N(q_0, w), \ a\big) \quad \text{[by induction hypothesis]}\\ &= \bigcup_{q \in \hat{\delta}_N(q_0, w)} \delta_N(q, a) \quad \text{[by definition of } \delta_D \text{]}\\ &= \hat{\delta}_N(q_0, w \cdot a) \quad \text{[by definition of } \hat{\delta}_N \text{]} \end{aligned}$$ > > ✓ Both sides match, completing the induction. > **Conclusion on Acceptance:** > $w \in L(D) \iff \hat{\delta}_D(\{q_0\}, w) \in F_D$ > $\iff \hat{\delta}_D(\{q_0\}, w) \cap F_N \neq \emptyset$ (by definition of $F_D$) > $\iff \hat{\delta}_N(q_0, w) \cap F_N \neq \emptyset$ (by the claim just proven) > $\iff w \in L(N)$ > $\square$ ## 3.2 Worked Example 1: NFA → DFA Conversion **Given NFA $N$** accepting strings over $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$ that end in `01`: ``` States: Q_N = {q0, q1, q2} Start: q0 Final: F_N = {q2} Transitions (δ_N): δ_N(q0, 0) = {q0, q1} δ_N(q0, 1) = {q0} δ_N(q1, 0) = ∅ δ_N(q1, 1) = {q2} δ_N(q2, 0) = ∅ δ_N(q2, 1) = ∅ ``` **Step 1: Compute all subsets of $Q_N = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$** (total $2^3 = 8$): $\emptyset, \{q_0\}, \{q_1\}, \{q_2\}, \{q_0,q_1\}, \{q_0,q_2\}, \{q_1,q_2\}, \{q_0,q_1,q_2\}$ **Step 2: Apply the subset transition rule** for the reachable subsets starting from $\{q_0\}$: | DFA State $S$ | $\delta_D(S, 0)$ | $\delta_D(S, 1)$ | |---------------|------------------|------------------| | $\{q_0\}$ | $\delta_N(q_0,0)=\{q_0,q_1\}$ | $\delta_N(q_0,1)=\{q_0\}$ | | $\{q_0,q_1\}$ | $\delta_N(q_0,0)\cup\delta_N(q_1,0)=\{q_0,q_1\}\cup\emptyset=\{q_0,q_1\}$ | $\delta_N(q_0,1)\cup\delta_N(q_1,1)=\{q_0\}\cup\{q_2\}=\{q_0,q_2\}$ | | $\{q_0,q_2\}$ | $\delta_N(q_0,0)\cup\delta_N(q_2,0)=\{q_0,q_1\}\cup\emptyset=\{q_0,q_1\}$ | $\delta_N(q_0,1)\cup\delta_N(q_2,1)=\{q_0\}\cup\emptyset=\{q_0\}$ | | $\emptyset$ (trap) | $\emptyset$ | $\emptyset$ | **Step 3: Identify DFA final states** (those containing $q_2$): $F_D = \{\{q_0,q_2\}\}$ **Step 4: Reachable DFA:** $$Q_D = \big\{\{q_0\}, \{q_0,q_1\}, \{q_0,q_2\}, \emptyset\big\}$$ This is just **4 states** instead of the worst-case **8** — reachable subsets are typically far fewer. ## 3.3 Worked Example 2: NFA with $\varepsilon$-moves **Given:** NFA-$\varepsilon$ over $\Sigma=\{a,b,c\}$: ``` δ(q0, a) = {q0} δ(q0, ε) = {q1} (i.e., c is free, automatically accepted) δ(q1, b) = {q1} δ(q1, ε) = {q2} δ(q2, c) = {q2} F = {q2} ``` Language: $L = c^* b^* a^* a b c^*$ (concatenation, where 'c' can come free via $\varepsilon$). **Step 1: Compute $\varepsilon$-closures:** - $\varepsilon$-closure$(q_0) = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$ - $\varepsilon$-closure$(q_1) = \{q_1, q_2\}$ - $\varepsilon$-closure$(q_2) = \{q_2\}$ - $\varepsilon$-closure$(q_0, q_1) = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$ **Step 2: Apply extended subset rule:** $\delta_D(S, x) = \varepsilon\text{-closure}\Big(\bigcup_{q \in S} \delta_N(q, x)\Big)$ For start state $S_0 = \{q_0\}$: $$\begin{aligned} \delta_D(\{q_0\}, a) &= \varepsilon\text{-closure}(\delta_N(q_0, a)) = \varepsilon\text{-closure}(\{q_0\}) = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\} \\ \delta_D(\{q_0\}, b) &= \varepsilon\text{-closure}(\delta_N(q_0, b)) = \varepsilon\text{-closure}(\emptyset) = \emptyset \\ \delta_D(\{q_0\}, c) &= \varepsilon\text{-closure}(\delta_N(q_0, c)) = \varepsilon\text{-closure}(\emptyset) = \emptyset \end{aligned}$$ Continue similarly for other reachable sets. The resulting DFA accepts the same language as the original $\varepsilon$-NFA. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Complexity Summary | Aspect | DFA | NFA | Conversion Cost | |--------|-----|-----|-----------------| | States | $n$ | $n$ | DFA may have up to $2^n$ | | Time per input | $O(\vert w \vert)$ | $O(\vert w \vert \cdot k)$ where $k$ is branching factor | Conversion: $O(\vert \Sigma \vert \cdot 2^n)$ | | Space | $n$ states | $n$ states | DFA table: $2^n \times \vert \Sigma \vert$ entries | | Acceptance | Unique path | Exists accepting path | Equivalent | | Construction difficulty | Easy to simulate | Easier to design from regex | Subset construction is polynomial in output size | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Architecture: DFA vs NFA Processing Model ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DFA["DFA Model - Deterministic"] D1["State q"] -->|reads a| D2["Unique Next State"] D2 -->|"δ(q,a) = single state"| D3["Decision: Accept or Reject"] end subgraph NFA["NFA Model - Nondeterministic"] N1["State q"] -->|reads a| N2{"Split into branches"} N2 -->|branch 1| N3["State p1"] N2 -->|branch 2| N4["State p2"] N2 -->|branch 3| N5["State p3"] N3 --> N6{"Any branch accepts?"} N4 --> N6 N5 --> N6 N6 -->|YES| N7["ACCEPT"] N6 -->|NO| N8["REJECT"] end ``` ## 4.2 Subset Construction Algorithm Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Start: NFA N with states QN"] --> B["Initialize DFA states with start set eq0 = eq0"] B --> C["Mark eq0 as unprocessed and add to worklist"] C --> D{"Worklist empty?"} D -->|NO| E["Pick a set S from worklist"] E --> F["For each input symbol a in Sigma"] F --> G["Compute T = union of deltaN over all q in S on a"] G --> H{"T already in DFA states?"} H -->|NO| I["Add T as new DFA state, mark unprocessed, add to worklist"] H -->|YES| J["Use existing state"] I --> K["Set deltaD of S on a = T"] J --> K K --> L["More symbols in Sigma?"] L -->|YES| F L -->|NO| M["Mark S as processed"] M --> D D -->|YES| N["Final states FD = all S such that S intersect FN is not empty"] N --> O["Done: Equivalent DFA constructed"] ``` ## 4.3 Worked Example 1 — Transition Diagram (DFA Form) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> A state "A = {q0}" as A state "B = {q0,q1}" as B state "C = {q0,q2} (Final)" as C state "D = empty (Trap)" as D A --> A: 1 A --> B: 0 B --> B: 0 B --> C: 1 C --> A: 1 C --> B: 0 D --> D: 0 D --> D: 1 ``` ## 4.4 Conversion Pipeline — High-Level Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Input["Problem Input Layer"] R["Regular Expression"] --> RN["NFA via Thompsons Construction"] RN --> N["NFA with epsilon moves"] end subgraph Engine["Equivalence Engine"] N --> SC["Subset Construction Algorithm"] SC --> DS["DFA with 2 to the n states"] DS --> HM["Hopcroft Minimization"] HM --> MD["Minimal DFA"] end subgraph Output["Deployment Layer"] MD --> TT["Transition Table for Compiler"] MD --> HW["Hardware FSM Implementation"] MD --> SW["Pattern Matcher in Software"] end ``` ## 4.5 Set-of-States Visualization ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PowerSet["Power Set of NFA States QN = q0, q1, q2"] direction TB L0["Level 0: empty set"] --> L1["Level 1: {q0}, {q1}, {q2}"] L1 --> L2["Level 2: {q0,q1}, {q0,q2}, {q1,q2}"] L2 --> L3["Level 3: {q0,q1,q2}"] end Note["DFA states = nodes of this lattice
Transitions go between nodes via subset rule"] -.-> PowerSet ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **"State and prove the equivalence of DFA and NFA."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Statement:** *For every NFA $N$, there exists a DFA $D$ such that $L(D) = L(N)$, and vice versa.* (1 mark) > **Proof Sketch:** Given $N = (Q_N, \Sigma, \delta_N, q_0, F_N)$, construct $D = (2^{Q_N}, \Sigma, \delta_D, \{q_0\}, F_D)$ where: $$\delta_D(S, a) = \bigcup_{q \in S} \delta_N(q, a) \quad \text{and} \quad F_D = \{S \subseteq Q_N \mid S \cap F_N \neq \emptyset\}$$ > (1 mark) > By induction on $|w|$, $\hat{\delta}_D(\{q_0\}, w) = \hat{\delta}_N(q_0, w)$. Hence $w \in L(D) \iff w \in L(N)$. (1 mark) --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **"Differentiate between DFA and NFA. Why do we prefer DFA over NFA in implementation?"** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Differentiation (2 marks):** > - DFA: $\delta: Q \times \Sigma \to Q$ (single next state); NFA: $\delta: Q \times \Sigma \to 2^Q$ (subset of states). > - DFA has exactly one transition per input symbol; NFA can have zero, one, or many. > - DFA execution is sequential; NFA conceptually explores multiple paths in parallel. > **Why DFA is preferred (1 mark):** DFA executes in $O(|w|)$ time using a single current-state variable; NFA would require backtracking or parallel simulation, both expensive. DFA maps directly to hardware (FSM) and software transition tables in compilers. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** Define NFA. Construct a DFA equivalent to the following NFA using subset construction.
