(7 marks) | State | $\delta(\cdot, 0)$ | $\delta(\cdot, 1)$ | |-------|-------------------|-------------------| | $\to q_0$ | $\{q_0, q_1\}$ | $\{q_0\}$ | | $q_1$ | $\emptyset$ | $\{q_2\}$ | | $*q_2$ | $\{q_2\}$ | $\{q_1\}$ | ($\to$ = start state, $*$ = final state) **Model Solution (7 marks):** > **Step 1: NFA Definition (1 mark):** $N = (\{q_0,q_1,q_2\}, \{0,1\}, \delta_N, q_0, \{q_2\})$ where $\delta_N$ is given in the table. > **Step 2: Initialize DFA start state** $A = \{q_0\}$. (0.5 mark) > **Step 3: Compute reachable subsets** (4 marks): > > - $A = \{q_0\}$: $\delta_D(A, 0) = \delta_N(q_0,0) = \{q_0,q_1\} = B$; $\delta_D(A, 1) = \delta_N(q_0,1) = \{q_0\} = A$. > - $B = \{q_0, q_1\}$: $\delta_D(B, 0) = \delta_N(q_0,0) \cup \delta_N(q_1,0) = \{q_0,q_1\} \cup \emptyset = B$; $\delta_D(B, 1) = \delta_N(q_0,1) \cup \delta_N(q_1,1) = \{q_0\} \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0,q_2\} = C$. > - $C = \{q_0, q_2\}$: $\delta_D(C, 0) = \delta_N(q_0,0) \cup \delta_N(q_2,0) = \{q_0,q_1\} \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0,q_1,q_2\} = D$; $\delta_D(C, 1) = \delta_N(q_0,1) \cup \delta_N(q_2,1) = \{q_0\} \cup \{q_1\} = \{q_0,q_1\} = B$. > - $D = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$: $\delta_D(D, 0) = \{q_0,q_1\} \cup \emptyset \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0,q_1,q_2\} = D$; $\delta_D(D, 1) = \{q_0\} \cup \{q_2\} \cup \{q_1\} = \{q_0,q_1,q_2\} = D$. > **Step 4: Identify final states (1 mark):** Any state containing $q_2$ is final: $F_D = \{C, D\}$. > **Step 5: Transition Table (0.5 mark):** > > | State | 0 | 1 | > |-------|---|---| > | $\to A$ | B | A | > | B | B | C | > | *C | D | B | > | *D | D | D | **(b)** Prove that the language accepted by an NFA $N$ with $n$ states can be accepted by a DFA with at most $2^n$ states. (7 marks) **Model Solution (7 marks):** > **Construction (3 marks):** As shown in part (a), construct $D = (2^{Q_N}, \Sigma, \delta_D, \{q_0\}, F_D)$ with: $$\delta_D(S, a) = \bigcup_{q \in S} \delta_N(q, a)$$ > The state set $2^{Q_N}$ has cardinality exactly $2^{|Q_N|} = 2^n$ when $n = |Q_N|$. > **Correctness via Induction (3 marks):** > Base: $|w|=0$: $\hat\delta_D(\{q_0\},\varepsilon)=\{q_0\}=\hat\delta_N(q_0,\varepsilon)$. > Inductive: Assume equality for $w$. Then $$\begin{aligned} \hat\delta_D(\{q_0\}, wa) &= \delta_D(\hat\delta_D(\{q_0\},w), a) \\ &= \delta_D(\hat\delta_N(q_0, w), a) \\ &= \bigcup_{q \in \hat\delta_N(q_0, w)} \delta_N(q, a) = \hat\delta_N(q_0, wa) \end{aligned}$$ > **Conclusion (1 mark):** By induction, $L(D) = L(N)$, and $|Q_D| \le 2^n$. $\blacksquare$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[Alternative Choice — KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **(a)** Construct a DFA equivalent to the following NFA-$\varepsilon$ using subset construction. Show the $\varepsilon$-closure computations explicitly. (7 marks) ``` NFA-ε: States: q0, q1, q2, q3 Alphabet: {a, b} Start: q0 Final: {q3} Transitions: δ(q0, a) = {q1} δ(q1, ε) = {q2} δ(q2, b) = {q2, q3} δ(q3, ε) = {q0} ``` **Model Solution (7 marks):** > **Step 1: Compute $\varepsilon$-closures (1.5 marks):** > - $\varepsilon$-closure$(q_0) = \{q_0\}$ > - $\varepsilon$-closure$(q_1) = \{q_1, q_2\}$ > - $\varepsilon$-closure$(q_2) = \{q_2\}$ > - $\varepsilon$-closure$(q_3) = \{q_0, q_3\}$ (since $q_3 \to^\varepsilon q_0$) > **Step 2: DFA start state** $S_0 = \varepsilon$-closure$(q_0) = \{q_0\}$. (0.5 mark) > **Step 3: Apply extended rule** $\delta_D(S, x) = \varepsilon\text{-closure}\big(\bigcup_{q \in S}\delta_N(q,x)\big)$ (4 marks): > > - $\delta_D(\{q_0\}, a) = \varepsilon\text{-closure}(\delta_N(q_0, a)) = \varepsilon\text{-closure}(\{q_1\}) = \{q_1, q_2\} = A$ > - $\delta_D(\{q_0\}, b) = \varepsilon\text{-closure}(\delta_N(q_0, b)) = \varepsilon\text{-closure}(\emptyset) = \emptyset = T$ (trap) > - $\delta_D(A, a) = \varepsilon\text{-closure}(\delta_N(q_1,a) \cup \delta_N(q_2,a)) = \varepsilon\text{-closure}(\emptyset) = T$ > - $\delta_D(A, b) = \varepsilon\text{-closure}(\delta_N(q_1,b) \cup \delta_N(q_2,b)) = \varepsilon\text{-closure}(\{q_2, q_3\}) = \{q_0, q_2, q_3\} = B$ > - $\delta_D(B, a) = \varepsilon\text{-closure}(\{q_1\} \cup \emptyset \cup \emptyset) = \{q_1, q_2\} = A$ > - $\delta_D(B, b) = \varepsilon\text{-closure}(\emptyset \cup \{q_2,q_3\} \cup \emptyset) = \{q_0, q_2, q_3\} = B$ > - $\delta_D(T, a) = \delta_D(T, b) = T$ > **Step 4: Final states** $F_D = \{B\}$ (only set containing $q_3$). (0.5 mark) > **Step 5: Transition table (0.5 mark):** > > | State | a | b | > |-------|---|---| > | $\to S_0$ | A | T | > | A | T | B | > | *B | A | B | > | T | T | T | **(b)** Two NFAs $N_1$ and $N_2$ are said to be equivalent if $L(N_1) = L(N_2)$. Show that any NFA can be converted to a DFA with at most $2^n$ states. Discuss the time complexity of this conversion. (7 marks) **Model Solution (7 marks):** > **Construction (3 marks):** As established earlier, for an NFA with $n$ states, the DFA's state space is $2^Q$, of size $2^n$. Every subset is a potential DFA state. > **Time Complexity (3 marks):** > - Number of reachable DFA states is at most $2^n$. > - For each state, we compute transitions for all $|\Sigma|$ input symbols. > - **Total conversion time:** $O(|\Sigma| \cdot 2^n)$. > - **Space for transition table:** $O(|\Sigma| \cdot 2^n)$. > **Discussion (1 mark):** Although worst-case is exponential, in practice: > - Reachable states are often far fewer than $2^n$. > - Hopcroft's algorithm can minimize the DFA to its canonical minimal form. > - Some languages (e.g., $(a+b)^*a(a+b)^k$) provably require $2^k$ states, showing the bound is tight. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully:** > 1. **Missing $\varepsilon$-closure step:** When converting NFA-$\varepsilon$, students often forget to apply $\varepsilon$-closure after the union, leading to wrong transitions. **Lose up to 3 marks.** > 2. **Wrong final-state identification:** A DFA state is final if it *contains* a final NFA state — not only if it *equals* one. The condition is $S \cap F_N \neq \emptyset$. **Lose 1 mark.** > 3. **Forgetting the trap/dead state:** When constructing the DFA, you must include the empty-set state $\emptyset$ if reachable, and self-loop it on all inputs. **Lose 1 mark.** > 4. **Confusing $L(M_1) = L(M_2)$ with structural equality:** Equivalence is **language-level**, not structural. Two very different-looking automata can be equivalent. > 5. **Skipping the induction base case in proofs:** A complete proof must explicitly show $|w|=0$ and the inductive step. Skipping either costs at least 1 mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Equivalence of DFA and NFA** - ☐ **Core Theorem:** Every NFA $N$ has an equivalent DFA $D$ with $L(D) = L(N)$. They have **identical expressive power** for regular languages. - ☐ **Construction:** Subset / Powerset construction — track *sets* of NFA states as single DFA states. - ☐ **Key Formula:** $\delta_D(S, a) = \bigcup_{q \in S} \delta_N(q, a)$ - ☐ **Start State:** $\{q_0\}$ — a singleton set, not $q_0$ alone. - ☐ **Final States:** $F_D = \{S \subseteq Q_N \mid S \cap F_N \neq \emptyset\}$ — *any* set containing at least one NFA final is a DFA final. - ☐ **For NFA-$\varepsilon$:** Add $\varepsilon$-closure around the union: $\delta_D(S,a) = \varepsilon\text{-closure}(\bigcup_{q\in S}\delta_N(q,a))$. - ☐ **Size Bound:** DFA has at most $2^n$ states — exponential blow-up is the worst case. - ☐ **Acceptance:** DFA accepts $w$ iff $\hat\delta_D(\{q_0\}, w) \in F_D$ — equivalent to NFA having *any* path to a final state. - ☐ **Trap/Death State:** Always include $\emptyset$ in $Q_D$ if reachable; it is never a final state. - ☐ **Equivalence means language equality:** $L(M_1) = L(M_2)$, not structural equality. - ☐ **Why DFA preferred:** Single-state execution → $O(|w|)$ time, easy hardware mapping, simpler code. - ☐ **Time Complexity of Conversion:** $O(|\Sigma| \cdot 2^n)$; space $O(|\Sigma| \cdot 2^n)$. - ☐ **Minimization follows:** After conversion, apply Hopcroft's algorithm to get the minimal DFA. - ☐ **Induction Template for Proofs:** Base ($w=\varepsilon$) + Step ($w \to wa$) on the extended transition function. > **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Equivalence of DFA and NFA**

topics.title: Regular Grammar (RG)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET414 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Regular Grammar (RG) # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Regular Grammar (RG) A **Regular Grammar (RG)** is a formal grammar that belongs to **Type 3** in the **Chomsky Hierarchy** and is used to generate exactly the class of **regular languages**. A grammar $G$ is formally defined as a 4-tuple: $$G = (V, T, P, S)$$ where the components are: - $V$ = Finite set of **non-terminal symbols** (variables) - $T$ = Finite set of **terminal symbols** (alphabet) - $P$ = Finite set of **production rules** - $S \in V$ = Designated **start symbol** A grammar $G$ is called a **Regular Grammar** if **every production rule** in $P$ is restricted to one of the following two restricted forms: ### Type A: Right-Linear Grammar (RLG) Every production must be of the form: $$A \rightarrow aB \quad \text{or} \quad A \rightarrow a$$ where $A, B \in V$ and $a \in T \cup \{\varepsilon\}$. ### Type B: Left-Linear Grammar (LLG) Every production must be of the form: $$A \rightarrow Ba \quad \text{or} \quad A \rightarrow a$$ where $A, B \in V$ and $a \in T \cup \{\varepsilon\}$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A grammar is regular **only if ALL** its production rules conform to one specific form (either all right-linear or all left-linear). **A mixture of right-linear and left-linear rules in the same grammar is NOT a regular grammar** — it becomes a Context-Free Grammar (CFG). This is a very common KTU exam trap. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Assembly Line Recipe Imagine a **toy factory assembly line** that builds toy cars: - **Non-terminals ($V$)** are like *workstations* on the line (e.g., "Chassis Station", "Wheel Station", "Paint Station"). Each station has a name, not a finished product. - **Terminals ($T$)** are the *raw materials* or *final parts* (e.g., metal, plastic, paint, stickers) that get added to the toy. - **Productions ($P$)** are the *standard operating procedures* posted at each station, e.g., "Take a metal plate AND pass it to the Wheel Station" ($A \rightarrow aB$) or "Add final paint, this is the end" ($A \rightarrow a$). - **Start symbol ($S$)** is the *factory entrance gate* — every toy starts its journey here. > A **Regular Grammar** is like an assembly line that follows a **strictly linear flow** — material only moves **forward** (right-linear) or **backward** (left-linear) by **exactly one workstation at a time**. It cannot branch, loop recursively, or nest things. That's why it can only build *simple, repetitive* patterns (regular languages), not nested structures like balanced parentheses. ## 1.3 Position in the Chomsky Hierarchy The **Chomsky Hierarchy** classifies formal grammars into four types by increasing expressive power: | Type | Grammar Class | Language Class | Automaton | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | **Type 0** | Unrestricted (Phrase Structure) | Recursively Enumerable | Turing Machine | | **Type 1** | Context-Sensitive | Context-Sensitive | Linear-Bounded Automaton | | **Type 2** | Context-Free | Context-Free | Pushdown Automaton | | **Type 3** | **Regular** | **Regular** | **Finite Automaton** | > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway:** Regular Grammar is the **least powerful** but **most efficient** (in terms of recognition) class. A language is regular **if and only if** it can be generated by some Regular Grammar **if and only if** it is accepted by some Finite Automaton **if and only if** it can be described by some Regular Expression. ## 1.4 The Grand Equivalence Theorem > [!IMPORTANT] > **Theorem (KTU High-Yield):** The following four formalisms are **equivalent** in expressive power: > 1. Regular Expressions (RE) > 2. Deterministic Finite Automata (DFA) > 3. Non-Deterministic Finite Automata (NFA) > 4. Regular Grammars (RG) — both Right-Linear and Left-Linear > > $$\text{RE} \equiv \text{DFA} \equiv \text{NFA} \equiv \text{RG}$$ This means anything you can do with one, you can do with the other (with possibly some conversion work). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Right-Linear Grammar (RLG) In a Right-Linear Grammar, the **non-terminal always appears on the rightmost end** of the production. This is the most common form tested in KTU exams. ### Allowed Production Forms | Form | Description | Example | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $A \rightarrow aB$ | Generate terminal $a$, move to non-terminal $B$ | $S \rightarrow aS$ | | $A \rightarrow a$ | Generate terminal $a$, terminate derivation | $S \rightarrow a$ | | $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | Empty string generation (if $A$ is nullable) | $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | ### Disallowed Forms (These make it CFG, not RG) | Form | Why Not Regular? | | :--- | :--- | | $A \rightarrow aBb$ | Two non-terminals — needs context-free rules | | $A \rightarrow BC$ | No terminal on right — violates terminal-nonterminal pattern | | $A \rightarrow aB \mid Ba$ | **Mixed** right-linear and left-linear | | $A \rightarrow aBbC$ | More than one non-terminal | ## 2.2 Anatomy of a Left-Linear Grammar (LLG) In a Left-Linear Grammar, the **non-terminal always appears on the leftmost end** of the production. ### Allowed Production Forms | Form | Description | Example | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $A \rightarrow Ba$ | Move from non-terminal $B$, generate terminal $a$ | $S \rightarrow Sa$ | | $A \rightarrow a$ | Generate terminal $a$, terminate derivation | $S \rightarrow a$ | | $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | Empty string generation | $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | ## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table summarizes every essential rule, definition, and conversion pattern needed for KTU examinations on Regular Grammar. Memorize this thoroughly. | # | Concept | Rule / Formula / Definition | | :---: | :---: | :---: | | 1 | Grammar Definition | $G = (V, T, P, S)$ | | 2 | Right-Linear Form | $A \rightarrow aB$ or $A \rightarrow a$ | | 3 | Left-Linear Form | $A \rightarrow Ba$ or $A \rightarrow a$ | | 4 | Chomsky Type | Type 3 | | 5 | Language Class Generated | Regular Language | | 6 | Recognition Automaton | Finite Automaton (DFA / NFA) | | 7 | FA → RLG: State Mapping | Each FA state $q_i$ becomes a non-terminal $Q_i$ | | 8 | FA → RLG: Transition Rule | $\delta(q_i, a) = q_j$ becomes $Q_i \rightarrow aQ_j$ | | 9 | FA → RLG: Final State | If $q_j \in F$, add $Q_j \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | | 10 | FA → RLG: Start Symbol | Initial state $q_0$ becomes start symbol $S$ | | 11 | RLG → FA: Non-terminal Mapping | Each non-terminal $A$ becomes a state $q_A$ | | 12 | RLG → FA: Production Mapping | $A \rightarrow aB$ becomes $\delta(q_A, a) = q_B$ | | 13 | RLG → FA: Terminal Production | $A \rightarrow a$ becomes $q_A \xrightarrow{a} q_f$ (final state) | | 14 | Equivalence Principle | $\text{RE} \equiv \text{DFA} \equiv \text{NFA} \equiv \text{RG} \equiv \text{LLG}$ | | 15 | Grammar Language | $L(G) = \{w \in T^* \mid S \Rightarrow^* w\}$ | ## 2.4 Why the Equivalence Works: The Core Logic The deep reason Regular Grammars and Finite Automata are equivalent lies in the **structural correspondence** between their computational models: - An **NFA** processes a string **one symbol at a time**, transitioning between states. This is a **left-to-right, sequential** process. - A **Right-Linear Grammar** builds a string by replacing the **leftmost non-terminal** with a terminal followed by a new non-terminal. This is also a **left-to-right, sequential** process. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The 'Why' Behind the Equivalence:** Both systems encode a **finite amount of memory** (the current state / the current non-terminal). Neither has any stack or recursion beyond the single current position. Since memory is bounded and the operation is local, they compute the **exact same class of languages**. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Regular Grammars and their equivalent automata are not just academic exercises — they are the **backbone of modern computing infrastructure**: | Application Domain | Use of Regular Grammars / FA | | :--- | :--- | | **Lexical Analysis** (Compilers) | Tokenizing source code (identifiers, keywords, numbers) | | **Pattern Matching** (grep, regex) | Searching text using regular expressions | | **Network Protocols** | TCP/IP header validation, packet filtering (firewalls) | | **Digital Circuit Design** | Sequential logic minimization (state machines in hardware) | | **Text Editors** | Find/Replace operations (Ctrl+F functionality) | | **Spell Checkers** | Validating dictionary words | | **DNA Sequence Analysis** | Identifying gene patterns in bioinformatics | ## 2.6 Worked Example: Identifying a Regular Grammar **Given Grammar $G$:** $$S \rightarrow aS \mid bA \qquad A \rightarrow aA \mid bB \qquad B \rightarrow aB \mid \varepsilon$$ **Analysis:** - All productions are of the form $A \rightarrow aB$ (e.g., $S \rightarrow aS$ has terminal $a$ then non-terminal $S$) or $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ (e.g., $B \rightarrow \varepsilon$). - **No production has a non-terminal in the middle** (e.g., no $A \rightarrow aBb$). - **All productions are right-linear** (non-terminals appear only at the right end). **Conclusion:** $G$ is a **Right-Linear Regular Grammar**. **Language Generated:** Strings over $\{a, b\}$ that contain **at least two $b$'s** followed by any number of $a$'s. $$L(G) = \{a^i b a^j b a^k \mid i, j, k \geq 0\}$$ # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section covers the **most important KTU exam derivations**: the explicit algorithms for converting between Finite Automata and Regular Grammars. These conversions are worth **7 to 14 marks** in typical KTU questions. ## 3.1 Derivation 1: NFA to Right-Linear Grammar (FA → RLG) ### The Algorithm (Step-by-Step Logic) > **Input:** A Non-Deterministic Finite Automaton $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ > **Output:** A Right-Linear Grammar $G = (V, T, P, S)$ such that $L(G) = L(M)$ **Step 1:** Initialize $V = Q$ (each state becomes a non-terminal), $T = \Sigma$ (input alphabet becomes terminal set), $S = q_0$ (start state becomes start symbol), and $P = \emptyset$ (no productions yet). **Step 2:** For **each transition** $\delta(q_i, a) = q_j$ in the NFA, add the production rule: $$q_i \rightarrow a q_j$$ to $P$. This production says: "When non-terminal $q_i$ is expanded, it produces terminal $a$ and then passes control to non-terminal $q_j$." **Step 3:** For **each final state** $q_f \in F$, add the production rule: $$q_f \rightarrow \varepsilon$$ to $P$. This production says: "If we reach a final state, the string is accepted (empty derivation completes the string)." **Step 4:** The grammar $G$ is now complete. $L(G) = L(M)$ by construction. ### Worked Numerical Example: NFA → RLG **Given NFA $M$ with:** - $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$ - $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$ - Start state: $q_0$ - Final states: $F = \{q_2\}$ - Transitions: $\delta(q_0, a) = q_1$, $\delta(q_1, b) = q_1$, $\delta(q_1, a) = q_2$ **Step-by-Step Conversion:** **Step 1:** Initialize the grammar components: $$V = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}, \quad T = \{a, b\}, \quad S = q_0, \quad P = \emptyset$$ **Step 2:** Process each transition: For $\delta(q_0, a) = q_1$, add the production: $$q_0 \rightarrow a q_1$$ For $\delta(q_1, b) = q_1$, add the production: $$q_1 \rightarrow b q_1$$ For $\delta(q_1, a) = q_2$, add the production: $$q_1 \rightarrow a q_2$$ **Step 3:** Process the final state $q_2 \in F$, add the production: $$q_2 \rightarrow \varepsilon$$ **Step 4:** Final grammar $G$: $$G = (\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}, \{a, b\}, P, q_0)$$ where $P$ consists of: $$q_0 \rightarrow a q_1, \quad q_1 \rightarrow b q_1, \quad q_1 \rightarrow a q_2, \quad q_2 \rightarrow \varepsilon$$ ### Verification: Trace a Sample String Let's verify the string $w = aba$ is accepted by the grammar: $$ \begin{aligned} S &\Rightarrow q_0 \\ &\Rightarrow a q_1 \quad &\text{(using } q_0 \rightarrow a q_1 \text{)} \\ &\Rightarrow a b q_1 \quad &\text{(using } q_1 \rightarrow b q_1 \text{)} \\ &\Rightarrow a b a q_2 \quad &\text{(using } q_1 \rightarrow a q_2 \text{)} \\ &\Rightarrow a b a \quad &\text{(using } q_2 \rightarrow \varepsilon \text{)} \\ \end{aligned} $$ The string $aba$ is successfully derived. The grammar is correct. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Right-Linear Grammar to NFA (RLG → FA) This is the **reverse direction** of the previous derivation, and is equally important for KTU exams. ### The Algorithm > **Input:** A Right-Linear Grammar $G = (V, T, P, S)$ > **Output:** An NFA $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ such that $L(M) = L(G)$ **Step 1:** Initialize $Q = V \cup \{q_f\}$, where $q_f$ is a **new dedicated final state** (it does not correspond to any non-terminal in $V$). This new state is needed to handle terminal-only productions uniformly. Set $\Sigma = T$ and $q_0 = S$. **Step 2:** For each production of the form: $$A \rightarrow aB$$ add the transition: $$\delta(A, a) = B$$ to the NFA. **Step 3:** For each production of the form: $$A \rightarrow a$$ add the transition: $$\delta(A, a) = q_f$$ to the NFA. The new final state $q_f$ absorbs the termination. **Step 4:** For each production of the form: $$A \rightarrow \varepsilon$$ (if $A$ is nullable and $\varepsilon \in L(G)$), mark $A$ as a final state, i.e., add $A$ to $F$. Also include $q_f$ in $F$ as the canonical final state. **Step 5:** Set $F = \{q_f\}$. (If $\varepsilon \in L(G)$, also add $S$ to $F$.) ### Worked Numerical Example: RLG → NFA **Given Grammar $G$:** - $V = \{S, A, B\}$, $T = \{a, b\}$ - Productions: $S \rightarrow aA$, $S \rightarrow bA$, $A \rightarrow aA$, $A \rightarrow bB$, $B \rightarrow aB$, $B \rightarrow bB$, $B \rightarrow \varepsilon$ **Step-by-Step Conversion:** **Step 1:** Define states and add new final state: $$Q = \{S, A, B, q_f\}, \quad \Sigma = \{a, b\}, \quad q_0 = S$$ **Step 2:** Convert each $A \rightarrow aB$ style production to a transition: | Production | NFA Transition | | :---: | :---: | | $S \rightarrow aA$ | $\delta(S, a) = A$ | | $S \rightarrow bA$ | $\delta(S, b) = A$ | | $A \rightarrow aA$ | $\delta(A, a) = A$ | | $A \rightarrow bB$ | $\delta(A, b) = B$ | | $B \rightarrow aB$ | $\delta(B, a) = B$ | | $B \rightarrow bB$ | $\delta(B, b) = B$ | **Step 3:** Convert each $A \rightarrow a$ style production: There are no productions of the form $A \rightarrow a$ in this grammar, so this step adds no transitions to the new final state $q_f$. **Step 4:** Handle the $\varepsilon$-production $B \rightarrow \varepsilon$: Since $B$ is nullable and $B$ is reachable, we add $B$ to the final state set. Set: $$F = \{q_f, B\}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Alternative Convention:** Some textbooks omit Step 4's addition of nullable non-terminals to $F$ and rely solely on the new $q_f$. However, the **most rigorous KTU-accepted form** is to include both, as shown above. **Step 5:** The resulting NFA is: $$M = (\{S, A, B, q_f\}, \{a, b\}, \delta, S, \{q_f, B\})$$ with the transitions as listed in Step 2. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Right-Linear Grammar to Left-Linear Grammar Sometimes KTU questions ask you to convert a RLG to an equivalent LLG (or vice versa). The algorithm involves reversing strings and using a non-terminal reversal technique. ### The Algorithm (RLG → LLG) **Step 1:** Identify all non-terminals $V = \{A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n\}$. **Step 2:** For each non-terminal $A_i$, introduce a new non-terminal $B_i$ (its "mirror image"). The set of $B_i$'s will be the new non-terminals of the LLG. **Step 3:** Reverse every production of the RLG: - $A_i \rightarrow a A_j$ becomes $B_i \rightarrow B_j a$ - $A_i \rightarrow a$ becomes $B_i \rightarrow a$ - $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ stays as $B_{\text{start}} \rightarrow \varepsilon$ **Step 4:** The new start symbol of the LLG corresponds to the original start symbol $S$ (or its mirror $B_S$). The resulting grammar is left-linear and generates the **reverse language**: $L(G_{\text{LLG}}) = (L(G_{\text{RLG}}))^R$. **Step 5:** To get a left-linear grammar for the **original** language $L(G)$ (not its reverse), you must carefully handle the start symbol: make the new start symbol correspond to the original start, and the productions capture the same derivations in mirror order. ### Quick Worked Example **Given RLG:** $$S \rightarrow aA, \quad A \rightarrow bA, \quad A \rightarrow b$$ **Applying the algorithm:** - $S \rightarrow aA$ becomes $S' \rightarrow A' a$ - $A \rightarrow bA$ becomes $A' \rightarrow A' b$ - $A \rightarrow b$ becomes $A' \rightarrow b$ **Resulting LLG:** $$S' \rightarrow A' a, \quad A' \rightarrow A' b, \quad A' \rightarrow b$$ This LLG generates $L = \{a b b b \ldots b \mid \text{at least one } b\}$, which is the **same language** as the original RLG, just expressed in left-linear form. ## 3.4 The Master Conversion Matrix This analytical matrix shows the **complete correspondence** between components of an FA and an RG: | Finite Automaton Component | Right-Linear Grammar Component | Mapping Rule | | :---: | :---: | :---: | | States $Q = \{q_0, q_1, \ldots\}$ | Non-terminals $V = \{Q_0, Q_1, \ldots\}$ | One-to-one bijection | | Alphabet $\Sigma$ | Terminals $T$ | Identical sets | | Start state $q_0$ | Start symbol $S$ | $S \leftrightarrow q_0$ | | Transition $\delta(q_i, a) = q_j$ | Production $Q_i \rightarrow a Q_j$ | Direct encoding | | Final state $q_f$ | Production $Q_f \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | Termination marker | | Acceptance of $w$ | Derivation $S \Rightarrow^* w$ | Equivalent operations | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Chomsky Hierarchy Pyramid The following diagram shows the **Chomsky Hierarchy** with Regular Grammar at the bottom (least powerful, most efficient): ```mermaid graph TD type0["TYPE 0: Unrestricted Grammar
Language: Recursively Enumerable
Automaton: Turing Machine"] type1["TYPE 1: Context-Sensitive Grammar
Language: Context-Sensitive
Automaton: Linear-Bounded Automaton"] type2["TYPE 2: Context-Free Grammar
Language: Context-Free
Automaton: Pushdown Automaton"] type3["TYPE 3: Regular Grammar
Language: Regular
Automaton: Finite Automaton"] type0 --> type1 type1 --> type2 type2 --> type3 style type0 fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px style type1 fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px style type2 fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px style type3 fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:3px ``` **Reading the Diagram:** Arrows go from more powerful to less powerful. **Type 3 (Regular)** is the most restricted but computationally cheapest. **Type 0 (Unrestricted)** is the most powerful but computationally undecidable in the general case. ## 4.2 The Equivalence Circle: RE ↔ FA ↔ RG The four equivalent formalisms form a closed equivalence class. The following diagram shows the conversion paths and the corresponding algorithms: ```mermaid graph LR RE["REGULAR EXPRESSION
e.g., a*b*"] DFA["DFA
Deterministic FA"] NFA["NFA
Non-Deterministic FA"] RLG["RIGHT-LINEAR GRAMMAR
G = V, T, P, S"] LLG["LEFT-LINEAR GRAMMAR
G = V, T, P, S"] RE -- "Thomson Construction" --> NFA NFA -- "Subset Construction" --> DFA NFA -- "State to Non-terminal" --> RLG RLG -- "Non-terminal to State" --> NFA RLG -- "Mirror Algorithm" --> LLG DFA -- "Direct Mapping" --> RLG style RE fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px style DFA fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px style NFA fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px style RLG fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px style LLG fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: NFA → RLG Conversion The following block diagram shows the **step-by-step data flow** when converting a Non-Deterministic Finite Automaton to a Right-Linear Grammar. This is the most commonly tested conversion in KTU exams. ```mermaid graph TD inputNFA["INPUT: NFA M
M = Q, Sigma, delta, q0, F"] block1["BLOCK 1: Component Mapping
V := Q, T := Sigma, S := q0, P := Empty"] block2["BLOCK 2: Transition Processing
For each delta qi, a = qj:
Add qi -> a qj to P"] block3["BLOCK 3: Final State Processing
For each qf in F:
Add qf -> epsilon to P"] outputRG["OUTPUT: Right-Linear Grammar G
G = V, T, P, S
L G equals L M"] inputNFA --> block1 block1 --> block2 block2 --> block3 block3 --> outputRG style inputNFA fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px style block1 fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px style block2 fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px style block3 fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px style outputRG fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.4 Production Rule Structure Decision Tree The following decision tree helps a student **classify a given grammar** as Regular, Context-Free, or higher based on its production rules: ```mermaid graph TD start["EXAMINE ALL PRODUCTIONS
OF GRAMMAR G"] q1{"Is EVERY production
of form A -> aB
or A -> a?"} q2{"Is EVERY production
of form A -> Ba
or A -> a?"} q3{"Is EVERY production
of form alpha A beta -> alpha gamma beta?"} r1["RG: Right-Linear Grammar
Type 3"] r2["RG: Left-Linear Grammar
Type 3"] r3["CFG: Context-Free Grammar
Type 2"] r4["CSG: Context-Sensitive
or Higher: Type 1 or 0"] start --> q1 q1 -- "Yes" --> r1 q1 -- "No" --> q2 q2 -- "Yes" --> r2 q2 -- "No" --> q3 q3 -- "Yes" --> r3 q3 -- "No" --> r4 style start fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px style r1 fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px style r2 fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px style r3 fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px style r4 fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Decision Tree:** Start at the top with any given grammar. Ask the questions in order. The **first "Yes" answer** determines the grammar type. A grammar is Regular **only if** it satisfies **either** the right-linear test **or** the left-linear test — not a mix. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]` **Q: Define Regular Grammar. Give an example of a right-linear grammar that generates the language $L = \{a^n b \mid n \geq 1\}$.** **Model Answer:** A **Regular Grammar (RG)** is a formal grammar $G = (V, T, P, S)$ in which every production rule is of the form: - $A \rightarrow aB$ (right-linear), or - $A \rightarrow a$ (right-linear terminal), or - $A \rightarrow Ba$ (left-linear), or - $A \rightarrow a$ (left-linear terminal) where $A, B \in V$ and $a \in T \cup \{\varepsilon\}$. **Example RLG for $L = \{a^n b \mid n \geq 1\}$:** Let $G = (\{S, A\}, \{a, b\}, P, S)$ where $P$ consists of: $$S \rightarrow aS \quad \text{(generate one or more } a\text{'s, looping)}$$ $$S \rightarrow b \quad \text{(terminate with exactly one } b\text{)}$$ **Verification:** Starting from $S$, applying $S \rightarrow aS$ exactly $n-1$ times followed by $S \rightarrow b$ once yields $a^{n-1} b$. For $n \geq 1$, this gives $a^n b$. The minimum case is $n=1$: $S \Rightarrow b$, which is correct. > **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1.5 marks) + Example grammar + verification (1.5 marks). --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2]` **Q: State the equivalence of Regular Grammar with Finite Automata. Why is a grammar with productions $S \rightarrow aSb \mid \varepsilon$ NOT a regular grammar?** **Model Answer:** **Equivalence Theorem:** A language $L$ is regular **if and only if** there exists a Regular Grammar $G$ that generates $L$. This is equivalent to saying $L$ is accepted by some Finite Automaton (DFA or NFA): $$L \text{ is regular} \iff \exists \text{ RG } G: L(G) = L \iff \exists \text{ FA } M: L(M) = L$$ **Why $S \rightarrow aSb \mid \varepsilon$ is NOT regular:** The production $S \rightarrow aSb$ has **two non-terminals** (well, one on each side of a terminal — but more critically, it has the form $A \rightarrow \alpha B \beta$ where $\alpha = a$ and $\beta = b$ are both non-empty). This is the form $A \rightarrow aB b$ which is **not allowed** in either right-linear or left-linear grammar. Such a production is characteristic of a **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)**, and it generates the non-regular language $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 0\}$, which requires counting (and thus a stack) — a feature FAs and RGs lack. > **Mark Distribution:** Equivalence statement (1.5 marks) + Justification of why not regular (1.5 marks). --- ## 5.2 PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** For 14-mark questions, students frequently lose marks by **(a)** forgetting the new final state $q_f$ in RLG→FA conversion, **(b)** missing the $\varepsilon$-productions for final states in FA→RLG conversion, **(c)** mixing up left-linear and right-linear rules within the same grammar, and **(d)** not verifying the result with a sample string. Always include the verification step in your answer. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: (a)** Consider the following NFA $M$ with states $\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$, alphabet $\{0, 1\}$, start state $q_0$, final state $\{q_2\}$, and transitions: - $\delta(q_0, 0) = \{q_0, q_1\}$ - $\delta(q_0, 1) = \{q_1\}$ - $\delta(q_1, 0) = \emptyset$ - $\delta(q_1, 1) = \{q_1, q_2\}$ - $\delta(q_2, 0) = \emptyset$ - $\delta(q_2, 1) = \emptyset$ **Construct an equivalent Right-Linear Regular Grammar.** **(7 marks)** **(b)** Convert the following Right-Linear Grammar to an equivalent NFA: $$S \rightarrow 0S \mid 1A, \quad A \rightarrow 0A \mid 1S \mid \varepsilon$$ **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a): **Step 1: Identify the components of the NFA** - States: $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$ - Alphabet: $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$ - Start: $q_0$ - Final: $F = \{q_2\}$ **Step 2: Initialize the grammar** Set $V = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$, $T = \{0, 1\}$, $S = q_0$, and $P = \emptyset$. **Step 3: Convert each transition to a production rule** For $\delta(q_0, 0) = \{q_0, q_1\}$ (NFA has two destinations): - $q_0 \rightarrow 0 q_0$ - $q_0 \rightarrow 0 q_1$ For $\delta(q_0, 1) = \{q_1\}$: - $q_0 \rightarrow 1 q_1$ For $\delta(q_1, 1) = \{q_1, q_2\}$: - $q_1 \rightarrow 1 q_1$ - $q_1 \rightarrow 1 q_2$ (No transitions from $q_2$, so no productions from $q_2$ as left side.) **Step 4: Add $\varepsilon$-productions for final states** Since $q_2 \in F$, add: - $q_2 \rightarrow \varepsilon$ **Step 5: Final Right-Linear Grammar** $$G = (\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}, \{0, 1\}, P, q_0)$$ where: $$P: \{q_0 \rightarrow 0q_0, \quad q_0 \rightarrow 0q_1, \quad q_0 \rightarrow 1q_1, \quad q_1 \rightarrow 1q_1, \quad q_1 \rightarrow 1q_2, \quad q_2 \rightarrow \varepsilon\}$$ **Verification (Trace string $w = 001$):** $$ \begin{aligned} q_0 &\Rightarrow 0 q_0 \quad &\text{(using } q_0 \rightarrow 0 q_0 \text{)} \\ &\Rightarrow 0 0 q_1 \quad &\text{(using } q_0 \rightarrow 0 q_1 \text{)} \\ &\Rightarrow 0 0 1 q_2 \quad &\text{(using } q_1 \rightarrow 1 q_2 \text{)} \\ &\Rightarrow 0 0 1 \quad &\text{(using } q_2 \rightarrow \varepsilon \text{)} \\ \end{aligned} $$ The string $001$ is correctly derived. **Grammar verified.** --- #### Model Solution for (b): **Given RLG:** - $V = \{S, A\}$, $T = \{0, 1\}$, start symbol $S$ - $P: \{S \rightarrow 0S, \quad S \rightarrow 1A, \quad A \rightarrow 0A, \quad A \rightarrow 1S, \quad A \rightarrow \varepsilon\}$ **Step 1: Construct NFA state set** $Q = V \cup \{q_f\} = \{S, A, q_f\}$, $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$, $q_0 = S$. **Step 2: Convert $A \rightarrow aB$ form productions to transitions** | Production | NFA Transition | | :---: | :---: | | $S \rightarrow 0S$ | $\delta(S, 0) = S$ | | $S \rightarrow 1A$ | $\delta(S, 1) = A$ | | $A \rightarrow 0A$ | $\delta(A, 0) = A$ | | $A \rightarrow 1S$ | $\delta(A, 1) = S$ | **Step 3: Convert $A \rightarrow a$ form productions** There are no productions of the form $A \rightarrow a$ in this grammar (only $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$). **Step 4: Handle $\varepsilon$-productions** The production $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ means $A$ is nullable. Add $A$ to the final state set: $F = \{q_f, A\}$. **Step 5: Final NFA** $M = (\{S, A, q_f\}, \{0, 1\}, \delta, S, \{q_f, A\})$ with transitions: - $\delta(S, 0) = S$ (self-loop on 0 at state $S$) - $\delta(S, 1) = A$ - $\delta(A, 0) = A$ (self-loop on 0 at state $A$) - $\delta(A, 1) = S$ **Transition Table Summary:** | State | Input 0 | Input 1 | | :---: | :---: | :---: | | $\rightarrow S$ | $S$ | $A$ | | $A$ (final) | $A$ | $S$ | | $q_f$ (final) | — | — | **Verification (Trace string $w = 11$):** - $S \xrightarrow{1} A$ (using $\delta(S, 1) = A$) - $A \xrightarrow{1} S$ (using $\delta(A, 1) = S$) - $S$ is **not** a final state, so $11$ is **rejected**. This is correct because $L(G) = \{w \in \{0,1\}^* \mid w \text{ has odd length}\}$, and $|11| = 2$ (even, rejected). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: (a)** Construct a Right-Linear Grammar for the language $L = \{w \in \{a, b\}^* \mid w \text{ starts and ends with the same symbol}\}$. Show at least three sample derivations. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Given the following DFA, construct an equivalent Right-Linear Regular Grammar: - States: $\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$ - Alphabet: $\{a, b\}$ - Start: $q_0$ - Final: $\{q_2\}$ - Transitions: $\delta(q_0, a) = q_1$, $\delta(q_0, b) = q_0$, $\delta(q_1, a) = q_1$, $\delta(q_1, b) = q_2$, $\delta(q_2, a) = q_1$, $\delta(q_2, b) = q_0$ **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a): **Analysis of the Language:** $L$ consists of all strings over $\{a, b\}$ that begin and end with the same symbol. This includes: - $\varepsilon$ (trivially, but typically excluded as both start and end are undefined) - $a$, $b$ (single-character strings) - $aa$, $bb$ (two-character strings) - $aba$, $aabaa$, $babab$, etc. (longer strings) **Strategy:** We need to track two things: (1) the **first symbol** seen, and (2) the **last symbol** produced. We'll use non-terminals that encode this state. **Constructed Grammar:** $$G = (\{S, A, B\}, \{a, b\}, P, S)$$ where the non-terminals mean: - $S$: Start state (no first symbol determined yet) - $A$: First symbol seen was $a$ (and the string so far ends with $a$) - $B$: First symbol seen was $b$ (and the string so far ends with $b$) Productions: $$ \begin{aligned} S &\rightarrow aA \quad &\text{(first symbol is } a\text{, currently ending with } a\text{)} \\ S &\rightarrow bB \quad &\text{(first symbol is } b\text{, currently ending with } b\text{)} \\ A &\rightarrow aA \quad &\text{(stay in } A\text{, keep adding } a\text{'s in middle)} \\ A &\rightarrow bA \quad &\text{(add } b\text{ in middle, but still ending with } a\text{... wait, NO)}\\ \end{aligned} $$ **Correction:** Let me redefine the non-terminals more carefully. We need to track the **first** symbol and the **current last** symbol. Let $A_x$ denote "first symbol was $x$, currently ending with $x$" and $A_y$ denote "first symbol was $x$, currently ending with $y \neq x$". **Refined Grammar:** Let $V = \{S, A_a, A_b, B_a, B_b\}$: - $S$: Start - $A_a$: First = $a$, last = $a$ (so far ends well) - $A_b$: First = $a$, last = $b$ (ends with $b$, needs a terminal $a$ to be accepted — but we cannot have terminal productions easily...) **Simpler correct approach:** Use only the **first** symbol, and add the **last** symbol via a "completion" production. **Final Correct Grammar:** $$G = (\{S, A, B\}, \{a, b\}, P, S)$$ Productions $P$: $$ \begin{aligned} S &\rightarrow a \mid b \mid aA \mid bB \\ A &\rightarrow aA \mid bA \mid a \\ B &\rightarrow aB \mid bB \mid b \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Explanation of productions:** - $S \rightarrow a$: Single $a$ — starts and ends with $a$. ✓ - $S \rightarrow b$: Single $b$ — starts and ends with $b$. ✓ - $S \rightarrow aA$: First symbol $a$ confirmed; move to non-terminal $A$ representing "we have started with $a$, more characters coming". - $S \rightarrow bB$: First symbol $b$ confirmed; move to $B$ meaning "we have started with $b$, more characters coming". - $A \rightarrow aA$, $A \rightarrow bA$: From state $A$ (started with $a$), keep appending $a$'s and $b$'s in the middle. - $A \rightarrow a$: Terminate with $a$ to make the string end with the same symbol it started with ($a$). ✓ - $B \rightarrow aB$, $B \rightarrow bB$: Similar logic for state $B$. - $B \rightarrow b$: Terminate with $b$ to match the start. ✓ **Sample Derivations:** **Derivation 1: $w = a$ (starts and ends with $a$)** $$ S \Rightarrow a \quad \text{(using } S \rightarrow a \text{)} $$ **Derivation 2: $w = aba$ (starts with $a$, ends with $a$)** $$ \begin{aligned} S &\Rightarrow aA \quad &\text{(using } S \rightarrow aA \text{)} \\ &\Rightarrow a b A \quad &\text{(using } A \rightarrow bA \text{)} \\ &\Rightarrow a b a \quad &\text{(using } A \rightarrow a \text{)} \end{aligned} $$ **Derivation 3: $w = bab$ (starts and ends with $b$)** $$ \begin{aligned} S &\Rightarrow bB \quad &\text{(using } S \rightarrow bB \text{)} \\ &\Rightarrow b a B \quad &\text{(using } B \rightarrow aB \text{)} \\ &\Rightarrow b a b \quad &\text{(using } B \rightarrow b \text{)} \end{aligned} $$ **Derivation 4: $w = aabbaa$ (starts and ends with $a$)** $$ \begin{aligned} S &\Rightarrow aA \Rightarrow aaA \Rightarrow aabA \Rightarrow aabbA \Rightarrow aabbaA \Rightarrow aabbaa \end{aligned} $$ All derivations produce strings in $L$. The grammar is correct. --- #### Model Solution for (b): **Given DFA:** - $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$, $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$, $q_0 = $ start, $F = \{q_2\}$ - Transitions: $\delta(q_0, a) = q_1$, $\delta(q_0, b) = q_0$, $\delta(q_1, a) = q_1$, $\delta(q_1, b) = q_2$, $\delta(q_2, a) = q_1$, $\delta(q_2, b) = q_0$ **Step 1: Map DFA components to grammar components** $V = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$, $T = \{a, b\}$, $S = q_0$, $P = \emptyset$. **Step 2: Convert each transition to a production** | DFA Transition | Grammar Production | | :---: | :---: | | $\delta(q_0, a) = q_1$ | $q_0 \rightarrow a q_1$ | | $\delta(q_0, b) = q_0$ | $q_0 \rightarrow b q_0$ | | $\delta(q_1, a) = q_1$ | $q_1 \rightarrow a q_1$ | | $\delta(q_1, b) = q_2$ | $q_1 \rightarrow b q_2$ | | $\delta(q_2, a) = q_1$ | $q_2 \rightarrow a q_1$ | | $\delta(q_2, b) = q_0$ | $q_2 \rightarrow b q_0$ | **Step 3: Add $\varepsilon$-production for the final state $q_2$** Since $q_2 \in F$, add: $q_2 \rightarrow \varepsilon$ **Step 4: Final Right-Linear Grammar** $$G = (\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}, \{a, b\}, P, q_0)$$ with productions: $$ P: \begin{cases} q_0 \rightarrow a q_1 \mid b q_0 \\ q_1 \rightarrow a q_1 \mid b q_2 \\ q_2 \rightarrow a q_1 \mid b q_0 \mid \varepsilon \end{cases} $$ **Verification (Trace $w = abb$):** $$ \begin{aligned} q_0 &\Rightarrow a q_1 \quad &\text{(using } q_0 \rightarrow a q_1 \text{)} \\ &\Rightarrow a b q_2 \quad &\text{(using } q_1 \rightarrow b q_2 \text{)} \\ &\Rightarrow a b b q_0 \quad &\text{(using } q_2 \rightarrow b q_0 \text{)} \end{aligned} $$ The string $abb$ is partially derived but does not terminate at a final state. According to the DFA, the path for $abb$ is $q_0 \xrightarrow{a} q_1 \xrightarrow{b} q_2 \xrightarrow{b} q_0$, ending at $q_0$ (not final). So $abb \notin L$. Correct. **Verification (Trace $w = ab$):** $$ \begin{aligned} q_0 &\Rightarrow a q_1 \quad &\text{(using } q_0 \rightarrow a q_1 \text{)} \\ &\Rightarrow a b q_2 \quad &\text{(using } q_1 \rightarrow b q_2 \text{)} \\ &\Rightarrow a b \quad &\text{(using } q_2 \rightarrow \varepsilon \text{)} \end{aligned} $$ String $ab$ is fully derived. According to the DFA: $q_0 \xrightarrow{a} q_1 \xrightarrow{b} q_2$ — ends at final state $q_2$. So $ab \in L$. **Verification successful.** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Rapid Revision Checklist — Regular Grammar (RG)** ### Definition & Classification - A **Regular Grammar** is a 4-tuple $G = (V, T, P, S)$ where every production is in **right-linear** form ($A \rightarrow aB$ or $A \rightarrow a$) **OR** **left-linear** form ($A \rightarrow Ba$ or $A \rightarrow a$). - Regular Grammar = **Type 3** in the Chomsky Hierarchy. - Regular Grammar generates exactly the **regular languages**. - A grammar with **mixed** right-linear and left-linear rules is **NOT regular** — it is Context-Free. ### Equivalence (Must Memorize) - The four-way equivalence: $\text{RE} \equiv \text{DFA} \equiv \text{NFA} \equiv \text{RG}$ - A language is regular **iff** it has a Regular Grammar, a Finite Automaton, and a Regular Expression. ### FA → RG Conversion (Algorithm) 1. Each state $q_i$ becomes a non-terminal $Q_i$. 2. Start state $q_0$ becomes start symbol $S$. 3. Each transition $\delta(q_i, a) = q_j$ becomes production $Q_i \rightarrow a Q_j$. 4. Each final state $q_f$ gets an additional $\varepsilon$-production: $Q_f \rightarrow \varepsilon$. ### RG → FA Conversion (Algorithm) 1. Each non-terminal $A$ becomes a state $q_A$. 2. Add a new dedicated final state $q_f$. 3. Start symbol $S$ becomes start state. 4. Each production $A \rightarrow aB$ becomes transition $\delta(q_A, a) = q_B$. 5. Each production $A \rightarrow a$ becomes transition $\delta(q_A, a) = q_f$. 6. Each $\varepsilon$-production makes the corresponding non-terminal a final state. ### Common Pitfalls (Avoid These!) - ⚠️ Forgetting the $\varepsilon$-productions in FA → RG conversion. - ⚠️ Forgetting the new final state $q_f$ in RG → FA conversion. - ⚠️ Mixing right-linear and left-linear rules. - ⚠️ Confusing non-terminals (uppercase) with terminals (lowercase) when writing productions. - ⚠️ Declaring $S \rightarrow aSb \mid \varepsilon$ as regular (it is **NOT** — it generates $\{a^n b^n\}$, which is non-regular). ### Quick Test for Regularity of a Grammar Ask: "Is **every** production of the form $A \rightarrow aB$ (right-linear) or $A \rightarrow Ba$ (left-linear) or $A \rightarrow a$?" - If **all** yes to one form → Regular Grammar. - If **any** production has more than one non-terminal, or non-terminals in the middle → NOT regular.

topics.title: Equivalence of RGs and DFA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET414 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Equivalence of RGs and DFA # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Regular Grammar (RG) A **Regular Grammar (RG)** is a formal grammar $G = (V, T, P, S)$ where every production is restricted to one of the following forms: - **Right-linear grammar:** $A \rightarrow aB$ or $A \rightarrow a$ - **Left-linear grammar:** $A \rightarrow Ba$ or $A \rightarrow a$ where $A, B \in V$ (non-terminals) and $a \in T$ (terminals). Every right-linear or left-linear grammar generates exactly a **regular language**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** A grammar is *regular* if and only if it is either right-linear or left-linear. The languages generated by RGs are precisely those recognized by Finite Automata. ## 1.2 Formal Definition of a DFA A **Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA)** is a 5-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ where: - $Q$ is a finite set of states - $\Sigma$ is a finite input alphabet - $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ is the transition function - $q_0 \in Q$ is the start state - $F \subseteq Q$ is the set of final/accepting states ## 1.3 The Equivalence Theorem (Central Result) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Equivalence Theorem:** A language $L$ is **regular** if and only if $L$ is generated by some regular grammar $G$, and recognized by some DFA $M$. > > $$\boxed{\;L(G) = L(M) \iff G \text{ is a regular grammar} \iff M \text{ is a DFA}\;}$$ This means for every RG, we can *mechanically construct* an equivalent DFA, and vice-versa. ## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Translation Engine" 🌍 Imagine two completely different "languages" used to describe the same set of journeys through a city: - **Regular Grammar** is like giving *turn-by-turn driving directions* ("From Home, take street-A to reach Market, then street-B to reach Park..."). The *symbols* (A, B) are like intersections, and *terminals* (a, b) are the actual street names. - **DFA** is like a *GPS map* with circles (states) and labeled arrows (transitions) showing which road you can take next. The **Equivalence Theorem** is the proof that the *written directions* and the *GPS map* describe **exactly the same set of reachable destinations**. You can convert one to the other without losing or gaining any journey. > [!NOTE] > The conversion is *bidirectional*: RG $\rightarrow$ DFA (mechanical, sometimes requires NFA as an intermediate step) and DFA $\rightarrow$ RG (very direct, one-to-one mapping of transitions to productions). ## 1.5 Why This Equivalence Matters - It unifies the **generative** view (Grammar) and **recognition** view (Automaton) of regular languages. - It enables **practical implementation**: When a designer specifies a pattern using a regular grammar, an equivalent DFA can be algorithmically built to act as the lexical analyzer (e.g., `grep`, `lex`). - It guarantees that the *closure properties* (union, intersection, complement) proven for DFAs also apply to languages generated by RGs. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Two Directions of Equivalence ### Direction 1: Regular Grammar $\rightarrow$ DFA (the harder direction) The standard procedure is **RG $\rightarrow$ NFA $\rightarrow$ DFA**: | Step | Action | |------|--------| | **1** | For each production $A \rightarrow aB$, add transition $\delta(A, a) = B$. | | **2** | For each production $A \rightarrow a$ (terminal production), add a transition from $A$ on $a$ to a **new final state** $F_{new}$. | | **3** | The start symbol $S$ of the grammar becomes the start state of the NFA. | | **4** | The new state $F_{new}$ (and any other introduced final state) becomes a final state of the NFA. | | **5** | Apply **Subset Construction** to convert the NFA into an equivalent DFA. | ### Direction 2: DFA $\rightarrow$ Regular Grammar (the direct direction) | Step | Action | |------|--------| | **1** | For each transition $\delta(A, a) = B$, add the production $A \rightarrow aB$. | | **2** | For each final state $F_i$, add the production $F_i \rightarrow \varepsilon$. | | **3** | The start state of the DFA becomes the start symbol of the grammar. | This direction produces a **right-linear grammar** whose language is exactly $L(M)$. ## 2.2 Why RG $\rightarrow$ DFA Needs the NFA Step When a non-terminal $A$ has *both* $A \rightarrow aB$ and $A \rightarrow a$ in the grammar, the resulting automaton would have *two transitions* from $A$ on the same symbol $a$: - One going to state $B$ - One going to a new final state $F_{new}$ This violates **determinism**. The intermediate NFA handles this non-determinism, which is then resolved using the **Subset Construction Algorithm** (Rabin-Scott). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | |---|---------|----------------| | 1 | Right-linear form | $A \rightarrow aB \;\; \vert \;\; A \rightarrow a$ | | 2 | Left-linear form | $A \rightarrow Ba \;\; \vert \;\; A \rightarrow a$ | | 3 | RG $\rightarrow$ NFA (binary prod.) | $A \rightarrow aB \implies \delta(A, a) = B$ | | 4 | RG $\rightarrow$ NFA (terminal prod.) | $A \rightarrow a \implies \delta(A, a) = F_{new}$ | | 5 | DFA $\rightarrow$ RG (transition) | $\delta(A, a) = B \implies A \rightarrow aB$ | | 6 | DFA $\rightarrow$ RG (final state) | $F_i \in F \implies F_i \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | | 7 | Subset Construction (start) | $DState_0 = \{S_{NFA}\}$ | | 8 | Subset Construction (move) | $Move(T, a) = \bigcup_{q \in T} \delta(q, a)$ | | 9 | DFA is final condition | $T \cap F_{NFA} \neq \emptyset \iff T$ is final in DFA | | 10 | Upper bound on DFA states | $\vert Q_{DFA} \vert \leq 2^{\vert Q_{NFA} \vert}$ | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** This conversion is the heart of every **lexical analyzer** in compilers. The grammar specified by the language designer (e.g., "identifiers are letter followed by letters/digits") is converted to a DFA, which then becomes a *finite-state machine* that scans source code in $O(n)$ time per token. ## 2.4 Closure Properties (Inherited via Equivalence) Because RGs and DFAs describe the **same class of languages (regular languages)**, properties proven for one automatically hold for the other: - Closed under **union** ($L_1 \cup L_2$) - Closed under **concatenation** ($L_1 \cdot L_2$) - Closed under **Kleene star** ($L^*$) - Closed under **intersection** and **complement** - Decidable: emptiness, finiteness, membership, equivalence # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example — RG to DFA (Direction 1) **Given:** Regular grammar $G = (V, T, P, S)$ over $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$: $$ \begin{aligned} S &\rightarrow aA \;\; \vert \;\; bS \\ A &\rightarrow aA \;\; \vert \;\; bA \;\; \vert \;\; b \end{aligned} $$ **Intuition check:** This grammar generates strings of the form $(a+b)^* a (a+b)^* b$ — strings that contain at least one 'a' **before** a 'b' at the end. ### Step 1: Convert Productions to NFA Transitions Apply the rule: - $A \rightarrow aB$ becomes $\delta(A, a) = B$ - $A \rightarrow a$ becomes $\delta(A, a) = F_{new}$ (where $F_{new}$ is a new state) | Production | NFA Transition | |------------|----------------| | $S \rightarrow aA$ | $\delta(S, a) = A$ | | $S \rightarrow bS$ | $\delta(S, b) = S$ | | $A \rightarrow aA$ | $\delta(A, a) = A$ | | $A \rightarrow bA$ | $\delta(A, b) = A$ | | $A \rightarrow b$ | $\delta(A, b) = F_{new}$ | Notice that state $A$ has **two transitions on input $b$**: one to $A$ and one to $F_{new}$. This is **non-deterministic**. ### Step 2: Build the NFA Transition Table $$ \begin{aligned} \text{State} &\quad a \quad\quad b \\ S &\quad A \quad\quad S \\ A &\quad A \quad\quad \{A, F_{new}\} \\ F_{new} &\quad \emptyset \quad \emptyset \end{aligned} $$ Start state: $S$ ; Final state: $\{F_{new}\}$ ### Step 3: Apply Subset Construction to Obtain DFA **Iteration 1: Start state** - $D_0 = \{S\}$ - $\delta(D_0, a) = \{A\}$ → call it $D_1$ - $\delta(D_0, b) = \{S\} = D_0$ **Iteration 2: Process $D_1 = \{A\}$** - $\delta(D_1, a) = \{A\} = D_1$ - $\delta(D_1, b) = \{A, F_{new}\}$ → call it $D_2$ **Iteration 3: Process $D_2 = \{A, F_{new}\}$** - $\delta(D_2, a) = \delta(A, a) \cup \delta(F_{new}, a) = \{A\} \cup \emptyset = \{A\} = D_1$ - $\delta(D_2, b) = \delta(A, b) \cup \delta(F_{new}, b) = \{A, F_{new}\} \cup \emptyset = \{A, F_{new}\} = D_2$ ### Step 4: Final DFA Transition Table $$ \begin{aligned} \text{State} &\quad a \quad\quad b \\ D_0 = \{S\} &\quad D_1 \quad\quad D_0 \\ D_1 = \{A\} &\quad D_1 \quad\quad D_2 \\ D_2 = \{A, F_{new}\} &\quad D_1 \quad\quad D_2 \end{aligned} $$ - **Start state:** $D_0 = \{S\}$ - **Final states:** All DFA states that contain $F_{new}$ from the NFA $\Rightarrow$ $\{D_2\}$ ### Step 5: Verify by Tracing a String Trace the string `aab` (should be ACCEPTED since it has 'a' before the final 'b'): $$ \begin{aligned} D_0 &\xrightarrow{a} D_1 \xrightarrow{a} D_1 \xrightarrow{b} D_2 \;\; (\text{final state} \Rightarrow \text{ACCEPT}) \end{aligned} $$ Trace the string `ba` (should be REJECTED since no 'a' precedes a 'b'): $$ \begin{aligned} D_0 &\xrightarrow{b} D_0 \xrightarrow{a} D_1 \;\; (\text{not a final state} \Rightarrow \text{REJECT}) \end{aligned} $$ ✅ The DFA correctly accepts $L(G)$. ## 3.2 Worked Example — DFA to RG (Direction 2) **Given:** DFA $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ where $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$, $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$, $F = \{q_2\}$, and: $$ \begin{aligned} \delta(q_0, 0) &= q_0, \quad \delta(q_0, 1) = q_1 \\ \delta(q_1, 0) &= q_1, \quad \delta(q_1, 1) = q_2 \\ \delta(q_2, 0) &= q_2, \quad \delta(q_2, 1) = q_2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 1: One production per transition** $$ \begin{aligned} q_0 &\rightarrow 0 q_0 \;\; \vert \;\; 1 q_1 \\ q_1 &\rightarrow 0 q_1 \;\; \vert \;\; 1 q_2 \\ q_2 &\rightarrow 0 q_2 \;\; \vert \;\; 1 q_2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Add $\varepsilon$-productions for final state $q_2$** $$ q_2 \rightarrow \varepsilon $$ **Resulting right-linear grammar $G$:** $$ \begin{aligned} S &\rightarrow 0S \;\; \vert \;\; 1A \quad (\text{renamed } q_0 \text{ to } S, \; q_1 \text{ to } A, \; q_2 \text{ to } B) \\ A &\rightarrow 0A \;\; \vert \;\; 1B \\ B &\rightarrow 0B \;\; \vert \;\; 1B \;\; \vert \;\; \varepsilon \end{aligned} $$ **Verification:** $L(M)$ consists of strings of the form $0^* 1 0^* 1 (0+1)^*$ — accepted when we reach $q_2$ (and once in $q_2$, we stay there). The grammar generates exactly the same language. ✅ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When Determinism Holds vs. Fails | Grammar Form | Resulting Automaton | Needs Subset Construction? | |--------------|---------------------|----------------------------| | Only $A \rightarrow aB$ (no terminal productions) | NFA = DFA (already deterministic) | **No** | | Mix of $A \rightarrow aB$ and $A \rightarrow a$ for same $(A, a)$ | Genuine NFA | **Yes** | | $A \rightarrow a$ only (no $A \rightarrow aB$ for that symbol) | NFA = DFA | **No** | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level Conversion Flowchart (RG → DFA) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Regular Grammar G] --> B{Identify All Productions} B --> C[Right-Linear: A → aB] B --> D[Terminal: A → a] C --> E[Add Transition δ A,a = B in NFA] D --> F[Add Transition δ A,a = F_new in NFA] E --> G[Build Complete NFA] F --> G G --> H{Is NFA Deterministic?} H -- Yes --> L[Result: Equivalent DFA] H -- No --> I[Apply Subset Construction Algorithm] I --> J[Compute Closure of Each Subset on Each Symbol] J --> K[Mark Subsets Containing F_new as Final] K --> L L --> M[End: Equivalent DFA M] ``` ## 4.2 NFA Built from the Worked Example (Section 3.1) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> S S --> S : b S --> A : a A --> A : a A --> A : b A --> Fnew : b Fnew --> [*] classDef startState fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px classDef finalState fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px classDef normalState fill:#E0E0FF,stroke:#000080,stroke-width:1px class S startState class Fnew finalState class A normalState ``` ## 4.3 Equivalent DFA After Subset Construction ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> D0 D0 --> D0 : b D0 --> D1 : a D1 --> D1 : a D1 --> D1 : b D1 --> D2 : b D2 --> D1 : a D2 --> D2 : a D2 --> D2 : b classDef startState fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px classDef finalState fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px classDef normalState fill:#E0E0FF,stroke:#000080,stroke-width:1px class D0 startState class D2 finalState class D1 normalState ``` ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: DFA → RG Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DFA_Input[Input DFA M] A1[States Q] A2[Alphabet Sigma] A3[Transitions delta] A4[Final States F] A5[Start State q0] end subgraph Mapping_Engine[Conversion Engine] B1[For each transition
delta A,a = B
emit A → aB] B2[For each final state
F_i in F
emit F_i → epsilon] B3[Use q0 as start symbol S] end subgraph Output[Output RG G] C1[Non-Terminals V] C2[Productions P] C3[Start Symbol S] end A1 --> B1 A3 --> B1 A4 --> B2 A5 --> B3 B1 --> C2 B2 --> C2 B3 --> C3 A1 --> C1 A2 --> C1 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]` **State the Equivalence Theorem for Regular Grammars and DFAs. Why is this equivalence important in compiler design?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Equivalence Theorem** states that a language $L$ is regular **if and only if** it can be generated by a **Regular Grammar (RG)** and recognized by a **Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA)**. Formally: $$L \text{ is regular} \iff \exists \text{ RG } G : L = L(G) \iff \exists \text{ DFA } M : L = L(M)$$ **Importance in compiler design:** Lexical analyzers (lexers) use **DFAs** to recognize tokens like identifiers, keywords, and operators. Designers often specify token patterns using **regular expressions / grammars**. The equivalence theorem guarantees that any such pattern can be **mechanically compiled** into a fast $O(n)$ DFA-based scanner. *(1 mark for theorem, 2 marks for explanation.)* --- ### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Series-2]` **Differentiate between right-linear and left-linear regular grammars with one example each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Right-Linear Grammar | Left-Linear Grammar | |---------|----------------------|---------------------| | Form | $A \rightarrow aB$ or $A \rightarrow a$ | $A \rightarrow Ba$ or $A \rightarrow a$ | | Recursion | Non-terminal at **right** end | Non-terminal at **left** end | | Derivation tree | Grows to the **right** | Grows to the **left** | **Right-linear example:** $S \rightarrow 0S \;\vert\; 1A, \quad A \rightarrow 0A \;\vert\; 1A \;\vert\; \varepsilon$ (generates $(0+1)^*$) **Left-linear example:** $S \rightarrow S0 \;\vert\; A1, \quad A \rightarrow A0 \;\vert\; A1 \;\vert\; \varepsilon$ (also generates $(0+1)^*$) --- ## 📕 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks): RG $\rightarrow$ DFA Conversion** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Convert the following regular grammar $G$ into an equivalent DFA:** $$ \begin{aligned} S &\rightarrow aA \;\vert\; bB \\ A &\rightarrow aA \;\vert\; bA \;\vert\; a \\ B &\rightarrow aB \;\vert\; bB \;\vert\; b \end{aligned} $$ #### **Part (a)** [7 marks] Construct the equivalent NFA and show the subset construction steps. **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Convert each production to NFA transitions.** For terminal productions $A \rightarrow a$ and $B \rightarrow b$, introduce new final state $F$. | Production | NFA Transition | |------------|----------------| | $S \rightarrow aA$ | $\delta(S, a) = A$ | | $S \rightarrow bB$ | $\delta(S, b) = B$ | | $A \rightarrow aA$ | $\delta(A, a) = A$ | | $A \rightarrow bA$ | $\delta(A, b) = A$ | | $A \rightarrow a$ | $\delta(A, a) = F$ | | $B \rightarrow aB$ | $\delta(B, a) = B$ | | $B \rightarrow bB$ | $\delta(B, b) = B$ | | $B \rightarrow b$ | $\delta(B, b) = F$ | **Step 2: NFA Transition Table** (showing the non-determinism on $A \xrightarrow{a} \{A, F\}$ and $B \xrightarrow{b} \{B, F\}$): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{State} &\quad a \quad\quad b \\ S &\quad A \quad\quad B \\ A &\quad \{A, F\} \quad A \\ B &\quad B \quad \{B, F\} \\ F &\quad \emptyset \quad \emptyset \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Subset Construction** *(showing each new subset derived)* - $D_0 = \{S\}$ (start) - $\delta(D_0, a) = \{A\}$ → $D_1$ - $\delta(D_0, b) = \{B\}$ → $D_2$ - $\delta(D_1, a) = \{A, F\}$ → $D_3$ (since $A \xrightarrow{a} \{A, F\}$) - $\delta(D_1, b) = \{A\}$ → $D_1$ (since $A \xrightarrow{b} A$) - $\delta(D_2, a) = \{B\}$ → $D_2$ (since $B \xrightarrow{a} B$) - $\delta(D_2, b) = \{B, F\}$ → $D_4$ (since $B \xrightarrow{b} \{B, F\}$) - $\delta(D_3, a) = \{A, F\}$ → $D_3$ ($A \xrightarrow{a} \{A, F\}$, $F$ has no transition) - $\delta(D_3, b) = \{A\}$ → $D_1$ - $\delta(D_4, a) = \{B\}$ → $D_2$ - $\delta(D_4, b) = \{B, F\}$ → $D_4$ #### **Part (b)** [7 marks] Write the final DFA transition table. Identify the start state and the set of final states. Trace acceptance of the string `aab` and `ba`. **Model Solution:** **Final DFA Transition Table:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{State} &\quad a \quad\quad b \\ D_0 = \{S\} &\quad D_1 \quad\quad D_2 \\ D_1 = \{A\} &\quad D_3 \quad\quad D_1 \\ D_2 = \{B\} &\quad D_2 \quad\quad D_4 \\ D_3 = \{A, F\} &\quad D_3 \quad\quad D_1 \\ D_4 = \{B, F\} &\quad D_2 \quad\quad D_4 \end{aligned} $$ - **Start state:** $D_0$ - **Final states:** $\{D_3, D_4\}$ (these subsets contain the NFA final state $F$) **Trace of `aab`:** $D_0 \xrightarrow{a} D_1 \xrightarrow{a} D_3 \xrightarrow{b} D_1$ — ends at $D_1$, **NOT a final state ⇒ REJECTED**. Wait — let us recheck: the string `aab` should be **accepted** since it has at least one 'a' and the language is "strings ending in 'a' or in 'b' preceded by an earlier symbol". Re-examination: from the grammar, the language is $(a+b)^+$, i.e., all non-empty strings. **Corrected trace of `aab`:** $D_0 \xrightarrow{a} D_1 \xrightarrow{a} D_3 \xrightarrow{b} D_1$ — ends at $D_1$ (non-final). Hmm — but $D_1 = \{A\}$ does not contain $F$. So `aab` is **REJECTED** by this DFA. Let us re-examine: From state $A$, we only reach $F$ via the production $A \rightarrow a$. The string `aab` reaches $A$ after the first two symbols, then consumes `b` and stays in $A$. It never produces a final symbol. So `aab` is **not in $L(G)$** because the grammar requires the **last** symbol to be `a` (from the $A$-branch) or `b` (from the $B$-branch), but also the string must start with the **first** symbol pushing it into the correct branch. Re-checking: `aab` — starts with `a` so $S \rightarrow aA$, then `a` so $A \rightarrow aA$, then `b` so $A \rightarrow bA$ but there's no $\varepsilon$ at the end. So `aab` is **not in $L(G)$** — the DFA correctly rejects it. ✅ **Trace of `ba`:** $D_0 \xrightarrow{b} D_2 \xrightarrow{a} D_2$ — ends at $D_2$, **NOT a final state ⇒ REJECTED**. `ba` starts with `b` (goes to $B$), then `a` (stays in $B$), and never reaches the terminal `b` needed to terminate. Correctly rejected. ✅ **Trace of `bab` (should be accepted):** $D_0 \xrightarrow{b} D_2 \xrightarrow{a} D_2 \xrightarrow{b} D_4$ — ends at $D_4$ ∈ final set ⇒ **ACCEPTED** ✅ > [!WARNING] > **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the new final state $F$** when handling terminal productions $A \rightarrow a$. This is the #1 reason students lose marks. (–2 marks) > 2. **Skipping subset construction** when the NFA has multiple transitions on the same symbol — examiners deduct heavily because the resulting "DFA" is actually non-deterministic. (–3 marks) > 3. **Wrong final-state identification** in the DFA: students often mark all states as final. Only subsets **containing the NFA's final state $F$** are final. (–2 marks) > 4. **Not labeling the subsets** with their actual contents, e.g., writing $D_1$ without showing $\{A\}$ — examiners can't give partial credit without the working. (–1 mark) > 5. **Confusing start state** with start symbol. The start *symbol* $S$ of the grammar becomes the start *state* of the DFA (after subset construction, the start state is $\{S\}$). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks): DFA $\rightarrow$ RG Conversion** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Construct an equivalent right-linear grammar for the following DFA:** The DFA $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ where: - $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$ - $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$ - $F = \{q_2\}$ - Transition table: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{State} &\quad 0 \quad\quad 1 \\ \rightarrow q_0 &\quad q_0 \quad q_1 \\ q_1 &\quad q_1 \quad q_2 \\ *q_2 &\quad q_2 \quad q_2 \end{aligned} $$ #### **Part (a)** [7 marks] State the conversion rule and write all productions derived from the DFA's transitions. **Model Solution:** **Conversion Rule:** For every transition $\delta(A, a) = B$ in the DFA, we add a production $A \rightarrow aB$ in the grammar. For every final state $F_i$, we add $F_i \rightarrow \varepsilon$. **Step 1: One production per transition** | DFA Transition | Grammar Production | |----------------|--------------------| | $\delta(q_0, 0) = q_0$ | $q_0 \rightarrow 0 q_0$ | | $\delta(q_0, 1) = q_1$ | $q_0 \rightarrow 1 q_1$ | | $\delta(q_1, 0) = q_1$ | $q_1 \rightarrow 0 q_1$ | | $\delta(q_1, 1) = q_2$ | $q_1 \rightarrow 1 q_2$ | | $\delta(q_2, 0) = q_2$ | $q_2 \rightarrow 0 q_2$ | | $\delta(q_2, 1) = q_2$ | $q_2 \rightarrow 1 q_2$ | **Step 2: Add $\varepsilon$-productions for final state $q_2$** $$ q_2 \rightarrow \varepsilon $$ #### **Part (b)** [7 marks] Write the final right-linear grammar (with renamed non-terminals) and identify its language. **Model Solution:** Renaming $q_0 \to S$, $q_1 \to A$, $q_2 \to B$ for clarity: $$ \begin{aligned} S &\rightarrow 0S \;\vert\; 1A \\ A &\rightarrow 0A \;\vert\; 1B \\ B &\rightarrow 0B \;\vert\; 1B \;\vert\; \varepsilon \end{aligned} $$ This is a **right-linear grammar** with $V = \{S, A, B\}$, $T = \{0, 1\}$, start symbol $S$. **Language Identification:** - $S \Rightarrow 0S \Rightarrow 00S \Rightarrow \ldots \Rightarrow 0^k S$ (any number of 0s) - Then $S \rightarrow 1A$ (must see a 1 next) - $A \Rightarrow 0A \Rightarrow 00A \Rightarrow \ldots \Rightarrow 0^m A$ (any number of 0s) - Then $A \rightarrow 1B$ (must see another 1 next) - $B \Rightarrow 0B \Rightarrow 1B \Rightarrow \ldots \Rightarrow (0+1)^n$ (any further symbols) - $B \rightarrow \varepsilon$ (terminate) Hence: $$L(G) = L(M) = 0^* \, 1 \, 0^* \, 1 \, (0+1)^*$$ i.e., all strings over $\{0, 1\}$ that contain **at least one `1` followed somewhere later by another `1`**. **Verification by tracing `0110` in the DFA:** $q_0 \xrightarrow{0} q_0 \xrightarrow{1} q_1 \xrightarrow{1} q_2 \xrightarrow{0} q_2$ — ends at $q_2$ (final) ⇒ **ACCEPTED** ✅ **Grammar derivation of `0110`:** $S \Rightarrow 0S \Rightarrow 01A \Rightarrow 011B \Rightarrow 0110B \Rightarrow 0110\varepsilon = 0110$ ✅ > [!WARNING] > **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting $F_i \rightarrow \varepsilon$** for final states — this is a compulsory production. (–2 marks) > 2. **Writing the production in the wrong direction**, e.g., $1q_1 \rightarrow q_0$ instead of $q_0 \rightarrow 1q_1$. (–2 marks) > 3. **Confusing left-linear and right-linear form** — KTU 2024 explicitly asks for one specific form. (–3 marks) > 4. **Not identifying the language** — many students stop after listing productions. The question demands language characterization. (–2 marks) > 5. **Producing extra/wrong $\varepsilon$-productions** for non-final states. Only final states get the $\varepsilon$-production. (–1 mark) --- ## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **🔑 Rapid Revision Checklist — Equivalence of RGs and DFAs** - **Equivalence Theorem:** A language is regular **iff** it is generated by an RG **iff** it is recognized by a DFA. - **Right-linear form** of production: $A \rightarrow aB$ or $A \rightarrow a$. **Left-linear form:** $A \rightarrow Ba$ or $A \rightarrow a$. - **RG $\rightarrow$ DFA** is a *two-stage* process: (i) RG $\rightarrow$ NFA via mechanical translation of productions, (ii) NFA $\rightarrow$ DFA via **subset construction** (whenever non-determinism arises from terminal productions). - **DFA $\rightarrow$ RG** is *direct*: each transition becomes a production, each final state gets a $\varepsilon$-production. The result is a right-linear grammar. - **New final state $F$** must be introduced for every terminal production $A \rightarrow a$ in the RG $\rightarrow$ NFA step. - **Final states in the DFA** are those subsets of NFA states that contain the NFA's final state(s). - **Start state of DFA** is always $\{S\}$ where $S$ is the grammar's start symbol (or the DFA's start state when going the other way). - **Upper bound:** $\vert Q_{DFA} \vert \leq 2^{\vert Q_{NFA} \vert}$, but in practice the constructed DFA is much smaller. - **Closure properties** (union, concatenation, Kleene star, complement, intersection) are inherited by RGs from DFAs *via* the equivalence theorem. - **Real-world use:** Lexical analyzers, pattern matchers (`grep`, `regex`), digital circuit design, and protocol validation all exploit this equivalence. - **The reverse direction (DFA $\rightarrow$ RG)** is *always* deterministic and never requires the NFA step. - **Don't confuse start symbol with start state** — they are conceptually the same object but live in different formalisms.

topics.title: Regular Expression (RE)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET414 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Regular Expression (RE) # Module 2 — Regular Expression (RE): Formal Foundation & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) A **Regular Expression (RE)** over an alphabet $\Sigma$ is a declarative, compact, algebraic notation used to characterize the **Regular Languages** — the class of languages recognized by Finite Automata. Formally, the set of Regular Expressions is defined **inductively (recursively)** as follows: Let $\Sigma$ be a finite alphabet. The Regular Expressions over $\Sigma$ and the languages they denote are defined as: | Base / Inductive Rule | Regular Expression | Language Denoted | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Base 1** | $\varnothing$ (or $\emptyset$) | $\emptyset$ — the empty language (no strings) | | **Base 2** | $\varepsilon$ | $\{\varepsilon\}$ — language containing only the empty string | | **Base 3** | $a$, where $a \in \Sigma$ | $\{a\}$ — language containing the single symbol $a$ | | **Inductive 1** | $R_1 \cup R_2$ (or $R_1 + R_2$) | $L(R_1) \cup L(R_2)$ — **Union** | | **Inductive 2** | $R_1 \cdot R_2$ (concatenation) | $L(R_1) \cdot L(R_2)$ — **Concatenation** | | **Inductive 3** | $R_1^{\star}$ | $L(R_1)^{\star}$ — **Kleene Star / Kleene Closure** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Reproduce verbatim):** "A regular expression is built up recursively from the empty set $\emptyset$, the empty string $\varepsilon$, and the symbols of $\Sigma$ using the three operations: union, concatenation, and Kleene star." This exact wording fetches full marks in **2-mark definition questions**. The **language denoted by a regular expression $R$** is written as $L(R)$ and is also called a **regular language**. Every regular language is recognized by a DFA, an NFA, or an $\varepsilon$-NFA, and vice versa (Kleene's Theorem). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pattern Recipe" View Imagine you are a chef writing a **recipe** for valid passwords on a website signup form. The recipe does not list every possible password (that would be infinite); instead, it describes a **pattern**: - "Start with one or more letters" - "followed by exactly three digits" - "optionally ending with an underscore or hyphen" That pattern description is precisely a **Regular Expression**. It is: - **Compact** — one line replaces an infinite enumeration. - **Declarative** — it says *what* is acceptable, not *how* to verify it. - **Recursive** — patterns are built from smaller patterns using **union** (OR), **concatenation** (AND-then), and **star** (repetition, including zero times). > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Mapping to Finite Automata:** A Regular Expression is the *mathematical recipe*; a Finite Automaton is the *machine* that executes the recipe by reading input symbols one by one. They are two equivalent languages for describing the same set of strings — this equivalence is the heart of **Kleene's Theorem**, a guaranteed KTU Module-2 question. --- ## 1.3 Standard Symbols, Identities & Boundary Values | Symbol | Name | Meaning | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $\varepsilon$ | Epsilon | The **empty string** (length 0) — *not* the empty language | | $\varnothing$ | Empty set | Language with **no strings at all** | | $+$ or $\cup$ or $\mid$ | Union | "OR" — one of two patterns | | $\cdot$ (often omitted) | Concatenation | "AND-then" — patterns in sequence | | $\ast$ | Kleene Star | Zero or more repetitions | | $+$ (superscript) | Positive closure | One or more repetitions: $R^{+} = RR^{\star}$ | | $?$ (in extended RE) | Optional | Zero or one occurrence | > [!WARNING] > **The Most Confused Distinction in TOC — Examiner's Favourite Trap:** > $\varepsilon \neq \varnothing$. The empty string $\varepsilon$ is **one valid string** (a string of length zero), so $L(\varepsilon) = \{\varepsilon\} \neq \emptyset$. Writing $\varepsilon = \varnothing$ in your KTU answer script will cost you 1 full mark instantly. --- ## 1.4 KTU Module 2 — Topics that Live Alongside RE For holistic Module-2 preparation, the following concepts are tightly coupled with Regular Expressions: 1. **Algebraic Laws of RE** — Distributive, Associative, Idempotent, Identity, Annihilator rules. 2. **Kleene's Theorem** — $L$ is regular $\iff$ $L = L(A)$ for some DFA/NFA $\iff$ $L = L(R)$ for some RE. 3. **RE $\rightarrow$ NFA construction** (via Thompson's construction rules). 4. **NFA/DFA $\rightarrow$ RE conversion** (via state elimination or Arden's Theorem). 5. **Arden's Theorem** — solving fixed-point equations of the form $R = Q + RP$. Each of these will appear in the upcoming sections of this study note. # Module 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operator Precedence (Examination Critical!) In any regular expression, the three operators have a strict binding order. **Always parenthesize to disambiguate** — but knowing the default order prevents parsing errors: $$\text{Highest Precedence:} \quad R^{\star} \quad > \quad \text{Concatenation } (R_1R_2) \quad > \quad \text{Union } (R_1 + R_2) \quad :\text{Lowest Precedence}$$ **Worked micro-example:** The RE $ab^{\star} + c$ parses as $(a \cdot b^{\star}) + c$, **not** as $a \cdot (b^{\star} + c)$. --- ## 2.2 The Twelve Indispensable Algebraic Laws of RE These identities are the **backbone of every "Simplify the given RE" question** in KTU ESE. Memorize them as a single block. | # | Law Name | Identity | Intuition | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Commutative (Union)** | $R + S = S + R$ | Order of "OR" does not matter | | 2 | **Associative (Union)** | $(R + S) + T = R + (S + T)$ | Grouping of "OR" does not matter | | 3 | **Associative (Concat)** | $(RS)T = R(ST)$ | Grouping of sequence does not matter | | 4 | **Distributive (Concat over Union)** | $R(S + T) = RS + RT$ | $a(b+c) = ab+ac$ | | 5 | **Distributive (Union over Concat)** | $(R + S)T = RT + ST$ | Right-distributive form | | 6 | **Identity (Union)** | $R + \varnothing = R$ | $\emptyset$ is the "zero" of OR | | 7 | **Identity (Concat)** | $R\varepsilon = \varepsilon R = R$ | $\varepsilon$ is the "identity" of AND | | 8 | **Annihilator (Concat)** | $R\varnothing = \varnothing R = \varnothing$ | Empty set kills concatenation | | 9 | **Idempotent (Union)** | $R + R = R$ | Duplicates in OR collapse | | 10 | **Star of $\varepsilon$** | $\varepsilon^{\star} = \varepsilon$ | Zero-or-more empty strings = just empty string | | 11 | **Star of $\varnothing$** | $\varnothing^{\star} = \varepsilon$ | Zero-or-more from empty set = empty string | | 12 | **Star / Concatenation Idempotence** | $R^{\star} = \varepsilon + RR^{\star} = (R + \varepsilon)^{\star}$ | Recursive definition of star | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Line:** The recursive law $R^{\star} = \varepsilon + R \cdot R^{\star}$ is the algebraic foundation behind **Arden's Theorem** and is tested almost every year. Write this on your answer sheet whenever you solve a fixed-point equation. --- ## 2.3 Worked Identities — Reading the "Why" **Identity 4 in action:** Why is $R(S + T) = RS + RT$? - The LHS says: pick $R$, then pick **either** $S$ **or** $T$. - The RHS says: pick $R$ followed by $S$ **OR** pick $R$ followed by $T$. - Both describe the exact same set of strings — proven by set-theoretic distribution. **Identity 12 in action:** Why does $R^{\star} = \varepsilon + RR^{\star}$? - $R^{\star}$ means: zero copies of $R$, or one copy, or two, or ... - Zero copies = $\varepsilon$. - One-or-more copies = $R$ followed by zero-or-more copies of $R$ = $RR^{\star}$. - Therefore $R^{\star} = \varepsilon + RR^{\star}$. --- ##
