**Step 1 — Identify $L$.** $L$ contains all strings over $\{a, b\}$ having $ab$ as a substring. Examples: $ab$, $aab$, $abb$, $bab$, $abab$, $aabb$, $abba$, etc.

**Step 2 — Apply $h$ to each string of $L$.** Since $h$ replaces every $a$ with $0$ and every $b$ with $1$, applying $h$ to any string of $L$ produces a string of $0$s and $1$s that **still contains the substring $01$** at the exact same positions.

**Step 3 — Formal description:**

$$
h(L) = \{\, w' \in \{0, 1\}^{*} \mid w' \text{ contains the substring } 01 \,\}
$$

Equivalently:

$$
h(L) = \{0, 1\}^{*} \cdot 01 \cdot \{0, 1\}^{*}
$$

In words, $h(L)$ is the language of all binary strings that contain $01$ as a contiguous substring. **(7 marks)** $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams**
> 1. **Forgetting $h(\varepsilon) = \varepsilon$:** Many students apply $h$ only to non-empty strings and forget the empty-string axiom. Always begin the proof with both axioms.
> 2. **Confusing $h(L)$ and $h^{-1}(L)$:** Image and inverse image are entirely different operations. Mixing them up loses **at least 3 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.
> 3. **Constructing a DFA for $h^{-1}(L)$ using $a$ instead of $h(a)$:** The transition in the new DFA must be on $h(a)$ (the *string* image), not on $a$ itself. This single error invalidates the entire construction.
> 4. **Not specifying the alphabet for the constructed machine:** In a 7-mark proof, you must explicitly state the components $(Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ of the new automaton.
> 5. **Assuming intersection is preserved:** The statement $h(L_1 \cap L_2) = h(L_1) \cap h(L_2)$ is **false** in general. The examiner will deduct a mark for an unverified claim.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Homomorphisms**
> - A **homomorphism** $h : \Sigma^{*} \rightarrow \Gamma^{*}$ satisfies $h(\varepsilon) = \varepsilon$ and $h(xy) = h(x)h(y)$.
> - It is **fully determined** by the values of $h(a)$ for $a \in \Sigma$.
> - $h(w) = h(a_1)h(a_2)\ldots h(a_n)$ for $w = a_1 a_2 \ldots a_n$.
> - **Image** of a language: $h(L) = \{h(w) \mid w \in L\}$.
> - **Inverse image**: $h^{-1}(M) = \{w \mid h(w) \in M\}$.
> - **Closure property 1:** Regular $\xrightarrow{h}$ Regular. (NFA construction by grouping symbols that map to the same image.)
> - **Closure property 2:** Regular $\xrightarrow{h^{-1}}$ Regular. (DFA construction by replacing transition on $a$ with the string transition on $h(a)$.)
> - **Closure property 3:** CFL $\xrightarrow{h}$ CFL.
> - **Closure property 4:** CFL $\xrightarrow{h^{-1}}$ CFL.
> - **Identity homomorphism** has $h(a) = a$ for all $a \in \Sigma$.
> - **Erasing homomorphism** has $h(a) = \varepsilon$ for some $a$.
> - Homomorphism is **not preserved** under intersection in general.
> - A function that is not concatenation-preserving is **not** a homomorphism.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Necessary Conditions for Regular Languages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET414 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Necessary Conditions for Regular Languages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition: The Pumping Lemma for Regular Languages

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pumping Lemma for Regular Languages (Formal Statement)**
>
> Let $L$ be a **regular language**. Then there exists a positive integer $p$ (called the **pumping length**) such that every string $s \in L$ with $\lvert s \rvert \geq p$ can be decomposed into three substrings $s = xyz$ satisfying the following three conditions simultaneously:
>
> 1. $\lvert y \rvert \geq 1$  (the middle block is **non-empty**)
> 2. $\lvert xy \rvert \leq p$  (the pumped region lies **within the first $p$ symbols**)
> 3. $xy^{i}z \in L$ for every integer $i \geq 0$  (the middle block can be **pumped** any number of times)

The pumping lemma is a **necessary condition** for regularity: every regular language *must* satisfy it, but not every language satisfying it is regular. It is therefore the standard tool to **prove that a language is *not* regular**.

### 1.2 What is a "Regular Language"?

> [!NOTE]
> A language $L \subseteq \Sigma^{*}$ is **regular** if and only if it is accepted by some **Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA)**, **Nondeterministic Finite Automaton (NFA)**, or described by a **regular expression**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Elevator with Limited Buttons**
>
> Imagine a building elevator that has only **5 floor buttons (states)**. If you press a long sequence of buttons (say 6 or more), then by the **Pigeonhole Principle** at least one button **must be pressed twice in a row pattern** somewhere. Once that pattern is identified, you can "pump" it: press it 1 time, 2 times, 100 times, or skip it entirely — and the elevator will still end up at a valid floor.
>
> - The **DFA's finite number of states** plays the role of the limited buttons.
> - **Pumping a substring** is like repeating (or skipping) a state-loop.
> - A language that **cannot** be pumped this way (e.g., a language where every length is unique) **cannot** be recognized by any finite machine.

### 1.4 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $p$ | Pumping length (depends only on $L$, not on the string) |
| $x, y, z$ | Decomposition of $s$ with $s = xyz$ |
| $y^{i}$ | The string $y$ concatenated with itself $i$ times |
| $\Sigma^{*}$ | Kleene closure: set of all finite strings over alphabet $\Sigma$ |
| $\lvert s \rvert$ | Length of string $s$ (number of symbols) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Conditions — Why Each One Exists

The pumping lemma has three structural conditions. Each has a precise theoretical justification rooted in the **structure of finite automata**:

1. **$\lvert y \rvert \geq 1$ — Non-emptiness of the pump**
   - **Why?** If $y$ were empty, pumping would do nothing, making the lemma trivially true and useless. Forcing $y$ to be non-empty ensures the substring being repeated is a **genuine loop** the automaton traversed.

2. **$\lvert xy \rvert \leq p$ — Locality of the loop**
   - **Why?** The pumping length $p$ is usually chosen to be the **number of states** in the DFA. By the Pigeonhole Principle, in the first $p+1$ states visited on processing a long string, some state **must repeat**. The loop between these two occurrences has length at most $p$. Hence, the loop is captured within the first $p$ symbols of the string.

3. **$xy^{i}z \in L$ for all $i \geq 0$ — Loop closure under repetition**
   - **Why?** Once the automaton enters a loop (state $q_k$ revisited), traversing that loop any extra number of times (or zero times) returns the automaton to the same state. Since the suffix $z$ is processed from the same state, **every** pumped variant must also be accepted.

### 2.2 Game-Theoretic Interpretation (Crucial for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adversary Game for Proving Non-Regularity**
>
> The Pumping Lemma is most often used in a **two-player game**:
> - **Player 1 (You, the prover)**: Want to show $L$ is not regular.
> - **Player 2 (Adversary)**: Claims $L$ is regular and chooses $p$.
>
> The proof strategy is **"you choose $s$, adversary chooses the decomposition"**:
> 1. Adversary picks $p$.
> 2. You choose a specific $s \in L$ with $\lvert s \rvert \geq p$.
> 3. Adversary splits $s = xyz$ respecting the rules.
> 4. You pick an $i$ such that $xy^{i}z \notin L$ — **contradiction!**

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Expression | Meaning / Usage |
| :--- | :--- |
| $s = xyz$ | Decomposition of the chosen string into three parts |
| $p$ | Pumping length (constant depending on $L$) |
| $\lvert y \rvert \geq 1$ | Pumpable substring must be non-empty |
| $\lvert xy \rvert \leq p$ | Loop occurs within first $p$ characters |
| $xy^{i}z \in L,\ \forall i \geq 0$ | Pumped string stays in $L$ |
| $a^{n}b^{n}$ | Canonical non-regular example ($n \geq 0$) |
| $\lvert s \rvert = p + k,\ k \geq 0$ | A safe choice for $s$ is just above $p$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick**: *"**Y**ou can't have an **e**mpty loop, the loop is **l**ocal, and the loop is **i**nvariant."* — **YELI** rule for the three conditions.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pumping Lemma results are foundational in:
- **Compiler Design**: Lexical analyzers are built on DFAs; pumping-lemma non-regularity tells us when a tokenization rule needs a **context-free grammar** (pushdown automaton) instead.
- **Network Protocol Verification**: Many real-time protocol constraints (e.g., balanced nesting of XML/HTML tags) are not regular, requiring richer models.
- **Pattern Matching Engines**: Engines like `grep` use regular expressions (regular); any feature requiring counting/balancing requires a more powerful formalism.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Proof of the Pumping Lemma (DFA Construction)

> [!NOTE]
> We prove: *If $L$ is regular, then the pumping lemma holds.*

**Step 1 — Assume $L$ is regular.** Then there exists a DFA $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ that accepts $L$, where $Q$ is the finite set of states with $\lvert Q \rvert = n$.

**Step 2 — Choose the pumping length.** Set $p = n$ (the number of states of $M$).

**Step 3 — Take any string $s = s_1 s_2 \cdots s_m \in L$ with $m \geq p$.**

**Step 4 — Apply the Pigeonhole Principle.**
Consider the sequence of states visited while processing $s$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_0 &= q_0 \\
r_1 &= \delta(q_0, s_1) \\
r_2 &= \delta(r_1, s_2) \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
r_m &= \delta(r_{m-1}, s_m) \in F
\end{aligned}
$$

This sequence has $m+1 \geq p+1 = n+1$ states, but only $n$ distinct states exist. By the **Pigeonhole Principle**, at least two states must coincide. Let $r_j = r_k$ for some $0 \leq j < k \leq p$.

**Step 5 — Define the decomposition.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= s_1 s_2 \cdots s_j \\
y &= s_{j+1} s_{j+2} \cdots s_k \\
z &= s_{k+1} s_{k+2} \cdots s_m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Verify the three conditions.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lvert y \rvert = k - j &\geq 1 \quad &\text{(since } j < k \text{)} \\
\lvert xy \rvert = k &\leq p \quad &\text{(since } k \leq p \text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Show $xy^{i}z \in L$ for all $i \geq 0$.** The state after reading $x$ is $r_j$. Reading $y$ returns to $r_k = r_j$. Thus reading $y$ any number of times keeps us at $r_j$. Finally reading $z$ ends at $r_m \in F$:

$$
\delta^{*}(q_0, xy^{i}z) = r_m \in F \quad \forall\, i \geq 0
$$

Therefore, $xy^{i}z \in L$ for all $i \geq 0$. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Application 1: Proving $L_1 = \{a^{n}b^{n} \mid n \geq 0\}$ is NOT Regular

**Step 1 — Assume $L_1$ is regular.** Then there exists a pumping length $p$.

**Step 2 — Choose a critical string.** Let
$$s = a^{p}b^{p} \in L_1, \quad \lvert s \rvert = 2p \geq p$$

**Step 3 — Apply decomposition constraint.** By the lemma, $s = xyz$ with $\lvert xy \rvert \leq p$. Since $s$ begins with $p$ a's, the substring $xy$ consists **entirely of a's**. Thus $y = a^{k}$ for some $k \geq 1$.

**Step 4 — Pump down ($i = 0$).** The pumped string is

$$
\begin{aligned}
xy^{0}z = xz = a^{p-k}b^{p}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Count the symbols.**
- Number of $a$'s: $p - k$
- Number of $b$'s: $p$
- Since $k \geq 1$, we have $p - k < p$.

**Step 6 — Conclude.** The number of $a$'s $\neq$ number of $b$'s, so $xz \notin L_1$. **Contradiction.** Hence $L_1$ is not regular. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Application 2: Proving $L_2 = \{ww \mid w \in \{0,1\}^{*}\}$ is NOT Regular

**Step 1 — Assume $L_2$ is regular** with pumping length $p$.

**Step 2 — Choose a critical string.** Let
$$s = 0^{p}1\,0^{p}1 \in L_2$$
Indeed, $s = ww$ where $w = 0^{p}1$. Note $\lvert s \rvert = 2p+2 \geq p$.

**Step 3 — Apply decomposition constraint.** Since $\lvert xy \rvert \leq p$, both $x$ and $y$ lie inside the first $p$ symbols. The first $p$ symbols of $s$ are all $0$'s, so $y = 0^{k}$ for some $k \geq 1$.

**Step 4 — Pump up ($i = 2$).** The pumped string is

$$
\begin{aligned}
xy^{2}z = 0^{p+k}1\,0^{p}1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Check membership.** For $xy^{2}z$ to be in $L_2$, it must equal $w'w'$ for some string $w'$. The first half is $0^{p+k}1$ and the second half is $0^{p}1$. For these to be equal, we would need $p + k = p$, i.e., $k = 0$, contradicting $k \geq 1$.

**Step 6 — Conclude.** $xy^{2}z \notin L_2$. **Contradiction.** Hence $L_2$ is not regular. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.4 Application 3: Proving $L_3 = \{0^{n} \mid n \text{ is prime}\}$ is NOT Regular

**Step 1 — Assume $L_3$ is regular** with pumping length $p$.

**Step 2 — Choose a critical string.** Let $q$ be a prime number with $q \geq p+2$ (such a prime exists by the infinitude of primes). Set
$$s = 0^{q} \in L_3$$

**Step 3 — Apply decomposition constraint.** $s = xyz$ with $\lvert xy \rvert \leq p$ and $\lvert y \rvert \geq 1$. So $y = 0^{k}$ for some $1 \leq k \leq p$.

**Step 4 — Pump to length $q + k$.** Consider
$$xy^{2}z = 0^{q+k} \in L_3 \implies q + k \text{ is prime}$$

**Step 5 — Choose $i$ that breaks primality.** Let $i = q + 1$:
$$\lvert xy^{q+1}z \rvert = q + (q+1)k = q(1+k) + k$$

This length is composite (it is a product of integers $\geq 2$). For example, when $i = q + 1$, the length is $q + (q+1)k$, which factors as $q + (q+1)k$. While we must show some $i$ gives a non-prime, choose $i = q$. Then:
$$\lvert xy^{q}z \rvert = q + (q-1)k = q(1+k) - k$$
A cleaner choice: set $i = q$, giving length $q + (q-1)k$. Since $k \geq 1$, this is $\geq 2q-1$, and is divisible by structure showing compositeness in many cases.

**Cleaner argument:** Consider $i = 1 + q/p$ (rounded). A simpler route: $i = q+1$ gives length $q + (q+1)k$, which is divisible by... Actually, the cleanest:

Set $i = 0$: length $= q - k$. Pick $i = 2$: length $= q + k$. By **Dirichlet-style** reasoning or simply choosing $i$ such that $i \cdot k + q$ is composite.

A standard pick is $i = 0$: gives length $q - k < q$, but this is just a number that may or may not be prime. **The foolproof choice:** Take $i$ such that the new length is a multiple of some integer $\geq 2$. Set $i = q$:

$$\lvert xy^{q}z \rvert = qk + q = q(k+1)$$

Since $k \geq 1$, $k+1 \geq 2$, so $q(k+1)$ is **composite** (and $\geq 2q > 1$).

**Step 6 — Conclude.** $xy^{q}z = 0^{q(k+1)} \notin L_3$. **Contradiction.** Hence $L_3$ is not regular. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Common Non-Regular Languages

| Language $L$ | Why not regular? (Sketch) | Critical $s$ to pick |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\{a^{n}b^{n}\mid n\geq 0\}$ | $a$'s and $b$'s must balance — uncountable in finite memory | $a^{p}b^{p}$ |
| $\{ww \mid w \in \Sigma^{*}\}$ | DFA cannot "remember" the first half to compare | $0^{p}10^{p}1$ |
| $\{0^{n} \mid n \text{ prime}\}$ | Pumping changes length by $k$, which can destroy primality | $0^{q},\ q$ prime $\geq p$ |
| $\{a^{n^{2}} \mid n\geq 0\}$ | Lengths grow as squares; pump disrupts quadratic growth | $a^{p^{2}}$ |
| $\{w \mid w \text{ has equal } 0\text{s and }1\text{s}\}$ | Counting argument identical to $a^{n}b^{n}$ | $0^{p}1^{p}$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Pumping Process Visualized

The following diagram illustrates how a long string $s$ decomposes into $xyz$ inside a DFA, with the substring $y$ corresponding to a **cycle in the state graph**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeQ0["q0 start state"]
    nodeX["Read x"]
    nodeQj["qj loop start state"]
    nodeY["Read y loops back to qj"]
    nodeQk["qk equals qj same state"]
    nodeZ["Read z"]
    nodeF["F accept state"]

    nodeQ0 --> nodeX
    nodeX --> nodeQj
    nodeQj --> nodeY
    nodeY --> nodeQk
    nodeQk --> nodeZ
    nodeZ --> nodeF

    nodeY -. "Pump i times" .-> nodeQj
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: The state $q_j$ is reached after reading $x$. Then $y$ is processed, taking the automaton through a cycle and returning to $q_j$. The remaining string $z$ is read to reach the accept state $F$. Since the cycle returns to $q_j$, repeating $y$ any number of times still leaves the automaton at $q_j$, so $xy^{i}z$ is always accepted.

---

### 4.2 The Adversary Game Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["STEP 1 Adversary chooses pumping length p"]
    startB["STEP 2 You choose string s in L with length at least p"]
    startC["STEP 3 Adversary decomposes s equals xyz with y nonempty and xy within first p symbols"]
    startD["STEP 4 You pick i such that xy sup i z is NOT in L"]
    startE["STEP 5 CONTRADICTION L is not regular"]

    startA --> startB
    startB --> startC
    startC --> startD
    startD --> startE

    classDef stepBox fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef endBox fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff

    class startA,startB,startC,startD stepBox
    class startE endBox
```

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Pumping Lemma as a Decision Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp["INPUT Language L and candidate string s"]
    chk1{"Is L regular assumed?"}
    pl["Apply Pumping Lemma with length p"]
    dec["Decompose s equals xyz"]
    pump["Pump y i times for chosen i"]
    val{"Is xy sup i z in L?"}
    reg["OUTPUT L is REGULAR consistent with pumping"]
    nreg["OUTPUT L is NOT REGULAR contradiction found"]

    inp --> chk1
    chk1 --> pl
    pl --> dec
    dec --> pump
    pump --> val
    val -- YES for all i --> reg
    val -- NO for some i --> nreg
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State the Pumping Lemma for Regular Languages. Explain the significance of the pumping length $p$.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Pumping Lemma Statement**: If $L$ is a regular language, then there exists a constant $p \geq 1$ (the **pumping length**) such that any string $s \in L$ with $\lvert s \rvert \geq p$ can be split as $s = xyz$ with:
> - $\lvert y \rvert \geq 1$
> - $\lvert xy \rvert \leq p$
> - $xy^{i}z \in L$ for all $i \geq 0$

**Significance of $p$:** The pumping length $p$ depends only on the language $L$ (not on the specific string). It is typically chosen as the number of states of the minimal DFA accepting $L$. It serves as the threshold beyond which any string is guaranteed to contain a **repeatable pattern**, ensuring the DFA must have visited some state twice (Pigeonhole Principle), thereby exposing a pumpable loop.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"The Pumping Lemma is a necessary but not sufficient condition for regularity." Justify this statement with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Necessary**: Every regular language satisfies the Pumping Lemma. Proof uses the Pigeonhole Principle on a DFA's finite states. Hence, the lemma is a property that *must* hold if $L$ is regular.

> **Not Sufficient**: There exist non-regular languages that *also* satisfy the Pumping Lemma. Classic counterexample:
> $$L = \{a^{n}b^{n}c^{n} \mid n \geq 0\} \cup \{b^{m}c^{m} \mid m \geq 0\}$$
> This language satisfies the Pumping Lemma but is **not regular** (the second part is non-regular on its own). The lemma is therefore used **only for non-regularity proofs**, never as a positive certification of regularity.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]*

> **(a)** Prove the Pumping Lemma for Regular Languages. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Using the Pumping Lemma, prove that the language $L = \{a^{n}b^{n} \mid n \geq 1\}$ is **not regular**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a): Proof of the Pumping Lemma

**Given:** $L$ is a regular language.

**To Prove:** There exists $p \geq 1$ such that every $s \in L$ with $\lvert s \rvert \geq p$ can be decomposed as $s = xyz$ satisfying the three conditions.

**Proof:**

**Step 1:** Since $L$ is regular, there exists a DFA $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ with $Q$ finite. Let $\lvert Q \rvert = n$.

**Step 2:** Set $p = n$.

**Step 3:** Take any $s = s_1 s_2 \cdots s_m \in L$ with $m \geq p$. Since $M$ accepts $s$, there exists a sequence of states $r_0, r_1, \ldots, r_m$ with $r_0 = q_0$, $r_{i+1} = \delta(r_i, s_{i+1})$, and $r_m \in F$.

**Step 4:** The sequence $r_0, r_1, \ldots, r_p$ has $p+1 = n+1$ states but only $n$ possible values. By the Pigeonhole Principle, $\exists\, j, k$ with $0 \leq j < k \leq p$ such that $r_j = r_k$.

**Step 5:** Define $x = s_1 \cdots s_j$, $y = s_{j+1} \cdots s_k$, $z = s_{k+1} \cdots s_m$.

**Step 6 (Verification):**
- $\lvert y \rvert = k - j \geq 1$ because $j < k$. ✓
- $\lvert xy \rvert = k \leq p$. ✓
- For any $i \geq 0$, $\delta^{*}(q_0, xy^{i}z) = r_m \in F$ because the loop from $r_j$ to $r_k = r_j$ preserves the state. ✓

Thus $xy^{i}z \in L$ for all $i \geq 0$. $\blacksquare$

#### Model Solution for (b): $L = \{a^{n}b^{n} \mid n \geq 1\}$ is Not Regular

**Proof by contradiction:**

**Step 1:** Suppose $L$ is regular. By the Pumping Lemma, there exists pumping length $p$.

**Step 2:** Choose $s = a^{p}b^{p} \in L$. Note $\lvert s \rvert = 2p \geq p$.

**Step 3:** By the Pumping Lemma, decompose $s = xyz$ with $\lvert xy \rvert \leq p$ and $\lvert y \rvert \geq 1$. Since the first $p$ symbols of $s$ are all $a$'s, both $x$ and $y$ consist solely of $a$'s. Write $y = a^{k}$ for some $k \geq 1$.

**Step 4:** Pump down with $i = 0$:
$$xy^{0}z = xz = a^{p-k}b^{p}$$

**Step 5:** This string has $p - k$ a's followed by $p$ b's. Since $k \geq 1$, we have $p - k < p$, so the number of $a$'s $\neq$ number of $b$'s. Therefore $xz \notin L$.

**Step 6:** This contradicts the Pumping Lemma's condition that $xy^{i}z \in L$ for all $i \geq 0$. Hence, $L$ is **not regular**. $\blacksquare$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]*

> **(a)** Explain the **adversary game** interpretation of the Pumping Lemma. Describe each step clearly. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Prove that the language $L = \{0^{n}1^{n} \mid n \geq 0\}$ is not regular using the Pumping Lemma. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a): The Adversary Game

The Pumping Lemma can be framed as a **two-player game** to prove that a language $L$ is **not regular**:

- **Player 1 (Prover)**: Aims to show $L$ is not regular.
- **Player 2 (Adversary)**: Claims $L$ is regular.

**The game proceeds in four steps:**

**Step 1 — Adversary picks $p$:** The adversary chooses a pumping length $p \geq 1$ (a positive integer). This must be done *before* seeing the prover's chosen string, ensuring the prover can later construct a "tough" input.

**Step 2 — Prover picks a string $s$:** The prover chooses a string $s \in L$ with $\lvert s \rvert \geq p$, **carefully designed** to make pumping impossible.

**Step 3 — Adversary decomposes $s$:** The adversary splits $s = xyz$ subject to the constraints $\lvert y \rvert \geq 1$ and $\lvert xy \rvert \leq p$. The adversary chooses the split to *try* to keep the pumped string in $L$.

**Step 4 — Prover picks $i$:** The prover selects an integer $i \geq 0$ (often $i = 0$ or $i = 2$) such that $xy^{i}z \notin L$.

**Outcome:** If the prover can always win, $L$ cannot be regular, contradicting the assumption in Step 1. This game-theoretic view emphasizes that the **prover's string choice is critical** — pick $s$ so that **every** possible decomposition becomes a trap.

#### Model Solution for (b): $L = \{0^{n}1^{n} \mid n \geq 0\}$ is Not Regular

**Proof by contradiction:**

**Step 1:** Assume $L$ is regular. Then the Pumping Lemma guarantees a pumping length $p$.

**Step 2:** Choose the string
$$s = 0^{p}1^{p} \in L, \quad \lvert s \rvert = 2p \geq p$$

**Step 3:** Apply the Pumping Lemma: there exists a decomposition $s = xyz$ with $\lvert y \rvert \geq 1$ and $\lvert xy \rvert \leq p$. Since $s$ begins with $p$ zeros, the substring $xy$ lies entirely within the leading zeros. Thus $y = 0^{k}$ for some $k \geq 1$.

**Step 4:** Consider $i = 0$:
$$xy^{0}z = xz = 0^{p-k}1^{p}$$

**Step 5 (Analysis):** The string $0^{p-k}1^{p}$ has $p-k$ zeros and $p$ ones. For it to belong to $L$, we need $p - k = p$, which requires $k = 0$. But $k \geq 1$ — **contradiction**.

**Step 6:** Therefore, $xz \notin L$, violating the Pumping Lemma. Hence $L$ is **not regular**. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Choosing $s$ too small:** A frequent mistake is picking $s = 0^{p}1^{p}$ but forgetting to ensure $\lvert s \rvert \geq p$. The Pumping Lemma only applies to strings of length **at least** $p$.
> 2. **Ignoring the constraint $\lvert xy \rvert \leq p$:** Some students assume the adversary can place $y$ anywhere. **Wrong!** The constraint forces $y$ to be in the first $p$ symbols — critical for the $a^{n}b^{n}$ family of proofs.
> 3. **Forgetting to specify the value of $i$:** A proof must explicitly choose an $i$ (typically $0$ or $2$) and **show** the pumped string is not in $L$. Vague statements like "pumping gives a non-member" lose marks.
> 4. **Mis-stating the conclusion:** Always end with "This contradicts the Pumping Lemma, so $L$ is not regular." Marks are awarded for **clean logical closure**.
> 5. **Confusing $y \geq 0$ with $y \geq 1$:** The lemma requires $\lvert y \rvert \geq 1$, NOT $y \geq 0$. Forgetting this invalidates the entire argument.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pumping Lemma for Regular Languages**
>
> ☐ The Pumping Lemma is a **necessary** (not sufficient) condition for regularity.
> ☐ The pumping length $p$ is the number of states in the minimal DFA.
> ☐ Decomposition $s = xyz$ must satisfy: $\lvert y \rvert \geq 1$, $\lvert xy \rvert \leq p$, $xy^{i}z \in L$ for all $i \geq 0$.
> ☐ The proof rests on the **Pigeonhole Principle** applied to a DFA's states.
> ☐ The **adversary game** is the standard framework for non-regularity proofs:
>   - Adversary picks $p$ → Prover picks $s$ → Adversary picks decomposition → Prover picks $i$.
> ☐ Choose $s$ so that **every** possible decomposition becomes a trap; pump with $i = 0$ or $i = 2$ most often.
> ☐ Canonical non-regular languages: $\{a^{n}b^{n}\}$, $\{ww\}$, $\{0^{n}1^{n}\}$, $\{a^{n}\mid n \text{ prime}\}$, $\{a^{n}b^{n}c^{n}\}$.
> ☐ The constraint $\lvert xy \rvert \leq p$ is what makes the proofs work — it traps the pumpable region.
> ☐ Closure properties (union, intersection, complement, reversal) of regular languages give **alternative necessary conditions** to test regularity.
> ☐ For $n$-ary counting or palindromic structure, regular languages are *not* sufficient — context-free grammars (Module 3) are needed.
> ☐ Always end non-regularity proofs with an explicit logical conclusion: *"Contradiction; hence $L$ is not regular."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Closure Properties of Regular Languages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET414 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Closure Properties of Regular Languages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Closure Property:**
> A class of languages $\mathcal{C}$ is said to be **closed** under an operation $\circ$ if, for every pair of languages $L_1, L_2 \in \mathcal{C}$, the language obtained by applying the operation $L_1 \circ L_2$ also belongs to $\mathcal{C}$. Formally:
> $$\forall L_1, L_2 \in \mathcal{C}, \quad L_1 \circ L_2 \in \mathcal{C}$$

In the context of the **Theory of Computation**, the class of **Regular Languages** (denoted $\mathcal{R}$) is one of the most well-behaved language families. The set $\mathcal{R}$ is closed under the standard set-theoretic and language-theoretic operations. The key result is:

> **Theorem:** The class of regular languages is closed under **union, intersection, complementation, concatenation, Kleene star, reversal, difference, symmetric difference, reversal, homomorphism, and inverse homomorphism**.

This theorem is **central to KTU Module 2** because it allows the engineer to *construct new regular languages from known regular languages* using algebraic operations, without re-proving regularity each time.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Workshop" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Metal Workshop:**
> Imagine a metallurgy workshop that specializes in making **aluminum (Al) products**: rods, sheets, foil, wires. If the workshop is *closed under* "joining two pieces by welding," then whenever you bring in two aluminum pieces and weld them, the resulting piece is **still aluminum** — it never accidentally turns into wood or glass.
>
> The class of regular languages behaves the same way. A **regular language is our "aluminum."** When you apply operations like *union* ($L_1 \cup L_2$) or *intersection* ($L_1 \cap L_2$) to regular languages, the result is **guaranteed to still be regular**. We never "escape" the class — regular languages are algebraically self-contained.

### 1.3 Why Closure Properties Matter in KTU Examinations

- They provide a **shortcut**: instead of constructing a DFA/NFA from scratch to prove a language is regular, you can use closure properties.
- They appear in **KTU Module 2 questions worth 7–14 marks** (typical ask: "Prove that the class of regular languages is closed under *X*" or "Use closure properties to show *L* is regular").
- They are the foundation for **decision algorithms** (emptiness, equivalence, membership).

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Notation

| Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $\mathcal{R}$ | The class of all regular languages |
| $\Sigma$ | Finite alphabet |
| $\Sigma^*$ | Set of all strings over $\Sigma$ |
| $\emptyset$ | Empty language (regular) |
| $\{\varepsilon\}$ | Language containing only the empty string (regular) |
| $L^c$ | Complement of $L$ w.r.t. $\Sigma^*$ |
| $L^R$ | Reversal of $L$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Complete List of Closure Operations for $\mathcal{R}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield List (Memorize this table):**
> The class of regular languages $\mathcal{R}$ is closed under:
> 1. **Union** $\rightarrow$ $L_1 \cup L_2$
> 2. **Intersection** $\rightarrow$ $L_1 \cap L_2$
> 3. **Complement** $\rightarrow$ $\overline{L} = \Sigma^* - L$
> 4. **Difference** $\rightarrow$ $L_1 - L_2$
> 5. **Concatenation** $\rightarrow$ $L_1 \cdot L_2$
> 6. **Kleene Star** $\rightarrow$ $L^*$
> 7. **Reversal** $\rightarrow$ $L^R = \{w^R : w \in L\}$
> 8. **Homomorphism** $\rightarrow$ $h(L) = \{h(w) : w \in L\}$
> 9. **Inverse Homomorphism** $\rightarrow$ $h^{-1}(L) = \{w : h(w) \in L\}$
> 10. **Symmetric Difference** $\rightarrow$ $L_1 \oplus L_2$
> 11. **Substitution** (and its special case, homomorphism)
> 12. **Quotient / Right Quotient** $L_1 / L_2$

### 2.2 Structured Logic — Proving Closure for Each Operation

The **standard proof technique** used in KTU valuations uses one of three engines:
- **DFA Construction** (deterministic, via product construction)
- **NFA Construction** (non-deterministic, simpler for union/concatenation/star)
- **Regular Expression Algebra** (using Kleene's theorem)

#### Operation 1 — Union ($L_1 \cup L_2$)

**Strategy:** Use NFA construction.
- Let $M_1 = (Q_1, \Sigma, \delta_1, q_1, F_1)$ accept $L_1$.
- Let $M_2 = (Q_2, \Sigma, \delta_2, q_2, F_2)$ accept $L_2$.
- Construct $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$:
  - $Q = Q_1 \cup Q_2 \cup \{q_0\}$
  - $q_0$ is a **new start state**
  - $F = F_1 \cup F_2$
  - $\delta(q_0, \varepsilon) = \{q_1, q_2\}$  (ε-transitions to both)
  - For $q \in Q_1$: $\delta(q, a) = \delta_1(q, a)$
  - For $q \in Q_2$: $\delta(q, a) = \delta_2(q, a)$

**Result:** $L(M) = L_1 \cup L_2$. Since NFA accepts $\iff$ regular, closure is proved.

#### Operation 2 — Concatenation ($L_1 \cdot L_2$)

**Strategy:** NFA with $\varepsilon$-transitions connecting accept states of $M_1$ to the start state of $M_2$.
- $Q = Q_1 \cup Q_2$
- $q_0 = q_1$
- $F = F_2$
- Add $\varepsilon$-transition from each $f \in F_1$ to $q_2$.

#### Operation 3 — Kleene Star ($L^*$)

**Strategy:** NFA with new start state $q_0$, ε-transitions to $q_1$, and ε-transitions from every $f \in F_1$ back to $q_1$, plus $q_0 \in F$ (to accept $\varepsilon$).

#### Operation 4 — Intersection ($L_1 \cap L_2$)

**Strategy:** **DFA Product Construction** (Cartesian product of states).
- Let $M_1$ and $M_2$ be DFAs.
- Construct $M = (Q_1 \times Q_2, \Sigma, \delta, (q_1, q_2), F_1 \times F_2)$.
- Transition: $\delta((p, q), a) = (\delta_1(p, a), \delta_2(q, a))$.
- A string is accepted iff the final state is in $F_1 \times F_2$, i.e., $M_1$ and $M_2$ both accept.

#### Operation 5 — Complement ($\overline{L}$)

**Strategy:** Given DFA $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$, construct $M' = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, Q \setminus F)$.
- The start state and transitions are **unchanged**; only the accept states are swapped to non-accept and vice versa.
- Result: $L(M') = \Sigma^* - L(M)$.

#### Operation 6 — Difference ($L_1 - L_2$)

**Strategy:** $L_1 - L_2 = L_1 \cap \overline{L_2}$. Apply closure under complement and intersection sequentially.

#### Operation 7 — Reversal ($L^R$)

**Strategy:** Convert NFA $M$ for $L$ to NFA $M^R$ for $L^R$ by **reversing all transitions** and swapping the start state with the (set of) accept states.

#### Operation 8 — Homomorphism ($h(L)$)

**Strategy:** A homomorphism $h : \Sigma \to \Gamma^*$ is extended to strings: $h(wa) = h(w)h(a)$. Given a regular expression $r$ for $L$, replace each symbol $a$ by $h(a)$ to get a new regular expression for $h(L)$.

#### Operation 9 — Inverse Homomorphism ($h^{-1}(L)$)

**Strategy:** Given DFA $M$ for $L$ over $\Gamma$ and a homomorphism $h : \Sigma \to \Gamma^*$, construct DFA $M'$ for $h^{-1}(L)$ by:
- $M'$ reads symbol $a \in \Sigma$ and simulates $M$ on $h(a) \in \Gamma^*$.
- $M'$ has the same states and accept states as $M$.
- $\delta'(q, a) = \delta^*(q, h(a))$, where $\delta^*$ is the extended transition.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Operation | Notation | Proof Engine | Resulting Model |
| :-: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Union | $L_1 \cup L_2$ | NFA + $\varepsilon$ | NFA |
| 2 | Concatenation | $L_1 \cdot L_2$ | NFA + $\varepsilon$ | NFA |
| 3 | Kleene Star | $L^*$ | NFA + $\varepsilon$ | NFA |
| 4 | Intersection | $L_1 \cap L_2$ | Product DFA | DFA |
| 5 | Complement | $\overline{L}$ | Swap F-states | DFA |
| 6 | Difference | $L_1 - L_2$ | Complement + Intersection | DFA |
| 7 | Reversal | $L^R$ | Reverse transitions | NFA |
| 8 | Homomorphism | $h(L)$ | Replace symbols in regex | Regex |
| 9 | Inverse Hom. | $h^{-1}(L)$ | Simulate on $h(a)$ | DFA |
| 10 | Symmetric Diff. | $L_1 \oplus L_2$ | $(L_1 \cap \overline{L_2}) \cup (\overline{L_1} \cap L_2)$ | DFA |
| 11 | Right Quotient | $L_1 / L_2$ | Modify DFA start | DFA |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU Exams:**
> - If the operation involves **branching alternatives** (union), use NFA with new start state.
> - If the operation involves **sequential composition** (concatenation), use ε-transitions.
> - If the operation involves **set logic on acceptance** (intersection, complement, difference), use **DFA product construction** (this is *only* clean for DFAs, not NFAs — a frequent KTU pitfall).
> - If the operation involves **string transformation** (reversal, homomorphism), transform the automaton/regular expression structurally.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Compiler Design:** Lexical analyzers (built on DFAs) need to combine token classes — closure under union guarantees the union of token sets is still tokenizable.
- **Pattern Matching Engines (grep, awk):** Use inverse homomorphism to match transformed input.
- **Network Security / Firewalls:** Regular-expression-based packet filters rely on closure to build complex matchers from simple ones.
- **Model Checking:** Automata-based verification uses complement and intersection to express "bad behavior avoidance."

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Proof — Closure under Union

**Theorem:** If $L_1$ and $L_2$ are regular languages over $\Sigma$, then $L_1 \cup L_2$ is regular.

**Proof:**

**Step 1 — Existence of NFAs:**
Since $L_1$ and $L_2$ are regular, by Kleene's Theorem there exist NFAs $N_1$ and $N_2$ such that $L(N_1) = L_1$ and $L(N_2) = L_2$.

$$N_1 = (Q_1, \Sigma, \delta_1, q_1, F_1), \quad N_2 = (Q_2, \Sigma, \delta_2, q_2, F_2)$$

**Step 2 — Assume Disjoint State Sets (WLOG):**
We can rename states of $N_2$ so that $Q_1 \cap Q_2 = \emptyset$. This is a standard assumption; renaming does not change the language accepted.

**Step 3 — Construct the New NFA $N$:**
Define $N = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ where:

$$Q = Q_1 \cup Q_2 \cup \{q_0\}$$

$$q_0 \notin Q_1 \cup Q_2 \text{ (a brand new start state)}$$

$$F = F_1 \cup F_2$$

For the transition function $\delta: Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \to 2^Q$:

$$\delta(q, x) = \begin{cases} \delta_1(q, x) & \text{if } q \in Q_1, \, x \in \Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\} \\[4pt] \delta_2(q, x) & \text{if } q \in Q_2, \, x \in \Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\} \\[4pt] \{q_1, q_2\} & \text{if } q = q_0 \text{ and } x = \varepsilon \\[4pt] \emptyset & \text{if } q = q_0 \text{ and } x \in \Sigma \end{cases}$$

**Step 4 — Show $L(N) = L_1 \cup L_2$:**

($\supseteq$) Let $w \in L_1$. Then $w \in L(N_1)$, so there is a path in $N_1$ from $q_1$ to some $f \in F_1$. In $N$, we can take the ε-transition $q_0 \to q_1$ and follow the same path, ending at $f \in F = F_1 \cup F_2$. Hence $w \in L(N)$. By symmetry, if $w \in L_2$, then $w \in L(N)$.

($\subseteq$) Let $w \in L(N)$. Then there is a path in $N$ from $q_0$ to some $f \in F = F_1 \cup F_2$. The first step must be an ε-transition to either $q_1$ or $q_2$ (since $q_0$ has no $\Sigma$-transitions). If the path goes to $q_1$, the rest of the path is entirely within $N_1$ (as $Q_1, Q_2$ are disjoint), and reaches $f \in F_1$. So $w \in L_1$. Symmetric argument for $q_2$. Hence $w \in L_1 \cup L_2$.

**Step 5 — Conclude:**
$L(N) = L_1 \cup L_2$, and $N$ is an NFA. By Kleene's Theorem, $L_1 \cup L_2$ is regular. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Proof — Closure under Intersection (Product Construction)

**Theorem:** If $L_1$ and $L_2$ are regular, then $L_1 \cap L_2$ is regular.

**Proof:**

**Step 1 — Convert to DFAs:**
Since $L_1, L_2$ are regular, there exist DFAs:
$$M_1 = (Q_1, \Sigma, \delta_1, q_1, F_1), \quad M_2 = (Q_2, \Sigma, \delta_2, q_2, F_2)$$
such that $L(M_1) = L_1$ and $L(M_2) = L_2$.

**Step 2 — Construct Product DFA $M$:**
$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$$
where:

$$Q = Q_1 \times Q_2$$

$$q_0 = (q_1, q_2)$$

$$F = F_1 \times F_2$$

For the transition function $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \to Q$:

$$\delta\big((p, q),\, a\big) = \big(\delta_1(p, a),\, \delta_2(q, a)\big)$$

**Step 3 — Show $L(M) = L_1 \cap L_2$:**

We prove by induction on $|w| = n$ that:

$$\delta^*\big((q_1, q_2),\, w\big) = \big(\delta_1^*(q_1, w),\, \delta_2^*(q_2, w)\big)$$

**Base case ($n = 0$):** $w = \varepsilon$. Then $\delta^*((q_1, q_2), \varepsilon) = (q_1, q_2) = (\delta_1^*(q_1, \varepsilon), \delta_2^*(q_2, \varepsilon))$. ✓

**Inductive step:** Assume true for $w$, consider $wa$:
- $\delta^*((q_1, q_2), wa) = \delta(\delta^*((q_1, q_2), w), a)$
- $= \delta((\delta_1^*(q_1, w), \delta_2^*(q_2, w)), a)$ (by induction)
- $= (\delta_1(\delta_1^*(q_1, w), a), \delta_2(\delta_2^*(q_2, w), a))$
- $= (\delta_1^*(q_1, wa), \delta_2^*(q_2, wa))$ ✓

**Step 4 — Acceptance Equivalence:**
$w \in L(M)$ $\iff$ $\delta^*((q_1, q_2), w) \in F_1 \times F_2$
$\iff$ $\delta_1^*(q_1, w) \in F_1$ AND $\delta_2^*(q_2, w) \in F_2$
$\iff$ $w \in L(M_1)$ AND $w \in L(M_2)$
$\iff$ $w \in L_1 \cap L_2$

**Step 5 — Conclude:**
$L(M) = L_1 \cap L_2$, and $M$ is a DFA, hence $L_1 \cap L_2$ is regular. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Proof — Closure under Complement

**Theorem:** If $L$ is a regular language over $\Sigma$, then $\overline{L} = \Sigma^* - L$ is regular.

**Proof:**

**Step 1 — DFA Existence:**
Since $L$ is regular, there exists a DFA $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ with $L(M) = L$.

**Step 2 — Note:** We need a **complete DFA** (every state has a transition on every symbol of $\Sigma$). If $M$ is incomplete, add a "trap" dead state $q_{dead}$ with self-loops on all $\Sigma$.

**Step 3 — Construct $M'$:**
$$M' = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, Q \setminus F)$$

The components are **identical** to $M$ **except** the accept set is swapped: $F' = Q \setminus F$.

**Step 4 — Show $L(M') = \overline{L}$:**

$w \in L(M')$
$\iff \delta^*(q_0, w) \in Q \setminus F$
$\iff \delta^*(q_0, w) \notin F$
$\iff w \notin L(M)$
$\iff w \notin L$
$\iff w \in \Sigma^* - L = \overline{L}$

**Step 5 — Conclude:**
$M'$ is a DFA (since $M$ is), so $\overline{L}$ is regular. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.4 Exhaustive Proof — Closure under Reversal

**Theorem:** If $L$ is regular, then $L^R$ is regular.

**Proof:**

**Step 1 — Start with an NFA $N = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ for $L$.**

**Step 2 — Construct $N^R$:**

To reverse, we **reverse every transition** and **swap** the role of start and accept states:
- $Q^R = Q$
- $q_0^R$ = any state in $F$ (use a new start state if $|F| > 1$)
- $F^R = \{q_0\}$
- For each transition $\delta(p, a) \ni q$ in $N$, add $\delta^R(q, a) \ni p$ in $N^R$.

**Step 3 — Show $L(N^R) = L^R$:**

($\subseteq$) Let $w = a_1 a_2 \cdots a_n \in L(N^R)$. There is a path $q_0^R \to \cdots \to f^R$ on $w$ in $N^R$. Each step corresponds to a *reversed* transition in $N$. Reading $w$ in reverse, $w^R = a_n \cdots a_2 a_1$ has a path $q_0 \to \cdots \to f$ in $N$ (where $q_0$ is original start, $f$ is original accept). So $w^R \in L$, i.e., $w \in L^R$.

($\supseteq$) Let $w \in L^R$. Then $w^R \in L$, so $N$ accepts $w^R$ via a path $q_0 \to \cdots \to f$. Reversing this path in $N^R$ gives a path on $w$ from some $f \in F$ to $q_0$. Since $F \subseteq$ start states of $N^R$ and $q_0 \in F^R$, $N^R$ accepts $w$. Hence $w \in L(N^R)$. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Use of Closure Properties in Proving Regularity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Question Type:** "Show that $L$ is regular by using closure properties."

| Target Language Form | Recommended Closure Strategy |
| :--- | :--- |
| $L = \{w : w \text{ has equal number of } 0\text{s and } 1\text{s}\}$ | **NOT regular** — needs pumping lemma |
| $L = \{w \in \{0,1\}^* : w \text{ does not contain } 01\}$ | Complement of "contains 01" — DFA exists |
| $L = L_1 \cup L_2$ where $L_1, L_2$ regular | Direct closure under union |
| $L = L_1 \cap L_2$ where $L_1, L_2$ regular | Product DFA |
| $L = \{w : w = w^R\}$ (palindromes) | **NOT regular** |
| $L = \{w : \vert w \vert_0 = \vert w \vert_1\}$ | **NOT regular** |
| $L = \{0^n 1^n : n \geq 0\}$ | **NOT regular** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake (KTU Valuation):** Students often *assume* the answer is "regular" and try to construct a DFA, when in fact the language is not regular. Always check the **pumping lemma** first if the language has a counting/structural property.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Closure under Union (NFA Construction)

```mermaid
graph LR
    q0((q0_new)) -- epsilon --> q1((q1_M1))
    q0 -- epsilon --> q2((q2_M2))
    q1 -- a --> p1((p1))
    q1 -- b --> p2((p2))
    p1 -- a --> f1((f1_in_F1))
    f1 -- a --> dead1((trap))
    q2 -- a --> p3((p3))
    q2 -- b --> p4((p4))
    p3 -- a --> f2((f2_in_F2))
    f2 -- b --> dead2((trap2))
    f1 -.accept.-> outA[Accept w in L1]
    f2 -.accept.-> outB[Accept w in L2]
    classDef startState fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef acceptState fill:#93c47d,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef trapState fill:#cccccc,stroke:#666
    class q0 startState
    class f1,f2 acceptState
    class dead1,dead2 trapState
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Closure under Concatenation (NFA ε-link)

```mermaid
graph LR
    s1((q1_M1)) -- a --> p1((p1))
    p1 -- b --> f1((f1_F1))
    f1 -- epsilon --> s2((q2_M2))
    s2 -- a --> p2((p2))
    p2 -- b --> f2((f2_F2))
    f2 -.accept.-> outX[Accept L1.L2]
    classDef startState fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef acceptState fill:#93c47d,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    class s1 startState
    class f2 acceptState
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Product Construction (Intersection)

```mermaid
graph TB
    sPQ((q1_p, q2_q)) -- a --> sP1Q1((delta1_q1_a, delta2_q2_a))
    sPQ -- b --> sP2Q2((delta1_q1_b, delta2_q2_b))
    sP1Q1 -- a --> sP1Q1A((p1, q1))
    sP1Q1 -- b --> sP2Q1((p2, q1))
    sP2Q2 -- a --> sP1Q2((p1, q2))
    sP2Q2 -- b --> sP2Q2A((p2, q2))
    sP1Q1A -.accept.-> outI[Accept if p1 in F1 AND q1 in F2]
    classDef startState fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef acceptState fill:#93c47d,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    class sPQ startState
    class sP1Q1A acceptState
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Architecture: How Closure Properties are Used in Practice

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[Regular Language L1] --> R1[DFA / NFA / Regex for L1]
    B[Regular Language L2] --> R2[DFA / NFA / Regex for L2]
    R1 --> OP{Choose Operation}
    R2 --> OP
    OP --> O1[Union: NFA with eps]
    OP --> O2[Intersection: Product DFA]
    OP --> O3[Complement: Swap F-states]
    OP --> O4[Concatenation: Eps-link]
    OP --> O5[Kleene Star: Self-loop]
    O1 --> C[New Regular Language L_new]
    O2 --> C
    O3 --> C
    O4 --> C
    O5 --> C
    C --> V{Is L_new Regular?}
    V -- YES via construction --> Z[Proved Regular]
    V -- NO need --> W[Apply Pumping Lemma to disprove]
    classDef inputBox fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#000
    classDef outputBox fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#000
    class A,B inputBox
    class C,Z outputBox
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Procedure for Equivalence

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[DFA M1 for L1] --> C[Construct Product DFA for L1 cap L2_complement]
    B[DFA M2 for L2] --> D[Construct M2' with swapped F-states]
    D --> C
    C --> E{Is L of product DFA empty?}
    E -- YES --> F[L1 = L2 : EQUIVALENT]
    E -- NO --> G[L1 != L2 : NOT EQUIVALENT]
    classDef startStyle fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000
    classDef successStyle fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#000
    classDef failStyle fill:#ea9999,stroke:#000
    class A,B,D startStyle
    class F successStyle
    class G failStyle
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State whether the class of regular languages is closed under complementation. Justify your answer with a brief construction.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Yes, the class of regular languages is closed under complementation. Let $L$ be a regular language accepted by a complete DFA $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$. Construct $M' = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, Q \setminus F)$ by swapping the accept and non-accept states. Then $L(M') = \Sigma^* - L = \overline{L}$, which is regular.

*(Marks: 1 — Statement, 1 — Construction, 1 — Conclusion.)*

---

#### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Define homomorphism of languages. Is the class of regular languages closed under homomorphism?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **homomorphism** is a function $h : \Sigma \to \Gamma^*$ from symbols to strings, extended to strings by $h(wa) = h(w)h(a)$ and to languages by $h(L) = \{h(w) : w \in L\}$. Yes, regular languages are closed under homomorphism. If $L$ has a regular expression $r$, then replacing each symbol $a$ in $r$ by $h(a)$ yields a regular expression for $h(L)$, so $h(L)$ is regular.

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Prove that the class of regular languages is closed under intersection.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Let $L = \{w \in \{a, b\}^* : w \text{ has } ab \text{ as a substring and } w \text{ ends with } b\}$. Using closure properties, show that $L$ is regular. Construct a DFA for $L$.

---

**Solution to Q-A (a):**

**Theorem:** If $L_1$ and $L_2$ are regular languages, then $L_1 \cap L_2$ is regular.

**Proof:**

*Step 1:* Since $L_1, L_2$ are regular, by definition there exist DFAs:
$$M_1 = (Q_1, \Sigma, \delta_1, q_1, F_1) \text{ accepting } L_1$$
$$M_2 = (Q_2, \Sigma, \delta_2, q_2, F_2) \text{ accepting } L_2$$

*Step 2:* Construct the product DFA $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$:
- $Q = Q_1 \times Q_2$
- $q_0 = (q_1, q_2)$
- $F = F_1 \times F_2$
- $\delta((p, q), a) = (\delta_1(p, a), \delta_2(q, a))$

*Step 3:* By induction on $|w|$, we prove:
$$\delta^*((q_1, q_2), w) = (\delta_1^*(q_1, w), \delta_2^*(q_2, w))$$
- Base ($w = \varepsilon$): both sides are $(q_1, q_2)$. ✓
- Inductive step: $wa$: $\delta((p, q), a) = (\delta_1(p, a), \delta_2(q, a))$ by definition; substituting the induction hypothesis gives the result.

*Step 4:*
$$w \in L(M) \iff \delta^*((q_1, q_2), w) \in F_1 \times F_2$$
$$\iff \delta_1^*(q_1, w) \in F_1 \text{ AND } \delta_2^*(q_2, w) \in F_2$$
$$\iff w \in L_1 \text{ AND } w \in L_2 \iff w \in L_1 \cap L_2$$

*Step 5:* Since $M$ is a DFA accepting $L_1 \cap L_2$, the language is regular. $\blacksquare$

---

**Solution to Q-A (b):**

**Strategy using closure:** Decompose $L$ into simpler regular pieces.

$$L_1 = \{w : w \text{ has } ab \text{ as a substring}\} = \Sigma^* ab \Sigma^*$$
$$L_2 = \{w : w \text{ ends with } b\} = \Sigma^* b$$
$$L = L_1 \cap L_2$$

Both $L_1$ and $L_2$ are regular (clear from regex). By **closure under intersection** (proved in part a), $L$ is regular.

**DFA Construction (Mermaid-formatted):**

```mermaid
graph LR
    A((q0)) -- a --> B((q1))
    A -- b --> A
    B -- a --> B
    B -- b --> C((q2_accept))
    C -- a --> B
    C -- b --> C
    classDef acceptState fill:#93c47d,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef startState fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    class A startState
    class C acceptState
```

**State Meanings:**
- $q_0$: No $a$ seen yet (or last char was $b$ with no $a$).
- $q_1$: An $a$ has been seen, awaiting $b$ to complete substring $ab$.
- $q_2$ (accept): Substring $ab$ has been seen; last char is $b$.

**Regex for $L$:** $(\Sigma^* ab) \cap (\Sigma^* b) = \{w : w \text{ has } ab \text{ AND ends with } b\}$. Equivalent regex (valid because closure ensures it): $\Sigma^* ab \Sigma^* b \cap \Sigma^* b$ — the regular expression that captures it is `b*(a+b)*ab(a+b)*b`, simplified.

**Conclusion:** $L$ is regular, demonstrated both by closure properties and explicit DFA construction. ✓ (7 marks)

---

#### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Prove that the class of regular languages is closed under union and concatenation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using closure properties, show that the language $L = \{w \in \{0,1\}^* : w \text{ has an even number of } 0\text{s and an odd number of } 1\text{s}\}$ is regular.

---

**Solution to Q-B (a):**

**(i) Union:** (3.5 marks)

Let $L_1, L_2$ be regular. By Kleene's Theorem, there exist NFAs:
$$N_1 = (Q_1, \Sigma, \delta_1, q_1, F_1), \quad N_2 = (Q_2, \Sigma, \delta_2, q_2, F_2)$$
with $L(N_1) = L_1$, $L(N_2) = L_2$. WLOG $Q_1 \cap Q_2 = \emptyset$.

Construct $N = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$:
- $Q = Q_1 \cup Q_2 \cup \{q_0\}$
- $F = F_1 \cup F_2$
- $\delta(q, x) = \delta_1(q, x)$ for $q \in Q_1$, $\delta_2(q, x)$ for $q \in Q_2$, $\{q_1, q_2\}$ for $q = q_0, x = \varepsilon$, $\emptyset$ for $q = q_0, x \in \Sigma$.

By case analysis on whether the path in $N$ enters $N_1$ or $N_2$ via the ε-transitions from $q_0$, we conclude $L(N) = L_1 \cup L_2$. Hence $L_1 \cup L_2$ is regular. $\blacksquare$

**(ii) Concatenation:** (3.5 marks)

Let $L_1, L_2$ be regular with NFAs $N_1, N_2$ as above. Construct $N = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_1, F_2)$:
- $Q = Q_1 \cup Q_2$
- Start state: $q_1$ (start of $N_1$)
- Accept states: $F_2$ (accept states of $N_2$)
- $\delta(q, x) = \delta_1(q, x)$ for $q \in Q_1$, $\delta_2(q, x)$ for $q \in Q_2$
- Add $\varepsilon$-transitions: for each $f \in F_1$, add $f \xrightarrow{\varepsilon} q_2$.

A string $w \in L(N)$ follows a path in $N_1$ on some $w_1$ reaching $F_1$, takes an ε-transition to $q_2$, then follows $N_2$ on $w_2$ to $F_2$. So $w = w_1 w_2$ with $w_1 \in L_1, w_2 \in L_2$, i.e., $w \in L_1 \cdot L_2$. The reverse direction is similar. Hence $L_1 \cdot L_2$ is regular. $\blacksquare$

---

**Solution to Q-B (b):**

**Strategy:** Express $L$ as an intersection of two simpler regular languages.

$$L_{\text{even 0s}} = \{w \in \{0,1\}^* : \#_0(w) \text{ is even}\}$$
$$L_{\text{odd 1s}} = \{w \in \{0,1\}^* : \#_1(w) \text{ is odd}\}$$
$$L = L_{\text{even 0s}} \cap L_{\text{odd 1s}}$$

**Show $L_{\text{even 0s}}$ is regular:** DFA with two states $\{E, O\}$ (even, odd count of 0s):
- $\delta(E, 0) = O, \quad \delta(E, 1) = E$
- $\delta(O, 0) = E, \quad \delta(O, 1) = O$
- Accept: $\{E\}$

**Show $L_{\text{odd 1s}}$ is regular:** DFA with two states $\{E', O'\}$ (even, odd count of 1s):
- $\delta(E', 0) = E', \quad \delta(E', 1) = O'$
- $\delta(O', 0) = O', \quad \delta(O', 1) = E'$
- Accept: $\{O'\}$

**Apply closure under intersection:** By the theorem proved in Question A(a), since both $L_{\text{even 0s}}$ and $L_{\text{odd 1s}}$ are regular, their intersection is regular. Hence $L$ is regular. $\blacksquare$

**Product DFA for $L$ (4 states):**

```mermaid
graph LR
    EE((E,E')) -- 0 --> OE((O,E'))
    EE -- 1 --> EO((E,O'))
    OE -- 0 --> EE
    OE -- 1 --> OO((O,O'))
    EO -- 0 --> OO
    EO -- 1 --> EE
    OO -- 0 --> EO
    OO -- 1 --> OE
    OO -.accept.-> done[Accept]
    classDef acceptState fill:#93c47d,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef startState fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    class EE startState
    class OO acceptState
```

The start state is $(E, E')$ (zero 0s — even, zero 1s — even). The accept state is $(O, O')$ (odd 0s, odd 1s). (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to make the DFA complete** before taking the complement — the examiner will deduct 1–2 marks if your complement construction allows missing transitions.
> 2. **Confusing NFA and DFA product construction** — product construction only works cleanly for **DFAs**. For NFAs, the subset construction must be applied first.
> 3. **Failing to specify the new start state** in union/concatenation/star constructions — the examiner expects to see a clearly identified $q_0$ added.
> 4. **Skipping the bidirectional proof** in closure — many students show only "$L(N) \subseteq L_1 \cup L_2$" or only the reverse. Both directions are required.
> 5. **Conflating closure with non-closure** — languages like $\{a^n b^n\}$ are **NOT** regular; do not claim they are.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ☐ **Closure under union, concatenation, Kleene star** → proved via **NFA + ε-transitions**.
- ☐ **Closure under intersection** → proved via **DFA product construction** ($Q_1 \times Q_2$, $F_1 \times F_2$).
- ☐ **Closure under complement** → **swap accept/non-accept states** of a complete DFA.
- ☐ **Closure under difference** → $L_1 - L_2 = L_1 \cap \overline{L_2}$.
- ☐ **Closure under reversal** → **reverse all transitions** of an NFA; swap start and accept.
- ☐ **Closure under homomorphism** → **substitute** $h(a)$ for each symbol $a$ in the regular expression.
- ☐ **Closure under inverse homomorphism** → simulate DFA on $h(a)$ for each input $a$.
- ☐ **Memorize the standard NFA constructions** for $L_1 \cup L_2$, $L_1 \cdot L_2$, $L^*$ — these appear in nearly every KTU exam.
- ☐ **Always make the DFA complete** (add trap state) before applying complement.
- ☐ **Regular languages are NOT closed** under operations like subset, superset, or "shuffle with a CFL."
- ☐ **Decision properties of regular languages** (emptiness, finiteness, membership, equivalence) are all **decidable** — use product DFA for equivalence.
- ☐ **For proving a language is NOT regular**, use the **Pumping Lemma for Regular Languages** (covered separately, but essential to contrast with closure proofs).
- ☐ **Symmetric difference** = $(L_1 \cap \overline{L_2}) \cup (\overline{L_1} \cap L_2)$ — also closed.
- ☐ **Right quotient** $L_1 / L_2 = \{x : \exists y \in L_2, xy \in L_1\}$ — closed for regular $L_2$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DFA State Minimization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET414 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** DFA State Minimization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DFA State Minimization** is the process of transforming a given Deterministic Finite Automaton $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ into an **equivalent minimal DFA** $M' = (Q', \Sigma, \delta', q_0, F')$ such that:
> 1. $L(M) = L(M')$
> 2. There is **no other DFA** with fewer states that accepts the same language
> 3. $Q'$ contains **no unreachable states** and **no pair of distinguishable-equivalent states** is split into different states

Formally, two states $p, q \in Q$ are said to be **equivalent (indistinguishable)** if for every input string $w \in \Sigma^*$:

$$\hat{\delta}(p, w) \in F \iff \hat{\delta}(q, w) \in F$$

If no such $w$ exists, the two states are called **distinguishable**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Redundant Office" Metaphor

Imagine a large company where several employees perform *exactly the same job*. A smart HR manager will merge their roles. **DFA State Minimization is the HR manager of automata**:
- **Unreachable states** = employees whose office door no one visits (no input sequence reaches them).
- **Equivalent states** = two employees who, given *any* task (string), will always produce the *same acceptance outcome*.

The minimal DFA is the *lean, optimized company* where every state has a unique behavioural role.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024

> [!NOTE]
> The minimal DFA for a regular language is **unique up to state renaming (isomorphism)**. This is the celebrated result linked to the **Myhill–Nerode Theorem**, which guarantees that for every regular language, there exists a *smallest* DFA, and that smallest DFA is essentially unique. The minimum number of states equals the number of **equivalence classes** of the Myhill–Nerode relation $\equiv_L$.

## 1.4 Mathematical Preliminaries

Let $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ be a DFA. Define the **distinguishability** relation $\not\equiv \subseteq Q \times Q$:

$$p \not\equiv q \iff \exists\, w \in \Sigma^* \text{ such that exactly one of } \hat{\delta}(p,w), \hat{\delta}(q,w) \text{ is in } F$$

The complement $\equiv$ is an **equivalence relation** (reflexive, symmetric, transitive), and the quotient $Q / \equiv$ gives the state set of the minimal DFA.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Two Pillars of DFA Minimization

| # | Concept | Formal Description | Algorithmic Role |
|---|---------|--------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Unreachability** | A state $q$ is unreachable if $\nexists\, w \in \Sigma^*$ with $\hat{\delta}(q_0, w) = q$ | Always eliminated first |
| 2 | **Equivalence ($\equiv$)** | $p \equiv q$ iff $\forall w \in \Sigma^*: \delta^*(p,w) \in F \leftrightarrow \delta^*(q,w) \in F$ | Merged via Table-Filling |
| 3 | **Distinguishability ($\not\equiv$)** | $p \not\equiv q$ iff $\exists\, w: \delta^*(p,w) \in F \oplus \delta^*(q,w) \in F$ | Forces separation |
| 4 | **Empty / Dead Language** | $\emptyset$ as a regular language is recognized by a 1-state DFA | Minimal DFA size = **1** |
| 5 | **Myhill–Nerode Index** | Number of distinct $\equiv_L$ classes = minimum number of DFA states | Lower bound theorem |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise the following consolidated table. **Avoid the pipe `|` character inside cells** — use $\vert$ or $\mid$ instead.

| Quantity / Property | Formula / Statement | Usage in Exam |
|---------------------|---------------------|---------------|
| Number of state pairs | $\binom{\vert Q \vert}{2} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | Size of distinguishability table |
| Time complexity of Table-Filling | $O(n^2 \cdot \vert\Sigma\vert)$ per iteration | Complexity analysis questions |
| Total time complexity | $O(n^2 \cdot \vert\Sigma\vert)$ after convergence | Bound derivation |
| Equivalence-class cardinality | $\vert Q \vert / \text{(merges performed)}$ | Final minimized DFA states |
| Myhill–Nerode lower bound | $\text{min states} \geq \text{number of } \equiv_L \text{ classes}$ | Proving minimality |
| Acceptance-preservation | $\delta^*(p, w) \in F \Leftrightarrow \delta^*(q, w) \in F$ | Definition of $\equiv$ |

## 2.3 The Myhill–Nerode Theorem (Most-Cited Result)

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem (Myhill–Nerode):** A language $L \subseteq \Sigma^*$ is regular **if and only if** the equivalence relation $\equiv_L$ defined by
> $$x \equiv_L y \iff \forall z \in \Sigma^*: (xz \in L \Leftrightarrow yz \in L)$$
> has a **finite number of equivalence classes**. Moreover, the minimum number of states in any DFA for $L$ equals the number of these classes.

This theorem is the **theoretical backbone** behind the uniqueness of the minimal DFA and is a frequent 7-mark question in KTU ESE.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

Minimal DFAs are deployed in:
- **Lexical analyzers** (compiler front-ends) to reduce memory footprint
- **Network intrusion detection** pattern matchers (Snort, Bro rules)
- **Hardware circuit synthesis** (state minimization in sequential logic)
- **Regular-expression engines** in text editors (grep, awk, sed)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Table-Filling Algorithm (Distinguishability Method)

### Algorithm 3.1 — Full Procedure

**Input:** A DFA $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ with no unreachable states.
**Output:** A partition of $Q$ into equivalence classes, yielding the minimal DFA.

**Step 1 — Removal of Unreachable States.**
Perform BFS/DFS from $q_0$; delete any state not visited.

**Step 2 — Initial Marking.**
For every pair $\{p, q\}$ with $p \neq q$, mark the pair if exactly one of $p, q$ is in $F$. This is the **0-distinguishability** step.

**Step 3 — Iterative Refinement.**
Repeat until no new marks:
For every *unmarked* pair $\{p, q\}$ and for every $a \in \Sigma$:
If $\{\delta(p, a), \delta(q, a)\}$ is already marked, then mark $\{p, q\}$.

**Step 4 — Equivalence Classes.**
All *unmarked* pairs are equivalent. Group them via transitive closure.

**Step 5 — Construction of Minimal DFA.**
Each equivalence class becomes a single state in $M'$. The start state is the class containing $q_0$; final states are classes intersecting $F$.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Complete Walkthrough

Consider the DFA $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, A, F)$ with:
- $Q = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$
- $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$
- $F = \{E, F\}$
- Transition table:

| State | $\delta(\cdot, 0)$ | $\delta(\cdot, 1)$ |
|:-----:|:------------------:|:------------------:|
| **A** | B | F |
| **B** | A | C |
| **C** | D | E |
| **D** | D | D |
| **E** | D | D |
| **F** | D | D |

### Step 1 — Reachability Analysis (BFS from A)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Level 0:} \quad & \{A\} \\
\text{Level 1:} \quad & \delta(A,0) = B,\ \delta(A,1) = F \Rightarrow \{B, F\} \\
\text{Level 2:} \quad & \delta(B,0)=A,\ \delta(B,1)=C,\ \delta(F,0)=D,\ \delta(F,1)=D \Rightarrow \{C, D\} \\
\text{Level 3:} \quad & \delta(C,0)=D,\ \delta(C,1)=E,\ \delta(D,0)=D,\ \delta(D,1)=D \Rightarrow \{E\} \\
\text{Level 4:} \quad & \delta(E,0)=D,\ \delta(E,1)=D \Rightarrow \{\}
\end{aligned}
$$

> All six states are reachable. No state is removed in Step 1.

### Step 2 — Initial 0-Distinguishability Table

We form an upper-triangular table of $\binom{6}{2} = 15$ pairs. Pairs containing one final and one non-final state are marked immediately with **X**.

| Pair | A | B | C | D | E | F |
|------|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| **A** | – |   |   |   | X | X |
| **B** |   | – |   |   | X | X |
| **C** |   |   | – |   | X | X |
| **D** |   |   |   | – | X | X |
| **E** | X | X | X | X | – |   |
| **F** | X | X | X | X |   | – |

### Step 3 — Iterative Marking Pass

We check each *currently unmarked* pair. A pair is marked if any input symbol leads to an *already marked* pair.

**Iteration 1 — Detailed Check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\{A,B\}: \quad & \delta(A,0)=B,\ \delta(B,0)=A \Rightarrow \{B,A\}=\{A,B\} \text{ (unmarked)} \\
             & \delta(A,1)=F,\ \delta(B,1)=C \Rightarrow \{F,C\}=\{C,F\} \text{ MARKED} \;\Rightarrow\; \{A,B\} \text{ marked} \\[4pt]
\{A,C\}: \quad & \delta(A,0)=B,\ \delta(C,0)=D \Rightarrow \{B,D\} \text{ (unmarked)} \\
             & \delta(A,1)=F,\ \delta(C,1)=E \Rightarrow \{E,F\} \text{ (unmarked)} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{not marked} \\[4pt]
\{A,D\}: \quad & \delta(A,0)=B,\ \delta(D,0)=D \Rightarrow \{B,D\} \text{ (unmarked)} \\
             & \delta(A,1)=F,\ \delta(D,1)=D \Rightarrow \{F,D\}=\{D,F\} \text{ MARKED} \;\Rightarrow\; \{A,D\} \text{ marked} \\[4pt]
\{B,C\}: \quad & \delta(B,0)=A,\ \delta(C,0)=D \Rightarrow \{A,D\} \text{ (now marked)} \;\Rightarrow\; \{B,C\} \text{ marked} \\[4pt]
\{B,D\}: \quad & \delta(B,0)=A,\ \delta(D,0)=D \Rightarrow \{A,D\} \text{ MARKED} \;\Rightarrow\; \{B,D\} \text{ marked} \\[4pt]
\{C,D\}: \quad & \delta(C,0)=D,\ \delta(D,0)=D \Rightarrow \{D,D\} \text{ (same state, never marked)} \\
             & \delta(C,1)=E,\ \delta(D,1)=D \Rightarrow \{E,D\}=\{D,E\} \text{ MARKED} \;\Rightarrow\; \{C,D\} \text{ marked} \\[4pt]
\{E,F\}: \quad & \delta(E,0)=D,\ \delta(F,0)=D \Rightarrow \{D,D\} \text{ (unmarked)} \\
             & \delta(E,1)=D,\ \delta(F,1)=D \Rightarrow \{D,D\} \text{ (unmarked)} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{NOT marked}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 2 — Re-check of $\{A, C\}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\{A,C\}: \quad & \delta(A,0)=B,\ \delta(C,0)=D \Rightarrow \{B,D\} \text{ (now marked in Iter 1)} \\
             & \Rightarrow \{A,C\} \text{ marked in this iteration}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Convergence — Table after all iterations:**

| Pair | A | B | C | D | E | F |
|------|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| **A** | – | **X** | **X** | **X** | X | X |
| **B** |   | – | **X** | **X** | X | X |
| **C** |   |   | – | **X** | X | X |
| **D** |   |   |   | – | X | X |
| **E** | X | X | X | X | – |   |
| **F** | X | X | X | X |   | – |

### Step 4 — Equivalence Class Identification

The **only unmarked pair** is $\{E, F\}$. By transitivity of $\equiv$:

$$[\{E, F\}] = \{E, F\}$$

All other states form **singletons**:
$$[A]=\{A\},\ [B]=\{B\},\ [C]=\{C\},\ [D]=\{D\},\ [EF]=\{E,F\}$$

### Step 5 — Construction of Minimal DFA $M'$

Let $Q' = \{A, B, C, D, EF\}$, with start state $A$ and final state $EF$.

| State | $\delta'(\cdot, 0)$ | $\delta'(\cdot, 1)$ |
|:-----:|:-------------------:|:-------------------:|
| **A**  | B  | EF |
| **B**  | A  | C  |
| **C**  | D  | EF |
| **D**  | D  | D  |
| **EF** | D  | D  |

**Verification of $L(M) = L(M')$:** The string `01` drives both automata to a final state. The string `10` is rejected in both. Any string ending in `0` reaches $D$ (dead) in both. Hence $L(M) = L(M')$, and the 5-state DFA $M'$ is the unique minimal DFA for the language.

### 3.3 Complexity Justification

Each iteration marks at least one new pair, so at most $\binom{n}{2}$ iterations. Each iteration scans $O(n^2)$ pairs and $\vert\Sigma\vert$ symbols, yielding:

$$T(n, \Sigma) = O\!\left(n^2 \cdot \vert\Sigma\vert\right)$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Original DFA $M$ — State Diagram (6 states)

```mermaid
graph LR
    sA((A)) -- "0" --> sB((B))
    sA((A)) -- "1" --> sF((F))
    sB((B)) -- "0" --> sA
    sB((B)) -- "1" --> sC((C))
    sC((C)) -- "0" --> sD((D))
    sC((C)) -- "1" --> sE((E))
    sD((D)) -- "0" --> sD
    sD((D)) -- "1" --> sD
    sE((E)) -- "0" --> sD
    sE((E)) -- "1" --> sD
    sF((F)) -- "0" --> sD
    sF((F)) -- "1" --> sD
    style sE fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style sF fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Legend:** Green-filled nodes are final states. Node $D$ is a **dead/trap state** (non-final, self-loops on every input).

## 4.2 Minimal DFA $M'$ — State Diagram (5 states)

```mermaid
graph LR
    sA((A)) -- "0" --> sB((B))
    sA((A)) -- "1" --> sEF((EF))
    sB((B)) -- "0" --> sA
    sB((B)) -- "1" --> sC((C))
    sC((C)) -- "0" --> sD((D))
    sC((C)) -- "1" --> sEF
    sD((D)) -- "0" --> sD
    sD((D)) -- "1" --> sD
    sEF((EF)) -- "0" --> sD
    sEF((EF)) -- "1" --> sD
    style sEF fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Key observation:** States $E$ and $F$ from the original DFA have been **merged** into a single state $EF$ because they are behaviourally indistinguishable — they have identical out-transitions and both are final.

## 4.3 Algorithm Flow — Table-Filling Procedure

```mermaid
graph TD
    sA["STEP 1: Remove unreachable states via BFS from start"]
    sB["STEP 2: Initialise distinguishability table"]
    sC["Mark all pairs (p,q) where p is final XOR q is final"]
    sD["STEP 3: Iterative refinement loop"]
    sE{"For every unmarked pair (p,q) and symbol a in Sigma: is delta-p-a, delta-q-a already marked?"}
    sF["Mark pair (p,q) as distinguishable"]
    sG["Leave pair (p,q) as candidate equivalent"]
    sH{"Any new marks added in this pass?"}
    sI["STEP 4: Group unmarked pairs by transitive closure"]
    sJ["STEP 5: Construct minimal DFA from equivalence classes"]
    sA --> sB --> sC --> sD --> sE
    sE -- "YES" --> sF --> sD
    sE -- "NO" --> sG --> sD
    sD --> sH
    sH -- "YES" --> sD
    sH -- "NO" --> sI --> sJ
```

## 4.4 Equivalence Class Mapping (Block Diagram)

```mermaid

    sOrig["Original Q = 6 states"]
    sA1["A"]
    sB1["B"]
    sC1["C"]
    sD1["D"]

    sF1["F"]
    sMin["Minimal Q' = 5 states"]
    sA2["A"]
    sB2["B"]
    sC2["C"]
    sD2["D"]
    sEF2["EF"]
    sOrig --> sA1
    sOrig --> sB1
    sOrig --> sC1
    sOrig --> sD1
    sOrig --> sE1
    sOrig --> sF1

    sB1 --> sB2

    sD1 --> sD2
    sE1 --> sEF2
    sF1 --> sEF2
    style sEF2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
```
