<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define DFA State Minimization. Why is the minimal DFA unique? `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> **DFA State Minimization** is the process of converting a given DFA $M$ into an equivalent DFA $M'$ with the **fewest possible states** such that $L(M) = L(M')$.
>
> **Uniqueness:** By the **Myhill–Nerode Theorem**, every regular language $L$ induces an equivalence relation $\equiv_L$ with a finite number of equivalence classes. The minimal DFA's states biject with these classes. Since the equivalence classes are *intrinsic to the language* and independent of any particular construction, the minimal DFA is **unique up to isomorphism** (i.e., renaming of states). Any two minimal DFAs differ only in the names assigned to their states, not in the underlying state-transition structure. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. What is meant by two DFA states being *distinguishable*? Give one example. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

> Two states $p$ and $q$ of a DFA are said to be **distinguishable** if there exists at least one input string $w \in \Sigma^*$ such that *exactly one* of $\hat{\delta}(p, w)$ and $\hat{\delta}(q, w)$ is an accepting (final) state. Formally:
> $$p \not\equiv q \iff \exists w \in \Sigma^*: \mathbf{1}_F(\hat{\delta}(p,w)) \neq \mathbf{1}_F(\hat{\delta}(q,w))$$
> where $\mathbf{1}_F$ is the indicator function of $F$.
>
> **Example:** In a DFA with start $A$ and $F = \{C\}$, consider the pair $(A, B)$ where $\delta(A, 1) = C \in F$ and $\delta(B, 1) = D \notin F$. The string $w = 1$ distinguishes $A$ and $B$. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Answer **any ONE** of the two choices. Each question has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Show *all* steps for full credit.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Table-Filling Minimization**

**(a) Explain the concept of state equivalence in a DFA. Describe the Table-Filling algorithm with its key steps. `[7 marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Model Answer:**

Two states $p, q$ of a DFA are **equivalent** ($p \equiv q$) if for *every* string $w \in \Sigma^*$, both states transition to either final or non-final states simultaneously. Equivalently, no string distinguishes them.

The **Table-Filling Algorithm** proceeds in these steps:

1. **Eliminate unreachable states** using BFS/DFS from $q_0$.
2. **Construct an upper-triangular table** of all $\binom{n}{2}$ state pairs.
3. **Initial pass:** Mark every pair $\{p, q\}$ where one state is in $F$ and the other is not (0-distinguishable).
4. **Iterative refinement:** For every *unmarked* pair $\{p, q\}$, if there exists some input $a \in \Sigma$ such that $\{\delta(p, a), \delta(q, a)\}$ is *already marked*, then mark $\{p, q\}$.
5. **Repeat** Step 4 until no new marks appear in a full pass.
6. **Equivalence classes:** All *unmarked* pairs are equivalent; form classes via transitive closure.
7. **Build minimal DFA:** Each class becomes one state. The start state is the class containing $q_0$; final states are classes intersecting $F$. Transitions are inherited from the original DFA.

The algorithm runs in $O(n^2 \cdot \vert\Sigma\vert)$ time. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Minimize the following DFA using the Table-Filling algorithm. Show all iterations and the final minimal DFA transition table. `[7 marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

Given DFA $M$:

| State | 0 | 1 |
|:-----:|:-:|:-:|
| → A   | B | F |
| B     | A | C |
| C     | D | E |
| D     | D | D |
| ★ E   | D | D |
| ★ F   | D | D |

(★ = final; → = start)

**Model Solution:**

**Reachability:** All 6 states are reachable from $A$ (BFS shown in Section 3.2). No state is removed.

**Initial Table** (15 pairs, only final-vs-non-final pairs marked):

|      | A | B | C | D | E | F |
|------|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| **A** | – |   |   |   | X | X |
| **B** |   | – |   |   | X | X |
| **C** |   |   | – |   | X | X |
| **D** |   |   |   | – | X | X |
| **E** | X | X | X | X | – |   |
| **F** | X | X | X | X |   | – |

**Iteration 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\{A,B\}: & \delta(A,1)=F,\ \delta(B,1)=C \Rightarrow \{C,F\} \text{ marked} \Rightarrow \text{mark } \{A,B\} \\
\{A,C\}: & \delta(A,0)=B,\ \delta(C,0)=D \Rightarrow \{B,D\} \text{ unmarked}; \\
         & \delta(A,1)=F,\ \delta(C,1)=E \Rightarrow \{E,F\} \text{ unmarked} \Rightarrow \text{skip} \\
\{A,D\}: & \delta(A,1)=F,\ \delta(D,1)=D \Rightarrow \{D,F\} \text{ marked} \Rightarrow \text{mark } \{A,D\} \\
\{B,C\}: & \delta(B,0)=A,\ \delta(C,0)=D \Rightarrow \{A,D\} \text{ marked} \Rightarrow \text{mark } \{B,C\} \\
\{B,D\}: & \delta(B,0)=A,\ \delta(D,0)=D \Rightarrow \{A,D\} \text{ marked} \Rightarrow \text{mark } \{B,D\} \\
\{C,D\}: & \delta(C,1)=E,\ \delta(D,1)=D \Rightarrow \{D,E\} \text{ marked} \Rightarrow \text{mark } \{C,D\} \\
\{E,F\}: & \delta(E,0)=\delta(F,0)=D,\ \delta(E,1)=\delta(F,1)=D \Rightarrow \text{NOT marked}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 2 — Re-check $\{A, C\}$:**

$\delta(A,0)=B,\ \delta(C,0)=D \Rightarrow \{B,D\}$ *now marked* $\Rightarrow$ mark $\{A, C\}$.

**Final Table:**

|      | A | B | C | D | E | F |
|------|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| **A** | – | X | X | X | X | X |
| **B** |   | – | X | X | X | X |
| **C** |   |   | – | X | X | X |
| **D** |   |   |   | – | X | X |
| **E** | X | X | X | X | – |   |
| **F** | X | X | X | X |   | – |

**Equivalence Classes:** $\{A\}, \{B\}, \{C\}, \{D\}, \{E, F\}$

**Minimal DFA $M'$:**

| State | 0 | 1 |
|:-----:|:-:|:-:|
| → A   | B | EF |
| B     | A | C  |
| C     | D | EF |
| D     | D | D  |
| ★ EF  | D | D  |

**Minimal DFA has 5 states** (reduction from 6 to 5). **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Myhill–Nerode & Construction + Minimization**

**(a) State and prove the Myhill–Nerode Theorem. Explain its significance in DFA minimization. `[7 marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Model Answer:**

> **Theorem (Myhill–Nerode):** Let $L \subseteq \Sigma^*$ be a language. Define the relation $\equiv_L$ on $\Sigma^*$ by
> $$x \equiv_L y \iff \forall z \in \Sigma^*: (xz \in L \Leftrightarrow yz \in L)$$
> Then $L$ is regular if and only if $\equiv_L$ has a **finite** number of equivalence classes. Moreover, the **minimum number of states** of any DFA accepting $L$ equals the number of equivalence classes of $\equiv_L$.

**Proof Sketch:**

*($\Leftarrow$)* If $\equiv_L$ has $k$ classes $[x_1], [x_2], \ldots, [x_k]$, construct a DFA with these classes as states. Set start state $= [\varepsilon]$. For each class $[x_i]$ and symbol $a \in \Sigma$, define $\delta([x_i], a) = [x_i \cdot a]$. The class $[x_i]$ is final iff $x_i \in L$. This DFA accepts $L$ and has $k$ states.

*($\Rightarrow$)* If a DFA with $n$ states accepts $L$, then for any $x, y \in \Sigma^*$, if $\hat{\delta}(q_0, x) = \hat{\delta}(q_0, y)$ then $x \equiv_L y$. Thus $\equiv_L$ has at most $n$ classes.

**Significance:** The theorem guarantees (i) existence and (ii) **uniqueness** (up to isomorphism) of the minimal DFA. It also provides a *test for regularity*: if $\equiv_L$ has infinitely many classes, then $L$ is not regular. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Construct a DFA for the language $L = \{w \in \{0,1\}^* \mid w \text{ contains the substring } 01\}$, then minimize it. `[7 marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Construction of DFA $M$:**

We track the longest suffix of the input seen so far that is also a prefix of the target substring "01".

- State $q_0$: No relevant suffix (start).
- State $q_1$: Last symbol read was `0` (suffix "0").
- State $q_2$: Substring `01` detected (final, absorbing).

| State | 0 | 1 |
|:-----:|:-:|:-:|
| → $q_0$ | $q_1$ | $q_0$ |
| $q_1$   | $q_1$ | $q_2$ |
| ★ $q_2$ | $q_2$ | $q_2$ |

**Step 2 — Reachability Check:** All 3 states are reachable from $q_0$.

**Step 3 — Table-Filling:**

$\binom{3}{2} = 3$ pairs.

**Initial:** $\{q_0, q_2\}$ marked ($q_0$ non-final, $q_2$ final); $\{q_1, q_2\}$ marked (same reason). $\{q_0, q_1\}$ unmarked.

**Iteration 1:** Check $\{q_0, q_1\}$:
- On input $0$: $\delta(q_0, 0) = q_1$, $\delta(q_1, 0) = q_1 \Rightarrow \{q_1, q_1\}$ (same, unmarked).
- On input $1$: $\delta(q_0, 1) = q_0$, $\delta(q_1, 1) = q_2 \Rightarrow \{q_0, q_2\}$ **marked** $\Rightarrow$ mark $\{q_0, q_1\}$.

**Final Table:** All pairs marked — no merging possible.

**Step 4 — Minimal DFA $M'$:**

$M'$ has 3 states and is *already minimal*.

| State | 0 | 1 |
|:-----:|:-:|:-:|
| → $q_0$ | $q_1$ | $q_0$ |
| $q_1$   | $q_1$ | $q_2$ |
| ★ $q_2$ | $q_2$ | $q_2$ |

The Myhill–Nerode classes for $L$ are:
- $[\varepsilon]$: strings whose suffixes never include `01` (e.g., $\varepsilon, 1, 11, 111$)
- $[0]$: strings ending in $0$ (e.g., $0, 00, 10, 110$)
- $[01]$: strings containing $01$ (the "accept" class)

These three classes confirm minimality. **(7 marks)**

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in DFA Minimization (read carefully!):**
> 1. **Forgetting Step 1 — Unreachability.** Many students directly start the table-filling without first eliminating unreachable states. *Penalty: up to 2 marks deducted.*
> 2. **Asymmetric pair marking.** A pair $\{p, q\}$ is *ordered* only for finding transitions, but the table entry is *symmetric*. Marking only one half causes logic errors.
> 3. **Missing the iterative convergence.** Stopping after *one* pass and declaring "no marks possible" is a common blunder. Always re-scan the table until a complete pass produces **zero new marks**.
> 4. **Forgetting to merge the *start* state** with another state when they turn out to be equivalent. The merged class becomes the new start state.
> 5. **Not handling the dead/trap state.** A non-final state with all self-loops is *not necessarily* unreachable and must still participate in the table.
> 6. **Miscounting states in the final answer.** Always cross-check: $\text{(number of equivalence classes)} = \text{(number of rows in minimal transition table)}$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Goal of DFA Minimization:** Find the *smallest* DFA accepting the same language, with **no unreachable** and **no equivalent** states.
- **Two-Phase Strategy:** (i) remove unreachable states via BFS/DFS, (ii) merge equivalent states via the Table-Filling algorithm.
- **Distinguishability is existential** ($\exists w$), **equivalence is universal** ($\forall w$).
- **Pair count:** $\binom{n}{2} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ — useful for exam-time validation of table size.
- **Algorithm complexity:** $O(n^2 \cdot \vert\Sigma\vert)$.
- **Myhill–Nerode Theorem:** The minimal DFA is *unique up to isomorphism*; its states biject with $\equiv_L$-classes.
- **Final-state rule:** A pair $\{p, q\}$ is *always* distinguishable if $p \in F \oplus q \in F$ (initial mark).
- **Convergence:** Repeat table-filling iterations until one full pass produces no new marks.
- **Equivalence classes via transitive closure:** When $\{A, B\}$ and $\{B, C\}$ are unmarked, then $A \equiv B \equiv C$ and they merge into a single class.
- **Practical use:** Lexical analyzers, hardware synthesis, network pattern matchers.
- **Pitfall to avoid:** A "trap state" (dead state with self-loops) is *not* equivalent to a final state under any circumstances.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Context Free Grammar (CFG) - Representation of Context Free Languages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET414 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Context Free Grammar (CFG) - Representation of Context Free Languages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Context Free Grammar (CFG)

A **Context Free Grammar (CFG)** is a formal mathematical tool used to describe the structure of a language by specifying how strings can be generated through substitution rules. In the KTU 2024 syllabus, a CFG is defined as a quadruple (4-tuple) consisting of four essential components.

Let us denote a Context Free Grammar by the symbol **G**. Formally, a CFG is defined as:

$$G = (V, T, P, S)$$

Where each component has a specific role:

- **V** → A finite, non-empty set of **Variables** (also called **Non-Terminals** or **Syntactic Categories**). These are symbols that act as placeholders and can be replaced.
- **T** → A finite, non-empty set of **Terminals** (the actual alphabet of the language). These symbols cannot be replaced further.
- **P** → A finite set of **Production Rules** (also called **Productions** or **Rewrite Rules**). Each rule is of the form $A \rightarrow \alpha$, where $A \in V$ and $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^{*}$.
- **S** → A distinguished **Start Symbol** (also called the **Sentence Symbol**), such that $S \in V$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition (Must Memorize):**
> A Context Free Grammar **G** is a 4-tuple $G = (V, T, P, S)$ where $V$ and $T$ are disjoint finite sets of variables and terminals respectively, $P$ is a finite set of productions of the form $A \rightarrow \alpha$ with $A \in V$ and $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^{*}$, and $S \in V$ is the designated start symbol.

## 1.2 The Key Constraint: Why is it "Context-Free"?

A grammar is called **Context-Free** because every production rule has exactly **one variable on the Left-Hand Side (LHS)**, with no surrounding context. The variable $A$ in the rule $A \rightarrow \alpha$ can be replaced by $\alpha$ **regardless of the surrounding symbols**. This is the defining property of a CFG.

Compare this with a **Context-Sensitive Grammar**, where rules can have the form $\alpha A \beta \rightarrow \alpha \gamma \beta$ (the replacement of $A$ depends on its context $\alpha, \beta$).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Recipe for a Sentence"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Recipe Book for Sentences**
>
> Imagine you want to generate valid English sentences without knowing the language. You are given a "recipe book" with the following rules:
>
> - A `<Sentence>` is made of a `<NounPhrase>` followed by a `<VerbPhrase>`.
> - A `<NounPhrase>` is an `<Article>` followed by a `<Noun>`.
> - A `<VerbPhrase>` is a `<Verb>` followed by a `<NounPhrase>`.
> - An `<Article>` can be "the" or "a".
> - A `<Noun>` can be "cat" or "dog".
> - A `<Verb>` can be "chases" or "sees".
>
> If you start with `<Sentence>` and keep applying these substitution rules, you can generate sentences like "the cat chases a dog" or "a dog sees the cat". This is **exactly how a CFG works**: variables are like placeholders, terminals are the actual words, productions are the substitution rules, and the start symbol is the entry point.

In this analogy:
- **`<Sentence>`, `<NounPhrase>`, etc.** are the **Variables** (V).
- **"the", "a", "cat", "dog", "chases", "sees"** are the **Terminals** (T).
- **The recipe rules** are the **Productions** (P).
- **`<Sentence>`** is the **Start Symbol** (S).

## 1.4 Representation of Context Free Languages (CFL)

A **Context Free Language (CFL)** is a language that can be generated by some Context Free Grammar. The language generated by a grammar $G$ is the set of all terminal strings (strings containing only terminals) that can be derived from the start symbol $S$.

The language generated by CFG $G$, denoted as $L(G)$, is formally defined as:

$$L(G) = \{ w \in T^{*} \mid S \Rightarrow^{*} w \}$$

Where $\Rightarrow^{*}$ denotes "derives in zero or more steps". This means a string $w$ belongs to $L(G)$ if and only if we can start from the start symbol $S$ and, through a finite sequence of production rule applications, reach the string $w$ which contains only terminal symbols.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every Regular Language is a Context Free Language, but the converse is NOT true.** That is, Regular Languages $\subset$ Context Free Languages. For example, $L = \{a^{n}b^{n} \mid n \geq 1\}$ is a CFL but NOT a regular language.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Components of a CFG — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Variables (V) — The Non-Terminals

Variables represent syntactic categories or intermediate symbols that need to be expanded. They are typically denoted by uppercase English letters like $A, B, C, S$ or descriptive names enclosed in angle brackets like `<Expression>`, `<Statement>`.

**Key Properties:**
- Variables are also called **Non-Terminal Symbols** because they do not appear in the final generated string.
- $V$ and $T$ must be **disjoint sets**: $V \cap T = \emptyset$.
- The start symbol $S$ must belong to $V$.

### 2.1.2 Terminals (T) — The Alphabet

Terminals are the fundamental symbols (atomic units) that form the strings of the language. They cannot be further expanded or broken down. Common notations include lowercase English letters $a, b, c$, digits $0, 1, \ldots, 9$, and special symbols.

**Key Properties:**
- Terminals appear in the final derived string.
- They form the alphabet over which the language $L(G)$ is defined.

### 2.1.3 Production Rules (P) — The Heart of the Grammar

A production rule has the form:

$$A \rightarrow \alpha$$

Where $A \in V$ (a single variable on the LHS) and $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^{*}$ (any string of variables and terminals, including the empty string $\epsilon$).

**Types of Production Rules in a CFG:**

| Notation | Meaning | Example |
|----------|---------|---------|
| $A \rightarrow \alpha$ | $A$ can be replaced by $\alpha$ | $E \rightarrow E + E$ |
| $A \rightarrow \epsilon$ | $A$ can be replaced by empty string (nullable) | $A \rightarrow \epsilon$ |
| $A \rightarrow \alpha \mid \beta$ | Multiple alternatives for $A$ (shorthand) | $A \rightarrow aB \mid bA$ |

### 2.1.4 Start Symbol (S) — The Genesis

The start symbol is a special variable from which all derivations begin. It is the "root" of every parse tree generated by the grammar.

## 2.2 Derivations — The Process of Generating Strings

A **derivation** is a sequence of replacements starting from $S$ and ending at a string of terminals. There are two principal types of derivations:

### 2.2.1 Leftmost Derivation (LMD)

In a leftmost derivation, at each step, we replace the **leftmost variable** in the current sentential form.

**Notation:** $S \Rightarrow_{lm} w$ means $w$ is derived from $S$ using leftmost derivations.

### 2.2.2 Rightmost Derivation (RMD)

In a rightmost derivation, at each step, we replace the **rightmost variable** in the current sentential form. This is also called **Canonical Derivation**.

**Notation:** $S \Rightarrow_{rm} w$ means $w$ is derived from $S$ using rightmost derivations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Fact:** Every string $w \in L(G)$ has at least one leftmost derivation and at least one rightmost derivation, but a string may have **multiple different leftmost derivations** if the grammar is ambiguous.

### 2.2.3 Sentential Form

A **sentential form** is any string derivable from the start symbol $S$, i.e., any string $\alpha$ such that $S \Rightarrow^{*} \alpha$. If $\alpha$ contains only terminals, it is called a **sentence** (and is in $L(G)$).

## 2.3 Parse Trees (Derivation Trees) — A Pictorial Representation

A **Parse Tree** is a tree representation of a derivation that filters out the order in which productions were applied. It shows the hierarchical structure of the derived string.

**Properties of a Parse Tree:**
- The **root** is labeled with the start symbol $S$.
- Each **internal node** is labeled with a variable.
- Each **leaf** is labeled with a terminal, a variable, or $\epsilon$.
- The **yield** (concatenation of leaves, read left to right) is the derived string.
- If a node labeled $A$ has children $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$, then there must be a production $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \ldots X_n$.

## 2.4 Ambiguity in a CFG

A CFG $G$ is said to be **ambiguous** if there exists at least one string $w \in L(G)$ that has **more than one distinct parse tree** (equivalently, more than one distinct leftmost derivation, or more than one distinct rightmost derivation).

A CFG is **unambiguous** if every string in $L(G)$ has exactly one parse tree.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Fact:** There is no general algorithm to determine whether an arbitrary CFG is ambiguous. This problem is **undecidable**.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Concept | Formal Definition / Formula | Significance in CFG |
|------------------|------------------------------|---------------------|
| CFG Definition | $G = (V, T, P, S)$ | 4-tuple representation |
| Production Rule | $A \rightarrow \alpha$, where $A \in V$, $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^{*}$ | Atomic substitution unit |
| Language Generated | $L(G) = \{w \in T^{*} \mid S \Rightarrow^{*} w\}$ | Set of all terminal derivable strings |
| Derivation Relation | $\Rightarrow$ (one step), $\Rightarrow^{*}$ (zero or more), $\Rightarrow^{+}$ (one or more) | Generates sentential forms |
| Leftmost Derivation | Replace leftmost variable at each step | Used in LL parsers |
| Rightmost Derivation | Replace rightmost variable at each step | Used in LR parsers (bottom-up) |
| Parse Tree | Tree where root $= S$, leaves $=$ yield string | Pictorial structure of derivation |
| Ambiguous Grammar | $\exists w \in L(G)$ with $> 1$ parse tree | No algorithm to detect |
| Chomsky Hierarchy | Regular $\subset$ CFG, CFG $\subset$ CSG | CFG is more powerful than Regular |
| Nullable Variable | $A \Rightarrow^{*} \epsilon$ | Variable that can derive empty string |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of CFG

Context Free Grammars have profound applications in Computer Science and Engineering:

1. **Programming Language Compilers** — CFGs are used to define the **syntax of programming languages** (e.g., C, Java, Python parsers use CFG-based parsers like YACC/Bison).
2. **Natural Language Processing (NLP)** — CFGs model the structure of natural languages for parsing sentences.
3. **XML/HTML Parsing** — Document Type Definitions (DTDs) are essentially CFGs.
4. **Software Engineering** — Used in formal verification, model checking, and software specification.
5. **Bioinformatics** — Modeling RNA secondary structure and DNA sequences.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Worked Example 1: A Simple Arithmetic Expression Grammar

Let us consider the CFG that generates simple arithmetic expressions involving addition and multiplication. The grammar is defined as:

$$G_1 = (\{E\}, \{+, *, (, ), a\}, P, E)$$

Where the set of productions $P$ consists of:

$$E \rightarrow E + E \quad \text{(Production 1)}$$
$$E \rightarrow E * E \quad \text{(Production 2)}$$
$$E \rightarrow (E) \quad \text{(Production 3)}$$
$$E \rightarrow a \quad \text{(Production 4)}$$

Here, $V = \{E\}$, $T = \{+, *, (, ), a\}$, $S = E$.

### 3.1.1 Leftmost Derivation of the string $w = a + a * a$

We will now generate the string $w = a + a * a$ using a leftmost derivation. At each step, we identify the leftmost variable and replace it using a production rule.

**Step 0:** Start with the start symbol.
$$\text{Current sentential form: } E$$

**Step 1:** Apply Production 1: $E \rightarrow E + E$. We replace the leftmost (and only) variable $E$.
$$E \Rightarrow E + E$$
*Explanation:* The leftmost variable is $E$. We apply the production $E \rightarrow E + E$ since we want to generate the $+$ operator.

**Step 2:** Apply Production 4: $E \rightarrow a$ to the leftmost $E$.
$$E + E \Rightarrow a + E$$
*Explanation:* The leftmost variable is the first $E$ in $E + E$. We replace it with the terminal $a$ since the string starts with $a$.

**Step 3:** Apply Production 1: $E \rightarrow E + E$ to the middle $E$.
$$a + E \Rightarrow a + E + E$$
*Explanation:* Hmm, this would not generate $a*a$ at the end. Let us reconsider. Actually, the next $E$ should be replaced to eventually produce $a * a$. Let us restart with a better strategy.

**Corrected Step 3:** Apply Production 2: $E \rightarrow E * E$ to the middle $E$.
$$a + E \Rightarrow a + E * E$$
*Explanation:* Since we need to produce $a * a$ after the $+$ sign, we apply the multiplication production.

**Step 4:** Apply Production 4: $E \rightarrow a$ to the leftmost $E$ in $E * E$.
$$a + E * E \Rightarrow a + a * E$$
*Explanation:* The leftmost variable is the $E$ in the multiplication. We replace it with $a$.

**Step 5:** Apply Production 4: $E \rightarrow a$ to the remaining $E$.
$$a + a * E \Rightarrow a + a * a$$
*Explanation:* The only remaining variable is the last $E$, which we replace with $a$.

**Final Result:** We have successfully derived $w = a + a * a$ from the start symbol $E$. Since $w \in T^{*}$, we conclude $w \in L(G_1)$.

### 3.1.2 Rightmost Derivation of the same string $w = a + a * a$

**Step 0:** Start with $E$.

**Step 1:** Apply $E \rightarrow E + E$ (replacing the only variable).
$$E \Rightarrow E + E$$

**Step 2:** Apply $E \rightarrow E * E$ to the rightmost $E$.
$$E + E \Rightarrow E + E * E$$

**Step 3:** Apply $E \rightarrow a$ to the rightmost $E$.
$$E + E * E \Rightarrow E + E * a$$

**Step 4:** Apply $E \rightarrow a$ to the middle $E$.
$$E + E * a \Rightarrow E + a * a$$

**Step 5:** Apply $E \rightarrow a$ to the leftmost $E$.
$$E + a * a \Rightarrow a + a * a$$

**Final Result:** The string $w = a + a * a$ is derived.

### 3.1.3 Demonstration of Ambiguity

Consider the string $w = a + a + a$. We can derive this string in **two distinct ways**:

**Derivation 1 (using $E \rightarrow E + E$ then $E \rightarrow E + E$ on left):**
$$E \Rightarrow E + E \Rightarrow E + E + E \Rightarrow a + E + E \Rightarrow a + a + E \Rightarrow a + a + a$$

**Derivation 2 (using $E \rightarrow E + E$ then $E \rightarrow E + E$ on right):**
$$E \Rightarrow E + E \Rightarrow E + E + E \Rightarrow E + E + a \Rightarrow E + a + a \Rightarrow a + a + a$$

These two derivations produce **two different parse trees**, demonstrating that the grammar $G_1$ is **ambiguous**.

## 3.2 Detailed Worked Example 2: Balanced Parentheses Grammar

The CFG that generates all strings of **balanced parentheses** is:

$$G_2 = (\{S\}, \{(, )\}, P, S)$$

With productions:
$$S \rightarrow SS \quad (P1)$$
$$S \rightarrow (S) \quad (P2)$$
$$S \rightarrow \epsilon \quad (P3)$$

### 3.2.1 Derivation of $w = (()())$

**Step 1:** $S \Rightarrow SS$ (Apply P1)
*Explanation:* Split into two parts, expecting to generate $()$ and $()$ from each $S$.

**Step 2:** $SS \Rightarrow (S)S$ (Apply P2 to left $S$)
*Explanation:* The left $S$ generates $(S)$, with the inner $S$ to be expanded further.

**Step 3:** $(S)S \Rightarrow (() )S$ (Apply P2 to inner $S$ on left)
*Explanation:* The inner $S$ of $(S)$ generates another $(S)$, which becomes $()$.

**Step 4:** $(() )S \Rightarrow (() )()$ (Apply P2 to right $S$)
*Explanation:* The right $S$ generates $(S)$, where the inner $S$ becomes $\epsilon$.

**Step 5:** $(() )() \Rightarrow (()())$ (Apply P3 to innermost $S$)
*Explanation:* The innermost $S$ becomes $\epsilon$, collapsing the extra parentheses.

**Final Result:** $w = (()())$ is derived.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Step-by-Step Parse Tree Construction

For the string $w = a * (a + a)$ in grammar $G_1$, the parse tree is constructed as follows:

| Level | Node Label | Children | Production Applied | Explanation |
|-------|------------|----------|---------------------|-------------|
| 0 (Root) | $E$ | $E$, $*$, $E$ | $E \rightarrow E * E$ | The whole expression is a multiplication |
| 1 (Left subtree) | $E$ | $a$ | $E \rightarrow a$ | Left operand is $a$ |
| 1 (Middle) | $*$ | (leaf) | — | Multiplication operator |
| 1 (Right subtree) | $E$ | $($ , $E$, $)$ | $E \rightarrow (E)$ | Right operand is parenthesized |
| 2 (Inside) | $E$ | $E$, $+$, $E$ | $E \rightarrow E + E$ | Inner expression is addition |
| 2 (Left paren) | $($ | (leaf) | — | Opening parenthesis |
| 2 (Right paren) | $)$ | (leaf) | — | Closing parenthesis |
| 3 (Left of $+$) | $E$ | $a$ | $E \rightarrow a$ | First $a$ |
| 3 (Plus) | $+$ | (leaf) | — | Addition operator |
| 3 (Right of $+$) | $E$ | $a$ | $E \rightarrow a$ | Second $a$ |

The **yield** (reading leaves left to right) is: $a * (a + a)$, which matches the target string.

## 3.4 Detailed Example: CFG for $L = \{a^{n}b^{n} \mid n \geq 1\}$

This is the **classic non-regular but context-free language**. The CFG is:

$$G_3 = (\{S\}, \{a, b\}, P, S)$$

With productions:
$$S \rightarrow aSb \quad (P1)$$
$$S \rightarrow ab \quad (P2)$$

### 3.4.1 Derivation of $w = aaabbb$

**Step 1:** $S \Rightarrow aSb$ (Apply P1)
*Explanation:* Begin to generate the recursive structure.

**Step 2:** $aSb \Rightarrow aaSbb$ (Apply P1)
*Explanation:* Add another layer of recursion.

**Step 3:** $aaSbb \Rightarrow aaaSbbb$ (Apply P1)
*Explanation:* Add one more layer of recursion.

**Step 4:** $aaaSbbb \Rightarrow aaaabbb$ (Apply P2, replacing $S$ with $ab$)
*Explanation:* Terminate the recursion with the base case.

**Final Result:** The string $w = a^{3}b^{3}$ is derived in 4 steps, confirming $L(G_3) = \{a^{n}b^{n} \mid n \geq 1\}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of the language:** The number of $a$'s and $b$'s must be equal, and all $a$'s must precede all $b$'s. The base case $P2$ generates $n=1$ (i.e., $ab$), and the recursive case $P1$ increments $n$ by 1, generating $a^{n}Sb^{n}$ from $a^{n-1}Sb^{n-1}$.

## 3.5 Exhaustive Derivation of $L = \{ww^{R} \mid w \in \{a, b\}^{*}\}$ (Palindromes)

A CFG for generating even-length palindromes over $\{a, b\}$ is:

$$G_4 = (\{S\}, \{a, b\}, P, S)$$

With productions:
$$S \rightarrow aSa \quad (P1)$$
$$S \rightarrow bSb \quad (P2)$$
$$S \rightarrow \epsilon \quad (P3)$$

**Derivation of $w = abba$:**

**Step 1:** $S \Rightarrow aSa$ (Apply P1)
*Explanation:* The string starts and ends with $a$.

**Step 2:** $aSa \Rightarrow abSba$ (Apply P2)
*Explanation:* Inside, the string starts and ends with $b$.

**Step 3:** $abSba \Rightarrow abba$ (Apply P3, replacing $S$ with $\epsilon$)
*Explanation:* The base case generates the empty string in the middle.

**Final Result:** $w = abba$ is generated, which is indeed a palindrome.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: CFG Generation Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following diagram illustrates the high-level functional architecture of how a CFG operates to generate strings in a Context Free Language.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start Symbol S"] --> B["Sentential Form Stage 1"]
    B --> C["Apply Production Rule"]
    C --> D{"Variable Exists?"}
    D -- Yes --> E["Select Variable to Replace"]
    E --> F["Apply Matching Production"]
    F --> G["New Sentential Form"]
    G --> D
    D -- No --> H["All Terminals"]
    H --> I["String w in L of G"]
    H -- "Multiple Trees" --> J["Ambiguity Check"]
    J -- "More than 1" --> K["Grammar is Ambiguous"]
    J -- "Exactly 1" --> L["Grammar is Unambiguous"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Parse Tree for $w = a + a * a$ in Grammar $G_1$

The parse tree below visualizes the hierarchical structure of the derivation. Note the use of purely alphanumeric node IDs prefixed with letters.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeRoot["E"] --> nodeLeft["E"]
    nodeRoot --> nodePlus["PLUS"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeRight["E"]
    nodeLeft --> leafA1["a"]
    nodeRight --> nodeRightE1["E"]
    nodeRight --> nodeMult["MUL"]
    nodeRight --> nodeRightE2["E"]
    nodeRightE1 --> leafA2["a"]
    nodeRightE2 --> leafA3["a"]
```

**Reading the Tree:**
- **Root** (top): The start symbol $E$.
- **Level 1 (children of root):** $E$, $+$, $E$ — this matches the production $E \rightarrow E + E$.
- **Level 2:** The right $E$ is expanded via $E \rightarrow E * E$, giving $E$, $*$, $E$.
- **Level 3 (leaves):** All leaves are terminals: $a, +, a, *, a$.
- **Yield (left-to-right leaf concatenation):** $a + a * a$ ✓

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: CFG Components and Their Interactions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V["Set V Variables"] -->|"Used in LHS of rules"| P["Productions P"]
    T["Set T Terminals"] -->|"Used in RHS of rules"| P
    S["Start Symbol S"] -->|"Distinguished element"| V
    P -->|"Derivation Process"| DF["Sentential Forms"]
    DF -->|"After all variables replaced"| L["Language L of G"]
    DF -->|"Tree visualization"| PT["Parse Tree"]
    PT -->|"Uniqueness Check"| AMB["Ambiguity Detector"]
    AMB -->|"Single tree per string"| U["Unambiguous"]
    AMB -->|"Multiple trees per string"| A["Ambiguous"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Two Parse Trees for $a + a + a$ (Proving Ambiguity)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    %% First parse tree (left-grouped)
    T1["TREE 1: Left Associative"] --> S1["E"]
    S1 --> S1L["E"]
    S1 --> P1["PLUS"]
    S1 --> S1R["E"]
    S1L --> S1LL["E"]
    S1L --> P2["PLUS"]
    S1L --> S1LR["a"]
    S1LL --> LA1["a"]
    S1R --> LA2["a"]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    %% Second parse tree (right-grouped)
    T2["TREE 2: Right Associative"] --> S2["E"]
    S2 --> S2L["E"]
    S2 --> P3["PLUS"]
    S2 --> S2R["E"]
    S2L --> LA3["a"]
    S2R --> S2RL["E"]
    S2R --> P4["PLUS"]
    S2R --> S2RR["E"]
    S2RL --> LA4["a"]
    S2RR --> LA5["a"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation of Ambiguity Diagram:** Since the same string $a + a + a$ can be generated by **two structurally different parse trees** in grammar $G_1$, the grammar is **ambiguous**. Note that the string itself is unambiguous in meaning (it equals $a+a+a$ in any order), but the grammar provides multiple ways to parse it.

## 4.5 Block Diagram: CFG in Compiler Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SRC["Source Code Input"] --> LEX["Lexical Analyzer"]
    LEX --> TOK["Token Stream"]
    TOK --> PAR["Parser Uses CFG Rules"]
    PAR --> AST["Abstract Syntax Tree"]
    PAR -.Uses.-> CFG["CFG for Language Syntax"]
    CFG -.Defines.-> PAR
    AST --> SEM["Semantic Analysis"]
    SEM --> CODE["Code Generation"]
```

**Explanation:** In a typical compiler, the **parser** uses a CFG (often specified in BNF or EBNF notation) to verify whether the token stream forms a syntactically valid program. If the parser can construct a parse tree (or derivation) for the input, the program is syntactically correct.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Define a Context Free Grammar (CFG). What are the four components of a CFG?**

**Model Answer:**

A Context Free Grammar (CFG) is a formal mathematical model used to generate Context Free Languages. It is formally defined as a 4-tuple:

$$G = (V, T, P, S)$$

The four components are:
1. **$V$**: A finite, non-empty set of **variables** (non-terminals) such as $\{E, S, A\}$.
2. **$T$**: A finite, non-empty set of **terminals** (the alphabet) such as $\{a, b, +, *\}$, with $V \cap T = \emptyset$.
3. **$P$**: A finite set of **production rules** of the form $A \rightarrow \alpha$, where $A \in V$ and $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^{*}$.
4. **$S$**: The **start symbol**, which is a distinguished element of $V$ ($S \in V$).

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for the four components with brief explanation)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between leftmost derivation and rightmost derivation with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Leftmost Derivation (LMD) | Rightmost Derivation (RMD) |
|--------|---------------------------|----------------------------|
| Replacement | At each step, the **leftmost variable** is replaced | At each step, the **rightmost variable** is replaced |
| Used in | Top-down parsers (e.g., LL parsers) | Bottom-up parsers (e.g., LR parsers) |
| Notation | $S \Rightarrow_{lm} w$ | $S \Rightarrow_{rm} w$ |

**Example:** For grammar $E \rightarrow E + E \mid E * E \mid a$ and string $a + a * a$:

- **LMD:** $E \Rightarrow E + E \Rightarrow a + E \Rightarrow a + E * E \Rightarrow a + a * E \Rightarrow a + a * a$
- **RMD:** $E \Rightarrow E + E \Rightarrow E + E * E \Rightarrow E + E * a \Rightarrow E + a * a \Rightarrow a + a * a$

*(3 marks — 1 for the difference, 2 for the worked example)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Consider the following Context Free Grammar (CFG) for generating arithmetic expressions:**
$$E \rightarrow E + T \mid T$$
$$T \rightarrow T * F \mid F$$
$$F \rightarrow (E) \mid id$$

**(a) [7 Marks] Generate the string $w = id + id * id$ using:**
   **(i) Leftmost derivation**
   **(ii) Rightmost derivation**

**(b) [7 Marks] Construct the parse tree for the string $w = id + id * id$ and verify the yield.**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a)(i) — Leftmost Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Start with $E$.
$$E$$
*Explanation:* The start symbol is $E$.

**Step 2:** Apply $E \rightarrow E + T$ to replace the leftmost variable $E$.
$$E \Rightarrow E + T$$
*Explanation:* Since the string contains $+$, we use the $E + T$ production.

**Step 3:** Apply $E \rightarrow T$ to the leftmost variable $E$.
$$E + T \Rightarrow T + T$$
*Explanation:* The leftmost $E$ (in $E + T$) must be expanded. We use $E \rightarrow T$.

**Step 4:** Apply $T \rightarrow F$ to the leftmost $T$.
$$T + T \Rightarrow F + T$$
*Explanation:* The leftmost $T$ is replaced by $F$ (no multiplication on the left).

**Step 5:** Apply $F \rightarrow id$ to the leftmost $F$.
$$F + T \Rightarrow id + T$$
*Explanation:* The leftmost $F$ becomes the terminal $id$.

**Step 6:** Apply $T \rightarrow T * F$ to the remaining $T$.
$$id + T \Rightarrow id + T * F$$
*Explanation:* The $T$ needs to expand to handle the multiplication $*$.

**Step 7:** Apply $T \rightarrow F$ to the leftmost $T$ in $T * F$.
$$id + T * F \Rightarrow id + F * F$$
*Explanation:* The leftmost $T$ in the multiplication expansion becomes $F$.

**Step 8:** Apply $F \rightarrow id$ to the leftmost $F$.
$$id + F * F \Rightarrow id + id * F$$
*Explanation:* First $F$ of multiplication becomes $id$.

**Step 9:** Apply $F \rightarrow id$ to the last $F$.
$$id + id * F \Rightarrow id + id * id$$
*Explanation:* The final $F$ becomes $id$.

**Final LMD:** $E \Rightarrow^{*} id + id * id$ in 9 steps. ✓

---

**Model Solution for Part (a)(ii) — Rightmost Derivation:**

**Step 1:** $E \Rightarrow E + T$ (Apply $E \rightarrow E + T$)

**Step 2:** $E + T \Rightarrow E + T * F$ (Apply $T \rightarrow T * F$ to rightmost $T$)

**Step 3:** $E + T * F \Rightarrow E + T * id$ (Apply $F \rightarrow id$ to rightmost $F$)

**Step 4:** $E + T * id \Rightarrow E + F * id$ (Apply $T \rightarrow F$ to rightmost $T$)

**Step 5:** $E + F * id \Rightarrow E + id * id$ (Apply $F \rightarrow id$ to rightmost $F$)

**Step 6:** $E + id * id \Rightarrow T + id * id$ (Apply $E \rightarrow T$ to rightmost $E$)

**Step 7:** $T + id * id \Rightarrow F + id * id$ (Apply $T \rightarrow F$ to rightmost $T$)

**Step 8:** $F + id * id \Rightarrow id + id * id$ (Apply $F \rightarrow id$ to rightmost $F$)

**Final RMD:** $E \Rightarrow^{*} id + id * id$ in 8 steps. ✓

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) — Parse Tree Construction:**

The parse tree for $w = id + id * id$ is constructed as follows:

| Tree Level | Node | Children | Production | Notes |
|------------|------|----------|------------|-------|
| 0 (Root) | $E$ | $E$, $+$, $T$ | $E \rightarrow E + T$ | Root decomposition |
| 1 (Left) | $E$ | $T$ | $E \rightarrow T$ | No addition on left |
| 1 (Middle) | $+$ | (leaf) | — | Plus operator |
| 1 (Right) | $T$ | $T$, $*$, $F$ | $T \rightarrow T * F$ | Multiplication on right |
| 2 (Left-T) | $T$ | $F$ | $T \rightarrow F$ | No more multiplication |
| 2 (Left-F) | $F$ | $id$ | $F \rightarrow id$ | First operand |
| 2 (Right-F) | $F$ | $id$ | $F \rightarrow id$ | Third operand |
| 3 (Right-T-F) | $F$ | $id$ | $F \rightarrow id$ | Second operand |

**Yield Verification:** Reading the leaves from left to right: $id + id * id$ ✓

**Visual Representation (Mermaid):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pE["E Root"] --> pEL["E"]
    pE --> pPlus["PLUS"]
    pE --> pTR["T"]
    pEL --> pT1["T"]
    pT1 --> pF1["F"]
    pF1 --> pid1["id"]
    pTR --> pT2["T"]
    pTR --> pMul["MUL"]
    pTR --> pF2["F"]
    pT2 --> pF3["F"]
    pF3 --> pid2["id"]
    pF2 --> pid3["id"]
    pMul --> X[" "]
```

*(Note: The above tree shows the structural hierarchy. The yield of this tree, when leaves are read left-to-right, is exactly $id + id * id$, confirming the parse is correct.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: (a) [7 Marks] Define ambiguity in a CFG. Show that the following grammar is ambiguous by finding two distinct parse trees for the string $a + a + a$:**
$$E \rightarrow E + E \mid E * E \mid a$$

**(b) [7 Marks] Design a CFG to generate the language $L = \{a^{n}b^{2n} \mid n \geq 1\}$ and show the derivation of the string $aabb$ using leftmost derivation.**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a) — Ambiguity Demonstration:**

**Definition (2 marks):** A CFG $G$ is said to be **ambiguous** if there exists at least one string $w \in L(G)$ that has **more than one distinct parse tree** (equivalently, more than one leftmost derivation or more than one rightmost derivation).

**Two Parse Trees for $a + a + a$ (5 marks):**

**Parse Tree 1 (Left-grouped):**

Derivation: $E \Rightarrow E + E \Rightarrow E + E + E \Rightarrow a + E + E \Rightarrow a + a + E \Rightarrow a + a + a$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    qE1["E Root"] --> qL1["E"]
    qE1 --> qP1["PLUS"]
    qE1 --> qR1["E"]
    qL1 --> qLL["E"]
    qL1 --> qP2["PLUS"]
    qL1 --> qLR["E"]
    qR1 --> qRA["a"]
    qLL --> qLA["a"]
    qLR --> qMA["a"]
```

**Parse Tree 2 (Right-grouped):**

Derivation: $E \Rightarrow E + E \Rightarrow E + E + E \Rightarrow E + E + a \Rightarrow E + a + a \Rightarrow a + a + a$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    qE2["E Root"] --> qL2["E"]
    qE2 --> qP3["PLUS"]
    qE2 --> qR2["E"]
    qL2 --> qLA2["a"]
    qR2 --> qRL["E"]
    qR2 --> qP4["PLUS"]
    qR2 --> qRR["E"]
    qRL --> qRA2["a"]
    qRR --> qMA2["a"]
```

Since the string $a + a + a$ has **two structurally different parse trees**, the grammar is **ambiguous**. ✓

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) — CFG Design for $L = \{a^{n}b^{2n} \mid n \geq 1\}$:**

**CFG Definition (3 marks):**

$$G = (\{S\}, \{a, b\}, P, S)$$

With productions:
$$S \rightarrow aSbb \quad (P1)$$
$$S \rightarrow abb \quad (P2)$$

**Verification of the Language (2 marks):**
- Base case ($P2$): Generates $n=1$: $a^{1}b^{2} = abb$ ✓
- Recursive case ($P1$): $S \Rightarrow aSbb$ adds one $a$ and two $b$'s at each step, maintaining the form $a^{n}b^{2n}$.
- By induction, $L(G) = \{a^{n}b^{2n} \mid n \geq 1\}$.

**Leftmost Derivation of $w = aabb$ (2 marks):**

**Step 1:** $S \Rightarrow aSbb$ (Apply $P1$)
*Explanation:* Start with the recursive production to add one layer of $a$ and $bb$.

**Step 2:** $aSbb \Rightarrow aabb$ (Apply $P2$ to the remaining $S$)
*Explanation:* Use the base case production to terminate the recursion.

**Final LMD:** $S \Rightarrow aSbb \Rightarrow aabb$ in 2 steps. ✓

The derived string is $aabb = a^{2}b^{4}$? Wait, let us reconsider — the string $aabb$ has 2 a's and 2 b's, which fits $n=1$ (i.e., $a^1 b^{2 \cdot 1} = abb$). So let us derive $w = abb$ instead.

**Corrected Derivation of $w = abb$:**

**Step 1:** $S \Rightarrow abb$ (Apply $P2$ directly)
*Explanation:* For $n=1$, the base case generates $abb = a^{1}b^{2}$ directly.

**Final LMD:** $S \Rightarrow abb$ in 1 step. ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Disjoint sets forgotten:** Students often forget to mention that $V \cap T = \emptyset$ (variables and terminals must be disjoint). **Loss: 0.5 to 1 mark.**
>
> 2. **Start symbol misidentified:** The start symbol $S$ must be a variable, not a terminal. Mentioning $S \in V$ is critical. **Loss: 0.5 mark.**
>
> 3. **Wrong production rule format:** Writing $A \rightarrow \alpha$ where $\alpha$ has terminals on the LHS, or omitting the constraint that LHS must be a **single variable** (not a string). **Loss: 1 mark.**
>
> 4. **Confusing LMD and RMD:** Mixing up which variable to replace at each step. Always explicitly state "we replace the **leftmost** variable" or "**rightmost** variable". **Loss: 1-2 marks.**
>
> 5. **Ambiguity definition too vague:** Students often say "a grammar is ambiguous if it can be parsed in multiple ways" — this is too informal. The precise definition is: "A CFG is ambiguous if **there exists a string** in the language with **more than one parse tree**." **Loss: 1 mark.**
>
> 6. **Skip steps in derivation:** Examiners require every single step to be shown with the production rule used. Do not skip from $S$ directly to the final string. **Loss: 1-2 marks.**
>
> 7. **CFG design for $a^{n}b^{2n}$:** Common mistake is using productions like $S \rightarrow aSb$ (which generates $a^{n}b^{n}$, not $a^{n}b^{2n}$). The correct production must add **two** $b$'s for each $a$. **Loss: 2-3 marks.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for CFG — Representation of CFL:**

- **CFG is a 4-tuple** $G = (V, T, P, S)$: Variables, Terminals, Productions, Start symbol.

- **Constraint:** $V \cap T = \emptyset$ and $S \in V$.

- **Production rule form:** $A \rightarrow \alpha$ where $A$ is a **single variable** and $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^{*}$ (can include $\epsilon$).

- **Language generated:** $L(G) = \{w \in T^{*} \mid S \Rightarrow^{*} w\}$ — only terminal strings count.

- **Context-free** means the LHS of any production is a single variable, replaceable regardless of context.

- **Two derivation types:** Leftmost (LMD, replace leftmost variable) and Rightmost (RMD, replace rightmost variable).

- **Parse tree properties:** Root is $S$, internal nodes are variables, leaves (read left to right) form the yield = derived string.

- **Ambiguity:** A CFG is ambiguous if **some** string has **more than one** parse tree (equivalently, multiple LMDs or RMDs).

- **No algorithm exists** to detect ambiguity in an arbitrary CFG (undecidable problem).

- **Hierarchy:** Regular Languages $\subset$ Context Free Languages $\subset$ Context Sensitive Languages.

- **Classic CFL examples:**
   - $L = \{a^{n}b^{n} \mid n \geq 1\}$: Grammar $S \rightarrow aSb \mid ab$
   - $L = \{a^{n}b^{2n} \mid n \geq 1\}$: Grammar $S \rightarrow aSbb \mid abb$
   - $L = \{ww^{R} \mid w \in \Sigma^{*}\}$ (palindromes): Grammar $S \rightarrow aSa \mid bSb \mid \epsilon$
   - $L = \{w \mid w$ has balanced parentheses$\}$: Grammar $S \rightarrow SS \mid (S) \mid \epsilon$

- **Every regular language is a CFL** (since DFAs/NFAs can be converted to CFGs), but the converse is FALSE.

- **Applications:** Compiler parsers (YACC, Bison), NLP, XML/HTML DTDs, formal verification.

- **Common exam questions:** (1) Define CFG, (2) Show LMD/RMD of a string, (3) Construct parse tree, (4) Prove ambiguity, (5) Design CFG for a given language.

- **Equation to remember:** $L(G) = \{w \in T^{*} \mid S \Rightarrow^{*} w\}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Derivation Trees and Ambiguity
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET414 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Derivation Trees and Ambiguity

# Derivation Trees and Ambiguity — KTU Premier Study Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition: Derivation Tree (Parse Tree)

A **derivation tree** (also called a **parse tree** or **syntax tree**) is an ordered, rooted tree used to represent the syntactic structure of a string derived from a Context-Free Grammar (CFG). Each node in the tree is labeled with a symbol from the grammar's alphabet $(V \cup \Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\})$, and the tree obeys the following strict rules:

1. **Root Node Rule:** The root is always labeled with the **start symbol** $S$ of the grammar $G$.
2. **Interior Node Rule:** Every interior (non-leaf) node is labeled with a **non-terminal** $A \in V$.
3. **Children Rule:** If an interior node has label $A$, and its children are labeled $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_k$ (left to right), then the production $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \cdots X_k$ must belong to $P$ (the production set).
4. **Leaf Node Rule:** Every leaf is labeled with a **terminal** $a \in \Sigma$ or with the empty string $\varepsilon$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Yield of a Derivation Tree:** The *yield* of a derivation tree is the string obtained by concatenating the labels of the leaves from **left to right**. A derivation tree is **valid** if and only if its yield belongs to the language $L(G)$.

### 1.2 Formal Definition: Ambiguity

A Context-Free Grammar $G = (V, T, P, S)$ is said to be **ambiguous** if there exists at least one string $w \in L(G)$ that has **two or more distinct derivation trees**. Equivalently, $w$ admits **two or more distinct leftmost derivations** (and correspondingly, two or more distinct rightmost derivations).

A Context-Free Language $L$ is said to be **inherently ambiguous** if **every** grammar $G$ that generates $L$ (i.e., $L(G) = L$) is ambiguous.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The textbook classic example of an inherently ambiguous language is:
> $$L = \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1\}$$
> No unambiguous grammar can generate this language.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "Family Tree of a Sentence"

Imagine you are writing an English sentence like *"The smart boy reads."* A grammar is like a **rulebook for sentence construction** (subject → noun phrase, noun phrase → article + adjective + noun, etc.). A **derivation tree** is essentially the **family tree of that sentence**:

- The **root** is the abstract idea "Sentence" (the start symbol $S$).
- The **branches** show how this abstract idea is broken down into grammatical components (NP, VP, etc.).
- The **leaves** are the actual words you utter.

Now, **ambiguity** is like a sentence that has *more than one valid family tree*. Consider the famous sentence: *"I saw the man with the telescope."* — Was the telescope used to *see*, or did the man *possess* the telescope? Two trees, one sentence. That is exactly what ambiguity means in formal language theory!

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Derivation Tree — Stepwise Logic

To construct the derivation tree for a string $w \in L(G)$, follow this deterministic procedure:

1. **Initialize:** Place the start symbol $S$ at the root of the tree.
2. **Expand a Leaf:** Pick a non-terminal leaf $A$. Choose a production $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \cdots X_k$.
3. **Attach Children:** Create $k$ children for $A$ in left-to-right order with labels $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_k$.
4. **Iterate:** Repeat step 2 until no non-terminal leaf remains (only terminals and $\varepsilon$ remain).
5. **Read the Yield:** Concatenate the leaf labels left-to-right; this must equal $w$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Derivation Trees Matter:** Derivation trees decouple *what* is derived (the string) from *how* it is derived (the sequence of production choices). Two different derivations can produce the same string — but if they produce *different trees*, the grammar is ambiguous.

### 2.2 The Three Views of Derivation

Every CFG generates strings via derivations, but there are three equivalent perspectives:

| # | Perspective | Definition | Order of Rule Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **General Derivation** | $S \Rightarrow^* w$ uses any non-terminal at any step | Any non-terminal at any step |
| 2 | **Leftmost Derivation ($\Rightarrow_{lm}$)** | At each step, the **leftmost** non-terminal is rewritten | Strictly left-to-right |
| 3 | **Rightmost Derivation ($\Rightarrow_{rm}$)** | At each step, the **rightmost** non-terminal is rewritten | Strictly right-to-left |

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem (KTU High-Yield):** A string $w$ has **two distinct derivation trees** $\iff$ $w$ has **two distinct leftmost derivations** $\iff$ $w$ has **two distinct rightmost derivations**. All three notions of ambiguity are equivalent for CFGs.

### 2.3 Conditions for Ambiguity

A grammar $G$ is ambiguous **if and only if** the following condition holds:
$$\exists\, w \in L(G) \text{ such that } \vert\{t \mid t \text{ is a derivation tree for } w\}\vert \geq 2$$

A grammar $G$ is **unambiguous** if and only if:
$$\forall\, w \in L(G), \text{ the number of derivation trees for } w \text{ is exactly } 1$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Symbol / Rule** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| Derivation Tree | $T$ | Ordered rooted tree representing derivation |
| Root Label | $S$ | Always the start symbol |
| Interior Label | $A \in V$ | Always a non-terminal |
| Leaf Label | $a \in \Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}$ | Terminal or $\varepsilon$ |
| Yield | $\text{yield}(T)$ | Left-to-right concatenation of leaf labels |
| Ambiguity Condition | $\exists w \in L(G)$ with $\geq 2$ trees | Grammar is ambiguous |
| Inherent Ambiguity | All grammars for $L$ are ambiguous | Language is inherently ambiguous |
| Inorder Traversal | Often yields the original expression | Useful for expression grammars |
| Leftmost Derivation | $\Rightarrow_{lm}^*$ | Leftmost non-terminal rewritten each step |
| Rightmost Derivation | $\Rightarrow_{rm}^*$ | Rightmost non-terminal rewritten each step |
| Classic Inherently Ambiguous Lang. | $L = \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1\}$ | No unambiguous CFG exists |

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Working Example 1: The Classic Ambiguous Arithmetic Grammar

Consider the grammar:
$$G_1: \quad E \rightarrow E + E \mid E * E \mid (E) \mid \text{id}$$

The string $w = \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$ has **two distinct derivation trees**, proving $G_1$ is ambiguous.

#### Derivation Tree 1 (Tree T₁): $+\text{ is the root}$

$$E \Rightarrow E + E \Rightarrow \text{id} + E \Rightarrow \text{id} + E * E \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * E \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$$

Tree T₁ structure: Root $E$ expands as $E + E$. Left child $E \rightarrow \text{id}$. Right child $E \rightarrow E * E$ which further expands into $\text{id}$, $*$, $\text{id}$.

#### Derivation Tree 2 (Tree T₂): $*$ is the root

$$E \Rightarrow E * E \Rightarrow E + E * E \Rightarrow \text{id} + E * E \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * E \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$$

Tree T₂ structure: Root $E$ expands as $E * E$. Left child $E \rightarrow E + E$ expands into $\text{id}$, $+$, $\text{id}$. Right child $E \rightarrow \text{id}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why are T₁ and T₂ different?** In T₁, the root applies the production $E \rightarrow E + E$, so $+$ is at the top level of the syntactic structure. In T₂, the root applies $E \rightarrow E * E$, so $*$ is at the top level. These correspond to two different *precedence assignments* — T₁ says $(+)$ binds tighter than $(\times)$ when grouped as $\text{id} + (\text{id} * \text{id})$, while T₂ says $(\times)$ binds tighter as $(\text{id} + \text{id}) * \text{id}$. Mathematically, the second interpretation is wrong because it changes the value, but the grammar $G_1$ permits both!

### 3.2 Working Example 2: Constructing an Unambiguous Replacement

To eliminate ambiguity from $G_1$, we impose **operator precedence** through a layered grammar:

$$G_2: \quad \begin{aligned} E &\rightarrow E + T \mid T \\ T &\rightarrow T * F \mid F \\ F &\rightarrow (E) \mid \text{id} \end{aligned}$$

**Step-by-step proof that $G_2$ is unambiguous for $w = \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$:**

Step 1: Apply $E \rightarrow E + T$ (the only production starting with $E$ and containing $+$):
$$E \Rightarrow E + T$$

Step 2: The left $E$ must derive $\text{id}$. Apply $E \rightarrow T$, then $T \rightarrow F$, then $F \rightarrow \text{id}$:
$$E \Rightarrow T \Rightarrow F \Rightarrow \text{id}$$

Step 3: The $T$ on the right must derive $\text{id} * \text{id}$. Apply $T \rightarrow T * F$, then $T \rightarrow \text{id}$, then $F \rightarrow \text{id}$:
$$T \Rightarrow T * F \Rightarrow F * F \Rightarrow \text{id} * F \Rightarrow \text{id} * \text{id}$$

Step 4: The complete unique leftmost derivation is:
$$E \Rightarrow_{lm} E + T \Rightarrow_{lm} T + T \Rightarrow_{lm} F + T \Rightarrow_{lm} \text{id} + T \Rightarrow_{lm} \text{id} + T * F \Rightarrow_{lm} \text{id} + F * F \Rightarrow_{lm} \text{id} + \text{id} * F \Rightarrow_{lm} \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$$

The structure is now **uniquely**: $\text{id} + (\text{id} * \text{id})$ — correct precedence! ✓

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Property** | **Grammar $G_1$** | **Grammar $G_2$** |
|---|---|---|
| Productions | $E \rightarrow E+E \mid E*E \mid (E) \mid \text{id}$ | $E \rightarrow E+T \mid T$; $T \rightarrow T*F \mid F$; $F \rightarrow (E) \mid \text{id}$ |
| Trees for $\text{id}+\text{id}*\text{id}$ | **2** | **1** |
| Ambiguous? | **Yes** | **No** |
| Same Language? | Yes ($L(G_1) = L(G_2)$) | Yes |
| Enforces Precedence? | No | Yes ($*$ binds tighter than $+$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** Compilers (like GCC, Clang, javac) use unambiguous grammars in their parser generators (YACC, ANTLR, Bison). An ambiguous grammar in a programming language would mean `a + b * c` could compile differently on different runs — a fatal flaw!

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Two Derivation Trees for `id + id * id` (Side-by-Side)

```mermaid
graph TD
    t1Root["T1 : E"] --> t1Left["E"]
    t1Root["T1 : E"] --> t1Plus["+"]
    t1Root["T1 : E"] --> t1Right["E"]
    t1Left["E"] --> t1id1["id"]
    t1Right["E"] --> t1Star1["E"]
    t1Right["E"] --> t1StarSym["*"]
    t1Right["E"] --> t1Star2["E"]
    t1Star1["E"] --> t1id2["id"]
    t1Star2["E"] --> t1id3["id"]

    t2Root["T2 : E"] --> t2Left["E"]
    t2Root["T2 : E"] --> t2Plus["+"]
    t2Root["T2 : E"] --> t2Right["E"]
    t2Left["E"] --> t2Star1["E"]
    t2Left["E"] --> t2StarSym["*"]
    t2Left["E"] --> t2Star2["E"]
    t2Right["E"] --> t2id3["id"]
    t2Star1["E"] --> t2id1["id"]
    t2Star2["E"] --> t2id2["id"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Unique Tree from Unambiguous Grammar $G_2$

```mermaid
graph TD
    uRoot["E"] --> uLeft["E"]
    uRoot["E"] --> uPlus["+"]
    uRoot["E"] --> uRight["T"]
    uLeft["E"] --> uT1["T"]
    uT1["T"] --> uF1["F"]
    uF1["F"] --> uid1["id"]
    uRight["T"] --> uT2["T"]
    uRight["T"] --> uStar["*"]
    uRight["T"] --> uF2["F"]
    uT2["T"] --> uF3["F"]
    uF3["F"] --> uid2["id"]
    uF2["F"] --> uid3["id"]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: How a Parser Uses Derivation Trees

```mermaid
graph LR
    src["Source String w"] --> lex["Lexer/Tokenizer"]
    lex --> tokens["Token Stream"]
    tokens --> cfg["Context-Free Grammar G"]
    cfg --> parse["Parser Engine"]
    parse --> dtree["Derivation Tree T"]
    dtree --> unamb{"Trees for w = 1?"}
    unamb -- YES --> success["Accept : Valid Syntax"]
    unamb -- NO --> amb["Ambiguity Detected"]
    amb --> disamb["Apply Disambiguation Rules: Precedence, Associativity"]
    disamb --> uniq["Unique Tree Selected"]
    uniq --> success
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. Define a derivation tree. What is meant by the yield of a derivation tree?**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model Pattern]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A derivation tree (or parse tree) is an ordered rooted tree that represents the syntactic derivation of a string from a Context-Free Grammar $G = (V, T, P, S)$. The root is labeled with the start symbol $S$, every interior node is labeled with a non-terminal, and the children of a node labeled $A$ correspond to a production $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \cdots X_k$. Leaves are labeled with terminals or $\varepsilon$. The **yield** is the string obtained by concatenating leaf labels from left to right. **A derivation tree is valid iff its yield belongs to $L(G)$.**

#### **Q2. State the conditions under which a Context-Free Grammar is called ambiguous.**

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model Pattern]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A CFG $G$ is **ambiguous** if there exists at least one string $w \in L(G)$ that has two or more distinct derivation trees. Equivalently, $w$ admits two distinct leftmost derivations (or two distinct rightmost derivations). A Context-Free Language $L$ is **inherently ambiguous** if *every* grammar generating $L$ is ambiguous. Example of an inherently ambiguous language: $L = \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1\}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Consider the grammar:**
$$G: \quad S \rightarrow aS \mid aSbS \mid \varepsilon$$

**(a)** Show that the string $w = ab$ has two distinct derivation trees. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Construct an unambiguous grammar $G'$ that generates the same language $L(G)$, and show a unique derivation tree for $w = aab$. **(7 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Two Derivation Trees for $w = ab$ (7 Marks):**

**Tree 1 (T₁):** Apply $S \rightarrow aSbS$, then $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$, then $S \rightarrow a$, then $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$:
$$S \Rightarrow aSbS \Rightarrow aS \Rightarrow aa \quad \text{(wrong — does not yield } ab\text{)}$$

Let us correctly construct two trees:

**Tree 1 (T₁):** Use $S \rightarrow aSbS$ with the first $S$ deriving $a$ and the second $S$ deriving $\varepsilon$:
$$S \Rightarrow aSbS \Rightarrow a \cdot b \cdot S \Rightarrow ab \cdot \varepsilon = ab$$

Here $S \rightarrow a$ gives 'a', then 'b' is matched, then $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$.

**Tree 2 (T₂):** Use $S \rightarrow aS$ repeatedly and then $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$:

Actually, this grammar requires careful reading. Let $w = ab$:

**T₁:** $S \Rightarrow aS \Rightarrow abS$ — but we have no production $S \rightarrow b$. Try:
$$S \Rightarrow aSbS \Rightarrow a b \varepsilon = ab$$
where $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ for the trailing $S$.

**T₂:** $S \Rightarrow aS \Rightarrow a a S b S$ — too many symbols. Let us try:
$$S \Rightarrow aS \Rightarrow a \cdot \varepsilon = a \quad \text{(only yields } a\text{, not } ab\text{)}$$

So with the given grammar, we need to examine carefully. **Both derivations** for $ab$:

**T₁:** $S \Rightarrow aSbS \Rightarrow ab\varepsilon = ab$ — using $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ for trailing position.

**T₂:** $S \Rightarrow aSbS \Rightarrow aS \Rightarrow ab$ — using $S \rightarrow aS$ to derive $a$ from the first $S$ inside.

Wait, $aS \Rightarrow ab$ requires $S \rightarrow b$, which is not in $P$. So:

**Correct T₁:** $S \Rightarrow aSbS \Rightarrow abS \Rightarrow ab \cdot \varepsilon = ab$

**Correct T₂:** Use $S \rightarrow aS$ first: $S \Rightarrow aS \Rightarrow a \cdot abS$? No — $S$ cannot derive $ab$ directly.

The intended answer: **Use $S \rightarrow aSbS$ twice with different groupings:**

**T₁:** $S \Rightarrow aSbS \Rightarrow a b S \Rightarrow ab$ (where $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$)

**T₂:** $S \Rightarrow aSbS \Rightarrow a \cdot S \cdot b \cdot \varepsilon$ where the inner $S \Rightarrow aS \Rightarrow a\varepsilon = a$: 
$$S \Rightarrow aSbS \Rightarrow a \cdot a \cdot b \cdot S \Rightarrow aab \quad \text{(gives } aab\text{, not } ab\text{)}$$

For the string $ab$, the simplest demonstration uses $S \rightarrow aS$ for first 'a' and $S \rightarrow b$? But $S \rightarrow b$ is not a production. Hence with the given grammar, $ab$ has only ONE derivation tree of the form $S \Rightarrow aSbS \Rightarrow ab$. Let me revise the example to a definitively ambiguous one.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Note for Q2(a):** If the question as posed doesn't yield true ambiguity for the string $ab$, students should use the **standard grammar** $S \rightarrow aS \mid aSbS \mid \varepsilon$ and demonstrate ambiguity on $w = aab$ instead, where T₁ gives $S \Rightarrow aS \Rightarrow aaSbS \Rightarrow aab\varepsilon$ and T₂ gives $S \Rightarrow aSbS \Rightarrow aaSbS \Rightarrow aab\varepsilon$ with the inner $S \rightarrow aS$ vs $S \rightarrow aSbS$ grouping.

**Two Derivation Trees for $w = aab$:**

**T₁ (Use $S \rightarrow aS$ for first 'a'):**
$$S \Rightarrow aS \Rightarrow aaSbS \Rightarrow aabS \Rightarrow aab$$

**T₂ (Use $S \rightarrow aSbS$ for grouping):**
$$S \Rightarrow aSbS \Rightarrow aaSbS \Rightarrow aab \cdot \varepsilon = aab$$
where the inner $S \Rightarrow aS \Rightarrow a\varepsilon = a$.

Both yields equal $aab$, but tree structures differ — grammar is ambiguous. ✓ **(7 marks awarded)**

**(b) Unambiguous Grammar $G'$ and Unique Tree for $w = aab$ (7 Marks):**

Recognize that $L(G) = \{w \in \{a,b\}^* \mid \text{every prefix of } w \text{ has } \#a \geq \#b\}$. This is the language of well-balanced sequences where $a$ acts as "push" and $b$ as "pop" but never negative.

**Unambiguous grammar $G'$:**
$$G': \quad S \rightarrow aS \mid aSbS \mid \varepsilon \quad \text{— but with convention that } aSbS \text{ consumes exactly one } b\text{ matched to one } a.$$

A cleaner unambiguous grammar is:
$$S \rightarrow aS \mid aSbS \mid \varepsilon \quad \text{is itself ambiguous, so we replace with:}$$

$$G': \quad S \rightarrow aS \mid T, \quad T \rightarrow aTbT \mid \varepsilon$$

This forces $T$ to be the "matched pair generator" ensuring balanced structures.

**Derivation of $w = aab$ in $G'$:**
$$S \Rightarrow aS \Rightarrow aT \Rightarrow a \cdot aTbT \Rightarrow aa \cdot T \cdot b \cdot T \Rightarrow aa \cdot \varepsilon \cdot b \cdot \varepsilon = aab$$

This derivation is **unique** — only one leftmost derivation exists. ✓ **(7 marks awarded)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**Consider the grammar:**
$$G: \quad E \rightarrow E + E \mid E * E \mid (E) \mid \text{id}$$

**(a)** Prove that $G$ is ambiguous by exhibiting two distinct derivation trees for the string $w = \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Rewrite $G$ into an unambiguous equivalent grammar $G'$ that enforces standard arithmetic precedence ($*$ binds tighter than $+$), and demonstrate the unique derivation tree for $w = \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$. **(7 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted Module 2 Pattern]**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Proof of Ambiguity for $w = \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$ (7 Marks):**

**Derivation Tree 1 (T₁):** Root expands as $E \rightarrow E + E$. Left child $\rightarrow \text{id}$, right child $\rightarrow E * E$, both sub-children $\rightarrow \text{id}$.

Leftmost derivation:
$$E \Rightarrow E + E \Rightarrow \text{id} + E \Rightarrow \text{id} + E * E \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * E \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$$

**Tree T₁ (in textual form):**
```
            E
          / | \
         E  +  E
         |    /|\
        id   E * E
             |   |
             id  id
```
Yields: $\text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$ ✓ **(3 marks for T₁ derivation)**

**Derivation Tree 2 (T₂):** Root expands as $E \rightarrow E * E$. Left child $\rightarrow E + E$, right child $\rightarrow \text{id}$.

Leftmost derivation:
$$E \Rightarrow E * E \Rightarrow E + E * E \Rightarrow \text{id} + E * E \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * E \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$$

**Tree T₂ (in textual form):**
```
            E
          / | \
         E  *  E
        /|\    |
       E + E  id
       |   |
      id  id
```
Yields: $\text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$ ✓ **(3 marks for T₂ derivation)**

**Conclusion:** Since $w = \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$ has **two distinct derivation trees**, grammar $G$ is **ambiguous**. **(1 mark)** ✓ **Total: 7/7**

**(b) Unambiguous Grammar $G'$ with Precedence (7 Marks):**

To enforce $* > +$ in precedence, introduce intermediate non-terminals:
$$G': \quad \begin{aligned} E &\rightarrow E + T \mid T \\ T &\rightarrow T * F \mid F \\ F &\rightarrow (E) \mid \text{id} \end{aligned}$$

**Why this works:**
- $E$ generates expressions (the lowest precedence, $+$).
- $T$ generates terms (intermediate precedence, $*$).
- $F$ generates factors (highest precedence, parentheses and atoms).
- This **stratification** ensures that $*$ binds tighter than $+$.

**Unique Derivation Tree for $w = \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$ in $G'$:**

**Tree (textual form):**
```
                    E
                  / | \
                 E  +  T
                 |    /|\
                 T   T * F
                 |   |   |
                 F   F   id
                 |   |
                id  id
```

**Leftmost derivation:**
$$E \Rightarrow E + T \Rightarrow T + T \Rightarrow F + T \Rightarrow \text{id} + T \Rightarrow \text{id} + T * F \Rightarrow \text{id} + F * F \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * F \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$$

**Inorder traversal yields:** $\text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$ — correctly grouped as $\text{id} + (\text{id} * \text{id})$! ✓ **(7 marks awarded)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing derivation sequence with derivation tree:** Writing only the leftmost derivation $\Rightarrow_{lm}^*$ does NOT prove ambiguity. You MUST draw or explicitly describe the **two distinct trees** with their branching structure. (Lose up to 4 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting inorder traversal equivalence:** When rewriting for precedence, mention that **inorder traversal** of the unambiguous tree yields the original expression. Examiners reward this insight.
> 3. **Drawing same tree twice in different orientations:** Two trees are distinct only if their **topological/branching structure** differs, not merely their drawing orientation.
> 4. **Claiming $L = \{a^n b^n c^n\}$ is unambiguous:** It is the textbook example of an **inherently ambiguous** language. No CFG can avoid ambiguity here.
> 5. **Missing the role of $\varepsilon$-productions:** When $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ is present, students often forget that leaves can be labeled $\varepsilon$, which counts as nothing in the yield.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 📌 **Derivation Tree = Parse Tree = Ordered rooted tree** with root $S$, interior labels in $V$, leaf labels in $\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}$.
- 📌 **Yield = left-to-right leaf concatenation**; valid tree ⟺ yield $\in L(G)$.
- 📌 **Ambiguous Grammar** ⟺ exists $w$ with **≥ 2 distinct derivation trees** ⟺ **≥ 2 distinct leftmost derivations** ⟺ **≥ 2 distinct rightmost derivations**.
- 📌 **Inherently Ambiguous Language** ⟺ *every* grammar generating it is ambiguous (e.g., $\{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1\}$).
- 📌 **Disambiguation via stratification** — introduce extra non-terminals ($E \rightarrow E+T \mid T$; $T \rightarrow T*F \mid F$; $F \rightarrow (E) \mid \text{id}$) to enforce operator precedence.
- 📌 **Inorder traversal** of an expression derivation tree yields the infix expression.
- 📌 **KTU favorite examples:** arithmetic expressions, palindromes, balanced parentheses, if-else dangling else problem (use *associativity* and *precedence* to disambiguate).
- 📌 **Three equivalent views:** general derivation $\Rightarrow^*$, leftmost $\Rightarrow_{lm}^*$, rightmost $\Rightarrow_{rm}^*$ — pick whichever is convenient.
- 📌 **Ambiguity is a property of the grammar, not the language** — a language may have both ambiguous and unambiguous grammars (inherently ambiguous languages are the exception).
- 📌 **Compiler connection:** YACC/Bison/ANTLR expect **unambiguous** grammars; ambiguous ones cause shift-reduce conflicts in LALR parsers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Normal Forms for CFGs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET414 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Normal Forms for CFGs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Context-Free Grammars (CFGs) — Quick Recap
A **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)** is a 4-tuple $G = (V, T, P, S)$, where:
- $V$ = Finite set of non-terminals (variables)
- $T$ = Finite set of terminals (alphabet)
- $P$ = Finite set of production rules of the form $A \rightarrow \alpha$, where $A \in V$ and $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^*$
- $S$ = Start symbol ($S \in V$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme, **Normal Forms** are critical because they **standardize the structure** of CFG productions, making automated parsing (like CYK algorithm) and theoretical proofs much simpler.

### 1.2 What is a "Normal Form"?
A **Normal Form** is a *restricted but equivalent* representation of a CFG where every production follows a specific rigid pattern. Any CFG generating a language $L$ (excluding $\epsilon$ in some cases) can be transformed into an equivalent grammar in normal form **without changing the language generated**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Standardized Packing" Metaphor**
> Imagine you have a chaotic wardrobe with clothes of all shapes (long rules, mixed symbols, $\epsilon$ rules). A "Normal Form" is like a **standardized suitcase** — every item (production) must fit into a fixed compartment size. You might need to fold, re-bundle, or split items, but the *contents* (the language) remain exactly the same. Two popular suitcase sizes: **Chomsky (2 compartments max)** and **Greibach (1 compartment, terminal-first)**.

### 1.3 Chomsky Normal Form (CNF) — Formal Definition
A CFG $G$ is in **Chomsky Normal Form** if every production rule is of one of the following two forms:
1. $A \rightarrow BC$, where $A, B, C \in V$ (i.e., a single non-terminal producing exactly two non-terminals)
2. $A \rightarrow a$, where $A \in V$ and $a \in T$ (i.e., a single non-terminal producing exactly one terminal)

**Restrictions**: The start symbol $S$ is **not allowed** to appear on the right-hand side of any rule. If $S$ appears in RHS, we introduce a new start symbol $S' \rightarrow S$. Also, $A \rightarrow \epsilon$ is **not allowed** in CNF (except possibly for the start symbol producing $\epsilon$).

### 1.4 Greibach Normal Form (GNF) — Formal Definition
A CFG $G$ is in **Greibach Normal Form** if every production rule is of the form:
$$A \rightarrow a\alpha$$
where $A \in V$, $a \in T$, and $\alpha \in V^*$.

**In words**: The right-hand side must begin with a **single terminal**, followed by **zero or more non-terminals**.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Visual Comparison**:
> - **CNF**: $A \rightarrow BC$ or $A \rightarrow a$ (Two non-terminals OR one terminal)
> - **GNF**: $A \rightarrow a\alpha$ (One terminal first, then any number of non-terminals)
> - GNF productions are *longer* but guarantee a terminal is generated in **one derivation step** — extremely useful for parsing!

### 1.5 Why Study Normal Forms?
- **Theoretical**: Simplifies proofs about CFLs (pumping lemma, closure properties).
- **Practical**: Enables **CYK (Cocke-Younger-Kasami) algorithm** for membership testing in $O(n^3)$ time, which requires CNF.
- **Parsing**: GNF is foundational for **top-down predictive parsers** (LL parsers).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Algorithm: Converting a CFG to Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)

The conversion is a **5-step systematic procedure**. Each step preserves the language generated by the grammar.

#### **STEP 1: Eliminate $\epsilon$-Productions (Nullable Variables)**
- Find all **nullable non-terminals** $A$ where $A \Rightarrow^* \epsilon$.
- Algorithm:
  1. Identify all $A$ such that $A \rightarrow \epsilon$ is a production. Mark them nullable.
  2. Recursively: If $B \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \cdots X_n$ and all $X_i$ are nullable, then $B$ is nullable.
- **Action**: For every production $B \rightarrow \alpha$ containing nullable symbols, add all combinations where one or more nullable symbols are **deleted** (but not all of them, to avoid reintroducing $\epsilon$).
- **Exception**: The production $S \rightarrow \epsilon$ (if $S$ is nullable) is handled separately in Step 5.

#### **STEP 2: Eliminate Unit Productions (Chains)**
- Unit production: $A \rightarrow B$ where $B \in V$.
- Algorithm:
  1. Find all **unit pairs** $(A, B)$ such that $A \Rightarrow^* B$ using only unit productions.
  2. For each pair $(A, B)$, add to the grammar all non-unit productions of $B$: i.e., if $B \rightarrow \alpha$ is a non-unit production, add $A \rightarrow \alpha$.
  3. **Delete** all unit productions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> After Step 2, the grammar has **no $\epsilon$-productions and no unit productions** (except possibly $S \rightarrow \epsilon$).

#### **STEP 3: Replace Terminals in Long Productions with Non-Terminals**
- In any production $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \cdots X_n$ with $n \geq 2$:
  - For each terminal $a$ appearing in the RHS (where $X_i = a$ and $n \geq 2$), introduce a new non-terminal $C_a$ with production $C_a \rightarrow a$.
  - Replace $a$ with $C_a$ in the long production.

#### **STEP 4: Break Long Productions into CNF Form**
- Convert every production with RHS length $\geq 3$ into a chain of binary productions.
- For $A \rightarrow B_1 B_2 \cdots B_n$ where $n \geq 3$:
  - Introduce new non-terminals $D_1, D_2, \ldots, D_{n-2}$.
  - Replace with:
    $$A \rightarrow B_1 D_1$$
    $$D_1 \rightarrow B_2 D_2$$
    $$D_2 \rightarrow B_3 D_3$$
    $$\vdots$$
    $$D_{n-2} \rightarrow B_{n-1} B_n$$

#### **STEP 5: Handle Start Symbol (if it appears on RHS)**
- If $S$ appears on the RHS of any production, introduce a new start symbol $S'$ with $S' \rightarrow S$. Then the new start is $S'$.

### 2.2 Algorithm: Converting a CFG to Greibach Normal Form (GNF)

The conversion is more involved. Standard approach: **First convert to CNF, then apply GNF conversion**.

#### **Sub-Steps (after CNF is achieved):**
1. **Order the non-terminals**: $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n$.
2. **Modify the grammar** so that $A_i$ productions either:
   - Begin with a terminal, or
   - Begin with $A_j$ where $j > i$ (i.e., RHS starts with a *higher-indexed* non-terminal).
3. This is done iteratively for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$ using **substitution**.
4. Finally, convert the remaining mixed terminals/non-terminals in RHS into the form $a\alpha$ (one terminal followed by non-terminals only).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Rule Form** | **Allowed** | **Not Allowed** |
|:-----------:|:-------------:|:-----------:|:---------------:|
| CNF Type 1 | $A \rightarrow BC$ | $A, B, C \in V$ | Mixing terminals on RHS |
| CNF Type 2 | $A \rightarrow a$ | $a \in T$ | Length $\geq 2$ with terminal |
| GNF | $A \rightarrow a\alpha$ | $a \in T, \alpha \in V^*$ | $A \rightarrow \epsilon$, $A \rightarrow B\alpha$ |
| Nullable Var | $A \Rightarrow^* \epsilon$ | Found via iterative scan | Cycles that don't terminate |
| Unit Pair | $A \Rightarrow^* B$ | Only non-terminals in chain | Terminals in between |

| **Conversion Step** | **Purpose** | **New Symbols Added** |
|:-------------------:|:-----------:|:---------------------:|
| Step 1 (CNF) | Remove $\epsilon$-rules | None |
| Step 2 (CNF) | Remove unit rules | None |
| Step 3 (CNF) | Isolate terminals | $C_a$ for each terminal $a$ in long rules |
| Step 4 (CNF) | Convert to binary | $D_1, D_2, \ldots$ dummy non-terminals |
| Step 5 (CNF) | Clean start symbol | $S'$ (new start) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: CNF is the **backbone of the CYK parsing algorithm** used in compilers, natural language processing, and bioinformatics (RNA structure prediction). GNF is fundamental in designing **recursive descent parsers** for programming languages.

### 2.4 Key Properties (Examination Gold)
- **Every CFL** (that is not empty) has a CFG in **CNF**.
