- **Every CFL** (that is not empty) has a CFG in **GNF**.
- If $G$ is in CNF, then **every derivation of a string of length $n \geq 1$** has exactly **$2n - 1$ steps**. This is crucial for the CYK algorithm and for proof-of-correctness arguments.
- GNF ensures **leftmost derivations produce a terminal at every step**, which makes the derivation tree very regular.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Conversion to CNF

**Given CFG $G$** with productions:
$$S \rightarrow ASA \mid aB$$
$$A \rightarrow B \mid S$$
$$B \rightarrow b \mid \epsilon$$

We need to convert this to CNF.

---

#### **Step 1: Eliminate $\epsilon$-Productions**

Identify nullable non-terminals:
- $B \rightarrow \epsilon$ is direct, so $B$ is nullable.
- $A \rightarrow B$, and $B$ is nullable, so $A$ is nullable.
- $S \rightarrow ASA$: since $A$ is nullable (both $A$'s on RHS are nullable), $S$ is nullable.

So nullable set = $\{A, B, S\}$.

Now add new productions by removing nullable variables from RHS (one at a time, at least one must remain):

For $S \rightarrow ASA$:
- Remove 1st $A$: $S \rightarrow SA$
- Remove 2nd $A$: $S \rightarrow AS$
- Remove both $A$'s: $S \rightarrow S$

For $S \rightarrow aB$:
- Remove $B$: $S \rightarrow a$

For $A \rightarrow B$:
- Remove $B$: $A \rightarrow \epsilon$ (delete this! $\epsilon$ elimination in progress)
- So $A \rightarrow \epsilon$ is removed. Keep $A \rightarrow B$ for now, will be deleted as unit.

For $A \rightarrow S$:
- Remove $S$: $A \rightarrow \epsilon$ (delete this!)

For $B \rightarrow b$: no nullable to remove.

Now **delete all $\epsilon$-productions** and add the new ones:

$$S \rightarrow ASA \mid aB \mid SA \mid AS \mid S \mid a$$
$$A \rightarrow B \mid S$$
$$B \rightarrow b$$

---

#### **Step 2: Eliminate Unit Productions**

Find all unit pairs $(X, Y)$ where $X \Rightarrow^* Y$ via unit productions:
- $(A, B)$, $(A, S)$, $(S, S)$ (trivial).

For each unit pair, add non-unit productions of $Y$ to $X$:
- $(A, B)$: $B \rightarrow b$ is non-unit. Add $A \rightarrow b$.
- $(A, S)$: $S$ has non-unit productions: $S \rightarrow ASA, aB, SA, AS, a$. Add all to $A$.
- $(S, S)$: trivial, ignored.

Delete all unit productions. Updated grammar:

$$S \rightarrow ASA \mid aB \mid SA \mid AS \mid a$$
$$A \rightarrow b \mid ASA \mid aB \mid SA \mid AS \mid a$$
$$B \rightarrow b$$

---

#### **Step 3: Replace Terminals in Long Productions**

Terminals in long productions: $a$ appears in $aB$, $ASA$, $aB$, $SA$, $AS$, $ASA$, $aB$, $SA$, $AS$.

Introduce new non-terminals:
- $C_a \rightarrow a$
- $C_b \rightarrow b$ (though $b$ only appears in length-1 production $B \rightarrow b$, this is fine; we don't need to replace it).

Replace terminals in productions of length $\geq 2$:

$$S \rightarrow ASA \mid C_a B \mid SA \mid AS \mid a$$
$$A \rightarrow ASA \mid C_a B \mid SA \mid AS \mid a \mid b$$
$$B \rightarrow b$$
$$C_a \rightarrow a$$

---

#### **Step 4: Break Long Productions into CNF Form**

Long productions: $S \rightarrow ASA$ (length 3), $A \rightarrow ASA$ (length 3).

For $S \rightarrow ASA$: Introduce $D_1$.
$$S \rightarrow A D_1$$
$$D_1 \rightarrow SA$$

For $A \rightarrow ASA$: Introduce $D_2$.
$$A \rightarrow A D_2$$
$$D_2 \rightarrow SA$$

For $S \rightarrow C_a B$ and $A \rightarrow C_a B$: length is 2, already in CNF.
For $S \rightarrow SA$, $S \rightarrow AS$, $A \rightarrow SA$, $A \rightarrow AS$: length 2, OK.
For $S \rightarrow a$, $A \rightarrow a$, $A \rightarrow b$, $B \rightarrow b$, $C_a \rightarrow a$: length 1, OK.

#### **Final CNF Grammar:**

$$\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow A D_1 \mid C_a B \mid SA \mid AS \mid a \\
A &\rightarrow A D_2 \mid C_a B \mid SA \mid AS \mid a \mid b \\
B &\rightarrow b \\
C_a &\rightarrow a \\
D_1 &\rightarrow SA \\
D_2 &\rightarrow SA
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Verification: Every production is either $A \rightarrow BC$ (two non-terminals) or $A \rightarrow a$ (one terminal). The CNF is achieved. ✓

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Decision Flowchart for CNF Conversion

| **Input Production Type** | **Action** | **Output** |
|:-------------------------:|:----------:|:----------:|
| $A \rightarrow \epsilon$ | Add all combinations without $A$ on RHS; delete | Non-$\epsilon$ rules |
| $A \rightarrow B$ (unit) | Add $B$'s non-unit rules to $A$; delete | Non-unit rules |
| $A \rightarrow a$ | Keep as is | $A \rightarrow a$ ✓ (CNF) |
| $A \rightarrow aB$ (length 2) | Replace $a$ with $C_a$; add $C_a \rightarrow a$ | $A \rightarrow C_a B$, $C_a \rightarrow a$ |
| $A \rightarrow B_1 B_2 \cdots B_n$ ($n \geq 3$) | Introduce $D_1, \ldots, D_{n-2}$ | Chain of binary rules |

### 3.3 GNF Conversion Sketch (Common KTU Pattern)

**Given a CNF grammar with ordered non-terminals** $A_1, A_2, A_3$:

$$A_1 \rightarrow A_2 A_3 \mid a$$
$$A_2 \rightarrow A_3 A_1 \mid b$$
$$A_3 \rightarrow A_1 A_2 \mid A_1 A_3 \mid c$$

**Goal**: Transform so each $A_i$ production starts with a terminal.

**Step**: For $i = 1$: Already has $A_1 \rightarrow a$ (good). For $A_1 \rightarrow A_2 A_3$: substitute $A_2$'s rules:
$$A_1 \rightarrow (A_3 A_1) A_3 \mid b A_3 \mid a$$
$$A_1 \rightarrow A_3 A_1 A_3 \mid b A_3 \mid a$$

**Continue iteratively** for $A_2, A_3$. The final GNF will have all productions of form $A_i \rightarrow a\alpha$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CNF Conversion Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Original CFG G"] --> B["Step 1: Identify Nullable Variables"]
    B --> C["Step 1: Add Combinations, Delete epsilon-rules"]
    C --> D["Step 2: Find Unit Pairs"]
    D --> E["Step 2: Substitute, Delete Unit Productions"]
    E --> F["Step 3: Isolate Terminals in Long Rules"]
    F --> G["Step 3: Introduce Ca for each terminal a"]
    G --> H["Step 4: Break Rules of Length 3+"]
    H --> I["Step 4: Introduce Dummy Non-terminals D1, D2"]
    I --> J["Step 5: Check Start Symbol on RHS"]
    J --> K{"S appears on RHS?"}
    K -->|Yes| L["Introduce S prime, S prime -> S"]
    K -->|No| M["Final CNF Grammar G prime"]
    L --> M
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram**: Each block represents a transformation stage. The final output $G'$ is a CFG in CNF that generates **exactly the same language** as the original $G$ (except possibly $\epsilon$).

### 4.2 Production Form Comparison Chart

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["General CFG: A -> alpha"] --> B{"Normal Form?"}
    B -->|CNF| C["A -> BC OR A -> a"]
    B -->|GNF| D["A -> a alpha where a is terminal"]
    C --> E["Two non-terminals max"]
    C --> F["Or single terminal"]
    D --> G["Terminal MUST be first symbol"]
    D --> H["Rest are non-terminals only"]
```

### 4.3 Derivation Tree Height Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CNF["CNF Derivation Tree"]
        S1["S"] --> X1["A"]
        S1 --> Y1["B"]
        X1 --> X2["C"]
        X1 --> X3["D"]
    end
    subgraph GNF["GNF Derivation Tree"]
        S2["S"] --> T1["a"]
        S2 --> N1["A"]
        N1 --> T2["b"]
        N1 --> N2["B"]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **CNF**, the derivation tree is a **binary tree** (every internal node has exactly 2 children). In **GNF**, the leftmost child is always a **terminal** (leaf in first level), simplifying leftmost derivation analysis.

### 4.4 Algorithm Decision Flowchart (Quick Revision)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1["Given CFG G"] --> Q2{"epsilon-productions?"}
    Q2 -->|Yes| R1["Apply Step 1: Eliminate"]
    Q2 -->|No| R2["Skip Step 1"]
    R1 --> R3{"Unit productions?"}
    R2 --> R3
    R3 -->|Yes| R4["Apply Step 2: Eliminate"]
    R3 -->|No| R5["Skip Step 2"]
    R4 --> R6["Apply Steps 3, 4: Convert to Binary"]
    R5 --> R6
    R6 --> R7["Apply Step 5: Start Symbol"]
    R7 --> OUT["CNF Grammar Achieved"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**Define Chomsky Normal Form (CNF) for a Context-Free Grammar. List the two conditions that every production must satisfy.**

**Model Answer**:
A CFG $G = (V, T, P, S)$ is in **Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)** if every production in $P$ is of one of the following two forms:
1. $A \rightarrow BC$, where $A, B, C \in V$ (non-terminal producing exactly two non-terminals)
2. $A \rightarrow a$, where $A \in V$ and $a \in T$ (non-terminal producing a single terminal)

The start symbol $S$ is not allowed to appear on the RHS of any production, and $\epsilon$-productions are not permitted (except possibly $S \rightarrow \epsilon$).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model QP]**
**What is Greibach Normal Form (GNF)? How does it differ from CNF in terms of the structure of the right-hand side of productions?**

**Model Answer**:
A CFG is in **Greibach Normal Form (GNF)** if every production is of the form:
$$A \rightarrow a\alpha$$
where $A \in V$, $a \in T$ is a **single terminal**, and $\alpha \in V^*$ is a (possibly empty) string of non-terminals.

**Key Differences from CNF**:
- In **CNF**, the RHS is either two non-terminals ($BC$) or one terminal ($a$).
- In **GNF**, the RHS **must begin with a single terminal** followed by zero or more non-terminals.
- GNF guarantees that a terminal is generated in **every derivation step**, while CNF may require up to $|w|$ steps to generate a single terminal.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Convert the following CFG into Chomsky Normal Form (CNF): **(7 Marks)**

$$S \rightarrow aAb \mid bBA$$
$$A \rightarrow abA \mid \epsilon$$
$$B \rightarrow bAB \mid aA$$
$$C \rightarrow aSb$$

where $S$ is the start symbol.

**(b)** Define Greibach Normal Form. Convert the following CNF grammar into GNF: **(7 Marks)**

$$S \rightarrow AA \mid a$$
$$A \rightarrow BS \mid b$$
$$B \rightarrow SA \mid c$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(a):**

**Step 1: Identify Nullable Variables**
- $A \rightarrow \epsilon$ is direct, so $A$ is nullable.
- Check other rules: No other variable is forced to be nullable.

Nullable set = $\{A\}$.

**Step 2: Eliminate $\epsilon$-Productions**

For $S \rightarrow aAb$: remove $A$ → $S \rightarrow ab$
For $S \rightarrow bBA$: no $A$ on RHS.
For $A \rightarrow abA$: remove $A$ → $A \rightarrow ab$
For $B \rightarrow bAB$: remove $A$ → $B \rightarrow bB$
For $B \rightarrow aA$: remove $A$ → $B \rightarrow a$
For $C \rightarrow aSb$: no $A$ on RHS.

Delete $A \rightarrow \epsilon$. Updated grammar:
$$S \rightarrow aAb \mid ab \mid bBA$$
$$A \rightarrow abA \mid ab$$
$$B \rightarrow bAB \mid bB \mid aA \mid a$$
$$C \rightarrow aSb$$

**Step 3: Eliminate Unit Productions**
No unit productions exist. Skip.

**Step 4: Replace Terminals in Long Productions**

Introduce $C_a \rightarrow a$ and $C_b \rightarrow b$.

Replace terminals in RHS of length $\geq 2$:
- $S \rightarrow aAb$ becomes $S \rightarrow C_a A C_b$
- $S \rightarrow ab$ becomes $S \rightarrow C_a C_b$
- $S \rightarrow bBA$ becomes $S \rightarrow C_b B A$
- $A \rightarrow abA$ becomes $A \rightarrow C_a C_b A$
- $A \rightarrow ab$ becomes $A \rightarrow C_a C_b$
- $B \rightarrow bAB$ becomes $B \rightarrow C_b A B$
- $B \rightarrow bB$ becomes $B \rightarrow C_b B$
- $B \rightarrow aA$ becomes $B \rightarrow C_a A$
- $C \rightarrow aSb$ becomes $C \rightarrow C_a S C_b$

**Step 5: Break Long Productions (length $\geq 3$)**

- $A \rightarrow C_a C_b A$ (length 3): Introduce $D_1$.
  $A \rightarrow C_a D_1$, $D_1 \rightarrow C_b A$
- $B \rightarrow C_b A B$ (length 3): Introduce $D_2$.
  $B \rightarrow C_b D_2$, $D_2 \rightarrow A B$
- $C \rightarrow C_a S C_b$ (length 3): Introduce $D_3$.
  $C \rightarrow C_a D_3$, $D_3 \rightarrow S C_b$

**Step 6: Check Start Symbol** — $S$ does not appear on RHS. Skip.

**Final CNF Grammar**:
$$\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow C_a A C_b \mid C_a C_b \mid C_b B A \\
A &\rightarrow C_a C_b \mid C_a D_1 \\
B &\rightarrow C_b B \mid C_a A \mid a \mid C_b D_2 \\
C &\rightarrow C_a D_3 \\
C_a &\rightarrow a \\
C_b &\rightarrow b \\
D_1 &\rightarrow C_b A \\
D_2 &\rightarrow A B \\
D_3 &\rightarrow S C_b
\end{aligned}$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(b):**

**Greibach Normal Form Definition**:
A CFG is in GNF if every production is of the form $A \rightarrow a\alpha$ where $A \in V$, $a \in T$, and $\alpha \in V^*$.

**Conversion to GNF:**

Given CNF grammar with order $S, A, B$ (i.e., $A_1 = S, A_2 = A, A_3 = B$):

$$S \rightarrow AA \mid a$$
$$A \rightarrow BS \mid b$$
$$B \rightarrow SA \mid c$$

**Iteration for $A_1 = S$**: $S \rightarrow a$ is in GNF form. For $S \rightarrow AA$, substitute $A$'s rules:
$$S \rightarrow (BS) A \mid bA \mid a$$
$$S \rightarrow BSA \mid bA \mid a$$

**Iteration for $A_2 = A$**: $A \rightarrow b$ is in GNF. For $A \rightarrow BS$, substitute $B$'s rules:
$$A \rightarrow (SA)S \mid cS \mid b$$
$$A \rightarrow SAS \mid cS \mid b$$

**Iteration for $A_3 = B$**: $B \rightarrow c$ is in GNF. For $B \rightarrow SA$, substitute $S$'s updated rules:
$$B \rightarrow (BSA) A \mid (bA) A \mid aA \mid c$$
$$B \rightarrow BSAA \mid bAA \mid aA \mid c$$

**Final check**: $B \rightarrow BSAA$ has $B$ on LHS beginning with non-terminal. We need to eliminate this by substituting again. Iterating once more:
$$B \rightarrow (SAS)SAA \mid (cS)SAA \mid bSAA \mid bAA \mid aA \mid c$$
$$B \rightarrow SASSAA \mid cSSAA \mid bSAA \mid bAA \mid aA \mid c$$

**GNF Grammar (Final)**:
$$\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow a \mid bA \mid BSA \\
A &\rightarrow b \mid cS \mid SAS \\
B &\rightarrow c \mid aA \mid bAA \mid bSAA \mid cSSAA \mid SASSAA
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1**: Forgetting to delete the original $A \rightarrow B$ unit production after substitution. Always remove the original rule once substituted.
> - **Common Mistake 2**: In GNF, failing to recursively substitute until the leading non-terminal has an index **greater** than the LHS variable.
> - **Common Mistake 3**: Missing the introduction of $S'$ when $S$ appears on the RHS during CNF conversion.
> - **Mark Loss**: Students often skip the "check" step. KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for verifying the final grammar is indeed in the claimed normal form.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a)** Convert the following CFG to CNF: **(7 Marks)**

$$S \rightarrow AB \mid \epsilon$$
$$A \rightarrow aA \mid bB$$
$$B \rightarrow b \mid bS$$

**(b)** Prove that every CFL (not containing $\epsilon$) has a CFG in CNF. Outline the proof structure with all 5 steps. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(a):**

**Step 1: Identify Nullable Variables**
- $S \rightarrow \epsilon$ is direct, so $S$ is nullable.
- $B \rightarrow bS$: $S$ is nullable, so $B$ has $b$ on RHS with nullable $S$ → $B$ is **not** automatically nullable (need all symbols nullable).
- $A \rightarrow bB$: requires $B$ nullable, but $B$ is not yet nullable.

Nullable set = $\{S\}$.

**Step 2: Eliminate $\epsilon$-Productions**

For $S \rightarrow AB$: remove $S$ → $S \rightarrow A$ (delete this as unit, handle in Step 3).
Wait, $A$ and $B$ are not nullable. So removing $S$ gives $S \rightarrow A$ and $S \rightarrow B$ if both were nullable. Since only $S$ is nullable, and $S$ is not on RHS of $AB$... 

Let me re-examine: $S \rightarrow AB$ — neither $A$ nor $B$ is nullable, so this rule contributes nothing when removing nullable symbols.

For $B \rightarrow bS$: remove $S$ → $B \rightarrow b$.

Delete $S \rightarrow \epsilon$. New productions:
$$S \rightarrow AB$$
$$A \rightarrow aA \mid bB$$
$$B \rightarrow b \mid bS \mid b$$

(Simplify: $B \rightarrow b$ appears twice, keep one)

**Step 3: Eliminate Unit Productions** — None exist.

**Step 4: Replace Terminals in Long Productions**
- $A \rightarrow aA$: length 2 with terminal. Introduce $C_a \rightarrow a$. Replace: $A \rightarrow C_a A$
- $A \rightarrow bB$: length 2 with terminal. $A \rightarrow C_b B$, $C_b \rightarrow b$
- $B \rightarrow bS$: length 2 with terminal. $B \rightarrow C_b S$
- $S \rightarrow AB$: length 2, no terminal. OK.
- $B \rightarrow b$: length 1, OK.
- $C_a \rightarrow a$, $C_b \rightarrow b$: length 1, OK.

**Step 5: Break Long Productions** — No rule has length $\geq 3$. Skip.

**Step 6: Start Symbol** — $S$ does not appear on RHS. Skip.

**Final CNF**:
$$\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow AB \\
A &\rightarrow C_a A \mid C_b B \\
B &\rightarrow b \mid C_b S \\
C_a &\rightarrow a \\
C_b &\rightarrow b
\end{aligned}$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(b):**

**Proof Outline**:
*Claim*: For every CFG $G = (V, T, P, S)$ with $L(G) \neq \emptyset$ and $\epsilon \notin L(G)$, there exists an equivalent CFG $G' = (V', T, P', S')$ in CNF.

**Proof by Construction** (5 steps):

1. **Step 1**: Construct $G_1$ from $G$ by eliminating all $\epsilon$-productions (except possibly $S \rightarrow \epsilon$ if $\epsilon \in L(G)$). This is done by:
   - Finding the set $\text{NULL}$ of nullable variables via fixed-point iteration.
   - For each rule $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \cdots X_n$, add all rules $A \rightarrow Y_1 Y_2 \cdots Y_n$ where each $Y_i$ is either $X_i$ or $\epsilon$ (but at least one $Y_i = X_i$).

2. **Step 2**: Construct $G_2$ from $G_1$ by eliminating unit productions. Find the set of unit pairs $U = \{(A, B) : A \Rightarrow^* B \text{ via unit productions}\}$. For each such pair, add the non-unit productions of $B$ to $A$. Delete all unit rules.

3. **Step 3**: Construct $G_3$ from $G_2$ by replacing terminals in RHS of length $\geq 2$ with new non-terminals. For each terminal $a$ in such a position, introduce $C_a \rightarrow a$.

4. **Step 4**: Construct $G_4$ from $G_3$ by breaking rules of length $\geq 3$ into binary rules using new dummy non-terminals $D_i$.

5. **Step 5**: Construct $G'$ from $G_4$ by ensuring the start symbol $S$ does not appear on the RHS. If it does, introduce $S'$ with $S' \rightarrow S$, and the new start is $S'$.

At each step, we must prove **language preservation** (i.e., $L(G_i) = L(G_{i-1})$). The final grammar $G'$ satisfies both CNF conditions, and $L(G') = L(G)$. $\blacksquare$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CNF** is a CFG where every production is $A \rightarrow BC$ (two non-terminals) or $A \rightarrow a$ (one terminal).
- **GNF** is a CFG where every production is $A \rightarrow a\alpha$ (one terminal first, followed by non-terminals only).
- The **5-step CNF conversion** is: (1) eliminate $\epsilon$, (2) eliminate unit, (3) isolate terminals, (4) break long rules, (5) clean start symbol.
- For CNF, a string of length $n \geq 1$ has a derivation of exactly **$2n - 1$ steps** — a key fact for parsing complexity.
- **GNF conversion** is harder and typically done *after* CNF, using ordered substitution.
- **Every non-empty CFL** (without $\epsilon$) admits both a CNF and a GNF grammar.
- **CYK algorithm** works **only on CNF grammars** — remember this for compiler design questions.
- In **KTU exams**, the most commonly tested conversion is **CNF** (7-mark sub-question in Part B).
- **Pitfall to avoid**: Always check whether the **start symbol** appears on the RHS after conversion — failure to do so is a frequent 1-mark deduction.
- **Identification tip**: If asked "convert to CNF", never directly write $A \rightarrow a$ rules; first do all eliminations, then break into binary form.
- **GNF subtlety**: Substitution order matters — always substitute from **lower-indexed to higher-indexed** non-terminals.
- **Practical significance**: CNF → CYK parser; GNF → predictive top-down parsers; both are foundational in compiler construction.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Trick**: Remember the mnemonic **"EUST-I"** for CNF steps: **E**psilon removal → **U**nit production removal → **S**ubstitute terminals → (break to **T**wo) → **I**ndependent start. For GNF, think **"Terminal First Always"**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nondeterministic Pushdown Automata (PDA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET414 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Nondeterministic Pushdown Automata (PDA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Pushdown Automaton (PDA)

A **Pushdown Automaton (PDA)** is a finite-state machine augmented with an auxiliary unbounded **stack** memory. The stack provides last-in, first-out (LIFO) access and is the only memory beyond the finite control. The *nondeterministic* variant (NPDA) allows multiple possible moves from a single configuration, which is essential for recognizing the full class of **Context-Free Languages (CFLs)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal 7-tuple Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A PDA is defined as a 7-tuple
> $$M = (Q,\ \Sigma,\ \Gamma,\ \delta,\ q_0,\ Z_0,\ F)$$
> where:
> - $Q$ = **finite** non-empty set of **states**
> - $\Sigma$ = finite **input alphabet**
> - $\Gamma$ = finite **stack alphabet**
> - $\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times \Gamma \longrightarrow \mathcal{P}_{\text{finite}}(Q \times \Gamma^*)$ = **transition function**
> - $q_0 \in Q$ = **start state**
> - $Z_0 \in \Gamma$ = **initial stack symbol** (always at the bottom of the stack)
> - $F \subseteq Q$ = set of **final (accepting) states**

The transition function output is a *finite* set of pairs $(p, \gamma)$, where $p$ is the next state and $\gamma \in \Gamma^*$ is the string of stack symbols that **replaces** the consumed top symbol.

## 1.2 Why "Nondeterministic"?

A PDA is **nondeterministic** when, for some triple $(q, a, X)$, the set $\delta(q, a, X)$ contains more than one pair. This is the *only* formal difference from a deterministic PDA (DPDA). The empty string $\varepsilon$ may also be read, allowing moves without consuming input — this is critical for matching operations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Determinism vs. Nondeterminism in PDA — KTU Critical Distinction:**
> - **DPDA**: $\delta(q, a, X)$ has at most one element for every $(q, a, X)$, and if $\delta(q, \varepsilon, X) \neq \emptyset$ then $\delta(q, a, X) = \emptyset$ for all $a \in \Sigma$.
> - **NPDA**: No such restriction. Multiple moves allowed at the same configuration.
> - **Theorem (KTU 2024 syllabus core result):** Nondeterminism in PDA is **strictly more powerful** than determinism. There exist CFLs (e.g., palindromes $L = \{ww^R\}$) that *cannot* be recognized by any DPDA.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Climbing the Stairs" Intuition

Imagine you are a **hiker** at the base of a mountain with a **backpack** (the stack). Your **eyes** (finite state) can only see a tiny part of the path, and you carry a **map** (the input tape) listing the steps. To climb safely, every time you step forward by an "**a**", you place a stone in your backpack. When you must step backward (after a "**b**"), you take one stone out. If you reach the summit with an **empty backpack** and the right state (e.g., "summit reached"), the trip is accepted.

The **nondeterminism** comes in when you reach a fork in the trail — multiple valid paths may exist, and the PDA "guesses" the correct one. The stack ensures that whatever was pushed must be popped in **reverse order** (LIFO), which is exactly the structural constraint of context-free languages.

## 1.4 Acceptance Modes — Two Equivalent Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mode 1 — Acceptance by Final State ($L(M)$):**
> A string $w$ is accepted if there exists a sequence of moves such that
> $$(q_0,\ w,\ Z_0) \vdash^*_M (q,\ \varepsilon,\ \gamma)$$
> for some $q \in F$ and any $\gamma \in \Gamma^*$ (stack may be non-empty).
>
> **Mode 2 — Acceptance by Empty Stack ($N(M)$):**
> A string $w$ is accepted if there exists a sequence of moves such that
> $$(q_0,\ w,\ Z_0) \vdash^*_M (q,\ \varepsilon,\ \varepsilon)$$
> for any $q \in Q$ (state is irrelevant; stack must be empty).

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalence Theorem (KTU High-Yield):** For every PDA $M_1$ accepting by final state, there exists a PDA $M_2$ accepting by empty stack such that $L(M_1) = N(M_2)$, and vice versa. The proof uses a stack-bottom marker $X_0$ that is destroyed on acceptance to transition to an "emptying state."

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Single Move

A transition $\delta(q, a, X) = \{(p_1, \gamma_1), (p_2, \gamma_2), \ldots, (p_k, \gamma_k)\}$ means:

- The PDA is in state $q$.
- The current input symbol is $a \in \Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}$.
- The current top-of-stack symbol is $X \in \Gamma$.
- Nondeterministically, the PDA **chooses** one of the $k$ alternatives:
  - Move to state $p_i$,
  - Pop $X$ from the stack,
  - Push the string $\gamma_i$ (leftmost symbol becomes new top).

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Cases (often tested in KTU):**
> - $|\gamma| = 0$ (empty string) ⇒ pure **POP** operation.
> - $|\gamma| = 1$ and the single symbol is $X$ ⇒ **NO-OP** (symbol replaced by itself).
> - $|\gamma| > 1$ ⇒ the leftmost symbol of $\gamma$ ends up on top.
> - Reading $\varepsilon$ allows the PDA to manipulate the stack without consuming input (e.g., to match a production of a CFG).

## 2.2 Instantaneous Description (ID) — The "Configuration"

An **Instantaneous Description (ID)** is a triple $(q, w, \gamma)$ where:
- $q \in Q$ is the current state,
- $w \in \Sigma^*$ is the **unread** input,
- $\gamma \in \Gamma^*$ is the **stack contents** (leftmost = top).

A *move* is denoted by $\vdash_M$, and a sequence of moves by $\vdash^*_M$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Turnstile Notation (KTU Standard):**
> If $(p, \beta) \in \delta(q, a, X)$, then for any $\alpha \in \Gamma^*$ and $w \in \Sigma^*$:
> $$(q,\ aw,\ X\alpha) \vdash_M (p,\ w,\ \beta\alpha)$$
> Note: $X\alpha$ is read as "stack-top $X$ followed by rest $\alpha$"; $\beta$ replaces $X$ but $\alpha$ remains below it.

## 2.3 Language of a PDA

$$L(M) = \{w \in \Sigma^* \mid (q_0, w, Z_0) \vdash^*_M (q, \varepsilon, \gamma),\ \text{for some } q \in F, \gamma \in \Gamma^*\}$$

$$N(M) = \{w \in \Sigma^* \mid (q_0, w, Z_0) \vdash^*_M (q, \varepsilon, \varepsilon),\ \text{for some } q \in Q\}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Mathematical Statement | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---------|------------------------|------------------|
| 1 | PDA tuple | $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$ | $\delta$ codomain is $\mathcal{P}_{\text{finite}}(Q \times \Gamma^*)$ |
| 2 | Move (with input) | $\delta(q, a, X) \ni (p, \gamma)$ | $a \in \Sigma$, $X \in \Gamma$ |
| 3 | Move (ε-input) | $\delta(q, \varepsilon, X) \ni (p, \gamma)$ | Allowed even at end of input |
| 4 | ID evolution | $(q, aw, X\alpha) \vdash (p, w, \beta\alpha)$ | $\alpha$ stays below $\beta$ |
| 5 | Final-state accept | $L(M) = \{w \mid (q_0, w, Z_0) \vdash^* (q, \varepsilon, \gamma),\ q \in F\}$ | Stack may remain non-empty |
| 6 | Empty-stack accept | $N(M) = \{w \mid (q_0, w, Z_0) \vdash^* (q, \varepsilon, \varepsilon)\}$ | State is irrelevant |
| 7 | Equivalence | $L(M_1) = N(M_2)$ always constructible | Use marker $X_0$ trick |
| 8 | NPDA power | $L \text{ is CFL} \iff \exists \text{ NPDA } M: L = L(M)$ | Sipser's Theorem (core KTU result) |
| 9 | CFG → PDA | Single-state PDA: $q = q_0$ for all rules | Terminals consumed, nonterminals expanded |
| 10 | Stack growth bound | No upper bound on $\vert \gamma \vert$ | Unbounded memory gives CFL-recognition power |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of PDAs

- **Compiler design**: parsing arithmetic expressions, balanced parentheses, and nested block structures.
- **XML/HTML validation**: every opening tag must be closed in LIFO order.
- **Programming language syntax**: function calls, recursion stack, scope nesting.
- **Network protocols**: matching open/close request-response frames in a session.
- **DNA sequence analysis**: detecting palindromic substructures in bioinformatics pipelines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Constructing an NPDA for $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\}$

**Strategy:** While reading $a$'s, push one $A$ per $a$. On the first $b$, switch to "matching" mode. For each $b$, pop one $A$. Accept if input ends exactly when stack empties to the initial marker $Z_0$.

**Formal Definition:**

$$M = (\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}, \{a, b\}, \{A, Z_0\}, \delta, q_0, Z_0, \{q_2\})$$

**Transitions** (each $\delta$-clause is a separate move):

| Transition | Meaning |
|------------|---------|
| $\delta(q_0, a, Z_0) = \{(q_0, A Z_0)\}$ | Read first $a$, push $A$, keep $Z_0$ at bottom |
| $\delta(q_0, a, A) = \{(q_0, A A)\}$ | Read more $a$'s, push $A$ on top |
| $\delta(q_0, b, A) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\}$ | First $b$: switch to matching mode, pop $A$ |
| $\delta(q_1, b, A) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\}$ | More $b$'s: pop one $A$ each |
| $\delta(q_1, \varepsilon, Z_0) = \{(q_2, Z_0)\}$ | Input empty, only $Z_0$ left → accept |

**Trace of $w = aaabbb$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(q_0,\ aaabbb,\ Z_0) &\vdash (q_0,\ aabbb,\ A Z_0) &&\text{read }a,\text{ push }A \\
&\vdash (q_0,\ abbb,\ AA Z_0) &&\text{read }a,\text{ push }A \\
&\vdash (q_0,\ bbb,\ AAA Z_0) &&\text{read }a,\text{ push }A \\
&\vdash (q_1,\ bb,\ AA Z_0) &&\text{read }b,\text{ pop }A,\text{ switch state} \\
&\vdash (q_1,\ b,\ A Z_0) &&\text{read }b,\text{ pop }A \\
&\vdash (q_1,\ \varepsilon,\ Z_0) &&\text{read }b,\text{ pop }A \\
&\vdash (q_2,\ \varepsilon,\ Z_0) &&\text{ε-move to accept state}
\end{aligned}
$$

Accepting state $q_2$ reached ⇒ $w \in L(M)$. ✓

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: NPDA for $L = \{w w^R \mid w \in \{a, b\}^*\}$ (Even-Length Palindromes)

**Strategy:** Push every symbol read. At some nondeterministically chosen point, switch to "guessing the middle" via an ε-move. Then match each new symbol against the top of the stack, popping on match.

**Formal Definition:**

$$M = (\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}, \{a, b\}, \{a, b, Z_0\}, \delta, q_0, Z_0, \{q_2\})$$

**Transitions:**

| # | Transition | Interpretation |
|---|------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\delta(q_0, a, Z_0) = \{(q_0, a Z_0)\}$ | Push $a$ on $Z_0$ |
| 2 | $\delta(q_0, b, Z_0) = \{(q_0, b Z_0)\}$ | Push $b$ on $Z_0$ |
| 3 | $\delta(q_0, a, a) = \{(q_0, a a)\}$ | Push $a$ on $a$ |
| 4 | $\delta(q_0, a, b) = \{(q_0, a b)\}$ | Push $a$ on $b$ |
| 5 | $\delta(q_0, b, a) = \{(q_0, b a)\}$ | Push $b$ on $a$ |
| 6 | $\delta(q_0, b, b) = \{(q_0, b b)\}$ | Push $b$ on $b$ |
| 7 | $\delta(q_0, \varepsilon, Z_0) = \{(q_2, Z_0)\}$ | Empty input is palindrome (length 0) |
| 8 | $\delta(q_0, \varepsilon, a) = \{(q_1, a)\}$ | **Guess middle** — top is $a$, switch state |
| 9 | $\delta(q_0, \varepsilon, b) = \{(q_1, b)\}$ | **Guess middle** — top is $b$, switch state |
| 10 | $\delta(q_1, a, a) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\}$ | Match and pop |
| 11 | $\delta(q_1, b, b) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\}$ | Match and pop |
| 12 | $\delta(q_1, \varepsilon, Z_0) = \{(q_2, Z_0)\}$ | Stack empty at $Z_0$ → accept |

**Trace of $w = abba$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(q_0,\ abba,\ Z_0) &\vdash (q_0,\ bba,\ a Z_0) &&\text{read }a,\text{ push }a \\
&\vdash (q_0,\ ba,\ ba Z_0) &&\text{read }b,\text{ push }b \\
&\vdash (q_1,\ ba,\ ba Z_0) &&\text{ε-guess middle; pop guess? No—keep }ba \\
&\text{(wrong guess; another path:)} \\
(q_0,\ ba,\ ba Z_0) &\vdash (q_0,\ a,\ bba Z_0) &&\text{read }b,\text{ push }b \\
&\vdash (q_1,\ a,\ bba Z_0) &&\text{ε-guess middle, pop top: }a \\
(q_1,\ a,\ bba Z_0) &\vdash (q_1,\ \varepsilon,\ b a Z_0) &&\text{mismatch! abort} \\
&\text{(correct path:)} \\
(q_0,\ abba,\ Z_0) &\vdash (q_0,\ bba,\ a Z_0) &&\text{push }a \\
&\vdash (q_0,\ ba,\ ba Z_0) &&\text{push }b \\
&\vdash (q_0,\ a,\ bba Z_0) &&\text{push }b \\
&\vdash (q_1,\ a,\ a Z_0) &&\text{ε-pop top }b \\
&\vdash (q_1,\ \varepsilon,\ Z_0) &&\text{match }a,\text{ pop }a \\
&\vdash (q_2,\ \varepsilon,\ Z_0) &&\text{ε-move to accept}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why DPDA fails here:** A deterministic PDA cannot "remember" the midpoint of an unknown-length string. The nondeterministic ε-move at the right instant is what gives NPDA its extra power. This makes $ww^R$ a **canonical non-deterministic CFL**.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Conversion from CFG to PDA (S → aSb | ε)

**Sipser's Construction** (single-state PDA):

$$M = (\{q\}, \{a, b\}, \{S, a, b, Z_0\}, \delta, q, Z_0, \emptyset)$$

**Rules:**

| # | Production | PDA Transition | Logic |
|---|------------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | $S \rightarrow aSb$ | $\delta(q, a, S) \ni (q, S b S)$ | Replace $S$ by right side |
| 2 | $S \rightarrow aSb$ | $\delta(q, b, b) = \{(q, \varepsilon)\}$ | Match terminal $b$, pop |
| 3 | $S \rightarrow aSb$ | $\delta(q, a, a) = \{(q, \varepsilon)\}$ | Match terminal $a$, pop |
| 4 | $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | $\delta(q, \varepsilon, S) = \{(q, \varepsilon)\}$ | Eliminate $S$ (leftmost derivation step) |
| 5 | Initial | $\delta(q, \varepsilon, Z_0) \ni (q, S Z_0)$ | Push start symbol $S$ |

**Trace of $w = aabb$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(q,\ aabb,\ Z_0) &\vdash (q,\ aabb,\ S Z_0) &&\text{push }S \text{ (rule 5)} \\
&\vdash (q,\ abb,\ SbSZ_0) &&\text{expand } S \to aSb \text{ (rule 1)} \\
&\vdash (q,\ bb,\ bSbSZ_0) &&\text{match } a \text{ (rule 3)} \\
&\vdash (q,\ b,\ SbSZ_0) &&\text{match } b \text{ (rule 2)} \\
&\vdash (q,\ b,\ bSZ_0) &&\text{expand } S \to aSb \text{ (rule 1)} \\
&\vdash (q,\ \varepsilon,\ SZ_0) &&\text{match } b \text{ (rule 2)} \\
&\vdash (q,\ \varepsilon,\ Z_0) &&\text{expand } S \to \varepsilon \text{ (rule 4)} \\
&\text{ACCEPT (empty stack mode)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Theorem (Sipser):** A language $L$ is context-free **if and only if** there exists an NPDA $M$ such that $L = L(M)$. This bidirectional result is the cornerstone of Module 3.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | DFA | DPDA | NPDA |
|----------|-----|------|------|
| Memory | Finite state only | Finite state + Stack | Finite state + Stack |
| Transitions per step | Exactly 1 | At most 1 (no ε with input) | Finite many allowed |
| Power | Regular langs | DCFL ⊂ CFL | Full CFL |
| Example language | $(ab)^*$ | $\{a^n b^n\}$ | $\{w w^R\}$ |
| Equivalence to CFG | No | No | **Yes** |
| Decidable emptiness? | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Decidable equivalence? | Yes | **No** (open in some texts) | **No** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PDA Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TAPE[INPUT TAPE]
        T1[... a b a b]
        T2[READ HEAD -->]
    end
    subgraph FSM[FINITE STATE CONTROL]
        S1[(Current state q in Q)]
    end
    subgraph STK[STACK MEMORY - LIFO]
        K1[Top symbol X]
        K2[Gamma string gamma]
        K3[... bottom Z0]
    end
    T2 -- "symbol a in Sigma" --> S1
    S1 -- "pop X, push gamma" --> K1
    K1 -- "new top X'" --> S1
    S1 -- "next state p" --> T2
    style S1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style K1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style K2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style K3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 State Transition Diagram for $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\}$

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> q0
    q0 --> q0 : a, Z0 / AZ0  AND  a, A / AA
    q0 --> q1 : b, A / epsilon
    q1 --> q1 : b, A / epsilon
    q1 --> q2 : epsilon, Z0 / Z0
    q2 --> [*]
    note right of q0 : PUSH phase
    note right of q1 : POP / match phase
    note left of q2 : ACCEPT (Final state)
```

## 4.3 CFG-to-PDA Conversion Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Context-Free Grammar G] --> B{Apply Sipser's Construction}
    B --> C[Single-state PDA with q start]
    B --> D[Stack alphabet = V union Sigma union Z0]
    B --> E[Transitions from productions]
    E --> F[Rule A to alpha: delta epsilon A contains alpha]
    E --> G[Rule for terminal a: delta a a contains epsilon]
    E --> H[Initial: delta epsilon Z0 contains S Z0]
    F --> I[Equivalent PDA M]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{Acceptance Mode}
    J --> K[Empty stack: N M equals L G]
    J --> L[Final state: add marker trick]
    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333
    style I fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style K fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#333
    style L fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333
```

## 4.4 NPDA Decision Tree for $w = abba$ (Palindrome Recognition)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[ID: q0, abba, Z0] --> R1[Read a: push a]
    R1 --> S1[ID: q0, bba, aZ0]
    S1 --> R2[Read b: push b]
    R2 --> S2[ID: q0, ba, baZ0]
    S2 --> G1{epsilon-guess middle}
    G1 -->|top b| S3a[ID: q1, ba, aZ0]
    G1 -->|top b after push b| S3b[ID: q0, a, bbaZ0]
    S3a -->|match a vs top a| M1[ID: q1, b, Z0]
    M1 -->|mismatch b vs Z0| Dead1[REJECT branch]
    S3b -->|push b| S4[ID: q1, a, aZ0]
    S4 -->|match a| S5[ID: q1, epsilon, Z0]
    S5 -->|epsilon-move| Accept[ID: q2, epsilon, Z0 - ACCEPT]
    style Start fill:#FFFAF0,stroke:#333
    style Accept fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px
    style Dead1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define a Pushdown Automaton. Mention how nondeterminism is incorporated in its transition function.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Pushdown Automaton (PDA) is a 7-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$ used to recognize context-free languages. It consists of a finite set of states $Q$, an input alphabet $\Sigma$, a stack alphabet $\Gamma$, a start state $q_0$, an initial stack symbol $Z_0$, and a set of accepting states $F$.

**Nondeterminism (1 mark):** The transition function has the form
$$\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times \Gamma \longrightarrow \mathcal{P}_{\text{finite}}(Q \times \Gamma^*)$$
The codomain is a *set of pairs*; when $|\delta(q, a, X)| \geq 2$, the PDA may choose among multiple (state, stack-string) continuations — this is the nondeterministic choice. **(Full 3 marks awarded for tuple + nondeterminism explanation.)**

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected]**
*Differentiate between acceptance by final state and acceptance by empty stack in a PDA. State the equivalence theorem.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Final State Acceptance $L(M)$ | Empty Stack Acceptance $N(M)$ |
|-------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| $w$ accepted if some sequence leads to $(q, \varepsilon, \gamma)$ with $q \in F$ | $w$ accepted if some sequence leads to $(q, \varepsilon, \varepsilon)$ |
| Stack contents irrelevant | Stack must be empty |
| Final state required | State irrelevant |

**Equivalence Theorem (1 mark):** For every PDA $M_F$ accepting by final state, there exists a PDA $M_N$ accepting by empty stack such that $L(M_F) = N(M_N)$, and vice versa. The construction uses an auxiliary bottom-of-stack marker that is destroyed upon entering a final state, after which the machine erases the rest of the stack.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 marks]** Construct a PDA that accepts the language $L = \{a^n b^{2n} \mid n \geq 1\}$ by empty stack. Show the trace for $w = aabbbb$.

**(b) [7 marks)** Convert the following CFG into an equivalent NPDA:
$$S \rightarrow aSS \mid b$$

---

### Model Solution — Question A(a)

**PDA Construction:**

$$M = (\{q\}, \{a, b\}, \{A, Z_0\}, \delta, q, Z_0, \emptyset)$$

**Transitions:**

| # | Transition | Purpose |
|---|------------|---------|
| 1 | $\delta(q, a, Z_0) = \{(q, AA Z_0)\}$ | First $a$: push **two** $A$'s (because $b$-count is $2 \times a$-count) |
| 2 | $\delta(q, a, A) = \{(q, AAA)\}$ | Each subsequent $a$: push **two** $A$'s |
| 3 | $\delta(q, b, A) = \{(q, \varepsilon)\}$ | Each $b$: pop one $A$ |
| 4 | $\delta(q, \varepsilon, Z_0) = \{(q, \varepsilon)\}$ | Stack empty down to $Z_0$, then pop $Z_0$ — ACCEPT |

**Trace of $w = aabbbb$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(q,\ aabbbb,\ Z_0) &\vdash (q,\ abbbb,\ AA Z_0) &&\text{rule 1: read }a,\text{ push }AA \\
&\vdash (q,\ bbbb,\ AAAA Z_0) &&\text{rule 2: read }a,\text{ push }AA \\
&\vdash (q,\ bbb,\ AAA Z_0) &&\text{rule 3: read }b,\text{ pop }A \\
&\vdash (q,\ bb,\ AA Z_0) &&\text{rule 3: read }b,\text{ pop }A \\
&\vdash (q,\ b,\ A Z_0) &&\text{rule 3: read }b,\text{ pop }A \\
&\vdash (q,\ \varepsilon,\ Z_0) &&\text{rule 3: read }b,\text{ pop }A \\
&\vdash (q,\ \varepsilon,\ \varepsilon) &&\text{rule 4: pop }Z_0\text{ — ACCEPT}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(7 marks: 4 for transitions + 3 for trace)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A(b)

**CFG:** $S \rightarrow aSS \mid b$

**Single-state PDA:** $M = (\{q\}, \{a, b\}, \{S, a, b, Z_0\}, \delta, q, Z_0, \emptyset)$

**Transitions (Sipser-style):**

| # | From CFG rule | PDA transition |
|---|---------------|----------------|
| 1 | $S \rightarrow aSS$ | $\delta(q, \varepsilon, S) \ni (q, a S S)$ |
| 2 | $S \rightarrow b$ | $\delta(q, \varepsilon, S) \ni (q, b)$ |
| 3 | Terminal $a$ | $\delta(q, a, a) = \{(q, \varepsilon)\}$ |
| 4 | Terminal $b$ | $\delta(q, b, b) = \{(q, \varepsilon)\}$ |
| 5 | Initial | $\delta(q, \varepsilon, Z_0) \ni (q, S Z_0)$ |

**Verification via derivation of $w = aabb$:**

Leftmost derivation: $S \Rightarrow aSS \Rightarrow aabS \Rightarrow aabb$. Hence $w \in L(G)$.

PDA trace:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(q,\ aabb,\ Z_0) &\vdash (q,\ aabb,\ SZ_0) &&\text{rule 5: push }S \\
&\vdash (q,\ aabb,\ aSSZ_0) &&\text{rule 1: }S \to aSS \\
&\vdash (q,\ abb,\ SSZ_0) &&\text{rule 3: match }a \\
&\vdash (q,\ abb,\ bSSZ_0) &&\text{rule 2: }S \to b \\
&\vdash (q,\ bb,\ SSZ_0) &&\text{rule 4: match }b \\
&\vdash (q,\ bb,\ bS Z_0) &&\text{rule 2: }S \to b \\
&\vdash (q,\ b,\ SZ_0) &&\text{rule 4: match }b \\
&\vdash (q,\ b,\ bZ_0) &&\text{rule 2: }S \to b \\
&\vdash (q,\ \varepsilon,\ Z_0) &&\text{rule 4: match }b \\
&\vdash (q,\ \varepsilon,\ \varepsilon) &&\text{rule 4: pop }Z_0\text{ — ACCEPT}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(7 marks: 3 for transition derivation + 2 for PDA definition + 2 for trace)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**(a) [7 marks]** Design a PDA that accepts $L = \{a^i b^j c^k \mid i + k = j,\ i, j, k \geq 1\}$ by final state.

**(b) [7 marks]** Prove that the language $L = \{w w \mid w \in \{a, b\}^*\}$ (the "double" language) is not context-free.

---

### Model Solution — Question B(a)

**Idea:** Read $i$ $a$'s, push nothing initially. Then read $j$ $b$'s; for the first $i$ of them, push one $A$ each. The remaining $(j - i)$ $b$'s are matched later by $c$'s. So the stack will hold $i + k = j - (j - i - k) = $ hmm, let us reason carefully.

**Refined Idea:** We need $i + k = j$, so $j - i = k$. Strategy:
- Read $i$ $a$'s — push $i$ markers (call them $X$).
- Read $j$ $b$'s — for the **first $i$** $b$'s, pop one $X$ each. The remaining $j - i$ $b$'s go unconsumed by the stack, and we must match them with $c$'s — but $k = j - i$, so this works.
- Read $k$ $c$'s — pop nothing (the stack should be empty back to $Z_0$).

**PDA:**

$$M = (\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}, \{a, b, c\}, \{X, Z_0\}, \delta, q_0, Z_0, \{q_2\})$$

**Transitions:**

| # | Transition | Meaning |
|---|------------|---------|
| 1 | $\delta(q_0, a, Z_0) = \{(q_0, XZ_0)\}$ | First $a$: push $X$ |
| 2 | $\delta(q_0, a, X) = \{(q_0, XX)\}$ | More $a$'s: push $X$ |
| 3 | $\delta(q_0, b, X) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\}$ | First $b$ of match phase: pop $X$, switch to $q_1$ |
| 4 | $\delta(q_1, b, X) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\}$ | Continue matching $b$'s to $X$'s |
| 5 | $\delta(q_1, b, Z_0) = \{(q_1, Z_0)\}$ | Remaining $b$'s: no stack action |
| 6 | $\delta(q_1, c, Z_0) = \{(q_2, Z_0)\}$ | $c$ reading; final state once we see $c$ (after all $b$'s consumed) |
| 7 | $\delta(q_2, c, Z_0) = \{(q_2, Z_0)\}$ | More $c$'s: stay in $q_2$ |

**Verification on $w = aabbbcc$ (where $i=2, j=3, k=2$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(q_0,\ aabbbcc,\ Z_0) &\vdash (q_0,\ abbbcc,\ XZ_0) &&\text{push }X \\
&\vdash (q_0,\ bbbcc,\ XXZ_0) &&\text{push }X \\
&\vdash (q_1,\ bbcc,\ XZ_0) &&\text{pop }X,\text{ switch to }q_1 \\
&\vdash (q_1,\ bcc,\ Z_0) &&\text{pop }X \\
&\vdash (q_1,\ cc,\ Z_0) &&\text{read remaining }b,\text{ no stack action} \\
&\vdash (q_2,\ c,\ Z_0) &&\text{read }c,\text{ accept state} \\
&\vdash (q_2,\ \varepsilon,\ Z_0) &&\text{read }c,\text{ accept state — ACCEPT}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(7 marks: 4 for transition design with strategy explanation + 3 for trace)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B(b)

**Statement:** $L = \{ww \mid w \in \{a, b\}^*\}$ is **not** a CFL.

**Proof by Pumping Lemma for CFLs (KTU 2024 syllabus):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pumping Lemma for CFLs:** If $L$ is a CFL, then $\exists p \geq 1$ (pumping length) such that every string $z \in L$ with $|z| \geq p$ can be written as $z = uvwxy$ satisfying:
> 1. $|vwx| \leq p$
> 2. $|vx| \geq 1$
> 3. $uv^i w x^i y \in L$ for all $i \geq 0$

**Proof by Contradiction:**

1. **Assume** $L$ is a CFL. Then there exists pumping length $p$.

2. **Choose** the string $z = a^p b\, a^p b \in L$ (so $w = a^p b$, $ww = a^p b\, a^p b$).
   - Length $|z| = 2p + 2 \geq p$ ✓
   - $z = uvwxy$ with $|vwx| \leq p$ and $|vx| \geq 1$.

3. **Case analysis on the position of $vwx$:**

   The substring $vwx$ has length $\leq p < p + 1$, so it **cannot contain both a $b$'s block and the second $a^p$ block**. Hence it lies entirely within one of the following regions:
   - **Case 1:** Entirely within the first $a^p$ block.
   - **Case 2:** Spans the first $b$ and possibly the second $a^p$ block.

   In **either case**, $vx$ contains only $a$'s (Case 1) or contains at most one $b$ (Case 2). Note that $vx$ is non-empty, so it contains at least one $a$ or one $b$.

4. **Pump down** ($i = 0$): The string $uwy$ is missing either:
   - some $a$'s from the first half (but the second $a^p$ is intact), so the two halves are unequal, **or**
   - includes the $b$ in $vx$ (Case 2), so the structure is no longer of the form $ww$ — the unique central $b$ either remains in only one half or shifts the midpoint.

5. **Formal argument (Case 1):** If $v, x$ lie inside the first $a^p$ block, then $|v|_a + |x|_a \geq 1$. After pumping down, the first half has $< p$ a's, the second half has exactly $p$ a's, so the two halves differ in length — but the language $L$ requires $uw$ and $wy$ to be identical for the string to remain in $L$. The first and second halves are no longer equal, so $uwy \notin L$. **Contradiction.**

6. **Case 2:** If $vwx$ includes the $b$, then $|vx|_b \geq 1$. Pumping $i = 2$ adds a $b$ that is not mirrored. The two halves would not have matching $b$-counts. **Contradiction.**

7. **Conclusion:** No decomposition satisfies all three conditions. Hence $L$ is **not** context-free. $\blacksquare$

**(7 marks: 2 for lemma statement + 3 for chosen string + 2 for case analysis)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **3-mark questions:** Students often lose **1 mark** by forgetting to mention *which* element of the tuple is "nondeterministic." Always explicitly say the **codomain of $\delta$** is a *set* of pairs.
> - **14-mark questions:**
>   - **Forgetting the initial transition** that pushes the start symbol $S$ on $Z_0$ costs **1 mark** in CFG-to-PDA.
>   - **Mixing up stack direction:** Remember that the **leftmost** symbol of the pushed string becomes the new top. Writing $AA$ means $A$ is the new top, $A$ is below.
>   - **Pumping Lemma proofs:** Picking $z = a^p b^p a^p b^p$ (which is **in** $L$ only if $w = a^p b$ — but $|a^p b| = p+1$, so the doubled string has length $2p+2$; this is fine, but make sure to specify the explicit $w$). Many students pick a string that is **not** in $L$, which is an instant 2-mark penalty.
>   - **Trace formatting:** Use the $\vdash$ symbol with the PDA name subscript or simply $\vdash$ — be consistent. Examiners deduct marks for sloppy arrows.
>   - **Final state vs. empty stack:** Always **state explicitly** which acceptance mode your PDA uses. Marks are reserved for the final acceptance condition line.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PDA = 7-tuple** $(Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$ — memorize the order.
- The transition function $\delta$ maps $Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times \Gamma$ to a **finite subset of $Q \times \Gamma^*$**.
- **Nondeterminism** is the *only* feature distinguishing a DPDA from an NPDA, but it is **strictly more powerful**.
- **Stack manipulation rules:** Pop one symbol $X$, then push any string $\gamma \in \Gamma^*$. The leftmost symbol of $\gamma$ becomes the new top.
- **Two acceptance modes** are equivalent: *final state* and *empty stack*; the equivalence is constructive (use a stack-bottom marker).
- **ID notation:** $(q, w, \gamma)$ where $q$ = state, $w$ = unread input, $\gamma$ = stack (leftmost on top).
- **Move rule:** $(q, aw, X\alpha) \vdash (p, w, \beta\alpha)$ whenever $(p, \beta) \in \delta(q, a, X)$.
- **Sipser's Theorem (core KTU result):** $L$ is a CFL **iff** some NPDA accepts $L$.
- **CFG → PDA conversion** uses a single state; expand nonterminals via $\varepsilon$-moves, consume terminals via input-matching moves.
- **CFLs that NPDAs accept but DPDAs cannot:** $ww^R$, $ww$, and languages with inherent ambiguity over palindromic structure.
- **Pumping Lemma for CFLs** is the standard tool to prove a language is *not* a CFL — remember the three conditions: $|vwx| \leq p$, $|vx| \geq 1$, $uv^iwx^iy \in L$ for all $i \geq 0$.
- **Real-world uses:** Parsing, balanced-parentheses validation, recursive-descent recognition, XML tag matching, compiler frontend design.
- **Common trace pitfall:** Forgetting that the rightmost symbol of the *pushed* string lies on top, not the leftmost — double-check the rule.
- **For ε-moves:** Allowed even at end of input; essential for the "guess the middle" trick in palindromes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deterministic Pushdown Automata (DPDA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET414 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Deterministic Pushdown Automata (DPDA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Deterministic Pushdown Automaton (DPDA)

A **Deterministic Pushdown Automaton (DPDA)** is a seven-tuple mathematical construct, formally defined as:

$$M = (Q,\ \Sigma,\ \Gamma,\ \delta,\ q_0,\ Z_0,\ F)$$

where each component is precisely defined below:

| Component | Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Finite set of **states** | $Q$ | Internal control configurations of the machine |
| Finite **input alphabet** | $\Sigma$ | Set of symbols read from the input tape |
| Finite **stack alphabet** | $\Gamma$ | Set of symbols that can be pushed onto the stack |
| **Transition function** | $\delta$ | Maps current configuration to next configuration |
| **Initial state** | $q_0$ | Starting configuration of the control unit |
| **Initial stack symbol** | $Z_0$ | The bottom-of-stack marker present initially |
| Set of **final states** | $F \subseteq Q$ | Accepting configurations |

The **transition function** for a DPDA is the *signature feature* that distinguishes it from a non-deterministic PDA (NPDA). Formally:

$$\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times \Gamma \longrightarrow Q \times \Gamma^{*}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Determinism Constraint:** Unlike the NPDA where the transition is a relation mapping into $2^{Q \times \Gamma^{*}}$, the DPDA transition function must be a **single-valued partial function**. This means that for any given state, input symbol (or $\varepsilon$), and stack-top symbol, **there is at most one** possible next move. This single-valuedness is the mathematical heart of determinism.

## 1.2 Determinism — The Three Restrictive Conditions

For the transition function $\delta$ of a DPDA, the following three conditions must be satisfied **simultaneously** to guarantee deterministic behavior:

1. **Single-Valuedness:** $\delta(q, a, X)$ is a set of cardinality $\le 1$ for every $(q, a, X)$.
2. **No $\varepsilon$-Conflict on Same Symbol:** If $\delta(q, a, X)$ is defined for some $a \in \Sigma$, then $\delta(q, \varepsilon, X)$ must **not** be defined.
3. **No Real-Symbol Conflict on $\varepsilon$-Move:** If $\delta(q, \varepsilon, X)$ is defined, then for every $a \in \Sigma$, $\delta(q, a, X)$ must **not** be defined.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these conditions?** They prevent the machine from having to "guess" between multiple applicable moves. At every step, the next action of the DPDA is *uniquely determined* by the current state, the current input symbol (or the $\varepsilon$-option), and the current stack top.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Deterministic Tollbooth

Imagine a **single-lane toll booth with a stack of receipts** on the desk:

- The **toll booth operator** is the *state* $q$ of the DPDA.
- The **car approaching** is the *input symbol* $a$ being read.
- The **top receipt on the stack** is the *current stack symbol* $X$.
- The **operator's hand-written action** (push a receipt, pop a receipt, do nothing) is the *transition output* $(p, \gamma)$.

Now, the key constraint: **a deterministic toll booth can never be in a situation where the operator must choose between two different actions for the same car and the same top receipt.** If a red car arrives and the top receipt is "unpaid," there must be exactly one prescribed action — not two competing options. The booth's operating manual (the transition function $\delta$) is written so that every (state, input, stack-top) triple maps to **at most one** action. This eliminates guesswork — and that is determinism.

## 1.4 Acceptance Modes of a DPDA

A DPDA accepts an input string $w$ by one of two equivalent-seeming but technically distinct mechanisms:

> **Acceptance by Final State:** $w$ is accepted if, after processing $w$, the DPDA halts in some state $q \in F$ (the final state set). The stack contents are **irrelevant**.

> **Acceptance by Empty Stack:** $w$ is accepted if, after processing $w$, the stack is completely empty (i.e., all symbols including $Z_0$ have been popped). The final state is **irrelevant**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** Unlike an NPDA (where acceptance by final state and by empty stack are equivalent in expressive power), for a **DPDA** these two acceptance modes are **not equivalent**. A DPDA accepting by empty stack can only recognize *prefix-free* languages (no string in the language is a proper prefix of another string in the language). However, every language accepted by a DPDA by empty stack can also be accepted by a (possibly different) DPDA by final state.

## 1.5 The Class of Languages: DCFL

The family of languages accepted by DPDAs is called the **Deterministic Context-Free Languages (DCFL)**. Formally:

$$\text{DCFL} = \{\, L(M) \mid M \text{ is a DPDA} \,\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Strict Hierarchy to Memorize for KTU:**
> $$\text{Regular} \subsetneq \text{DCFL} \subsetneq \text{CFL}$$
> 
> Every regular language is a DCFL, every DCFL is a CFL, but neither inclusion is reversible. The classic counterexample for DCFL $\subsetneq$ CFL is $L = \{ww^{R} \mid w \in \{a,b\}^{*}\}$, which is CFL but not DCFL.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Instantaneous Description (ID) of a DPDA

The configuration of a DPDA at any instant is captured by an **Instantaneous Description (ID)**, a triple:

$$(q,\ w,\ \gamma)$$

where:
- $q \in Q$ is the current state
- $w \in \Sigma^{*}$ is the unread suffix of the input
- $\gamma \in \Gamma^{*}$ is the current stack contents (leftmost symbol = top)

A **move** (one computational step) is denoted by the turnstile symbol $\vdash$. For a DPDA, if $\delta(q, a, X) = (p, \alpha)$, then:

$$(q,\ a w,\ X \beta) \vdash (p,\ w,\ \alpha \beta)$$

The reflexive-transitive closure of $\vdash$ is denoted $\vdash^{*}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Determinism of Moves:** If $(q, a w, X\beta) \vdash (p, w, \alpha\beta)$ in a DPDA, then this transition is the **only** one possible for the ID $(q, a w, X\beta)$. There is no branching — the entire future computation is uniquely determined.

## 2.2 Language Accepted by Final State

$$L(M) = \{\, w \in \Sigma^{*} \mid (q_0,\ w,\ Z_0) \vdash^{*} (q,\ \varepsilon,\ \gamma),\ \text{for some } q \in F,\ \gamma \in \Gamma^{*} \,\}$$

**Acceptance by final state** cares only about the final state — the stack contents $\gamma$ at termination can be non-empty.

## 2.3 Language Accepted by Empty Stack

$$N(M) = \{\, w \in \Sigma^{*} \mid (q_0,\ w,\ Z_0) \vdash^{*} (q,\ \varepsilon,\ \varepsilon),\ \text{for some } q \in Q \,\}$$

**Acceptance by empty stack** cares only that the entire stack is consumed — the final state $q$ can be any state in $Q$.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Property | Formula / Statement | KTU Frequency |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | DPDA Definition | $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$ | ★★★★★ |
| 2 | Transition Function Signature | $\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times \Gamma \to Q \times \Gamma^{*}$ (single-valued) | ★★★★★ |
| 3 | Single-valuedness | $\vert \delta(q, a, X) \vert \le 1$ for all $q \in Q$, $a \in \Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}$, $X \in \Gamma$ | ★★★★★ |
| 4 | $\varepsilon$-Move Restriction | $\delta(q, a, X)$ defined $\Rightarrow$ $\delta(q, \varepsilon, X)$ undefined, and vice-versa | ★★★★☆ |
| 5 | Language Class | $\text{DCFL} = \{L(M) \mid M \text{ is a DPDA}\}$ | ★★★★★ |
| 6 | Strict Hierarchy | $\text{REG} \subsetneq \text{DCFL} \subsetneq \text{CFL}$ | ★★★★★ |
| 7 | Prefix-Free Property | If $L = N(M)$ for some DPDA, then $L$ is prefix-free | ★★★★☆ |
| 8 | Non-DCFL Example | $L = \{ww^{R} \mid w \in \{a,b\}^{*}\}$, $L = \{a^{n}b^{n}c^{n} \mid n \ge 0\}$ | ★★★★☆ |
| 9 | DCFL Closure | DCFL closed under complement, reversal, inverse homomorphism, MIN, MAX | ★★★★☆ |
| 10 | DCFL Non-Closure | DCFL **not** closed under union, intersection, concatenation, Kleene star | ★★★★★ |
| 11 | Equivalence to LR(k) | DPDAs (by final state) $\equiv$ LR(k) parsers for $k \ge 1$ | ★★★☆☆ |
| 12 | DCFL Undecidability | Membership, emptiness, finiteness, equivalence are decidable for DCFL | ★★★☆☆ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** DPDAs form the theoretical foundation of **compiler parsers**. The class LR(1) (and its extensions LALR(1), SLR) used in tools like YACC, Bison, and ANTLR are *precisely* deterministic pushdown automata operating by final state. This is why programming languages are designed as DCFLs — they admit linear-time, deterministic parsing.

## 2.5 The Prefix-Free Constraint — Why It Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Prefix-Free Acceptance):** If $L = N(M)$ for some DPDA $M$ (acceptance by empty stack), then $L$ must be **prefix-free**: for any $w \in L$ and any non-empty $x \in \Sigma^{*}$, $wx \notin L$.

**Intuitive Proof Sketch:** Suppose $w, wx \in N(M)$ for some non-empty $x$. After processing $w$, the stack is empty. To process $wx$ further, the DPDA would need to make an $\varepsilon$-move (since the stack is empty, it cannot match any symbol). But any $\varepsilon$-move made on an empty stack is ambiguous — and crucially, for determinism, the DPDA on empty stack with no input symbol has only one option, but the moment a new input $x$ arrives on the empty stack, determinism is broken because the machine has no symbol to "key on." Hence prefix-freeness is forced.

## 2.6 Closure Properties of DCFL — The Crucial Asymmetry

The class **DCFL is not closed under union or intersection**, but **is closed under complement**. This is a striking result, because for the broader CFL class, the situation is precisely reversed (CFL closed under union, not under complement). This asymmetry is a favorite KTU question.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write that DCFL is closed under union because CFL is. This is **FALSE**. The language $L_1 = \{a^{n}b^{n} \mid n \ge 0\}$ and $L_2 = \{a^{n}b^{2n} \mid n \ge 0\}$ are both DCFL, but $L_1 \cup L_2 = \{a^{n}b^{n} \mid n \ge 0\} \cup \{a^{n}b^{2n} \mid n \ge 0\}$ requires the machine to *guess* whether each $b$ is in ratio 1:1 or 1:2, which is impossible deterministically.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Constructing a DPDA for $L = \{a^{n}b^{n} \mid n \ge 0\}$

This is the **canonical DCFL example** and appears in nearly every KTU exam. We will construct it from first principles.

### Step 1 — Identify the Language's Structural Pattern

The language $L = \{a^{n}b^{n} \mid n \ge 0\}$ consists of strings of the form $a^{n}b^{n}$: some number of $a$'s followed by the *same* number of $b$'s. Examples: $\varepsilon, ab, aabb, aaabbb, \ldots$

The DPDA's strategy:
- **Phase 1 (Reading $a$'s):** For each $a$ read, push a marker $A$ onto the stack. This *counts* the number of $a$'s.
- **Phase 2 (Reading $b$'s):** For each $b$ read, pop one $A$. This *verifies* the count matches.
- **Phase 3 (Acceptance):** When the input is exhausted and the stack contains only $Z_0$, transition to the final state.

### Step 2 — Define the Components

We design:
- $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_f\}$ — three states
- $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$ — input alphabet
- $\Gamma = \{Z_0, A\}$ — stack alphabet (with $Z_0$ as the initial symbol)
- $q_0$ — initial state
- $Z_0$ — initial stack symbol
- $F = \{q_f\}$ — final state set

### Step 3 — Derive the Transition Function

The transition function $\delta$ is defined **one rule at a time**, with each rule justified.

**Rule 1 (Start pushing):** On reading $a$ with $Z_0$ on top, push $A$ above $Z_0$ and stay in $q_0$.

$$\delta(q_0,\ a,\ Z_0) = (q_0,\ A Z_0)$$

**Reasoning:** This handles the very first $a$. It pushes an $A$ on top of the initial stack marker.

**Rule 2 (Continue pushing):** On reading $a$ with $A$ on top, push another $A$.

$$\delta(q_0,\ a,\ A) = (q_0,\ A A)$$

**Reasoning:** For every subsequent $a$, we add another marker.

**Rule 3 (Switch to popping — first $b$):** On reading $b$ with $A$ on top, pop the $A$ and move to state $q_1$.

$$\delta(q_0,\ b,\ A) = (q_1,\ \varepsilon)$$

**Reasoning:** The transition from $a$'s to $b$'s occurs naturally when we see the first $b$. The state $q_1$ signals "we are now in the popping phase."

**Rule 4 (Continue popping):** On reading $b$ with $A$ on top, pop the $A$ and stay in $q_1$.

$$\delta(q_1,\ b,\ A) = (q_1,\ \varepsilon)$$

**Reasoning:** Continue matching $b$'s to $A$'s.

**Rule 5 (Accept — empty string case):** On $\varepsilon$-input with $Z_0$ on top, transition to final state.

$$\delta(q_0,\ \varepsilon,\ Z_0) = (q_f,\ Z_0)$$

**Reasoning:** This handles the empty string $w = \varepsilon \in L$. Without this rule, $n = 0$ would be rejected.

**Rule 6 (Accept after matching):** On $\varepsilon$-input with $Z_0$ on top (in state $q_1$), transition to final state.

$$\delta(q_1,\ \varepsilon,\ Z_0) = (q_f,\ Z_0)$$

**Reasoning:** This handles the termination after all $a$'s and $b$'s have been matched.

### Step 4 — Trace the Computation on $w = aabb$

We trace the DPDA on input $aabb$ to verify correctness.

$$
\begin{aligned}
(q_0,\ aabb,\ Z_0) &\vdash (q_0,\ abb,\ A Z_0) && \text{via Rule 1, read first } a\\
&\vdash (q_0,\ bb,\ A A Z_0) && \text{via Rule 2, read second } a\\
&\vdash (q_1,\ b,\ A Z_0) && \text{via Rule 3, read first } b \text{ (pops one } A\text{)}\\
&\vdash (q_1,\ \varepsilon,\ Z_0) && \text{via Rule 4, read second } b \text{ (pops second } A\text{)}\\
&\vdash (q_f,\ \varepsilon,\ Z_0) && \text{via Rule 6, accept by final state}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The DPDA halts in $q_f \in F$, so $aabb \in L(M)$. The trace shows **exactly one** path at every step — confirming determinism.

### Step 5 — Trace the Rejection of $w = aab$

$$
\begin{aligned}
(q_0,\ aab,\ Z_0) &\vdash (q_0,\ ab,\ A Z_0) && \text{via Rule 1}\\
&\vdash (q_0,\ b,\ A A Z_0) && \text{via Rule 2}\\
&\vdash (q_1,\ \varepsilon,\ A Z_0) && \text{via Rule 3 (pops one } A\text{)}\\
&\vdash \text{HALT (no transition defined)} && \text{input exhausted in non-final state}
\end{aligned}
$$

The machine halts in state $q_1 \notin F$ with input $b$ still unread. So $aab \notin L(M)$. ✓

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Constructing a DPDA for $L = \{wcw^{R} \mid w \in \{a, b\}^{*}\}$

This language (even-length palindromes marked by a center symbol $c$) is a classic DCFL.

### Step 1 — Design the Strategy

- **Phase 1 (Read $w$):** Push $a$ or $b$ for each symbol, until $c$ is encountered.
- **Phase 2 (Read $w^{R}$):** Match each input symbol against the stack top.

### Step 2 — Define the Transition Function

States: $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_f\}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0,\ a,\ Z_0) &= (q_0,\ a Z_0) \\
\delta(q_0,\ b,\ Z_0) &= (q_0,\ b Z_0) \\
\delta(q_0,\ a,\ a) &= (q_0,\ a a) \\
\delta(q_0,\ b,\ b) &= (q_0,\ b b) \\
\delta(q_0,\ a,\ b) &= (q_0,\ a b) \\
\delta(q_0,\ b,\ a) &= (q_0,\ b a) \\
\delta(q_0,\ c,\ Z_0) &= (q_1,\ Z_0) \\
\delta(q_0,\ c,\ a) &= (q_1,\ a) \\
\delta(q_0,\ c,\ b) &= (q_1,\ b) \\
\delta(q_1,\ a,\ a) &= (q_1,\ \varepsilon) \\
\delta(q_1,\ b,\ b) &= (q_1,\ \varepsilon) \\
\delta(q_1,\ \varepsilon,\ Z_0) &= (q_f,\ Z_0)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Determinism Check:** The transitions $\delta(q_0, a, a)$ and $\delta(q_0, a, b)$ both push $a$, but the *stack top differs*. So the machine is keyed on the *top of stack*, not just the input — this is what makes it deterministic. There is no $(q_0, a, \text{anything})$ conflict with $(q_0, \varepsilon, \text{anything})$ because no $\varepsilon$-moves are defined in $q_0$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: DPDA Design Patterns for Common Languages

| Target Language | DCFL? | DPDA Strategy | Key Challenge |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| $L = \{a^{n}b^{n} \mid n \ge 0\}$ | ✓ | Push on $a$, pop on $b$ | None — straightforward |
| $L = \{wcw^{R} \mid w \in \Sigma^{*}\}$ | ✓ | Push on $w$, match on $w^{R}$ | Stack symbol encodes both phases |
| $L = \{a^{n}b^{n}c^{n} \mid n \ge 0\}$ | ✗ (CFL only) | N/A — requires counting two things | Need to *compare* two counts |
| $L = \{ww^{R} \mid w \in \{a,b\}^{*}\}$ | ✗ (CFL only) | N/A — no center marker | Cannot determine palindrome midpoint |
| $L = \{a^{n}b^{m} \mid n \le m\}$ | ✓ | Push on $a$, pop on $b$ but allow extra $b$'s | Distinguish "matched" from "extra" $b$'s |
| $L = \{w \in \{a,b\}^{*} \mid n_a(w) = n_b(w)\}$ | ✗ (CFL only) | N/A — must track difference globally | No stack discipline to handle it |
| $L = \{a^{n} \mid n \ge 0\}$ (regular) | ✓ | $\delta(q_0, a, Z_0) = (q_0, Z_0)$, $\delta(q_0, \varepsilon, Z_0) = (q_f, Z_0)$ | DPDA degenerates to DFA |
| $L = $ balanced parentheses | ✓ | Push on `(`, pop on `)` | Classic compiler use case |

## 3.4 Proof Sketch: Why $L = \{ww^{R} \mid w \in \{a,b\}^{*}\}$ is not a DCFL

**Proof (by contradiction using the pumping-style argument for DCFLs):**

Assume for contradiction that some DPDA $M$ accepts $L = \{ww^{R}\}$. Choose a sufficiently long palindrome $w_0 = a^{N} b a^{N} b (a^{N} b)^{R}$ where $N$ is large enough to force the DPDA to revisit configurations. As $M$ processes the first $a^{N}$, by the pigeonhole principle on the finite control states, the stack content at some two points must "agree" in a way that allows pumping. The pumped string violates the palindrome structure (asymmetric halves), but $M$ cannot detect the asymmetry because its deterministic stack operations cannot distinguish "mid-palindrome" from "end-of-palindrome." Hence $M$ either accepts a non-palindrome or rejects a palindrome — contradiction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** The midpoint of $ww^{R}$ is unmarked. A deterministic machine has no way to know "I am now at the midpoint" because it cannot look ahead. An NPDA can guess the midpoint, but a DPDA cannot.

## 3.5 Conversion Theorem: DPDA by Empty Stack → DPDA by Final State

**Theorem:** If $L = N(M_1)$ for some DPDA $M_1$, then there exists a DPDA $M_2$ such that $L = L(M_2)$ (acceptance by final state).

**Construction Sketch:**

1. $M_2$ simulates $M_1$, but tracks one extra piece of state: a flag indicating whether the stack of $M_1$ is "empty" (i.e., only $M_1$'s $Z_0$ remains).
2. To detect $M_1$'s empty stack without breaking determinism, $M_2$ uses a *separate bottom-of-stack marker* $X_0$ and a *parallel tracking state*.
3. When $M_2$ observes that $M_1$ would have emptied its stack (i.e., $M_1$ is about to pop its own $Z_0$), it transitions to a final accepting state.

The reverse direction (final state → empty stack) is *not* generally possible for DPDAs — it works only for prefix-free languages.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: The DPDA Architecture

The following block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of a DPDA, showing the data flow between its three primary components.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    InputTape["INPUT TAPE\nRead-Only Head"] --> ReadController
    ReadController["READ CONTROLLER\nCurrent Input Symbol"] --> StateUnit
    StateUnit["FINITE STATE UNIT\nQ = Set of States"] --> TransitionLogic
    StackTop["STACK TOP REGISTER\nHolds Top Symbol X"] --> TransitionLogic
    TransitionLogic["DETERMINISTIC TRANSITION LOGIC\ndelta: Single-Valued Function"] --> OutputState
    OutputState["NEXT STATE\np in Q"] --> StateUnit
    TransitionLogic --> StackOp
    StackOp["STACK OPERATION UNIT\nPush or Pop Gamma*"] --> StackMemory
    StackMemory["STACK MEMORY\nLIFO Storage"]
    StackMemory --> StackTop
    StateUnit -.->|Final Check| AcceptUnit
    AcceptUnit["ACCEPTANCE UNIT\nq in F?"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **single arrow from the transition logic** to each of "next state" and "stack operation" enforces determinism: exactly one output tuple $(p, \gamma)$ is produced per input triple $(q, a, X)$.

## 4.2 State Transition Diagram for DPDA Accepting $L = \{a^{n}b^{n} \mid n \ge 0\}$

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> q0
    q0 --> q0 : a, Z0 / A Z0\npush A
    q0 --> q0 : a, A / A A\npush A
    q0 --> q1 : b, A / epsilon\npop A
    q1 --> q1 : b, A / epsilon\npop A
    q0 --> qf : epsilon, Z0 / Z0\naccept empty string
    q1 --> qf : epsilon, Z0 / Z0\naccept after matching
    qf --> [*]
    q0 : q0 [Pushing phase]
    q1 : q1 [Popping phase]
    qf : qf [Final / Accepting]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Legend Convention:** Each transition is labeled as `input, stack-top / stack-replacement`. For example, `a, Z0 / A Z0` means: read $a$, top of stack is $Z_0$, replace it with $A Z_0$ (i.e., push $A$).

## 4.3 DPDA Decision Flow — Algorithm for Acceptance Check

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Read input symbol]) --> CheckInput{Input\nremaining?}
    CheckInput -- No --> CheckFinal{State in F?}
    CheckFinal -- Yes --> Accept([ACCEPT w])
    CheckFinal -- No --> Reject([REJECT w])
    CheckInput -- Yes --> ReadSym[Read next input symbol a]
    ReadSym --> LookStack[Inspect stack top X]
    LookStack --> Lookup{delta of q, a, X\ndefined?}
    Lookup -- Yes --> Apply[Apply transition:\nNew state p\nNew stack top gamma]
    Lookup -- No --> Reject2([REJECT w: no transition])
    Apply --> CheckEps{Is next move\nepsilon move?}
    CheckEps -- Yes --> StayEps[Take epsilon move\nwhile possible]
    StayEps --> CheckFinal
    CheckEps -- No --> Start
```

## 4.4 Hierarchy Diagram — Regular, DCFL, CFL Classes

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph OUTER ["CFL Class (Context-Free Languages)"]
        subgraph MIDDLE ["DCFL Class (Deterministic CFL)"]
            subgraph INNER ["REG Class (Regular Languages)"]
                REG["REG\nDFA / NFA / regex"]
            end
            DCFL_EX["DCFL only\n{ wcwR }\n{ anbn }"]
        end
        CFL_EX["CFL only\n{ wwR }\n{ anbncn }"]
    end
    REG -. "subsetneq" .-> DCFL
    DCFL -. "subsetneq" .-> OUTER
    CFL_EX -. "Example only in CFL" .-> OUTER
    DCFL_EX -. "Example only in DCFL" .-> MIDDLE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The innermost region is Regular, properly contained in DCFL, which is properly contained in CFL. The labels `DCFL_EX` and `CFL_EX` indicate example languages that lie in the corresponding *exclusion zone* — in DCFL but not regular, and in CFL but not DCFL, respectively.

graph LR

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a Deterministic Pushdown Automaton (DPDA). List the three conditions that must be satisfied by its transition function to ensure deterministic behavior.**

**Model Answer:**

A Deterministic Pushdown Automaton is a seven-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$ where the transition function $\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times \Gamma \to Q \times \Gamma^{*}$ is a **single-valued partial function** (output is at most one tuple).

The three determinism conditions are:
1. For every $(q, a, X) \in Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \times \Gamma$, $\vert \delta(q, a, X) \vert \le 1$.
2. If $\delta(q, a, X)$ is defined for some $a \in \Sigma$, then $\delta(q, \varepsilon, X)$ is undefined.
3. If $\delta(q, \varepsilon, X)$ is defined, then $\delta(q, a, X)$ is undefined for every $a \in \Sigma$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation (3 marks):** 1 mark for the seven-tuple definition, 1 mark for the transition function signature, 1 mark for the three conditions.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Compare the language classes Regular, DCFL, and CFL. Give one example of a language that is CFL but not DCFL.**

**Model Answer:**

The hierarchy of language classes is:
$$\text{REG} \subsetneq \text{DCFL} \subsetneq \text{CFL}$$

- **Regular** languages are accepted by finite automata (DFA/NFA).
- **DCFL** languages are accepted by DPDAs — a strict superset of Regular.
- **CFL** languages are accepted by general (possibly non-deterministic) PDAs — a strict superset of DCFL.

**Example of a CFL but not DCFL:** $L = \{ww^{R} \mid w \in \{a, b\}^{*}\}$ (the set of even-length palindromes over $\{a, b\}$ without a center marker). This language is context-free (generated by the grammar $S \to aSa \mid bSb \mid \varepsilon$) but cannot be recognized by any DPDA because the DPDA cannot deterministically identify the midpoint of the palindrome.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, candidates answer **one** of the two questions below. Each question is divided into two sub-parts worth 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Construct a DPDA that accepts the language $L = \{a^{n}b^{2n} \mid n \ge 1\}$ by final state. Clearly define all components of the DPDA and justify the transition function. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Trace the execution of your DPDA on the input string $w = aabbbb$. Show every instantaneous description (ID). What happens if you feed the input $w = abbb$? Justify. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Components of the DPDA:**

- $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_f\}$
- $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$
- $\Gamma = \{Z_0, A\}$
- $q_0$ is the initial state
- $Z_0$ is the initial stack symbol
- $F = \{q_f\}$

**Strategy:** Each $a$ pushes two $A$'s onto the stack (since each $a$ is matched by two $b$'s). Each $b$ pops one $A$.

**Transition Function:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0,\ a,\ Z_0) &= (q_0,\ A A Z_0) \\
\delta(q_0,\ a,\ A) &= (q_0,\ A A A) \\
\delta(q_0,\ b,\ A) &= (q_1,\ \varepsilon) \\
\delta(q_1,\ b,\ A) &= (q_1,\ \varepsilon) \\
\delta(q_1,\ \varepsilon,\ Z_0) &= (q_f,\ Z_0)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Justification:**
- **Rule 1 & 2:** Each $a$ in the input contributes two $b$'s, so we push two $A$'s per $a$ to maintain a 1:2 balance.
- **Rule 3:** The first $b$ pops one $A$ and transitions to the popping phase ($q_1$).
- **Rule 4:** Each subsequent $b$ pops one $A$.
- **Rule 5:** When all $b$'s are matched and only $Z_0$ remains, accept.

**Determinism Check:** For each $(q, a, X)$ triple, exactly one transition is defined. No $\varepsilon$-move conflicts with real-symbol moves. ✓

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Trace on $w = aabbbb$ (which is in $L$ since $n=1$, so $a^1 b^2$ matches the pattern... actually $aabbbb = a^2b^4$ where $n=2$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(q_0,\ aabbbb,\ Z_0) &\vdash (q_0,\ abbbb,\ AA Z_0) && \text{Rule 1, read 1st } a, \text{ push } AA\\
&\vdash (q_0,\ bbbb,\ AAAA Z_0) && \text{Rule 2, read 2nd } a, \text{ push } AA\\
&\vdash (q_1,\ bbb,\ AAA Z_0) && \text{Rule 3, read 1st } b, \text{ pop } A\\
&\vdash (q_1,\ bb,\ AA Z_0) && \text{Rule 4, read 2nd } b, \text{ pop } A\\
&\vdash (q_1,\ b,\ A Z_0) && \text{Rule 4, read 3rd } b, \text{ pop } A\\
&\vdash (q_1,\ \varepsilon,\ Z_0) && \text{Rule 4, read 4th } b, \text{ pop } A\\
&\vdash (q_f,\ \varepsilon,\ Z_0) && \text{Rule 5, accept}
\end{aligned}
$$

The DPDA accepts $aabbbb$ in state $q_f \in F$. ✓

**Trace on $w = abbb$ (not in $L$ since $1 \ne 2 \cdot 1$ for the $b$ count requirement):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(q_0,\ abbb,\ Z_0) &\vdash (q_0,\ bbb,\ AA Z_0) && \text{Rule 1}\\
&\vdash (q_1,\ bb,\ A Z_0) && \text{Rule 3, pop one } A\\
&\vdash (q_1,\ b,\ Z_0) && \text{Rule 4, pop the second } A\\
&\vdash \text{HALT} && \text{No rule for } (q_1, b, Z_0)
\end{aligned}
$$

The DPDA halts in non-final state $q_1$ with unread input $b$. Hence $abbb \notin L(M)$. ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often forget the **double push** when the ratio is 1:2 (pushing one $A$ per $a$ would only give a 1:1 match). The number of $A$'s pushed per $a$ must equal the *ratio denominator* (here 2). Also, students sometimes omit the $\varepsilon$-transition to the final state, breaking acceptance of the empty-stack condition.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Define Deterministic Context-Free Language (DCFL). Prove that the language $L = \{a^{n}b^{n}c^{n} \mid n \ge 0\}$ is **not** a DCFL. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Construct a DPDA accepting the language $L = \{w \in \{a, b\}^{*} \mid n_a(w) = n_b(w) \text{ and } w \text{ is a palindrome}\}$ — that is, palindromes with equal number of $a$'s and $b$'s. Provide transition rules and verify with input $w = abba$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition:** A language $L$ is a **Deterministic Context-Free Language (DCFL)** if and only if there exists a Deterministic Pushdown Automaton $M$ such that $L = L(M)$ (acceptance by final state).

**Proof that $L = \{a^{n}b^{n}c^{n} \mid n \ge 0\}$ is not a DCFL:**

We argue by contradiction. Suppose $M$ is a DPDA that accepts $L$. Consider the strings $a^{N}$ for large $N$ (where $N$ is chosen so that the DPDA must revisit states by the pigeonhole principle on $|Q| \times |\Gamma|^{k}$ configurations).

**Step 1 — Bound the Stack Depth:** As $M$ processes $a^{N}$, each $a$ must push at least one symbol (or the DPDA cannot later "know" the count of $a$'s). So the stack grows to depth at least $N$.

**Step 2 — Pumping Argument:** Let $s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_{N+1}$ be the stack-top symbols just before each $a$ is read in $a^{N}$. By pigeonhole on the finite stack alphabet $\Gamma$, some stack-top symbol $X$ repeats: $s_i = s_j = X$ with $i < j$.
