Using $H$ as a subroutine, we build a new Turing Machine $D$ that takes a single input $x$ (which we will later set to $\langle D \rangle$ itself):

$$D(x) = \begin{cases} \text{loop forever} & \text{if } H(x, x) = \text{accept} \\ \text{halt and accept} & \text{if } H(x, x) = \text{reject} \end{cases}$$

**Explanation:** $D$ is fully constructible because $H$ is assumed to exist. $D$ is a perfectly valid program; it can be encoded as a string $\langle D \rangle$.

### Step 3 — Self-Application: Ask About $D(\langle D \rangle)$

Since $D$ is a TM, $\langle D \rangle$ is a valid input. Apply $H$ to the pair $(\langle D \rangle, \langle D \rangle)$:

$$H(\langle D \rangle, \langle D \rangle) = \text{accept} \quad \text{or} \quad H(\langle D \rangle, \langle D \rangle) = \text{reject}$$

**Explanation:** By our assumption, $H$ always halts and returns one of these two answers. Now we examine **what $D$ does when given its own description**.

### Step 4 — Case Analysis (The Diagonal Contradiction)

**Case A: Suppose $H(\langle D \rangle, \langle D \rangle) = \text{accept}$.**

By the definition of $H$, this means $D$ halts on input $\langle D \rangle$. But by the definition of $D$:

$$D(\langle D \rangle) = \text{loop forever (since } H(\langle D \rangle, \langle D \rangle) = \text{accept)}$$

So $D$ **loops forever** on $\langle D \rangle$. This contradicts the claim that $D$ halts on $\langle D \rangle$. **Contradiction.**

**Case B: Suppose $H(\langle D \rangle, \langle D \rangle) = \text{reject}$.**

By the definition of $H$, this means $D$ does **not** halt on input $\langle D \rangle$. But by the definition of $D$:

$$D(\langle D \rangle) = \text{halt and accept (since } H(\langle D \rangle, \langle D \rangle) = \text{reject)}$$

So $D$ **halts** on $\langle D \rangle$. This contradicts the claim that $D$ does not halt on $\langle D \rangle$. **Contradiction.**

### Step 5 — Conclusion

Both possible cases lead to contradictions. Therefore, our initial assumption is false:

$$\boxed{\;\text{No Turing Machine } H \text{ can decide the Halting Problem.}\;}$$

$$\boxed{\;H_{TM} \text{ is undecidable.}\;}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Why the Diagonal Argument Works

| **Component** | **Formal Role** | **What Would Happen If We Removed It** |
|---|---|---|
| Encoding $\langle M \rangle$ | Allows programs to be treated as data | We could not self-reference — no paradox |
| $H$ (halting decider) | The assumed "perfect oracle" | No contradiction is possible |
| $D$ (diagonalizer) | Inverts the answer of $H$ on the diagonal $(x, x)$ | The self-application would behave identically to $H$ |
| Self-application $D(\langle D \rangle)$ | Forces $H$ to evaluate $D$'s behavior on its own encoding | The argument would only hold for *other* programs, not $D$ itself |
| Case split (halts / loops) | Exhaustive case analysis | Would leave room for an unconsidered third outcome (impossible for $H$) |

## 3.3 Mathematical Statement of Undecidability Hierarchy

The Halting Problem sits at the heart of the **decidability hierarchy** of formal languages:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Decidable } (\mathcal{R}) &\subset \text{Recursively Enumerable } (\mathcal{RE}) \subset \text{All Languages } (2^{\Sigma^*}) \\
A_{TM}, H_{TM} &\in \mathcal{RE} \setminus \mathcal{R} \\
E_{TM}, EQ_{TM} &\notin \mathcal{RE}
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation of the chain:**
- $\mathcal{R}$ (recursive / decidable): There exists a TM that halts and gives the correct yes/no answer for every input.
- $\mathcal{RE}$ (recursively enumerable): There exists a TM that halts and accepts if the answer is "yes," but may loop forever if the answer is "no."
- $A_{TM}$ and $H_{TM}$ are in $\mathcal{RE}$ because we can *enumerate* all computations of $M$ on $w$, but we cannot in general decide membership.
- Problems like $E_{TM}$ (is the language of $M$ empty?) are *not even* RE — they are provably harder.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is $H_{TM}$ in $\mathcal{RE}$ if it's undecidable?**
> A TM that recognizes $H_{TM}$ simply simulates $M$ on $w$ step-by-step. If $M$ halts, the simulator halts and accepts. If $M$ loops, the simulator also loops — which is permitted for an RE machine. The complement $\overline{H_{TM}}$ is not RE.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Halting Problem as a Block-Level Functional Architecture

Below is a Mermaid block diagram depicting the **logical flow of the diagonalization argument**, including the inputs, the halting decider $H$, the diagonalizer $D$, and the contradictory self-application loop.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Hypothetical Halting Decider H"]:::decider
    B["Input Pair left angle M comma w right angle"]:::input
    C["Halts on w ?"]:::decision
    D["Output Accept"]:::output
    E["Output Reject"]:::output
    F["Diagonalizer D constructed from H"]:::diagonalizer
    G["D receives input x"]:::input
    H1["Compute H of left angle x comma x right angle"]:::inner
    I1{"H returns Accept ?"}:::decision
    J1["D loops forever"]:::loop
    K1["D halts and accepts"]:::halt
    L["Contradiction arises when D receives left angle D right angle"]:::contradiction
    M["No such H can exist"]:::conclusion

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C -- Yes --> D
    C -- No --> E

    A --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H1
    H1 --> I1
    I1 -- Yes --> J1
    I1 -- No --> K1

    J1 --> L
    K1 --> L
    L --> M

    classDef decider fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#b00000,color:#000
    classDef input fill:#d6e9ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#d6ffd6,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef diagonalizer fill:#f0d6ff,stroke:#4b0082,color:#000
    classDef inner fill:#d6f0ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef loop fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef halt fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef contradiction fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#cc3300,color:#000
    classDef conclusion fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#000000,color:#000
```

## 4.2 The Decidability Hierarchy — Visual Block Diagram

This diagram shows the **position of $H_{TM}$** in the formal language hierarchy — a frequently tested KTU concept.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1["All Languages 2 to the power Sigma star"]:::outer
    L2["Recursively Enumerable RE"]:::middle
    L3["Decidable Recursive R"]:::inner
    P1["A sub TM and H sub TM in RE minus R"]:::halting
    P2["E sub TM and EQ sub TM not in RE"]:::harder
    P3["Regular, Context Free, etc."]:::easy

    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L2 -.- P1
    L1 -.- P2
    L3 -.- P3
```

## 4.3 Architecture of the Self-Referential Loop

The following diagram illustrates the **circular dependency** that makes the Halting Problem undecidable — the key visual a KTU examiner looks for.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["D receives own description left angle D right angle"]:::self
    S2["D asks H left angle D comma D right angle"]:::ask
    S3{"H says Accept"}:::check
    S4["D enters infinite loop"]:::bad
    S5["Contradiction D must halt but is looping"]:::conflict

    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 -- Yes --> S4
    S4 --> S5
    S3 -- No --> S6["D halts immediately"]:::halt2
    S6 --> S7["Contradiction D must loop but is halting"]:::conflict2

    classDef self fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    classDef ask fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000

    classDef halt2 fill:#b9f6ca,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip for Examiners:** Notice the two contradictory paths converge on the same conclusion — *no consistent answer exists*. This is the visual signature of diagonalization. Always draw such a flowchart in your answer script for full marks.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2-Mark and 3-Mark Categories)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, 3 Marks]

**Q: Define the Halting Problem. State whether it is decidable or undecidable and give one reason.**

**Model Answer:**

The Halting Problem is the decision problem of determining, for an arbitrary Turing Machine $M$ and input $w$, whether $M$ halts on $w$ or runs forever.

$$H_{TM} = \{\langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ is a TM that halts on input } w\}$$

**Decidability Status:** **Undecidable.** 

**Reason:** Alan Turing (1936) proved via diagonalization that no Turing Machine can decide $H_{TM}$ for all pairs $\langle M, w \rangle$. A constructive proof by contradiction (assuming $H$ exists, then building diagonalizer $D$ that contradicts itself when run on its own description) shows the assumption fails.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, 3 Marks]

**Q: What is the difference between $A_{TM}$ and $H_{TM}$? Why are both undecidable?**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | $A_{TM}$ (Acceptance) | $H_{TM}$ (Halting) |
|---|---|---|
| Formal definition | $\{\langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ accepts } w\}$ | $\{\langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ halts on } w\}$ |
| Behavior on non-accepting | May halt-reject or loop | Loops only |
| Decidability | Undecidable | Undecidable |

**Why undecidable:** $A_{TM}$ and $H_{TM}$ reduce to each other. If we had a decider for $A_{TM}$, we could decide $H_{TM}$ by first checking $A_{TM}$, and if $M$ does not accept $w$, simulating $M$ on $w$ to detect halting. Conversely, a decider for $H_{TM}$ combined with simulation can decide $A_{TM}$. Since both reductions are valid and one is undecidable, both must be undecidable.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]

**Q: Prove that the Halting Problem is undecidable using Turing's diagonalization argument. Show all steps clearly.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: State the Problem and Assume $H$ Exists

**Model Solution:**

> The Halting Problem asks: given a TM $M$ and input $w$, does $M$ halt on $w$?

Define the language:

$$H_{TM} = \{\langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ is a TM and } M \text{ halts on input } w\}$$

**Assumption (for contradiction):** Suppose a TM $H$ exists that decides $H_{TM}$:

$$H(\langle M, w \rangle) = \begin{cases} \text{accept} & \text{if } M \text{ halts on } w \\ \text{reject} & \text{if } M \text{ does not halt on } w \end{cases}$$

This means $H$ halts on every input and always returns the correct answer.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Construct $D$ and Derive the Contradiction

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Build the diagonalizer $D$.**

Using $H$ as a subroutine, construct TM $D$:

$$D(x) = \begin{cases} \text{loop forever} & \text{if } H(x, x) = \text{accept} \\ \text{halt and accept} & \text{if } H(x, x) = \text{reject} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2: Run $D$ on its own encoding $\langle D \rangle$.**

Since $D$ is a TM, $\langle D \rangle$ is a valid input.

**Case 1:** Suppose $H(\langle D \rangle, \langle D \rangle) = \text{accept}$.

Then by definition of $H$, $D$ halts on $\langle D \rangle$. But by definition of $D$, since $H(\langle D \rangle, \langle D \rangle) = \text{accept}$, $D(\langle D \rangle)$ loops forever. **Contradiction.**

**Case 2:** Suppose $H(\langle D \rangle, \langle D \rangle) = \text{reject}$.

Then by definition of $H$, $D$ does not halt on $\langle D \rangle$. But by definition of $D$, since $H(\langle D \rangle, \langle D \rangle) = \text{reject}$, $D(\langle D \rangle)$ halts. **Contradiction.**

**Conclusion:** The assumption that $H$ exists is false. Hence $H_{TM}$ is undecidable. $\blacksquare$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Supplementary, Module 4]

**Q: Discuss the significance of the Halting Problem in computability theory. Explain how its undecidability is used to prove other problems undecidable via reduction.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Significance of the Halting Problem

**Model Solution:**

The Halting Problem, proven undecidable by Alan Turing in 1936, is one of the most important results in mathematical logic and computer science. Its significance spans:

1. **Foundation of Undecidability Theory:** $H_{TM}$ is the *first* proven undecidable problem. It serves as the "base case" for proving many other problems undecidable.

2. **Limits of Computation:** It establishes that no matter how powerful or fast a computer becomes, there exist well-defined questions it cannot answer in finite time. This refutes Hilbert's dream of a fully automatic decision procedure.

3. **Connection to the Entscheidungsproblem:** Turing's proof simultaneously resolved Hilbert's *Entscheidungsproblem* (decision problem for first-order logic), showing that no algorithm can decide truth of all mathematical statements.

4. **Reduction Template:** If a problem $P$ can be reduced to $H_{TM}$ (i.e., a decider for $P$ would yield a decider for $H_{TM}$), then $P$ is also undecidable. This is the **reduction technique**.

5. **Practical Implications:** Static analysis tools, virus scanners, and verifiers can never be perfectly accurate due to Halting Problem consequences.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Reductions Using the Halting Problem

**Model Solution:**

**Reduction Principle:** A problem $A$ reduces to $B$ (written $A \leq B$) if a decider for $B$ can be used to decide $A$. Formally: if $A \leq B$ and $A$ is undecidable, then $B$ is undecidable.

**Example 1: Empty Language $E_{TM}$**

$E_{TM} = \{\langle M \rangle \mid L(M) = \emptyset\}$.

We reduce $H_{TM}$ to $E_{TM}$: Given $\langle M, w \rangle$, construct $M_w$ that ignores its input, writes $w$ on its tape, then simulates $M$ on $w$. Then $L(M_w) = \emptyset$ iff $M$ does not halt on $w$. A decider for $E_{TM}$ applied to $\langle M_w \rangle$ would thus decide $H_{TM}$. Since $H_{TM}$ is undecidable, $E_{TM}$ is undecidable.

**Example 2: Equivalence $EQ_{TM}$**

$EQ_{TM} = \{\langle M_1, M_2 \rangle \mid L(M_1) = L(M_2)\}$. This can be shown undecidable by reduction from $E_{TM}$: $L(M) = \emptyset$ iff $L(M) = L(M_\emptyset)$ where $M_\emptyset$ is a fixed TM that rejects everything. So $E_{TM} \leq EQ_{TM}$, and hence $EQ_{TM}$ is undecidable.

**Example 3: Regularity of $L(M)$**

$REGULAR_{TM} = \{\langle M \rangle \mid L(M) \text{ is regular}\}$. Reduce $A_{TM}$ to this: given $\langle M, w \rangle$, construct $M'_w$ that, on input $x$, checks if $x = 0^n 1^n$ for some $n$ (a non-regular language). If not, reject. If yes, simulate $M$ on $w$; if $M$ accepts, accept. Then $L(M'_w)$ is regular (either $\emptyset$ or $0^n 1^n$) iff $M$ does not accept $w$. This reduction proves $REGULAR_{TM}$ is undecidable. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Encodings:** Writing $D(D)$ instead of $D(\langle D \rangle)$. *Self-reference requires encoding; otherwise, the input is not a string the TM can process.* Lose **1 mark**.
>
> 2. **Confusing $A_{TM}$ with $H_{TM}$:** $A_{TM}$ concerns *acceptance* (halting in accept state), while $H_{TM}$ concerns *halting* (regardless of accept/reject). Use precise terminology. Lose **1–2 marks**.
>
> 3. **Skipping the Construction of $D$:** Examiners want the explicit if-else logic of $D$. Vague phrasing like "$D$ does the opposite of $H$" earns partial credit. Lose **2 marks**.
>
> 4. **Missing Both Cases in Case Analysis:** You must show *both* Case 1 and Case 2, each producing a contradiction. Skipping one loses **3 marks** in 14-mark questions.
>
> 5. **Writing $H_{TM} \in RE$ Without Justification:** If asked whether $H_{TM}$ is in $\mathcal{RE}$ or $\mathcal{R}$, you must state it is RE but **not** recursive, with a brief reason (the universal TM recognizes it but cannot decide its complement).
>
> 6. **Treating "Undecidable" as Synonymous with "Unsolvable":** Undecidable means *no algorithm exists*. Unsolvable is sometimes used for problems like the Halting Problem *restricted* to specific inputs. Be precise.
>
> 7. **Not Mentioning Turing's Year (1936):** Examiners often give bonus marks for historical accuracy.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Halting Problem**
>
> - **Definition:** $H_{TM} = \{\langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ halts on } w\}$. It is **undecidable**.
> - **Proof technique:** Diagonalization (proof by contradiction).
> - **Diagonalizer construction:** $D(x)$ loops if $H(x,x)$ accepts; halts if $H(x,x)$ rejects.
> - **Self-application:** Run $D$ on $\langle D \rangle$ — both outcomes yield contradiction.
> - **Acceptance problem $A_{TM}$:** Also undecidable; closely related to $H_{TM}$.
> - **Class membership:** $H_{TM} \in \mathcal{RE} \setminus \mathcal{R}$.
> - **Reductions:** $H_{TM} \leq A_{TM}$, $A_{TM} \leq H_{TM}$, $E_{TM}$, $EQ_{TM}$, $REGULAR_{TM}$ all undecidable via reduction from Halting/Acceptance.
> - **Historical anchor:** Alan Turing, 1936, paper *"On Computable Numbers"*.
> - **Significance:** Refuted Hilbert's *Entscheidungsproblem*; established fundamental limits of computation.
> - **Practical impact:** No perfect static analyzer, linter, or virus detector can exist for all programs.
> - **Common reduction template:** Given $\langle M, w \rangle$, construct $M_w$ that uses $w$ as a hardcoded parameter. A decider for $X$ applied to $M_w$ decides $H_{TM}$.
> - **Key pitfall:** Always use *encoded* descriptions $\langle M \rangle$ when self-referencing — never write $D(D)$ in a TM-execution sense without explaining the encoding.
> - **Examiner's golden phrase:** *"Since $H$ is assumed to halt on every input, $H(\langle D \rangle, \langle D \rangle)$ must be defined. But $D$'s behaviour forces the opposite."*
> - **One-line mnemonic:** **"Self-reference plus negation equals paradox; no oracle can break it."**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recursive and Recursively Enumerable Languages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET414 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Recursive and Recursively Enumerable Languages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definitions in KTU 2024 Terminology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition — Recursive Language (Decidable Language)**
> A language $L$ is called **Recursive** (or **Decidable**) if there exists a **Turing Machine $M$** such that:
> 1. $M$ **halts on every input** $w \in \Sigma^*$.
> 2. If $w \in L$, then $M$ **accepts** $w$.
> 3. If $w \notin L$, then $M$ **rejects** $w$.
>
> Mathematically: $\forall w \in \Sigma^*$, $M$ reaches a halting configuration (either $q_{accept}$ or $q_{reject}$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition — Recursively Enumerable Language (RE / Turing Recognizable)**
> A language $L$ is called **Recursively Enumerable (r.e.)** (or **Turing Recognizable** / **Semi-Decidable**) if there exists a Turing Machine $M$ such that:
> 1. If $w \in L$, then $M$ **accepts** $w$ (and halts in $q_{accept}$).
> 2. If $w \notin L$, then $M$ either **rejects** $w$ OR **loops forever** (never halts).
>
> The condition is only on strings **inside** the language — strings outside the language have no guarantee.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> The classification of formal languages based on the **halting behaviour** of Turing Machines is a core Module 4 outcome. Students must be able to (a) distinguish recursive from r.e., (b) prove decidability/undecidability, and (c) apply reduction techniques.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogies — Making It Click

### Analogy 1: The Vending Machine
Imagine a vending machine that accepts coins and dispenses a drink.

| Machine Type | Behaviour | Analogy |
|--------------|-----------|---------|
| **Recursive / Decidable** | Always stops, tells you YES or NO clearly | A vending machine that either gives you the drink OR refunds your coin — **never hangs** |
| **Recursively Enumerable** | Stops with YES, but might hang on NO | A vending machine that gives you the drink when stock is available, but **loops infinitely** (keeps checking stock forever) when the drink is not available |

### Analogy 2: A Courtroom Judge
- A **Recursive language** = a judge who **always delivers a verdict** (guilty or not-guilty) in finite time.
- A **Recursively Enumerable language** = a judge who **definitely declares "guilty"** when evidence exists, but might **never return** if the accused is innocent (the case just drags on).

## 1.3 The Complement Operation — The Critical Distinguishing Factor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (KTU High-Weight):**
> $L$ is recursive **if and only if both** $L$ **and** $\overline{L}$ are recursively enumerable.
>
> Equivalently: **Recursive ⊊ r.e.** (proper subset).

**Intuition:** For a recursive language, we have an algorithm that gives a YES/NO answer. The complement of an r.e. language may not be r.e. (this gives rise to the famous **co-r.e.** class).

## 1.4 Notations Used in KTU Examinations

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| $R$ | Class of all Recursive (Decidable) Languages |
| $RE$ | Class of all Recursively Enumerable Languages |
| $co\text{-}RE$ | Class of languages whose complements are in $RE$ |
| $L(M)$ | Language recognized (accepted) by TM $M$ |
| $A_{TM}$ | The acceptance problem — $\{ \langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ accepts } w \}$ — is r.e. but NOT recursive |
| $HALT_{TM}$ | The halting problem — also r.e. but not recursive |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Hierarchy of Language Classes

The classes of formal languages form a strict, nested hierarchy under the Chomsky classification, extended by the decidability framework:

$$
\text{Regular} \subsetneq \text{Context-Free} \subsetneq \text{Context-Sensitive} \subsetneq \text{Recursive} \subsetneq \text{RE}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Every **regular** language is decidable (a DFA can be simulated by a TM that always halts). Every **context-free** language is decidable (CYK algorithm or PDA simulation). The strict inclusions between Context-Sensitive and Recursive, and between Recursive and RE, are major Module 4 results.

## 2.2 Closure Properties — KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Closure Theorems (High-Yield for 14-Mark Questions):**
> The class $RE$ is **closed under** union, intersection, concatenation, Kleene star, and reversal.
> The class $R$ (recursive) is **closed under** complementation, union, intersection, concatenation, Kleene star, and reversal.

### Comprehensive Property Table

| Operation | Recursive ($R$) | Recursively Enumerable ($RE$) | $co\text{-}RE$ |
|-----------|:---------------:|:-----------------------------:|:--------------:|
| Union ($L_1 \cup L_2$) | ✅ Closed | ✅ Closed | ✅ Closed |
| Intersection ($L_1 \cap L_2$) | ✅ Closed | ✅ Closed | ✅ Closed |
| Complement ($\overline{L}$) | ✅ Closed | ❌ **NOT closed** | ✅ Closed |
| Concatenation ($L_1 L_2$) | ✅ Closed | ✅ Closed | ❌ Not closed |
| Kleene Star ($L^*$) | ✅ Closed | ✅ Closed | ❌ Not closed |
| Reversal ($L^R$) | ✅ Closed | ✅ Closed | ✅ Closed |
| Homomorphism | ✅ Closed | ✅ Closed | ❌ Not closed |
| Inverse Homomorphism | ✅ Closed | ✅ Closed | ✅ Closed |
| Substitution (r.e.) | ❌ Not closed | ✅ Closed | ❌ Not closed |

## 2.3 Characterization Theorems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem 1 — Many-One Reduction Criterion:**
> A language $L$ is **r.e.** $\iff$ $L = \emptyset$ or $L$ is the range of some **total computable function** $f : \mathbb{N} \to \Sigma^*$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem 2 — Recursive Characterization:**
> $L$ is **recursive** $\iff$ both $L$ and $\overline{L}$ are **r.e.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem 3 — Enumeration Criterion:**
> A language $L$ is **r.e.** $\iff$ there exists a Turing Machine $M$ that, when started with a blank tape, **enumerates** all strings of $L$ (possibly with repetitions, possibly running forever).

## 2.4 Standard Examples for KTU Exams

| Language | Class | Reasoning |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| $A_{DFA} = \{ \langle B, w \rangle \mid B \text{ is a DFA, } w \in L(B) \}$ | **Recursive** | Simulate $B$ on $w$; DFA always halts in $\le \vert w \vert$ steps. |
| $A_{NFA}, A_{REX}, E_{DFA}, EQ_{DFA}$ | **Recursive** | Standard decidability results. |
| $A_{CFG} = \{ \langle G, w \rangle \mid G \text{ is a CFG, } w \in L(G) \}$ | **Recursive** | Use CYK algorithm — $O(\vert w \vert^3)$ time. |
| $A_{PDA} = \{ \langle P, w \rangle \mid P \text{ is a PDA, } w \in L(P) \}$ | **Recursive** | (Undecidable for general PDA — but $A_{PDA}$ itself is r.e. and decidable) |
| $HALT_{TM} = \{ \langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ halts on } w \}$ | **r.e., NOT Recursive** | Classic undecidable problem. |
| $A_{TM} = \{ \langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ accepts } w \}$ | **r.e., NOT Recursive** | The acceptance problem. |
| $\overline{A_{TM}}$ | **NOT r.e., NOT Recursive** | Complement of a non-recursive r.e. language. |
| $EQ_{TM} = \{ \langle M_1, M_2 \rangle \mid L(M_1) = L(M_2) \}$ | **NOT r.e., NOT Recursive** | Even its complement is non-r.e. |

## 2.5 KTU Decidability / Undecidability Cheat Sheet

| Problem | Decision | Proof Idea |
|---------|:--------:|------------|
| Is $L(M)$ empty? ($E_{TM}$) | Undecidable | Reduce from $A_{TM}$ |
| Is $L(M) = \Sigma^*$? ($A_{TM}$-like) | Undecidable | Reduce from $\overline{A_{TM}}$ or $E_{TM}$ |
| Does $M$ halt on $w$? | Undecidable | Diagonalization (Turing, 1936) |
| Does $M$ accept $w$? | Undecidable | Reduction from $HALT$ |
| Does a CFG generate all strings? | Undecidable | Reduction from $HALT$ |
| Is a given Diophantine equation solvable? | Undecidable | Matiyasevich's theorem (Hilbert's 10th problem) |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compiler design:** Decidability of $A_{CFG}$ underpins the existence of parsers (YACC, ANTLR). Undecidability of $A_{PDA}$ for general pushdown automata shows why parser generators use restricted forms (LL($k$), LR($k$)).

- **Software verification:** Undecidability of $HALT$ and $E_{TM}$ proves that **no perfect, fully automatic bug detector** can exist — motivating the field of static analysis with soundness/completeness trade-offs.

- **Cryptography & security:** Many cryptographic reductions rely on the **undecidability** of certain problems (e.g., Post's Correspondence Problem is undecidable), making some attacks provably impossible to automate.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Formal Proof — Closure of RE Under Union

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem:** If $L_1$ and $L_2$ are recursively enumerable languages, then $L_1 \cup L_2$ is also recursively enumerable.

### Proof by Explicit Construction

**Step 1 — Hypothesis.**
By definition, there exist TMs $M_1$ and $M_2$ such that $L(M_1) = L_1$ and $L(M_2) = L_2$.

**Step 2 — Construct $M$ for $L_1 \cup L_2$.**
We construct a 2-tape TM $M =$ "On input $w$":

1. Run $M_1$ on $w$ on tape 1.
2. Run $M_2$ on $w$ on tape 2.
3. If either $M_1$ or $M_2$ accepts, then $M$ **accepts**.
4. If both reject, then $M$ **rejects**.

**Step 3 — Multi-tape to Single-tape Conversion.**
A 2-tape TM can be simulated by a single-tape TM with quadratic slowdown (a known standard construction). So without loss of generality, $M$ can be a single-tape TM.

**Step 4 — Correctness.**
- If $w \in L_1 \cup L_2$, then $w \in L_1$ or $w \in L_2$ (or both). At least one of $M_1, M_2$ will accept $w$, so $M$ accepts. ✅
- If $w \notin L_1 \cup L_2$, then $w \notin L_1$ and $w \notin L_2$. Both machines may either reject or loop; $M$ will only reject if **both** reject. But $M$ may loop if one loops. This is **acceptable** for r.e. (we only need to recognize $L$, not its complement). ✅

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**
$L(M) = L_1 \cup L_2$. Hence $L_1 \cup L_2 \in RE$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Formal Proof — RE is NOT Closed Under Complement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem:** There exists a recursively enumerable language $L$ such that $\overline{L}$ is **not** recursively enumerable.

### Proof by Existence

**Step 1 — Recall the known fact.**
The acceptance problem $A_{TM} = \{ \langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ is a TM and } M \text{ accepts } w\}$ is **r.e.** but **not recursive**.

**Step 2 — Key Lemma.**
For any language $L$: if both $L$ and $\overline{L}$ are r.e., then $L$ is recursive.

**Proof of Lemma:** Suppose $L, \overline{L} \in RE$. Then there exist TMs $M_1$ for $L$ and $M_2$ for $\overline{L}$. Construct a decider $D$ for $L$:

1. On input $w$, run $M_1$ and $M_2$ in parallel (dovetailing) on $w$.
2. If $M_1$ accepts, $D$ **accepts**.
3. If $M_2$ accepts, $D$ **rejects**.

Since $w \in L$ or $w \in \overline{L}$ (exclusively), one of $M_1, M_2$ must accept. So $D$ always halts. Thus $D$ decides $L$, so $L$ is recursive. $\blacksquare$

**Step 3 — Apply the Lemma to $A_{TM}$.**
$A_{TM}$ is r.e. Suppose $\overline{A_{TM}}$ were also r.e. By the Lemma, $A_{TM}$ would be recursive. But $A_{TM}$ is **known** to be non-recursive (a Module 4 cornerstone). **Contradiction.**

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**
$\overline{A_{TM}}$ is **not** r.e. Therefore, the class $RE$ is **not closed under complementation**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Reduction from $HALT_{TM}$ to $A_{TM}$ — Worked Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem:** $A_{TM}$ is **undecidable** (i.e., not recursive).
> **Proof Strategy:** Reduce $HALT_{TM}$ to $A_{TM}$.

### Step-by-Step Reduction

**Step 1 — Assumption (for contradiction).**
Suppose there exists a decider $R$ for $A_{TM}$. We will construct a decider $S$ for $HALT_{TM}$, contradicting the known undecidability of $HALT_{TM}$.

**Step 2 — Construction of $S$.**
Define $S =$ "On input $\langle M, w \rangle$, where $M$ is a TM and $w$ is a string":

1. Construct a new TM $M' =$ "On input $x$:
   a. Run $M$ on $w$.
   b. If $M$ halts, **accept** $x$."
2. Run the decider $R$ on input $\langle M', w \rangle$.
3. If $R$ accepts, $S$ **accepts** (meaning $M$ halts on $w$).
4. If $R$ rejects, $S$ **rejects** (meaning $M$ does not halt on $w$, or never accepts $w$).

**Step 3 — Case Analysis.**

| Case | Behaviour of $M$ on $w$ | Behaviour of $M'$ on $w$ | Output of $R$ on $\langle M', w \rangle$ | Output of $S$ on $\langle M, w \rangle$ |
|:----:|:----------------------:|:------------------------:|:---------------------------------------:|:-------------------------------------:|
| 1 | $M$ halts and accepts | $M'$ accepts $w$ | Accept | $S$ accepts ✅ |
| 2 | $M$ halts and rejects | $M'$ accepts $w$ | Accept | $S$ accepts ✅ (correctly: $M$ halts) |
| 3 | $M$ loops forever | $M'$ loops forever on $w$ | Reject | $S$ rejects ✅ (correctly: $M$ does not halt) |

**Step 4 — Equivalence.**
From the matrix, $S$ accepts $\langle M, w \rangle$ $\iff$ $M$ halts on $w$. So $S$ decides $HALT_{TM}$.

**Step 5 — Contradiction.**
$HALT_{TM}$ is undecidable (proved by Turing via diagonalization). Hence $R$ cannot exist.

**Step 6 — Conclusion.**
$A_{TM}$ is undecidable. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — The Big Picture of RE Classes

This matrix is the **single most important figure** for KTU 14-mark questions on this topic.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RE} \cap \text{co-RE} &= \text{Recursive} \\
\text{RE} \setminus \text{co-RE} &\neq \emptyset \quad (\text{e.g., } A_{TM}) \\
\text{co-RE} \setminus \text{RE} &\neq \emptyset \quad (\text{e.g., } \overline{A_{TM}}) \\
(\text{RE} \setminus \text{co-RE}) \cup (\text{co-RE} \setminus \text{RE}) \cup \text{Recursive} &= \text{RE} \cup \text{co-RE} \subsetneq \mathcal{P}(\Sigma^*)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Where do all languages live?** Outside $RE \cup co\text{-}RE$, there are languages that are **neither r.e. nor co-r.e.** — e.g., $EQ_{TM}$. These are the "most undecidable" of all problems.

---

## 3.5 Closure Proof — R is Closed Under Complement

**Step 1 — Hypothesis.** Let $L \in R$. Then there is a decider $D$ for $L$ that always halts.

**Step 2 — Construction.** Build $D' =$ "On input $w$: run $D$ on $w$. If $D$ accepts, **reject**. If $D$ rejects, **accept**."

**Step 3 — Correctness.** $D'$ accepts $w$ $\iff$ $D$ rejects $w$ $\iff$ $w \notin L$ $\iff$ $w \in \overline{L}$.

**Step 4 — Halting.** Since $D$ always halts, $D'$ also always halts. Hence $D'$ decides $\overline{L}$, so $\overline{L} \in R$. $\blacksquare$

## 4.1 The Language Class Hierarchy (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["ALL LANGUAGES P(Sigma*)"]:::outer
    B["Recursively Enumerable RE"]:::re
    C["Recursive R = Decidable"]:::rec
    D["Context-Sensitive"]:::cs
    E["Context-Free"]:::cf
    F["Regular"]:::reg
    G["co-RE"]:::core
    H["Outside RE U co-RE"]:::outside

    A --> B
    A --> G
    A --> H
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    B -. "not subset" .-> G

    classDef outer fill:#fbe9e7,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef re fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** $RE$ and $co\text{-}RE$ are **incomparable** (neither is a subset of the other) but their intersection is exactly the class of **Recursive** languages.

## 4.2 TM Behaviour Flowchart — Decider vs Recognizer

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Start
    Start --> ReadInput: Read w
    ReadInput --> Compute: Simulate M on w
    Compute --> Accept: w in L
    Compute --> Reject: w not in L
    Compute --> Loop: r.e. machine may loop on NO

    Accept --> [*]
    Reject --> [*]
    Loop --> [*]

    note right of Loop
        Only the r.e. (Turing Recognizable)
        machine can reach this state.
        A Decider (Recursive) NEVER loops.
    end note
```

## 4.3 Reduction Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Known Undecidable Problem X"]:::src --> R["Reduction Function f"]:::red
    R --> B["Target Problem Y"]:::tgt
    B --> D["Suppose Decider for Y exists"]:::dec
    D --> A

    classDef src fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
    classDef red fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef tgt fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef dec fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The standard reduction template. Assume $Y$ is decidable (by decider $D$). Use $D$ to construct a decider for $X$. But $X$ is known to be undecidable. Contradiction. Hence $Y$ is undecidable.

## 4.4 Dovetailing Construction (Parallel Simulation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S0["Start: input w"]:::s --> S1["Step i = 1"]
    S1 --> S2["Run M1 on w for 1 step"]
    S2 --> S3["Run M2 on w for 1 step"]
    S3 --> S4{"Did M1 accept?"}
    S4 -->|Yes| S5["Accept w (D accepts)"]:::ok
    S4 -->|No| S6{"Did M2 accept?"}
    S6 -->|Yes| S7["Reject w (D rejects)"]:::bad
    S6 -->|No| S8["i = i + 1, continue"]
    S8 --> S2

    classDef s fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    classDef ok fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef bad fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Dovetailing** is the key technique used in the proof that "RE ∩ co-RE = Recursive". Each simulated machine is given one more step in alternating fashion. Since at least one must eventually halt on any input $w$, the simulation is guaranteed to terminate.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the following: (i) Recursive language, (ii) Recursively enumerable language. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

> **(i) Recursive Language:** A language $L$ is called **recursive** (or **decidable**) if there exists a Turing Machine $M$ that halts on every input and accepts $w$ iff $w \in L$.
>
> **Example:** $A_{DFA} = \{ \langle B, w \rangle \mid B \text{ is a DFA and } w \in L(B) \}$.
>
> **(ii) Recursively Enumerable Language:** A language $L$ is **recursively enumerable** (r.e.) if there exists a TM $M$ such that $M$ accepts $w$ whenever $w \in L$ (but may loop on $w \notin L$).
>
> **Example:** $A_{TM} = \{ \langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ is a TM that accepts } w \}$.

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State and prove: $L$ is recursive iff both $L$ and $\overline{L}$ are r.e.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Statement:** A language $L$ is recursive $\iff$ both $L$ and $\overline{L}$ are recursively enumerable.
>
> **($\Rightarrow$)** If $L$ is recursive, decider $D$ for $L$ is an r.e. machine for $L$. By swapping accept/reject, $D$ becomes an r.e. machine for $\overline{L}$. So both are r.e.
>
> **($\Leftarrow$)** If $L$ and $\overline{L}$ are r.e., let $M_1$ and $M_2$ recognize them. Construct decider $D$: dovetail $M_1$ and $M_2$ on input $w$. Exactly one will accept. If $M_1$ accepts, $D$ accepts; if $M_2$ accepts, $D$ rejects. $D$ always halts, so $L$ is recursive. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Define the terms recursive language and recursively enumerable language. Prove that if a language $L$ and its complement $\overline{L}$ are both recursively enumerable, then $L$ is recursive. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Show that the Halting Problem $HALT_{TM} = \{\langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ is a TM that halts on input } w\}$ is undecidable. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Definitions.** (As given in Section 1.1, awarded 2 marks.)

**Step 2 — Construction of Decider.** Assume $L, \overline{L} \in RE$. So there exist TMs $M_1$ (for $L$) and $M_2$ (for $\overline{L}$). Construct $D$:

$$
D = \text{"On input } w:
\begin{cases}
\text{Step 1: Run } M_1 \text{ and } M_2 \text{ on } w \text{ in parallel (dovetailing).} \\
\text{Step 2: If } M_1 \text{ accepts first, ACCEPT.} \\
\text{Step 3: If } M_2 \text{ accepts first, REJECT.}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — Halting Argument.** For any $w \in \Sigma^*$, exactly one of $w \in L$ or $w \in \overline{L}$ holds. So one of $M_1, M_2$ **must** eventually accept. Therefore $D$ always halts in finite time.

**Step 4 — Correctness.**

$$
w \in L \iff M_1 \text{ accepts } w \iff D \text{ accepts } w.
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion.** $D$ decides $L$, so $L$ is recursive. $\blacksquare$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Assume for contradiction** that $HALT_{TM}$ is decidable. Then there exists a decider $R$ that, on input $\langle M, w \rangle$, accepts if $M$ halts on $w$ and rejects otherwise.

**Step 2 — Use $R$ to construct decider $S$ for $A_{TM}$** (the acceptance problem). $A_{TM}$ is known to be undecidable — we will reach a contradiction.

$$
S = \text{"On input } \langle M, w \rangle:
\begin{cases}
\text{Step 1: Run } R \text{ on } \langle M, w \rangle. \\
\text{Step 2: If } R \text{ rejects, REJECT (since } M \text{ does not halt on } w\text{).} \\
\text{Step 3: If } R \text{ accepts, run } M \text{ on } w. \\
\quad \text{ If } M \text{ accepts, ACCEPT.} \\
\quad \text{ If } M \text{ rejects, REJECT.}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — Correctness Analysis.**

| Case | $M$ on $w$ | $R$ on $\langle M, w \rangle$ | $S$ on $\langle M, w \rangle$ |
|:----:|:----------:|:----------------------------:|:----------------------------:|
| $M$ accepts $w$ | Halts & Accepts | Accepts | Accepts ✅ |
| $M$ halts & rejects | Halts & Rejects | Accepts | Rejects ✅ |
| $M$ loops forever | Loops | Rejects | Rejects ✅ |

**Step 4 — Conclusion.** $S$ decides $A_{TM}$, contradicting the known undecidability of $A_{TM}$. Hence $HALT_{TM}$ is undecidable. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Not specifying the reduction direction clearly.** Always state: "We reduce $A_{TM}$ to $HALT_{TM}$" or "We reduce $HALT_{TM}$ to $A_{TM}$" — be explicit.
> 2. **Forgetting the halting loop case.** Students often miss the third row of the table (where $M$ loops forever). The case analysis must be **exhaustive**.
> 3. **No proper assumption-for-contradiction framing.** Marks are reserved for stating the assumption and the contradiction clearly.
> 4. **Mixing up r.e. and recursive.** Always clarify *what* you are proving: undecidable = not recursive (NOT the same as "not r.e.").

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** What is the difference between a recursive and a recursively enumerable language? Show that the class of recursively enumerable languages is closed under union. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Prove that $A_{TM} = \{ \langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ is a TM that accepts } w \}$ is undecidable. Show that its complement $\overline{A_{TM}}$ is not recursively enumerable. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Difference Table.** (3 marks)

| Property | Recursive | Recursively Enumerable |
|:---------|:---------:|:----------------------:|
| Halts on every input | ✅ Yes | ❌ Not necessarily |
| Decides membership | ✅ Yes | ❌ Only accepts strings in $L$ |
| Complement is r.e. | ✅ Yes | ❌ Not always |
| Equivalent class | Decidable | Semi-decidable / Turing-recognizable |

**Step 2 — Closure Under Union (Proof).** (4 marks)

Let $L_1, L_2 \in RE$ with recognizers $M_1, M_2$. Construct a 2-tape TM $M$:

$$
M = \text{"On input } w:
\begin{cases}
\text{1. Copy } w \text{ onto tape 1 and tape 2.} \\
\text{2. Run } M_1 \text{ on tape 1 and } M_2 \text{ on tape 2 in parallel.} \\
\text{3. If either accepts, ACCEPT.}
\end{cases}
$$

**Correctness:** $w \in L_1 \cup L_2 \iff w \in L_1 \text{ or } w \in L_2 \iff M_1 \text{ or } M_2 \text{ accepts } w \iff M \text{ accepts } w$. So $L(M) = L_1 \cup L_2$, hence $L_1 \cup L_2 \in RE$. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> Multi-tape TMs can be simulated by single-tape TMs with quadratic time overhead, so the construction is valid in the standard TM model.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Undecidability of $A_{TM}$ (Proof by contradiction).** (4 marks)

Assume $A_{TM}$ is decidable. Then there exists a decider $R$ for $A_{TM}$. Use $R$ to construct a decider for the Halting Problem:

$$
H = \text{"On input } \langle M, w \rangle:
\begin{cases}
\text{1. Construct } M' = \text{"On input } x\text{: run } M \text{ on } w\text{; if } M \text{ halts, accept } x\text{"}. \\
\text{2. Run } R \text{ on } \langle M', w \rangle. \\
\text{3. If } R \text{ accepts, ACCEPT (meaning } M \text{ halts on } w\text{).} \\
\text{4. If } R \text{ rejects, REJECT.}
\end{cases}
$$

$M$ halts on $w$ $\iff$ $M'$ accepts $w$ $\iff$ $R$ accepts $\langle M', w \rangle$ $\iff$ $H$ accepts $\langle M, w \rangle$.

This makes $H$ a decider for $HALT_{TM}$, contradicting its undecidability. Hence $A_{TM}$ is undecidable. $\blacksquare$

**Step 2 — $\overline{A_{TM}}$ is not r.e.** (3 marks)

By the theorem proved in Part (a) of Question A: if $L$ and $\overline{L}$ are both r.e., then $L$ is recursive. Since $A_{TM}$ is r.e. (it is recognized by the universal TM) but **not recursive**, its complement $\overline{A_{TM}}$ cannot be r.e. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Deductions for Part (b):**
> 1. **Circularity:** Many students use the undecidability of $A_{TM}$ to prove the undecidability of $HALT_{TM}$ and then use $HALT_{TM}$ to prove $A_{TM}$ undecidable. This is **circular**. Use a *third* known undecidable problem (e.g., the diagonalization language $L_d$) for one of the proofs.
> 2. **Not invoking the complement theorem:** For "$\overline{A_{TM}}$ is not r.e.", you MUST cite the theorem "$L$ recursive $\iff$ $L, \overline{L}$ both r.e." — without it, marks are lost.
> 3. **Forgetting that $A_{TM}$ is r.e.:** A common error. $A_{TM}$ is recognized by the **Universal Turing Machine** $U$, hence is r.e. This must be stated.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (for the night before the exam):**

- 🔹 **Recursive = Decidable** = TM that **always halts** (accept OR reject). ✅
- 🔹 **Recursively Enumerable = Turing Recognizable** = TM that accepts strings in $L$, but **may loop** on strings not in $L$. ⚠️
- 🔹 The **proper inclusion** holds: **Regular ⊊ CFL ⊊ CSL ⊊ Recursive ⊊ RE**.
- 🔹 The class **$RE$ is NOT closed under complementation**. This is the single most-tested property.
- 🔹 The class **$R$ (Recursive) is closed under complementation** (by swapping accept/reject states of the decider).
- 🔹 **$L$ recursive $\iff$ $L, \overline{L}$ both r.e.** (the dovetailing construction is the proof).
- 🔹 $A_{TM}$ is r.e. but **not recursive**. Therefore $\overline{A_{TM}}$ is **not r.e.**.
- 🔹 $HALT_{TM}$ is r.e. but **not recursive**.
- 🔹 All regular and context-free languages are **recursive** (decidable).
- 🔹 **Reduction is the standard technique for proving undecidability.** Reduce a known undecidable problem to the target.
- 🔹 **Diagonalization** (Turing, 1936) is the technique for the *base* undecidability result.
- 🔹 **Dovetailing** = simulate multiple TMs in parallel, one step at a time alternately. Used in RE ∩ co-RE = R proof.
- 🔹 Rice's Theorem: **Every non-trivial semantic property** of TMs is undecidable. (Mention this if asked for "general" undecidability.)
- 🔹 Remember the standard deciders: $A_{DFA}, A_{NFA}, A_{REX}, E_{DFA}, EQ_{DFA}, A_{CFG}$ — all **recursive**.
- 🔹 Remember the standard undecidables: $A_{TM}, HALT_{TM}, E_{TM}, EQ_{TM}, REGULAR_{TM}, A_{PDA}$ — all **not recursive**.
- 🔹 **No decider exists** for $HALT_{TM}$ — this is the cornerstone of theoretical computer science. It implies no perfect virus detector, no perfect program verifier, no perfect compiler optimizer exists.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for the Hierarchy:** **"Raja's Children Fight Strange Recursive RE-ptiles"**
> **R**egular → **C**ontext-free → **C**ontext-**S**ensitive → **R**ecursive → **RE**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chomsky Classification of Formal Languages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET414 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Chomsky Classification of Formal Languages ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition The **Chomsky Hierarchy** is a containment hierarchy of four classes of formal grammars that generate four corresponding classes of formal languages. Proposed by linguist **Noam Chomsky** in **1956**, it provides a strict mathematical classification based on the structural restrictions placed on the **production rules** of a grammar. A formal grammar is a 4-tuple **G = (V, T, P, S)** where: - **V** = Finite set of non-terminal (variable) symbols - **T** = Finite set of terminal symbols - **P** = Finite set of production rules - **S ∈ V** = Start symbol The four types are: - **Type 0** — Unrestricted (Recursively Enumerable) - **Type 1** — Context-Sensitive - **Type 2** — Context-Free - **Type 3** — Regular > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is fundamental for understanding *which class of languages can be recognized by which class of machines*. Every higher type strictly contains the lower types — i.e., **Type 3 ⊂ Type 2 ⊂ Type 1 ⊂ Type 0**. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Languages as Buildings" Imagine formal languages as buildings in a city: - A **Regular Language** is a small **single-story hut** — built from simple, repeating bricks. A person with just a flashlight (Finite Automaton) can patrol it. - A **Context-Free Language** is a **multi-story apartment** with nested rooms (matching brackets, palindromes). You need a person with a backpack and stack (PDA) to remember which floor you came from. - A **Context-Sensitive Language** is a **building whose walls change based on the room you are in** — context matters. You need a careful inspector with bounded memory (LBA). - A **Recursively Enumerable Language** is the **entire universe of possible structures** — you may or may not ever finish inspecting it. You need a full Turing Machine. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** As we go *up* the hierarchy, the production rules become *less restrictive*, allowing us to describe *more* (and more complex) languages — but the recognizing machines also become *more powerful* and *harder to implement*. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Four Grammar Types — Detailed Breakdown #### **Type 3 — Regular Grammar (Most Restrictive)** Production rules of the form: - **Right-Linear:** $A \rightarrow aB$ or $A \rightarrow a$ - **Left-Linear:** $A \rightarrow Ba$ or $A \rightarrow a$ Where **A, B ∈ V** and **a ∈ T ∪ {ε}**. - Corresponding Language Class: **Regular Languages (L₃)** - Recognized by: **Finite Automaton (DFA/NFA)** - Example Application: Lexical analysis in compilers, pattern matching, regex. #### **Type 2 — Context-Free Grammar (CFG)** Production rules of the form: $$A \rightarrow \gamma, \quad A \in V, \quad \gamma \in (V \cup T)^{*}$$ The Left-Hand Side (LHS) is a *single* non-terminal; the Right-Hand Side (RHS) can be any string. - Corresponding Language Class: **Context-Free Languages (L₂)** - Recognized by: **Pushdown Automaton (PDA)** - Example Application: Syntax of programming languages, XML/HTML parsing, arithmetic expressions. #### **Type 1 — Context-Sensitive Grammar (CSG)** Production rules of the form: $$\alpha A \beta \rightarrow \alpha \gamma \beta, \quad A \in V, \alpha, \beta \in (V \cup T)^{*}, \gamma \in (V \cup T)^{+}$$ Equivalently, written as: $$\alpha \rightarrow \beta \quad \text{where} \quad \mid \alpha \mid \leq \mid \beta \mid$$ The non-terminal **A** is replaced by **γ** only when it appears in the specific *context* **α … β**. The string length never decreases. - Corresponding Language Class: **Context-Sensitive Languages (L₁)** - Recognized by: **Linear Bounded Automaton (LBA)** - Example Application: Natural language syntax, certain biological sequence patterns. #### **Type 0 — Unrestricted Grammar (No Restrictions)** Production rules of the form: $$\alpha \rightarrow \beta, \quad \alpha \in (V \cup T)^{+}, \quad \beta \in (V \cup T)^{*}$$ The LHS must contain **at least one non-terminal** (ensuring derivation is possible). - Corresponding Language Class: **Recursively Enumerable Languages (L₀)** - Recognized by: **Turing Machine (TM)** - Example Application: Modeling any computable function, general problem solving. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Grammar Type | Production Rule Form | Automaton (Recognizer) | Language Class | Containment | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **Type 0** | $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ (no restriction) | Turing Machine | Recursively Enumerable (RE) | $L_0$ (Largest) | | **Type 1** | $\alpha A \beta \rightarrow \alpha \gamma \beta$ with $\mid \gamma \mid \geq 1$ | Linear Bounded Automaton | Context-Sensitive (CSL) | $L_1 \subset L_0$ | | **Type 2** | $A \rightarrow \gamma$ | Pushdown Automaton | Context-Free (CFL) | $L_2 \subset L_1$ | | **Type 3** | $A \rightarrow aB$ or $A \rightarrow a$ | Finite Automaton | Regular (RL) | $L_3 \subset L_2$ (Smallest) | > [!NOTE] > **Length Property of Type 1:** $ \mid \alpha \mid \leq \mid \beta \mid $. This is the **monotonic** property — strings never shrink during derivation. The single exception is the rule $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$, permitted only if **S does not appear on the RHS** of any rule. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Compiler Design:** Regular grammars (Type 3) generate tokens; Context-Free grammars (Type 2) generate parse trees for program syntax. - **Network Protocols:** Many protocol message formats are specified using Context-Free or Context-Sensitive grammars. - **Bioinformatics:** DNA/Protein pattern matching uses regular and context-free models. - **Software Verification:** Model checking uses automata theory rooted in this hierarchy. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Derivation — String Generation Using a Context-Free Grammar Consider the following **Context-Free Grammar (Type 2)**: $$G = (\{S, A, B\}, \{a, b\}, P, S)$$ With production set **P**: - $S \rightarrow aA \mid bB$ - $A \rightarrow aA \mid aB$ - $B \rightarrow bB \mid \varepsilon$ **Goal:** Derive the string **$aab$** and identify the language. **Step 1 — Start with the start symbol S.** $$S$$ **Step 2 — Apply rule $S \rightarrow aA$.** $$ S \Rightarrow aA $$ **Step 3 — Apply rule $A \rightarrow aA$.** $$ S \Rightarrow aA \Rightarrow aaA $$ **Step 4 — Apply rule $A \rightarrow aB$.** $$ S \Rightarrow aA \Rightarrow aaA \Rightarrow aaaB $$ **Step 5 — Apply rule $B \rightarrow \varepsilon$ to eliminate B.** $$ S \Rightarrow aA \Rightarrow aaA \Rightarrow aaaB \Rightarrow aaa $$ So $aaa$ is in the language. **General Language Identified:** $$L(G) = \{a^{n} b^{m} \mid n \geq 1, m \geq 0\}$$ ### 3.2 Analytical Method — Classifying a Given Grammar To classify a grammar, examine the **production rules** and apply the decision table: | If every production has the form... | Then the grammar is... | |:---|:---:| | $A \rightarrow aB$ or $A \rightarrow a$ (or left-linear variant) | **Type 3 (Regular)** | | $A \rightarrow \gamma$ (single non-terminal on LHS) | **Type 2 (Context-Free)** | | $\alpha A \beta \rightarrow \alpha \gamma \beta$ with $ \mid \alpha \mid \leq \mid \beta \mid $ | **Type 1 (Context-Sensitive)** | | $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ with no other restrictions | **Type 0 (Unrestricted)** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Classification Rule of Thumb:** Always classify to the **highest** (most general) type that a grammar satisfies. For example, if a grammar is Regular, it is *also* technically a CFG, but we always classify it as **Type 3**. ### 3.3 Proof Sketch — Hierarchy Containment $L_3 \subset L_2 \subset L_1 \subset L_0$ **Claim:** Every Regular Language is also a Context-Free Language. **Reasoning:** Step 1: A Regular Grammar G has only rules of the form $A \rightarrow aB$ or $A \rightarrow a$. Step 2: These rules are a *special case* of CFG rules $A \rightarrow \gamma$ where $\gamma = aB$ or $\gamma = a$. Step 3: Therefore, every production of a Regular Grammar is *already* a valid CFG production. Step 4: Hence, the set of all regular grammars is a subset of all CFGs. Step 5: So, $L_3 \subseteq L_2$. Since there exist CFGs that are not regular (e.g., $S \rightarrow aSb \mid \varepsilon$ generates $\{a^{n}b^{n}\}$), the inclusion is **strict**: $L_3 \subset L_2$. By identical logic, $L_2 \subset L_1 \subset L_0$. ### 3.4 Closure and Decision Properties Table | Property | Type 3 (Regular) | Type 2 (CF) | Type 1 (CS) | Type 0 (RE) | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | Union | ✓ Closed | ✓ Closed | ✓ Closed | ✓ Closed | | Intersection | ✓ Closed | ✗ Not Closed | ✓ Closed | ✓ Closed | | Complement | ✓ Closed | ✗ Not Closed | ✓ Closed | ✗ Not Closed | | Membership Testing | $O(n)$ (DFA) | $O(n^3)$ (CYK) | PSPACE | Undecidable | | Emptiness | Decidable | Decidable | Decidable | Undecidable | | Equivalence | Decidable | Decidable | Decidable | Undecidable | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The Chomsky Hierarchy — Language Class Containment ```mermaid graph TD L0["Type 0 — Recursively Enumerable
L0 = L RE"] L1["Type 1 — Context-Sensitive
L1 subset L0"] L2["Type 2 — Context-Free
L2 subset L1"] L3["Type 3 — Regular
L3 subset L2"] L0 ==> L1 L1 ==> L2 L2 ==> L3 ``` ### 4.2 Grammar-to-Automaton Correspondence (Block-Level Architecture) ```mermaid graph LR G0["Type 0 Grammar
Unrestricted"] G1["Type 1 Grammar
Context-Sensitive"] G2["Type 2 Grammar
Context-Free"] G3["Type 3 Grammar
Regular"] A0["Turing Machine
Unlimited Tapes"] A1["Linear Bounded Automaton
Bounded Tape"] A2["Pushdown Automaton
Stack Memory"] A3["Finite Automaton
No Memory"] L0["L0: Recursively Enumerable"] L1["L1: Context-Sensitive"] L2["L2: Context-Free"] L3["L3: Regular"] G0 --> A0 G1 --> A1 G2 --> A2 G3 --> A3 A0 --> L0 A1 --> L1 A2 --> L2 A3 --> L3 ``` ### 4.3 Production Rule Power vs. Recognizing Machine Power ```mermaid graph TD subgraph "Increasing Power of Machines" direction LR FA["FA
Finite Automaton"] PDA["PDA
Pushdown Automaton"] LBA["LBA
Linear Bounded"] TM["TM
Turing Machine"] end subgraph "Increasing Power of Grammars" direction LR REG["Regular"] CFG["Context-Free"] CSG["Context-Sensitive"] UNR["Unrestricted"] end REG -.generates.-> FA CFG -.generates.-> PDA CSG -.generates.-> LBA UNR -.generates.-> TM ``` ### 4.4 Membership Decision Process for "Is a string in L(G)?" ```mermaid graph TD A0[Start: String w and Grammar G] --> B0{Identify Grammar Type} B0 -- Type 3 --> C3["Use DFA / NFA
O to the n simulation"] B0 -- Type 2 --> C2["Use CYK Algorithm
O to the n cubed"] B0 -- Type 1 --> C1["Use LBA Simulation
PSPACE"] B0 -- Type 0 --> C0["Use Turing Machine
May loop forever"] C3 --> D3[Answer: ACCEPT or REJECT] C2 --> D2[Answer: ACCEPT or REJECT] C1 --> D1[Answer: ACCEPT or REJECT] C0 --> D0[Answer: ACCEPT or LOOP] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Define the Chomsky Hierarchy. List the four types of grammars in increasing order of generative power.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Chomsky Hierarchy** is a classification of formal grammars proposed by Noam Chomsky in 1956, organized by the restrictions placed on production rules. In **increasing order of generative power** (i.e., decreasing restriction): 1. **Type 3 — Regular Grammar** (most restricted) 2. **Type 2 — Context-Free Grammar** 3. **Type 1 — Context-Sensitive Grammar** 4. **Type 0 — Unrestricted Grammar** (least restricted) Each type corresponds to a specific class of languages and recognizing automata. *(Full 3 marks for listing all four types in correct order with hierarchy name.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]** **State the general form of production rules for a Context-Sensitive Grammar. What is the corresponding language recognizer?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A Context-Sensitive Grammar (Type 1) has production rules of the form: $$\alpha A \beta \rightarrow \alpha \gamma \beta, \quad A \in V, \, \alpha, \beta \in (V \cup T)^{*}, \, \gamma \in (V \cup T)^{+}$$ Equivalently, $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ with the constraint that $\mid \alpha \mid \leq \mid \beta \mid$ (monotonic property), with the optional exception of $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ if S never appears on the RHS. The corresponding language recognizer is a **Linear Bounded Automaton (LBA)** — a Turing Machine whose tape length is linearly bounded by the input length. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** Explain the four types of grammars in the Chomsky Hierarchy with the general form of production rules. Identify the automaton that recognizes each language class. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Consider the following grammar: $$G: S \rightarrow aSBC \mid abC, \quad CB \rightarrow BC, \quad bB \rightarrow bb, \quad bC \rightarrow bc, \quad cC \rightarrow cc$$ Show that the string $aabbcc$ is in the language generated by G. Classify the grammar. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution for Question A #### Part (a) — 7 Marks | Type | Production Form | Automaton | Language | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | Type 0 | $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$, $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^{+}$, $\beta \in (V \cup T)^{*}$ | Turing Machine | Recursively Enumerable | | Type 1 | $\alpha A \beta \rightarrow \alpha \gamma \beta$ with $ \mid \gamma \mid \geq 1 $ | Linear Bounded Automaton | Context-Sensitive | | Type 2 | $A \rightarrow \gamma$, $A \in V$, $\gamma \in (V \cup T)^{*}$ | Pushdown Automaton | Context-Free | | Type 3 | $A \rightarrow aB$ or $A \rightarrow a$ (right-linear) | Finite Automaton | Regular | Each higher type is a strict superset of the lower type, i.e., $L_3 \subset L_2 \subset L_1 \subset L_0$. *(7 marks awarded for correct general forms, automaton names, and strict containment property.)* #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Derivation of $aabbcc$:** Step 1: $S \rightarrow aSBC$ (apply rule 1) Step 2: $S \rightarrow aabCBC$ (apply rule 1) Step 3: $S \rightarrow aabBC \cdot C$ (apply rule 1: $abC$) Step 4: $S \Rightarrow aabBCC$ (already there) Step 5: Apply $CB \rightarrow BC$: $$aabBCC \Rightarrow aabBCC$$ (rearranging CB to BC): $aabBC \cdot C \Rightarrow aabBCC \Rightarrow aabBCC$ After applying $CB \rightarrow BC$ repeatedly: $$aabCBC \Rightarrow aabBCC$$ $$aabBCC \Rightarrow aabBC \cdot C$$ Apply $bB \rightarrow bb$: $$aabBCC \Rightarrow aaBBC \cdot C \Rightarrow aabbCC$$ Apply $bC \rightarrow bc$: $$aabbCC \Rightarrow aabbcC$$ Apply $cC \rightarrow cc$: $$aabbcC \Rightarrow aabbcc \checkmark$$ **Classification:** Since the LHS contains non-terminals with surrounding context (e.g., $CB \rightarrow BC$) and rule $bB \rightarrow bb$ changes based on context, this is a **Type 1 (Context-Sensitive) Grammar**. The language generated is $L = \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1\}$. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]** **(a)** State and explain the Chomsky Hierarchy. For each grammar type, write a sample production rule and the language class it generates. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Construct a Context-Free Grammar that generates the language $L = \{a^n b^{2n} \mid n \geq 1\}$. Prove that your grammar is not regular. Classify your grammar. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution for Question B #### Part (a) — 7 Marks The **Chomsky Hierarchy** is a containment hierarchy of formal grammars classified by production rule restrictions: - **Type 0 — Unrestricted:** $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$. Example: $aBc \rightarrow abc$. Generates **Recursively Enumerable Languages**. - **Type 1 — Context-Sensitive:** $\alpha A \beta \rightarrow \alpha \gamma \beta$. Example: $aAb \rightarrow aBBb$. Generates **Context-Sensitive Languages**. - **Type 2 — Context-Free:** $A \rightarrow \gamma$. Example: $S \rightarrow aSb$. Generates **Context-Free Languages**. - **Type 3 — Regular:** $A \rightarrow aB$ or $A \rightarrow a$. Example: $S \rightarrow aS \mid b$. Generates **Regular Languages**. *(7 marks — 1 mark for hierarchy statement + 1.5 marks per type.)* #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Grammar Construction:** Let $G = (\{S\}, \{a, b\}, P, S)$ with productions: $$S \rightarrow aSbb \mid abb$$ **Derivation for $aabb$ (n=1):** $S \Rightarrow abb$ ✓ **Derivation for $aaabbbbbb$ (n=2):** $S \Rightarrow aSbb \Rightarrow a \cdot abb \cdot bb = aabbbbbb$... wait, let's recheck. For n=2 we want 2 a's and 4 b's: $aabbbb$. $$S \Rightarrow aSbb \Rightarrow a \cdot abb \cdot bb = aabbbb \checkmark$$ **Proof that G is not Regular:** Assume for contradiction that $L$ is regular. Then by the **Pumping Lemma for Regular Languages**, there exists a pumping length $p$ such that any string $s \in L$ with $\mid s \mid \geq p$ can be written as $s = xyz$ satisfying: 1. $\mid xy \mid \leq p$ 2. $\mid y \mid \geq 1$ 3. $xy^{i}z \in L$ for all $i \geq 0$ Choose $s = a^{p} b^{2p}$. Since $s \in L$ and $\mid s \mid \geq p$, conditions (1) and (2) force $y = a^{k}$ for some $1 \leq k \leq p$. Pump down with $i = 0$: $xz = a^{p-k} b^{2p}$. For this to be in $L$, we need $p - k = n$ and $2p = 2n$ for some $n$. The first gives $n = p - k$. The second gives $n = p$, which contradicts $n = p - k$ (since $k \geq 1$). Therefore, $xz \notin L$, contradicting the Pumping Lemma. Hence, $L$ is **not regular**. **Classification:** Since the LHS is a single non-terminal $S$ and the RHS contains any string, this is a **Type 2 (Context-Free) Grammar** generating a non-regular context-free language. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Misclassifying mixed grammars:** A single rule of form $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ with no restriction *forces* the entire grammar to be Type 0, regardless of other rules. Always classify to the **highest** (most general) type. > 2. **Forgetting the monotonic property in Type 1:** A rule like $aA \rightarrow \varepsilon$ is **NOT** Type 1 because $ \mid \alpha \mid > \mid \beta \mid $ (length decreases). > 3. **Confusing the hierarchy direction:** As you go **up** the hierarchy (Type 3 → 0), languages get **larger** but machines get **more powerful**. Many students invert this. > 4. **Writing $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ carelessly:** This is only allowed in Type 2 and Type 3, and in Type 1 only for the start symbol S (under specific conditions). > 5. **In Pumping Lemma proofs:** Always choose a string that **forces a contradiction**, not a generic one. Pick $a^{p}b^{2p}$, not $a^{0}b^{0}$. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Chomsky Hierarchy (1956):** Four levels of grammars classified by production rule restrictions — Type 0 (Unrestricted) > Type 1 (Context-Sensitive) > Type 2 (Context-Free) > Type 3 (Regular). - **Strict Containment:** $L_3 \subset L_2 \subset L_1 \subset L_0$. Each level is a *proper* subset of the one above. - **Type 0 — Unrestricted:** Any $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ rule; recognized by a **Turing Machine**; generates **Recursively Enumerable Languages**. - **Type 1 — Context-Sensitive:** $\alpha A \beta \rightarrow \alpha \gamma \beta$ with $ \mid \gamma \mid \geq 1 $; recognized by a **Linear Bounded Automaton**. - **Type 2 — Context-Free:** $A \rightarrow \gamma$ with single non-terminal on LHS; recognized by a **Pushdown Automaton**. - **Type 3 — Regular:** $A \rightarrow aB$ or $A \rightarrow a$; recognized by a **Finite Automaton**; also expressible using regular expressions. - **Monotonic Property:** In Type 1, derivations never shorten the string. The only exception is $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ when S never appears on any RHS. - **Classification Strategy:** Always assign the **highest** type a grammar belongs to. A regular grammar is technically also CF, CS, and Unrestricted, but is *called* Type 3. - **Pumping Lemma:** Essential for proving languages are *not* regular (or not CF for the context-free version). - **Decidability Cliff:** Membership testing gets progressively harder. Regular is linear, CF is cubic (CYK), and Type 0 may not halt. - **Engineering Relevance:** Regular grammars → lexer tokens; CF grammars → parser/syntax trees; higher types → advanced verification and computational models. ---
